review_text: These water bottles are great. Small size is perfect for little hands. Even better, they don't leak. I used this hiking and carried extra water for the kids in my camelback since the bottles are pretty small.

Original Review:I was skeptical at first because I've tried many facial products, but this clay is amazing! I have extremely oily skin and this saved my skin. I also have very sensitive/acne prone skin so when I followed the directions at first, I think I put too much apple cider vinegar in it & it burned my skin. I did not want to give up so I changed my recipe. Here is what I do: 2 tbsp clay + 1 tbsp water + 1.5 tsp apple cider vinegar. Then I blend all with the bottom of a foundation brush and spread the mixture onto my face with the [wet] brush (optional). When I first started using this mask, I did it once a day for the first week, but for a few months now, just once a week does the trick. That's the combination I am successful with. Because we all have different preferences, consistency may vary, so experiment and you will find the right combination of ingredients for you. Also experiment with how often you will need to apply this mask. Good luck!By the way, it feels amazing on your face. If done correctly, your face really does pulsate and I personally love that feeling.Edited Review:I still love using this mask, but I just use my hands to spread it on my face. Doesn't look as pretty though. I also forgot to mention that I like my mixture on the thick side, so if you want a smoother/liquid mixture, obviously just add more water or vinegar.

What can I say? The product is excellent. As of the day I bought it, I could get the best graphics card on the market and it would run fine off this PSU. I wouldn't be able to run SLI with top notch cards but I could manage decent graphics w/o SLI. It has plenty of cables, and as others have mentioned, it has too many but that's only in my case which is a regular mid-size tower (I actually had to hide some of the cables in the empty optical drive space so it wouldn't mess with the airflow in the tower). For those of you who don't tinker much with your computer, it's not difficult to install a PSU (or any other component for that matter). The product is well priced and from what I've investigated it is of exceptional quality. The only reason I give it four stars is for the jumble of cables.As for what I'm running:P4 3.0 Ghz (I know :D, P4, ugh)2GB DDR2 RAM1 DVD/CD Burner2 SATA HD (150, and 500 GB1 Nvidia 8600 GT (Going to upgrade soon to at least a 9800GTX)

A utilitarian case if it is the right size for your equipment. I already had the smaller DCB-304 case for hybrids/compacts so I knew my Sony a3000 with an 18-55mm or larger lens would not fit easily. Unfortunately, while I have opted to use this case for the present it is a little too roomy for my camera. At the cost, I believe it is a better option for transport of a larger bodied DSLR but not necessarily the most preferable option for protection.The two zippered side pockets are useful for storage of extra batteries or cables and the top flap has another zippered compartment that works well for SDHC cards however the bag is not large enough to handle additional lenses.  Padding on all sides is passable but with the requirement to place in the bag lens first, I wouldn't expect the base of the bag to provide a high degree of protection in the event of a drop or inadvertent direct hit of moderate force.

I hate advertising hype. Companies make claims/names for their products and rarely meet those claims/names. Here we have a "Genius Wicked Smart Charger" that cannot even determine the temperature, so much for being smart. Nor cannot it determine the type or size of battery. If it were truly Smart then it should be able to do that. I can never remember which battery is AGM and which is not. We have a lot of equipment that may sit idle for long periods (like a generator) and so we keep the batteries on charge. We currently use Deltran chargers and have had really good luck and I highly recommend Deltran as you can leave them connected indefinitely. Same with this NOCO G3500.Comparing Warranties between Deltran, NOCO and CTEK;Deltran is 10 years and free shipping and zero cost first 90 days, then $7.95 to $25 plus customer pays shipping.NOCO is 5 years with no charges for first 2.5 years but customer pays shipping.CTEK is 2 years but charges/shipping charges are unknown.The NOCO G3500 is a nice charger (with above exceptions) and with lots of features and capability. I really like the cables and cable connectors and much better than Deltran. We currently have quite a few Deltran Chargers with the SAE type connector and now realize that NOCO offers an adaptor to convert the G3500 connect to the SAE type connector (as used on the Deltran chargers). That is a big plus and a good idea, now we can retain our current wire harnesses but use the G3500.Overall I highly recommend this NOCO G3500. If you are using chargers with different connectors, NOCO offers many adapters at reasonable cost.Keeping a battery on charge is only recommended with chargers like the NOCO G3500 that are specifically designed for being connected indefinitely. Using a conventional charger for extended periods can result in damaging the battery, causing the battery to expand and possibly open with acid spilling onto the equipment and causing serious damage.

I've been curious about gel manicures for quite a few months now; really, ever since I first started seeing those Sally Hansen ads for the complete kit on TV. But after looking at the actual products in-store, and seeing that I'd have to shell out seventy bucks for a few tiny bottles of polish and an itty-bitty lamp, I had to stop and think about it. I waited a few months, then mentioned to my best friend that I wished I could get my nail polish to last more than just twelve hours before it started chipping off. That's when she told me that her sister - a chemistry teacher - had decided to try doing gel manicures, and had raved about how beautiful her nails looked weeks after doing them herself, even with having her hands in chemicals all day!I knew then that it was time to come to Amazon and start looking for the perfect products to outfit my set with, that I was going to treat myself to something super-girly for my 40th birthday, and gel manicure components were gonna be IT! The top-rated UV lamp that came up was this Royal Nails dryer, and it turns out that the popularity and high ratings are for GOOD reason! This thing is AWESOME!!!When I got my box, I was amazed at how big this lamp is! It really does, VERY comfortably, accommodate both hands or both feet for simultaneous curing, which is vital to keep from spending an hour trying to get your gel nails done, start to finish. Putting the bulbs in was also not at all difficult, once i'd unpacked the lamp, turned it over, unsnapped the two hooks that keep the bottom closed, and flipped the bottom open (be sure to remove the blue plastic mirror protection film - it's vital to ensuring a full, hard cure of the gel polish)! Once that was done, I was ready to go! The control panel of the lamp is at the back, behind the starburst fan vent, and it's easy enough to figure out on your own how to control it (even though the unit does come with an information pamphlet). There's no 60 second setting - it just goes directly to 90 seconds (1 1/2 minutes), then up from there. Curing my nails for 90, as opposed to 60, seconds has been great for curing them as needed for the foundation coat, and pretty much every other layer needs two minutes, which is the 120 second setting, so the lamp covers all the bases beautifully.With the UV lamp on for 120 seconds, by the end of it, my fingers are just starting to feel warmed, as if I've just laid in the sun, and there's absolutely no discomfort. To ensure that I don't have to worry about too much exposure, I bought myself a pair ofROYAL NAILS ANTI UV GLOVES FOR UV LIGHT/LAMP NAIL DRYER ONLY NAIL EXPOSED, which fit my hands snugly, keeping the fabric out of the way as I apply my polishes while wearing them. The polishes that I bought to round out my manicure set areHarmony Gelish Top and Base "Set of 2" Good Deal!!!, which have been great, and were the full half ounce size, as opposed to the tiny mini bottles that Sally Beauty Supply stocks, for a higher price than I paid here, even! To go with the base and top coats, I got a bottle each ofHarmony Gelish Uv Soak Off Gel Polish -Bronzed (0.5 Oz)- a beautiful, neutral glittery sand color, andHarmony Gelish UV Soak Off Gel Polish Good Gossip, a beautiful, sparkly candy apple or ruby slipper red.I followed all of the steps recommended, both by Harmony Gelish, and various websites, blogs, and in multiple YouTube videos, to successfully do my gel manicures. If you've already done a manicure, the next few steps would be for soaking off the old polish:* A tip that I read recommended filing the surface of the existing gel manicure's polish to make it easier for the Acetone-based polish remover to get through the layers of polish. I then soaked the lint-free pads I'd pre-cut into quarters in standard nail polish remover with Acetone, placed them over my nails, and wrapped them with strips of aluminum foil that I'd pre-cut, and waited about 20 minutes for the remover to soak through the polish (your fingers will start to feel chilled once you've wrapped your fingers in the foil. It's a sign that the aluminum has activated the acetone in your polish remover, and is working as it should).* Once that was done, and the polish was softened and peeling away, I used an orange stick to push off the rest of the polish, then continued prepping my nails, washing my hands thoroughly to begin my new manicure. Once my hands and nails were washed, I used an orange stick to push EVERY bit of cuticle back, and a sharp implement (pocket knife, cuticle trimmer, etc) to carefully trim any cuticle (if you leave cuticle adhered to the nail, it'll cause your gel manicure to lift off). I then used a nail buffer - medium/fine side - to buff any sheen from my ENTIRE nail surface (skip this step and the foundation layer of gel polish may not adhere to your nail, and your polishes may lift). Once each nail was fully, but lightly, buffed, then filed to the shape/length I wanted, I soaked a lint-free pad in regular Isopropyl/rubbing alcohol (91% at least is recommended for gel nails), and wiped the surface of each nail clean. Once that's done, it's on to polish application!* Everything I've read online stresses NOT to touch the polish to your skin! Not so much for safety/health reasons, as that, once the polish adheres to your skin, it WILL cause your manicure to lift! Use an alcohol-soaked Q-tip to wipe away any polish that touches your skin before curing, and remember that, until you cure the polish under the UV/LED light, it's still effectively wet and movable. The first manicure I did, I applied each layer of polish too thickly - like I would've using regular nail polish (which this is NOT!), so the excess polish pooled around my nails' edges, and my manicure started to lift on about the 7th day on a few of my nails. My second time around, I made sure to gently scrub the foundation layer onto my nail using the brush's short bristles, but with a THIN layer, making sure I got all corners of my nail, but pushing out the bulk of the polish to my nail's edges so that each was coated in a thin layer, to the very edge of my nail. After curing, each subsequent layer (which will require a curing PER layer), was applied with an even, thin coat of polish, and each layer was "capped" by wiping a light layer of the polish used for that layer over my nail tips, to seal the color and polish in - this helps to avoid chips. I did four layers of polish overall - foundation (which I wiped with a DRY, lint free wipe or paper towel, just for a dry clean, as the Harmony Gelish instructions suggest), two coats of color - each cured individually, four fingers first for two minutes per coat, then the thumbs by themselves (to prevent the color from running to the side, as happened the first time I did a gel manicure!), two minutes again per layer. Once that was done, I applied a thin layer of topcoat (just don't make it TOO thin!), then cured that for two minutes on my fingers, then cured just my thumbs for the final two minutes.You'll be AMAZED once you've taken your nails out of the lamp after the topcoat layer, and wiped the sticky coating off using a lint free pad or paper towel! The shine and hardness is AMAZING! I'm still shocked by how fully dry and hard my nails are each time I do a gel manicure, and I LOVE looking down at my nails TWO WEEKS LATER to see that my polish job is STILL just as sparkly, wet-shine glossy, and gorgeous as the day I first did them!I did acrylic nails years ago, only for a year, and once I stopped, my nails were TRASHED! So far, my nails are growing out longer with each week, and they look healthy and gorgeous when I soak off my color. With the color on, they're feminine and beautiful! I couldn't be happier, and I have no doubt that THIS lamp is the way to go! Others may be a few bucks less, but they're all smaller, and not strong enough to fully cure your nails for a weeks-long manicure! This lamp looks like one you'd find in a professional salon, if not better, and works just as well, if not better, than the others out there! This thing is worth EVERY PENNY!!! LOVE IT!!!P.S. If you're willing to take a risk, check out ebay for cheap gel polishes from China. The wide selection of colors there is pretty mindblowing, so I've ordered a whole slew of polishes, but I have yet to receive mine, so I'm not sure how they'll compare to the "good" stuff like Harmony Gelish or Ulta's Red Carpet Manicure (which I used to add an extra layer of iridescent glitter over my Good Gossip Gelish polish during my last manicure - it worked just fine with Gelish)! The polishes I got on that other auction site all shipped free, and with 0.5 oz bottles that are about half to a third of the price of what I've gotten here and looked at at my local beauty supply store, for nearly double the product available in local stores, I figure it's worth the risk. I believe all of these polishes are made in China anyway, so I'm willing to risk it to see if they ARE effectively the same! I'll update here if I find my Chinese knock-offs are abysmal! Keeping my fingers crossed, though! ;)*UPDATE*I've received, and used, my first two (compatible)colors of less-expensive made in China gel polish, and so far, it's gone on and cured just fine. I LOVE Harmony Gelish polishes, as they have a gorgeous luster and sheen, and now that I've taken the time to look to see that they're made in America, that's a little more incentive for me to look for more colors when I really want to splurge (but I'll only buy them on Amazon, IF they're the half ounce size). My only real problem is getting gel polishes in a wider variety of colors, which is why I decided to try the made in China colors I found on ebay. So far, the sky blue and corresponding clear chunky glitter gel polish that I've received, have cured just as well as my Harmony Gelish colors did, and they're doing great sandwiched between my Harmony Gelish foundation and Top It Off top coat. I'm very tempted to say that the Harmony Gelish colors seem to have more luster and a more intense, glassy sheen to them, but I'm also being hyper critical of these miC (made in China) soak-off colors.*FURTHER UPDATE: Chinese gel removal issues!*The Chinese gels look pretty decent going on, but the one pair that I used with each other (again, sandwiched between my Harmony Gelish foundation and top coat) hadn't soaked off after 45 MINUTES - DOUBLE the time it takes my American-made Harmony Gelish, my new favorite, IBD Just Gel, or my Red Carpet Manicure glitters - I ended up having to SCRAPE them off with my sharp metal MiC cuticle pusher. So, I rescind my previous declaration - I DON'T think the made-in-China gels are worth the risk (all other Chinese products have been fine, though, except for the pink-and-clear fingertip nail soakers)! Besides, with the Customs restrictions that were put in place at the end of September 2013, it's taken an average of about EIGHT WEEKS for me to get those polishes! Other products arrive closer to the claimed time frame, but the polishes don't, and they smell MUCH stronger than the higher-priced, quality brands, along with looking inconsistent in the polish texture. I've had polishes arrive looking partially dried up and gloppy, with blobs of polish in the bottle, and the average MiC polish I've received is much thicker overall in texture, with far less of the luster and sheen of my Gelish, IBD, and RCM gels. I've now seen, eight bottles over, that the MiC gels I bought on eBay are another case of "you get what you pay for". They're cheap for a reason! NOT worth the money, and the damage to my nails to SCRAPE them off!

Let me start by saying that my wife is not a big fan of hair care products from Dove. My wife's hair is extremely thin and delicate, and therefore needs more attention and special products that do not dramatically burden her allready flat hair.My wife was pleasantly surprised by the result of using this product.  She said her hair fealt nearly as light as a feather and noticed the volume looked better. Usually, all the products that claims "hydration and volume in one" make hair very heavy and result in the scalp getting oily at an alarming rate.  This is not the case with Dove Hair Styling Oxygen Moisture Root Lift Spray; this is the first spray which both deeply moisturizes and adds volume (with strain on the hair).Despite the great volume and hydration, she does not know if she will buy Dove Hair Styling Oxygen Moisture Root Lift Spray again because she already has a favorite product for providing volume. Vaseline Intensive Rescue Repairing Moisture Lotion is a great all-purpose lotion product that should work well for many people.This Vaseline lotion is your typical milky white color and is dispensed in a thin stream out of the pump.  The texture is very light and it's easy to apply to your skin as it's absorbed rather quickly (15-20 seconds of gentle application over a large area will get it to absorb).  I have moderately sensitive skin and this stuff didn't cause any blemishes or irritation during daily use for a week in a dry, winter environment.  It works well on dry spots above my ankles and the backs of my hands.  You can apply it to your face in a pinch, but if you're looking for a face lotion I might go with something just a tad bit lighter.  This Vaseline flavor is touted as fragrance-free, as no perfumes are added.  However, it still smells like...well, lotion...but it's very mild and I'm only taking a half star off because of it.  Some of the "Men" branded lotions in particular have way too strong of a scent that gives me a headache and makes me look like I'm overly anxious about finding a date.  I'm happy to report that's not the case here with this product.  The other half star is lost due to a somewhat tacky feeling I get between my fingers after I apply a generous amount after a shower.  My legs, arms, and backs of my hands are not really sticky after being moisturized, but for some reason the sides of my fingers stick together more than I'd like.  Not a huge problem, but I'll still take another half star off for that.Overall, I think this a bottle of lotion that everyone in the family can use due to it's mild scent and effective, all-purpose nature.

This is a great little player. Very light weight, really negligible, also use this player at the gym and at work while walking around checking pumps and heaters. . Each battery lasts quite a while, over a full day each so far. At 30 Watts RMS, Logitech's Z323 speaker system is the smaller sibling to their 40 Watt RMS Z523 system. As I indicate in my Amazon review of the Z523 system, I am mightily impressed with it, as I am with the Z323 system. As I say in my review of the Z523 system, it makes the other PC/MP3-player type speaker systems I own and have owned obsolete (one major reason for this being that the system features "360 degree" or omnidirectional sound). The decrease in wattage comes at a thirty dollar decrease in the manufacturer's suggested retail price. The subwoofer is smaller (approximately 8 1/2 inches high measuring from the bottom of the feet by 5 1/2 inches wide by 8 inches deep, compared to approximately 10 inches high by 9 X 9 for the Z523 sub), and unlike the bass control knob for the Z523 system, which is conveniently located right below the volume knob on the right satellite, the bass control for the Z323 system is inconveniently located on the upper rear of the sub. I say inconveniently because I like to be able to change the bass level depending on what I'm listening to, and in order to change the bass level on the Z323 system I have to get up and walk around my desk and get down on the floor. With the Z523 system I just reach in front of me to the right speaker.The Z323 satellites are virtually the same size, shape, and design as the Z523 satellites. (One difference is that the Z323 satellites have ports on the front while the Z523 have rear-facing ports.) A side by side "experiential" comparison with the Z523 satellites leads me to rate the Z323 satellites equal or perhaps a tad better, and the highs seem slightly warmer. When playing a selection by the German band Seabound on which the speaker whispers some lyrics (in English), I could make out the words when listening with the Z323s whereas I could not make them out as easily when listening with the Z523s.Classical music sounds great on the Z323 system. A side by side comparison of the Z323 with the Z523 systems playing selections from the recently released recording of Mozart's opera Idomeneo conducted by Rene Jacobs, as well as a sampling of BBC Radio 3, leads me to rate each system equal in sound quality.When listening to rock music played through my laptop with the laptop volume and the subwoofer bass knob turned to the maximum levels, the Z323 subwoofer's sound became distorted and I had to turn the bass knob down to nearly the half-way point before it was undistorted. I have never experienced that with the Z523 system, and sometimes I play that system very loud (e.g., when working out in an adjacent room).Like the Z523 system, the Z323 system delivers "360 degree" or omnidirectional sound, made possible by the fact that the satellites have speakers on their backs as well as their fronts.And like the Z523 system, the Z323 system sounds great whether the source is my laptop, MP3 player, or portable CD player.If I had to choose one system over the other, assuming floor space to accommodate the subwoofer is not an issue and assuming the additional cost of the Z523 over the Z323 is not a deal-breaker, I would choose the Z523 system over the Z323 system for three reasons: Location of the bass control knob is considerably more convenient (for me, at least); I don't like that the Z323 subwoofer sound gets distorted when I turned the audio volume and subwoofer volume to their maximum levels; and because the speakers on the Z323 satellites are unprotected, unlike the speakers on the Z523 satellites which are protected by metal grills. (Regarding the distortion at high volume and high bass level, nothing appears to be loose; the screws that secure the subwoofer speaker to the cabinet are tight and the port cone is not at all loose. My guess is that the smaller subwoofer simply can't handle that much volume. Regarding the unprotected speakers on the Z323 satellites, this matters to me because I seem to be prone to knocking over PC speakers, causing them to fall from desktop to floor, and I anticipate that one of these days the cat will knock one over.) Having said all that, if my set up was such that I worked in a small room and I could place the subwoofer close enough to me on the floor that I could reach down behind it to adjust the bass knob, and I didn't anticipate ever wanting to play the system very loud (by which I mean really blasting it), then I think the Z323 system would be an excellent choice. (I rated it 3 out of 5 stars for "Comfort" and 4 for "Ease of use" because of what to me is the inconvenient location of the bass knob.)

I wanted the EC14 1.4x teleconverter primarily to extend the range of my 70-300 mm lens for bird photography. I'd read all the reviews, critiques and forums beforehand, asked for advice and practical tips and knew what I was getting myself into: more zoom, one stop less of light, meaning 840 mm reach (the four thirds system used by Olympus automatically doubles the range of any lens because of sensor peculiarities) and f/8.0 with the lens fully extended.There's no denying I had my qualms before closing the deal. For that price I could get a decent shorter lens instead, or save up towards another zoom lens. But what sold me on the EC14 was the prospect of getting closer to the birds without lugging a ton of equipment around. So I bought it......And waited for a week before a bright, sunny day came along. I attached it to the E-520 and the 70-300 mm (it couldn't be easier and more fool-proof) and set out to test it. The results were beyond my expectations. I got sharp, bright, close photos of birds and anything else at which I pointed my camera, the lens behaved exactly as it usually does, focus was as good as it is without the teleconverter and f/8.0 was just fine for the light conditions. In fact, the photo geeks always say that the 70-300 mm's "sweet spot" -- the setup in which this lens will produce its sharpest images -- is exactly f/8.0, and now I have to agree with them (at 300 mm I always used this lens at its minimum aperture, 5.6, to get faster shutter speeds).I am really, really happy with the EC14, and having this range with a setup that is still light and easy to carry around the whole day is a dream come true.

I attempted to patch the tube and re-inflate it. The pump did it's job, the patch didn't, and a mile later, I was on a flat again.  I changed the tube, a few more pumps, and off I went again. What a useless little device.  Its really nothing more than a glorified Youtube player.  If you are tech-savvy you probably already have a smart internet-connected TV or a Roku or a laptop with HDMI output, all of which do way more than this guy.  You can "throw" Youtube from your computer to your TV but that's about it.  Oh...and it does Netflix but not if you're running Linux ('cause Netflix refuses to support Linux on philosophical grounds...but that's another tale.)  I didn't test Netflix since we're a Linux household, but I'd expect it works fine.  But again, there are so many other ways to watch Netflix who needs an extra dongle.  By the time you click your remote to select this HDMI device, you could have selected the native Netflix app on the TV or cranked up your Roku.  It does Google Play Movies as well as Google Play Music but this device doesn't add any functionality.  My Google Music connects via bluetooth directly to my sound system.  Why would I want to run the TV just so I can throw music from my phone or computer to the TV and then back through the sound system.Lastly and this is no big deal, but some really smart programmers figured out how to root the Chromecast within days of its release.  They added all kinds of functionality but Google quickly found a way to re-handicap its device forcing an automatic "upgrade" as soon as it powers up the first time.  This blocked the root exploit.  So rather than create a device and let smart people all over the world figure out how to make it do the coolest things, they chose to prevent its real use by keeping it handicapped.So buy this if you really have no way to watch Netflix on your TV.  Or if you want to buy movies from Google Play.  Or just can't get enough Youtube.  (As if....)Google continues to prove its not in the business of creating new and innovative hardware or delivering content in new ways.  Its about collecting information and selling it.This thing didn't add anything to my technology experience.  Thanks to Amazon for accepting a return.

There's few things that suck more than being miles from nowhere with a bike tire that needs to be inflated. The Ultraflate Plus Tire Inflator can bail you out.It's lightweight and small, so it won't take up much room in your bike tool bag. It comes with one 16 gram CO2 cartridge. Replacements can also be obtained from most any local bike shop. Fortunately for those on a budget, the 12 gram CO2 cartridges that you can buy from the sporting goods department at Walmart, Target or K-Mart also work, unaltered. The 16 gram CO2 cartridges can be a bit pricier, so you can save yourself some $$$ by buying the much less expensive 12 gram CO2 cartridges. Most people I've talked to state that they never use the entire contents of the 16 gram CO2 cartridge anyways, when they fill their tires up with a CO2 inflater. I carry a couple of spare 12 gram CO2 cartridges with me in my tool kit.I highly recommend this product.

I have longish, slightly curled eyelashes that I curl only for special occasions.  I've used Revlon and Tarte eyelash curlers before this one, so will compare it with those two brands.  In comparison, I'd give this curler a slight edge even with the caveat (below).Pros:1.  Construction is solid.  This is a sturdy, well-functioning eyelash curler, which I would expect from Tweezerman.  These are definitely better constructed than the Revlon pair, and about on par with my Tarte curler.2.  Comfortable and easy to use.  This a very lightweight curler (even with the solid design), and the finger loops are just the right size for me.  I find the finger loops are easier to manage than the Tarte cushion-grip, and I find it ridiculously easy to fit on my eyelashes.  I don't fumble at all like I do with the Tarte pair.3.  Extra pads included.  They included three extra silicon pads in the package, which is a really nice touch.  The packaging suggests replacing the pad every 3 months or so (with regular use I guess), so this could get annoying if you don't have them on-hand.  Tweezerman took care of this issue, and I don't foresee needing to hunt down new pads for quite some time.4.  Effective curling.  This is the real test, right?  The curler doesn't ever pinch my eyelid, and the silicon pad doesn't even stick to (fully dry) mascara if I use it after makeup.  I generally heat the curler for a few seconds with the hairdryer, which helps no matter what curler I'm using.Caveat:I don't call this a "con," since it's really a matter of preference - BUT this is not a spring-loaded curler, like the Revlon pair.  All the action happens from your fingers manually opening and closing the curler, rather than the curler springing back open after you squeeze.  I don't find this mattered at all, especially since the curler worked pretty much perfectly.  It would be a real issue if the hinge were tight or the pair didn't align properly, but that's not the case.Overall, I'd recommend this curler with confidence for everyday or occasional use.  Great little tool, no need for another  pair.

This cooling solution is, by far, the most superior solution I have implemented in any PC to date. The stock fan in my HTPC wasn't too quiet to begin with, but had gotten increasingly whiny over time. I really wanted:1) A way to keep the CPU cool2) A low profile cooler that would fit into my Silverstone GD05B case3) Most importantly - a fan that was as close to silent as could be, without spending big bucks on liquid coolingYou can tell that this company takes pride in their product. The first thing that struck me was the packaging. The design and layout of how this product was packaged told me a lot about how effective it was going to be. From the flip out box to the color instructions, this company put as much thought into packaging as they did into the product.This fan fit my needs, and then some. First, please know that I do not overclock or do gaming on this PC, so I can't comment on how effective it would be for that type of use. I use it strictly as an HTPC to record and watch TV, stream netflix/Hulu, and convert recorded tv to a more manageable format (this is CPU intensive). For this application, my stock cooler would keep the temp at around 60C. I installed this cooler, and the temp has not gone above 40C since. Temp aside, the most important factor for me was noise level. YOU CAN NOT HEAR THIS FAN AT ALL. I put my head directly next to it to see if it was on. At about 3-4 inches a way, you can only hear it if you really concentrate and listen. Zero noise from across the room.Installation -Installation was a little tricky. You will most likely need to remove the MB to secure the brackets to the backplate if you have an AMD chip, as I do. I used Artic Silver 5 compound, as I have had really good results in the past with it. Couple of notes about installation:1) Make sure you connect the MB connections prior to placing/securing this cooler. Some of the connections (CPU fan and 4 pin MB power) would not have been inaccessible after installation. Plan accordingly.2) Once you place the cooler onto the CPU, you secure it to the brackets with two screws. To access these screws, the screw driver is actually threaded through the heatsink fins (there are 2 square holes on top of heatsink). This is incredible engineering, but it felt a little dangerous, as you could not see exactly where the screwdriver was going. Just take your time with it, and it will work out.3) I used the low noise adapter. I did not test the installation without it, so I am not sure how much impact this had on the noise level. If you want a silent implementation, I would recommend using it. It does not appear to impact cooling performance, and in this configuration, was dead silent.4) I used the 1 fan configuration. This provided all the cooling I needed, so I didn't even try it with the 2nd fan.Couple of pros/cons:Pros:1) Incredibly engineered - Does what it is supposed to - keeps everything very cool, and you cannot hear it2)Packaging, at least to me, justified the higher cost.3) With the 6 year warranty, you can tell that this company stands behind their product.Cons:1) If I had to list one, the only con I would say is that it is pricey. For me, the price for blissful silence is worth it, so not really a con for me.If you are looking for a completely silent CPU fan for you HTPC, I have no hesitation recommending this product. I am overly impressed with Noctua, and will be a repeat customer.

I would have liked to have given this board a full 5 stars...but couldn't. Its a good board, lots of eye candy for over-clockers and gamers, gets full marks for features, however there seems to be some quality control issues with Asus. One of the reasons i bought this board is because it is made by Asus, who are considered la creme-de-la-creme as far MBs go, however all over the internet people are complaining about the heating issue withe the NB and SB heat pipe and cooler, if I had known about it before, i may not have ordered this board. So sure enough when i got this board, i took off the heat pipe and I had the same issue like mostly everybody else. The fix. I had to grind down the NB standoffs to the same height as the SB standoffs, clean of the lame wax Asus used as a Thermal pad, (the NB wasn't even making a proper connection to the wax), apply some OCZ Freeze and then re-seat the heat pipe. My SB temps before the fix was 50c at idle and 55 load, which is the threshold btw, after it dropped to 41 at idle, doesn't reach 50c on load, I live in the tropics and there is no A/C in my room, ambient temps are 26-28c. Asus could have done a lot better. There was no point in RMA'ing the board as the issue is so prevalent, surveys on other websites show as much as 58% of owners of this board have this issue. That was the bad stuff. I bought this together with the AMD Phenom II X6, and its a breeze to OC, just a couple of changes in the Bios and boom!...4Ghz.....nice. I should have taken some pics though, sorry, but u just need too google it, and you'll see. Btw amazon shipped this the next day, cool.

I have recently tried this, the Phantom, the other extreme, the Xenon, the Alp-X and the Countdown jackets. I absolutely love the feel of this insulated and very supple (and waterproof fabric). For me, the arms are a tiny bit long, the shoulders a tiny bit tight in the M size, but in the L size it is clearly overall to big. For what is is worth, the M Phantom is my size, the M Countdown, the L Alp-X and the L-Xenon.It is important to note that the back panel of this is like regular jersey fabric for super breathability but hence NO WATERPROOF/RESIST on the back panel. This is by design, but you should know it. The rest of it is waterproof, however.I love the way this has little short sleeves built into it so you can really turn it into a jersey without the sleeves on it. My main complaint is that for me the sleeves are a tiny bit snug and a tad too long and heavier than I would like. I kind of would like this exactly as it is but with the sleeves in two varieties A) like the ones with it now for extra cold B) really light weight and packable for 'emergency' that you carry all the time. The sleeves as they are now are so bulky I can't see packing them unless the start of the ride is really cold, so I can imagine wishing I had them sometimes out on the road. The Alp-X and Countdown (much lighter) have sleeves so light and compact you can easily cart them along in the pockets.I have now ridden in similar conditions in all the above, and find that the Phantom fits more comfortably and snuggly (a plus) than the Alp-X and Countdown except that the shoulders are a bit too tight and one notices this even when it is sleeveless. It opens up nicely for air flow and the open back panel means that it is not too hot for the warm part of a ride, however.All of these, Alp-X, Phantom and Countdown with the sleeveless aspect have convinced me that when done right it is not just a dumb gimmick.The Alp-X and Countdown are so light when just as vests, that they make the concept of a pure rain shell like the Xenon not important any more for me. I find the Xenon way too overpriced ($199 at the moment). Amazon has the Phantom now for about $120 and the Countdown in my size was just $76 - so the Xenon doesn't cut it.UPDATEBackcounty misrepresents their stock. I ordered a sale Phantom from backcountry through  Amazon here. It went through. Then the company said they had none. They refused to back order. I since then (only a few days later all within about a week) ordered from the SAME company the SAME jacket for a slightly higher price. It arrived today. I called and they say that the jacket is OUT OF STOCK - but amazon STILL lists them as selling it with it IN STOCK.Again, they refuse to honor their price.BACKCOUNTRY IS NOT HONEST

None of the reviews here at the time of my purchase really answered what I wanted to know.  I'm reading about "rough heels" and thinking "I think I need something more powerful than something that makes one's heels smooth."Still, I was torn between this, the Diamancel #11 and the #20 callus device.  The ball of my foot has a major callus that I've tussled with all my adult life.  The #20 looked like it might be akin to a cheese shredder and I already have devices like that- they do work, but leave your skins ripply and ripped up looking.  Maybe #20 was not as unkind as that, but maybe the #11 would be quite enough.For those who want to cut to the chase, yes, this baby was able to work on a serious callus in pretty short order.  The UPS truck dropped it off and I literally sat right down and got to work with it.  Honestly, 5 minutes later and both calluses (the other on the outside of the foot, below the pinkie toe) were about halfway gone.  I can plainly see this works very quickly and will have these painful thick spots gone with a little more work, and leave my skin in much better shape.If you have a ball of the foot callus, you might want to read on below for more thoughts, but if you just want to know if this buffer will work on a terminal stage callus, the answer is heck yeah.- - - - - - - - - -The recurrent callus on the ball of my one foot is the result of a Morton's toe.  Not to bore you, but when you walk, the pressure gets transferred forward from your heel up to your big toe.  If that transfer doesn't happen correctly, you end up moving the pressure to the ball of the foot.  The foot then responds by building up dead skin, and mine gets thick quickly.  Left to its own devices, that callus would allow me to tap dance barefoot if it didn't hurt so much.  Since the transfer is sending pressure away from the big toe and outwards, I've also developed a callus on the outside of the foot below the little toe, like a little tripod balance.  Isn't nature wonderful?I have a freaking collection of files, rollers with blades, a flatter thing with a Solingen steel blade in it, a PediEgg, a knockoff of a PediEgg, acid laced callus discs and so on.  All have their merits and failings.  Most of the stuff files your skin, or acts like a cheese shredder.  For efficiency, the bladed items couldn't be beat.  Yes, the blades have cut me a few times, but they have been the go-to tool to cut off thick skin quickly.  Unfortunately the ball spot had gotten to the point where the skin was so smooth and hard that even the blades couldn't get ahold of anything, and slid right off.  That's why I turned to this "buffer" - which is an innocuous name for a file that works seriously well.If you get calluses on the ball of your foot, you might have a Morton's toe too.  A lot of places incorrectly say it's when your second toe (next to the big one) is longer than the big one, but this is wrong.  It's an internal thing that has to do with bone lengths and is not always visble.The ball of your foot hurts, so your instinct is to put padding where it hurts, as I did for a long time myself.  The answer actually is to put the padding NEXT to the callous - along the big toe down to the side of the ball of the foot.  Then that part of your foot hits the ground before the ball and takes the pressure in the right path.  I did this with thick moleskin and couldn't believe what a difference it made.  The thing is, it's easy to get lazy about doing this, as the moleskin gets wet with daily showering, doesn't stick properly, and is unsightly.  It's also expensive enough that you may not want to replace it daily.  I think the answer for me might be to get a full foot length insole and cut away most of the toe area, leaving just the big toe area down to aside of the ball, and shift it from shoe to shoe.Anyway, if that doesn't work well, no biggie, because this buffer is going to do a terrific job keeping the ball callus away. This light is either the most or one of the most highly reviewed lights on the market, and for good reason.This is a dual beam light which I prefer in the highly unlikely event that a LED would burn out on a ride the other would get you home.  The light is actually brighter then the MagicShine 1100 lumen light I compared it against!  You get what you pay for.  I had no issues with being concerned about overriding the light even when I got up to 32 mph, in fact most of the time I run this light on the medium setting because the high setting is too much, unless I get on a pitch dark country road then I will run it on high just to get more detail, and in well lit city streets I run it on low. I really don't see why someone would want a brighter light unless you're riding off road doing single track stuff.  The light has great side spill to the sides, long range projection, and the light is a white light not a blue white.The light has an OSD feature that allows you to change either the brightness of any of the 3 levels of lighting, or you can change the flash speed.  I didn't find a lot of use for that, but I did change the medium light setting because from the factory the medium setting was just a tad dimmer then high and a lot brighter then low, so I dimmed down the medium setting to about in between what low and high should have been.  I only use the light on flash mode on rainy or foggy days because of it's superior brightness, otherwise I use a separate flasher at night in conjunction with the headlight so people are attracted to me faster.The battery run time is exactly as Cygolite claims it to be at 3.5 hours on high and about 17 hours on low, with the medium setting at around 10 hours.  This battery actually runs longer then the much larger MagicShine battery.  It takes 4 hours to charge using a standard wall outlet, it does not come with a USB charger; the MagicShine took 8 hours to charge.The light kit comes complete with one short cable attached to the battery, and one longer extension cable that attaches to the main cable if you need more length.  It also comes with mounts for both the bars and helmet; and a small cloth bag for the head light to go into for storage.I rode with this light at night once and incurred a surprise downpour rain, the light never once faltered nor has any evidence of moisture getting inside...unlike me who was drenched!

I was happy with my headphones and recently decided to call Sennheiser to see if they could check their authenticity. I left a message and a rep just called me back. He asked where I got them from, I told them a seller on Amazon, fulfilled by amazon. He told me they haven't produced CX 300's in 2 years (please note they still produce the CX 300 II) and that even if Amazon fulfilled the order they were most likely fake. I told him that they sounded good and everything looked real but he insisted that given the circumstances (price, seller, discontinued product) that they were almost definitely fake. I am going to return them and will be looking for other headphones. While I do think they sound pretty good for $17 I just can't justify keeping fake ones.

As soon as I removed this UBU Super Softy Extra Large Soft Powder Brush from the package, it fell apart (yes, just as it did with other reviewers). The ferrule stayed with the bristles/fibers, thank goodness. I considered gluing the handle to the ferrule to prevent it from falling apart again. But when I put the brush back together, I immediately noticed that the end of the handle was sharp, unfinished, poorly weighted, and unpleasant in my hand (just as other reviewers found it to be). So I decided to use this more as a kabuki type brush, without the handle. Next I noticed the terrible smell. So I washed the fibers, which the description says are "natural" (natural what?) with a mild soap and carefully and thoroughly rinsed it. The odor didn't go away. It smells like a wild animal took a dip in a vat of chemicals. Is that the smell of the pink dye, which so profusely bled all over my hands as I washed the brush? Is it the smell of the fibers themselves? Is it going to stay or go away? So far, it's staying. Ugh. Such a cute pink brush with the cute name of "UBU Super Softy." So disappointing.I am going back to my e.l.f. brushes, which cost about $3 each and even less when bought as a set. The e.l.f. studio powder brush has been reviewed by almost 1,400 women (on makeupalley) and 94% of them would buy it again and have given it a 4.7 star rating. I give it 5 stars, love its smooth, tapered handle, its soft fibers, the fact that it is synthetic and vegan-friendly. I love it even more now that I have tried the disappointing UBU Super Softy. A cute name and cute color do not a useful brush make!UBU (Urban Beauty United, a registered trademark of QVS Global China) brush is made in China.

I am giving this drive one star only to highlight the fact that, unlike all other USB flash drives, this one is configured by the manufacturer as a fixed disk vs. removable disk. Recently it is being done by Sandisk to their flash drives to meet the newest certification requirements by Microsoft. This type of configuration has all kinds of ramifications for "power users". Average computer users will not know any difference.I would suggest to the manufacturer to offer both types of configuration of flash drives for sale, one type will satisfy Microsoft, and the other type will satisfy the rest of the world, while both types will keep perfectly happy those who don't know the difference. World peace might ensue.Drive comes preloaded with some sort of "security encryption" software. I gently wiped that stuff clean and formatted the drive.Other than that, the drive works fine for its bargain cost, transfer speeds are about average. Recommended for simple storage.

Just got it a couple of weeks ago and so far I'm very happy with it.  The screen is bright and beautiful (LED technology) and it comes with an HDMI connection. I gotta say the vertical viewing angle is not the greatest (looking up at the screen is slightly darker...looking down at it brighter).  But then again it's very much a standard for monitors in this price range.In the end, there's one thing you have to keep in mind: while it's probably one of the very best monitors you can get for under $200 (if not THE)...it's not exactly the best monitor money can buy.  Having done my research, the ones starting $400-$500 range are basically in a different ballpark.  So you pretty much get what you pay for.  And in this case, for this price, you get PLENTY...in fact WAY MORE than you ever could before. Pro: Very easy online programming using Windows XP. Programmed six items without a problem.  Customized remote buttons but later realized the functions were programmed automatically during set up. Components include Onkyo 7.1 surround system (w/multiple listening modes), Panasonic blu-ray, Wii, Motorola HD receiver, Samsung HD TV, and InFocus HD projector. All functions translate nicely to this remote. Nice blue back-light glow. Help button available if needed.Con: Size, balance and layout of remote require some getting used to. The unit is very thin and slick. The channel number buttons are somewhat small and located at bottom of remote. Takes some manual dexterity to operate with one hand. Would not recommend for the arthritic. Another idiosyncrasy is the need to continue to point the remote at the components while they turn on, makes sense, but takes getting used to.Summary: Overall pleased with the device. Still getting used to the layout. If large buttons are needed, look elsewhere.

I purchased the Swivel6 specifically to protect an espresso machine requiring a minimum 1200 joules suppression capability; I also need to keep a number of other appliances plugged into the same receptacle. With its 1500-joule rating, this was the only 6-outlet, wall-plugin unit I could find that met or exceeded spec.As others have noted, however, there is a serious fly in the Tripp Lite ointment. With all six outlets occupied, I am CONSTANTLY reseating this thing for lack of a securing center screw--major design fail and major pain. If I had a newly-installed or hospital grade receptacle, this might be mitigated, and this might in fact be an acceptable solution were this not a rented condo we plan to move out of in the near future. Maybe I'll try the Velcro tape someone suggested in a comment.As someone mentioned in another review, Belkin's six-outlet wall-mounted surge units have these screws, and I would switch one of those in if any of them had the required specs; but alas they don't.Perhaps the beefier internal electronics in Tripp Lite don't provide sufficient room to put a screw right through the middle. Maybe I should just be grateful that it does what I need it to do.

I really really wanted this bag to work.  I still use it, but it doesn't work right. I'm a pro-photographer and use this bag to shoot at weddings, events, and concerts.The Problem: If you use a 70-200, 24-70, or 17-55mm, which I do, none of them fit right with the lens hood stowed on the lens.  I'm not talking about leaving the lens hood in working position.  Basically this bag is too small by about one inch or so to fit the lens-hood of the pro-lenses.  So you fumble around trying to stuff one oversized lenses in one hole so you can grab the other one.  But it doesn't work.  Now, I put the 70-200 under my arm, grab the short zoom, put it on the body and then put the long zoom in the bag.  I bought the bag to avoid that, its faster to use a standard camera bag.  So if you shoot with kit lenses then this bag works great but you don't really need a bag like this for kit lenses now do you.Pros:  Very well made, It will not fall apart on you, the lenses cap holders on the side are a great option. I love it, I bought 2 Skeeter Beeter pros & 1 regular Skeeter Beeter.I bought my first one over 2 years ago and let my tentless friend borrow it, he is a farm boy and at least 6'4" and 180lbs. He loved it, said he was going to get one soon after.I can share  it with my husband, our combined weight only being 260 and still be comfortable- the hammock is double wide! Since then I bought one for my husband and another just as a spare.The difference between the models is the quality of the fabric, the pros are silky and the regular is not silky. Why does this matter? When you are sleeping in this huge hammock and you want to pull yourself up higher on the hammock to get your feet to touch the ground first (instead of your butt), the silky fabric makes this move easy, nothing catches. The silky fabric also does not collect lint or pet hair- just based on the texture. The netting is no-seeum mesh and allows for great views. To use it without, don't attach the elastic cord and just zip it all the way shut, flip you hammock over to its upside down position and sit. This tensions the netting tight against the silky nylon part of the hammock.It's easy to set up and take down. You just keep it in its sack that is sewn onto it, hang one of the hooks on your rope, then unroll out the rest of the hammock. It never needs to touch the ground. The whole thing packs to the size of a bicycle helmet, weighs about 2lbs. Great for bicycle or kayak camping. I recommend getting the Hammock Camping book by Ed Speer- it's just a really great, informative read on the Leave No Trace aspect of hammock camping. You won't be disappointed.

I bought my Xacti about a year and a half ago on Lightning Sale. It was a serious steal. It was probably the best impulse buy I've ever made on here (and I've made a lot).  It takes beautiful crisp photos and great video.  I've read some of the negative reviews and think the problems lay with the users not the product in most cases.  True, it does no have a plug in wall cord.  The battery charger plugs in but that's it.  That's not a bother to me because I have no need to watch the video on the camera for a prolonged period of time. The camera's USB is fine. I've not had a problem with plugging it into any port on either of my computers or my sister's. If you have the ability to hook a laptop to your tv, you can watch video directly from the camera that way if you do not wish to watch it from your computer.I've taken short video with ease with no stabilization issues.  Recently, I filmed my niece's wedding. There were stabilization problems, but it was my fault. I was tired of holding it and overly excited. That equaled shakey shakey. It's obvious I was using handheld and not a tripod. I had the wedding photos printed and they were beautiful. My advice is to carry an extra charged battery for events, especially if you switch from camera to video. I learned that lesson the hard way.In fact my only problem with my Xacti is lighting, but I have a feeling there's a setting I haven't found yet.  I have yet to crack open the manual.  Yes, I'm one of those. I rather play and find things out on my own. Even with the lighting I still give it 5 stars. It's more than worth the money. I used to be nothing but a Canon girl, but I can't see buying anything but another Xacti. It's light weight, travels well, takes gorgeous photo, video, and photos from video, but be forewarned... Xacti will scare security at x-ray machines.  The idiots think it's a gun.  Be prepared to prove it's a camera.  Seriously!

Those of you who have read my reviews will note that I am really interested in high end headphones.  These were rated as extremely good, for the money, in general, great bass, and most importantly, to me, surround sound.Imagine my surprise when I noticed that the unit connects to whatever using a stereo plug!  My Turtle Beach surround sound headphones used an optical input, and even if they are fake surround sound, at least they have the possibility of having legitimate 7.1 given the optical input.  (they did sound great, BTW)  I have tried the Bose AE2W bluetooth headphones, I have tried audio technica, as well as other wired headphones and have had mixed results.so here is the long and short.Positives:  Outstanding quality headphones for listening to music.  the Bass sits in where it is not too over bearing, and yet, there is no question that it is there and thumping.  Sound does not leak as much as others, so in that regard, they are perfect for listening to music when people are asleep in the house.Negatives:  I do not believe the statement when Sennheiser says that these have Surround Sound.  I know what surround sound is, I know what 7.1  is supposed to be like.  These do not do what they say they do.  Enough said about that.My personal rating of 3 sets of wireless headphones.Comfort out of the box:Bose AE2W wins this hands down followed by the Sennheiser RS170 and third is the Turtle Beach XP510Sound  It is extremely difficult to say which sounds better over all, but if I had to do a top down judgement, it would go RS170 first, AE2W second, and XP510 third...  but again, this is a very close contest.Surround  Hands Down, XP510.  The most realistic surround sound in a set of headphones I have heard so far in my quest for the perfect head phones..  RS170 does actually immerse one in sound, so in that regard, it does simulate surround sound.  Bose has excellent sound, and is very comfortable to wear, but does not do surround at all, not even a little bit...  LOLSummary:For the money, you could do worse, but for me, I am still looking for that perfect set of headphones that make me happy about spending more than $200.00.  All headphones that I have talked about here are sitting in my living room right now, so if anyone is wondering, I have experienced all 3 of these, and many more.

I also had the pleasure of evaluating this device during the Beta.  I rated the design a 10, and I firmly believe that. Of course, the 5.0 GHz band doesn't cover as much ground, but the 2.4 GHz was plenty when furthest away from the router.I believe the price is just about right, given we're still relatively early in the transition to the faster 802.11ac spec. This is the best point and shoot camera for indie filmmakers and no one knows about it.I've owned a Canon S95, Panasonic LX5, and a Casio FH100.  While they each had their advantages, the TL350 had the best bang for the buck.Let's look at the specs that matter for video on all these cameras - they all have manual control over exposure, shutter speed, aperture, white balance, and ISO, so I'm skipping that:Canon S95- 1/1.7" CCD- 28-105mm- f/2.0-f/4.9- MPEG-4/H.264 .MOV- Stereo Linear PCM audio- 1280x720 @ 24fpsPanasonic LX5- 1/1.63 CCD- 24-90mm- f/2.0-f/3.3- AVCHD Lite/MJPEG- Dolby audio- 1280x720 @ 30fpsCasio FH100- 1/2.3 CMOS- 24-240mm- f/3.2-f/5.7- AVI/MJPEG- IMA-ADPCM stereo audio- 1280x720 @ 30fpsSamsung TL350- 1/2.4 CMOS- 24-120mm- f/2.4-f/5.8- MPEG-4/H.264- AAC, WAV Stereo audio- 1920x1080 & 1280x720 @ 30fpsHere's why the Samsung TL350 is the best:(1) Price. Don't know if this is a big deal for you, but for me, under $250 is a bargain.  Especially for the only camera to shoot 1080p video.  The S95 has been pushing $400 since it's been released.  Way too much.(2) Best Focal length/F-stop balance.  The LX5 only goes up to 90mm.  The S95 only goes down to 28mm.  The FH100 has a great 24-240mm range, but suffers horribly from a slow lens at only f/3.2 - it's not that great in low light.(3) Back display.  I don't know the exact specs, but the display on the TL350 blows all the other compacts out of the water.  It's sharper and brighter and reminds me of the display from the iPhone 4.(4) Build quality.  The S95 is too small.  The LX5 is too big.  The FH100 is too thick.  The TL350. . .just right.  Also, the old school battery and storage gauges on the top of the TL350 makes it fast and easy to always know how much power and card space you have.  You think that doesn't matter, but it's very convenient.(5) Video quality.  Now this has a lot to do with a combination of factors.  It starts with the sensor, then the lens, and finally the frame rate and codec.  The LX5 gives the best video quality.  There's no contest here.  LX5 is brilliant.  So why is it not better than the TL350?  Because you would never bring an LX5 with you.  It's a monster.  It will not fit in your pocket.  It has a lens cap that will probably get lost.  You might as well get the Panasonic GH2 or GF2 or the Olympus Pen.  So saying the LX5 has better video quality than the TL350 is like saying the Canon 7D has better video quality.  Yeah, of course it does, because it's a completely different kind of camera.If you want a small compact point and shoot that you can put in your pocket and take anywhere AND that will give you excellent film-like video.  Get the Samsung TL350.  It boggles my mind that everyone is beating off to the Canon S95 when the TL350 has more features, 1080p video, and for almost half the price.

It's been 6 years since I bought a laptop with an optical drive, so a good external disc drive is pretty important. I had been using a feature rich LG Lightscribe for the last 4 years, but it finally gave up the ghost and left me without a reliable method to read or write CD's.For my next drive I wanted something smaller, as there was nothing portable about the old LG. Enter the Buffalo MediaStation. I haven't had it very long, but I can report one 3 important facts.1. You can run it off one USB port. The drive has two USB cables wrapped around the side and on the undercarriage. Whether or not you can run this off a single port depends entirely on your computer. The drive does make this easy for you by lighting up red or blue depending on your power needs. If it's blue you're golden; red and you're going to need that second USB port.2. The drive is pretty fast for a bus-powered external. On a 1.3ghz Intel Dual Core 2 laptop with 4gb of RAM, iTunes encoded 160 kbps MP3's at 20x.3. The DVD drive can be noisy. There's not a lot of room for insulation in this device, so it's going to rattle around some.I can't speak for longevity, but so far it's been an excellent buy.

I received one of these towels complimentarily in exchange for my unbiased review.  Having long, thick hair and having two daughters in my house; one with long thick hair and the other with short thinner hair to say that I know about managing hair from day to day would be an understatement!  I'm always on the lookout for products that make managing the three heads of hair in my household easier or at the very least more enjoyable.One of the problems I come across is towels.  Between my eldest daughter who has long hair and my own long locks towels just don't seem to cut it.  They get soaked through and heavy way too quickly to do any real good when it comes to drying.  Far too often when she or I take our hair down from the towel it's still sopping wet and dripping.  While this towel isn't big enough to be able to do the "flip, wrap, twist" turban like thing that many do with their bath towels unless you have shorter, thinner hair I still love these towels.  The microfiber wicks water away from the hair very quickly meaning that there's less water left in the hair.  Finally no dripping from sopping wet hair down your back!  It also seemed to dry very quickly which is a plus.I first used this to dry my own hair last night after receiving it in the mail.  I was impressed at how much drier my own very long and very thick hair felt.  Next day I used it on my youngest's hair (just below shoulder length, fine and thinner) before I got down to detangling it and styling it in preparation for Easter tomorrow.  It cut down the time it took me to blow dry her hair!By the time my eldest had emerged from the shower and was ready for me to get on her hair I had her use this towel to dry her hair as she waited for me to get down with drying and straightening her sister's hair before I moved on to her.  Again it worked like a dream and wound up cutting down on blow drying time with her as well!To boot the towel was only slightly damp by the time I finishes with my eldest's hair!  Not that in a salon environment you're going to use towels on multiple people or anything like that but within a household especially when you as mom have yourself and a couple of daughters you very well may use things like towels for hair from one kid to the next.  If you fit that bill and are like me consider investing in these towels.  The ten pack should be enough for most households especially if you have older children who may not want to share a towel for the purposes of drying hair or for styling.  If you're more like me and have kids that don't care or who are younger then get a pack and consider sharing with your family or friends who have daughters!  Would definitely make for a neat stocking stuffer for Christmas!

I'm on my third bottle of this. I first got introduced to HA serum years ago when my retin-a prescription came with some samples of an HA serum that's no longer available. I didn't even look at the samples...but I guess I over did the retin-a and it literally ate my face off. It was sooo miserably painful. Anything I put on my face to moisturize it and help soothe my poor skin just made it worse or caused stinging and burning so bad I was crying. I tried just basic face lotion, skin oil, everything. I even put an aloe burn gel on my face and that hurt so bad I had to sit down because the pain made me dizzy and I nearly fainted. Finally, my boyfriend went to get the bag from the pharmacy to see if there were directions or something & he found the HA samples. He said "hey what's this?" and I said "I don't know, it came with the retin-a"...so he googled it and then suggested I put it on my face (and then I felt stupid for not trying that first!)Oh my goodness! Instant relief! It didn't burn at all. No stinging...my skin just drank it up & it was so helpful. After I used up all the samples I tried to get more and I learned that it had been taken off the market because the manufacturer was only offering it as an injection moving forward. I was so bummed and I started looking for alternatives. That's when I got my first bottle of Timeless HA serum. since I haven't made an ongoing habit of burning my face off...I rarely need it to sooth a retin-a crisis...but I find lots of uses for it otherwise. For example, as soon as the dry heat kicked on this winter I was blowing my nose and trying to keep it hydrated, but after a few days, the skin around my left nostril cracked and it just kept re-cracking. everything I put on it stung except for my HA serum. I just made a habit of adding a drop to that spot every couple of hours and it finally healed within a day and a half.It lasts forever. A little goes a long way. It feels really nice and is absorbed almost immediately. It's not oily at all. It's only slightly thicker than water. Most of the research I've done on HA has suggested applying it all over your face underneath your regular moisturizer to fight wrinkles and such. I'm not struggling with wrinkles just yet, but if I get a cut or scrape anywhere I will first apply a drop of this underneath some neosporin & band aid and it mitigates any scrabbling or scarring and generally seems to heal more quickly.I have really sensitive skin. If I get something suspect near my eyes they swell up so bad I only have little slits to see out of. I have to be really careful. I apply this around my eyes when that part of my face feels dry & it's never bothered me. It's a lot cheaper than having to buy a seperate eye cream. Maybe when I'm older I will go through it more quickly but for now it's the least expensive (and most valuable) item in my skin toolbox. The first bottle I go was one ounce and that lasted 5 or 6 months. Since then I've purchased the two ounce bottles and they each lasted about a year. It's a great serum to stock at home  for many reasons, but if you have a lot of skin allergies or if you're acne prone - this is about the safest way to possibly go to avoid irritation, clogged pores, breakouts & even more rapid healing of popped blemishes.Something else important to note, much of what I've read about HA serum as a daily moisturizer all over has recommended not using it alone with any regularity. I'm paraphrasing, but basically the idea is that it helps draw moister from the air and pull it to your skin & I helps with absorption of your other moisturizer....but when used by itself, in certain climates/environments, it could start to draw the moister from your skin rather than from the air and it could lead to dryness. Ive used it by itself as well as underneath something at different times. It definitely has a better effect when used in conjunction with something else....but i have my reasons for occasionally using it alone & it has only worked in my favor. I've not experienced it as being drying at all.. So, just be aware.

I bought these spoons to carry with me on my racing bike for the occasional flat on the road.  I have only used them a few times on the road, as I usually use them in my shop when I'm doing maintenance, but I would not order them again.  Firstly, they are not stainless steel, they are chrome plated, meaning that after you use them, the plating starts to scratch off and you're left with metal that rusts very quickly afterward.  Secondly, they are very roughly stamped out parts and as such have very sharp edges from the stamping process.  Sharp edges versus soft rubber inner tube..see a problem here??  I had to spend about half an hour with a file smoothing the sharp edges off and then using a buffing wheel to polish the metal afterward..all of which took off the chrome plating..see another problem?? As soon as the chrome plating is gone the metal underneath starts to rust..so, all in all, I would say, don't waste your money on these cheap imitations..look online for better tools from Park or one of their competitors...you may pay a little more, but what you save in cut tubes and labor making these work will be worth it..

I've been trying a wide variety of hair conditioners and I've found that not one of them is a jack of all trades. Some are better for color enhancement or shine, others for texture (softness), body, frizz reduction, ease of combing out or styling. Based on price point as well as other considerations, this is my number one choice as a conditioner.The main benefits are that it is thick and goes into wet hair easily. It also does reduce frizz, but it will not eliminate it if you have hair which has never been salon treated, colored, or is not regularly trimmed. I don't do any of those things and I noticed that I have some frizz after using this, but less than before.You don't have to use a ton of it (though you do have to work it through carefully if you don't use a lot). It makes for ease of comb out (and I have super long, thick hair so this is very important to me) and produces beautifully soft dry hair. The resulting shine is perfectly serviceable, but it is inferior to the Suave Moroccan Oil or Nexxus conditioners (which produce pretty impressive shine, but less textural enhancements). For the price per ounce, if I had to go with one consistent conditioner, this would be it.

be sure to go to [...]to get the latest firmware.  it improves white balance and adds 1:1 aspect ratios.  my straight from amazon gift had firmware 1.1, which is several revisions old.Although the LX3 and d-lux4 are basically the same camera, you do get a few extras:+ significantly better industrial design :)+ phase one capture one raw processing software - this is a pro-level raw processing tool by a medium format manufacturer that outclasses most other tools out there.  i love it.+ 3 yr warranty - register on [...] to get the extended warranty+ marginally less processed JPEGs+ cachetin all, i think the first 3 points make the $200 premium over the panasonic LX3 worthwhile.i've been a canon shooter my entire life (EOS 1Ds, G10, SD790IS make up my current arsenal), so i feel comfortable with this comparison:d-lux4 vs G10:+ astounding lens. wide open aperture (f2.0) is still sharp but great low/available light performance.  there are shots only this compact or an SLR with a large lens (or the new GF1) can take.  Also significantly less chromatic aberration than the G10 lens in my shots.+ significantly smaller/lighter+ better noise characteristics.  there is significantly more chroma noise on the G10 than D-lux4/LX3 at high ISOs.  G10 does a ton of noise reduction in JPEGs.+ 720p video recording.  however, the camera applies a LOT of sharpening to the video, you're limited to 24 fps, and they use the antiquated MJPEG codec, meaning you can't record as much as per GB as you can with AVC.- shorter telephoto.  at 60mm equivalent vs. 140mm equivalent on the long end, there are some shots you will simply not get on the D-lux4/LX3.- fiddly joystick, vs the machined dials.  changing exposure on the G10 is downright easy & fun - there's a dial for EV, a dial for ISO, and a dial for aperture (my usual setup) or shutter.  on the d-lux4/lx3, you have to use this tiny joystick.  it's not significantly slower, but it detracts a little from the 'this is a serious camera' feel.-/+ lens cap.  yes it's an issue.  for getting the camera out and ready to shoot it's not too bad - i'm able to one-handedly draw my D-lux4 out of its case, use one finger to pry off the lens and turn on the camera at the same time.  putting the cap back on still needs 2 hands though.- hard to use with gloves.  the G10 is very usable with gloves, the D-lux4/lx3, not so much.  gloves have to come off.+ better RAW processing software.  actually, if you take a free upgrade to capture one (to 4.8x), it will support the G10 as well!+/- about the same performance around auto focus, and shot-to-shot latency.X-factor - ultimately, there was a pop in the JPEGs out of the leica that struck me the first time i looked at my test shots, and even more lovely detail when i worked the RAWs.  this is a remarkable camera with a remarkable lens. the different film modes make you think creatively.  i still keep/use by G10.  here's what it boils down to:- stopped down (about F4.0), in bright light (ISO 80), nothing beats the G10. it has a super-usable range 28mm-140mm, is easy to use one-handed and has incredible resolution.- for all other purposes, i take the D-lux4 - indoor available light shooting, early morning/late evening, wide panoramas.  most of the usability issues can be worked around by setting up custom modes (you can save up to 4 settings), and setting up the FN button to something useful.

The product description for Clear Men Scalp and Hair 2 in 1 Shampoo and Conditioner, Citrus Fresh (whew!) is "clinically proven to hydrate the scalp." I don't know the details of the clinical experiments from which that conclusion was reached, but I would have to say that the claim is not hyperbole.
