Choosing a hair straightener can be a rather intimidating process these days.  A lot of people throw around advice, and most recommend high-end hair straighteners costing from one to two hundred dollars.  Some recommend basic ceramic plate straighteners, while others say only the Tourmaline plates should be used, since they emit negative ions, which straighten hair for longer and leave a shiny look to your hair. My oldest daughter was asking for one of these "professional quality" straighteners recently, even though she already owns two lower priced straighteners.  Over a hundred dollars for a couple of heated plates in a squeeze handle?  I just couldn't see spending that kind of money, so I was delighted when I had the opportunity to receive and test the TRESemmé Philips HP4669/05 Thermal Creations 1" Straightener.  It sounded like a decent product and can be had for a reasonable price.  This model does not boast the Tourmaline plates, as my daughter's other straighteners claim to have, but instead comes with "Nano Diamond" ceramic plates, which are supposed to provide smooth gliding and shiny hair.Upon opening the box, I found the following items contained within:*  TRESemmé -Philips Thermal Creations 1" Hair Straightener (model HP4669/05)* Heat-Resistant Storage Pouch* TRESemmé Thermal Creations Heat Tamer Spray (2.0 oz. sample size)* Operator's Manual (small pamphlet)* Warranty and Registration PaperworkThe operator's manual is rather simple and easy to understand.  Of course it contains obvious warnings, which is standard for nearly any electrical device.  Some of these warnings made me chuckle, such as "Never use while sleeping" and also advising you not to let your eyes (or any bare skin) touch the heated surfaces of the device.  So after giving my teenage daughter a stern warning about not straightening her hair while taking a nap, and to not jam the heated portion of the straightener in her eyes (I guess Philips figures the non-heated parts of the device are safe for your eyes!), I turned the device over to her to compare against her other straighteners.The HP4669/05 Thermal Creations Straightener comes with a generous 9 foot power cord, which is attached to the handle with a handy swivel base.  This allows easy maneuvering of the device without fighting with a tangling cord.  Pressing the on/off button powers up the device, and the default 230 degree heat setting is displayed.  This is the lowest heat setting, but it can be increased up to a maximum of 425 degrees.  The temperature is displayed on the integrated backlit LCD display, and can be controlled using the + and - buttons, located on each side of the on/off button.  These buttons are quite small but easy to operate; a little too easy, as I will explain later.  The ceramic plates heat up very quickly, and the heat up time can be shortened if you leave the plates locked together when the device is powered on (usually within 30 seconds or less).  Once the device is ready for use, the word "READY" will appear in the LCD display, just below the selected temperature.  The device is now ready to begin straightening hair.My daughter has used this straightener on her hair, both when wet and dry, and has stated that this device is definitely not as good for straightening wet hair as her Tourmaline straighteners.  However, when used on dry hair she said that it works extremely well, and that her hair glides through the diamond-ceramic plates effortlessly.  She really likes the included TRESemmé heat tamer spray, but it is such a small bottle that she used it up within the first week.  Larger bottles can be acquired from local retailers, but prices vary a great deal so you need to do some research to find decent pricing.  My daughter loves how quickly the HP4669/05 heats up, as she is usually running behind when getting ready for school in the morning.  Her hair is fairly thick and moderately wavy, and she has shown me the results after using this straightener on her hair.  Within minutes her hair comes out very straight and rather shiny.  She wanted me to remind everyone that you should never brush or comb the treated hair until it has completely cooled down, otherwise it will upset the straightening treatment before it has had a chance to set.  This is also noted in the operator's manual as well, but it is easy to miss if you aren't reading it carefully.  As a parent, I really like the auto-shutoff feature of this device as well, since it is not uncommon for my daughter to rush out the door, forgetting to shut the thing off.  Once powered on the straightener's built-in timer is automatically activated, and will shut itself off after an hour if left on.  The LCD display show a series of bars, which indicate how much operating time is left before auto-shutoff occurs.  If one should want the device to operate beyond the one hour limit, you simply power cycle the device off and back on, which resets the timer.  Once you are finished using the device, and it has cooled down, you can lock the plates together with the handy lock switch located under the hinge area of the handle.  The operator's manual also recommends that you wipe the plates and outer surface of the appliance with a damp cloth after each use.  The device can then be stored in the included travel/storage pouch if desired.Overall I am quite impressed with the TRESemmé Philips HP4669/05 Thermal Creations 1" Straightener.  It has a look and feel of high quality, and the results of using this product are excellent.  It is backed by a 2-year limited warranty, to protect against defects and any potential design flaws.  My daughter is extremely pleased with how quickly the device heats up, and how well it straightens her medium length wavy hair, while also producing a moderate shiny appearance to her hair as well.  Does it perform as well as a high-end, $150.00 hair straightener?  Well unless my daughter raises enough money to buy one of those herself I guess we will never know.  However, the HP4669/05 seems to perform very well and meets all of her current needs.  In fact, the only thing preventing me from giving this product a perfect 5 stars is the button placement on the handle.  While using the straightener it is too easy to accidentally change the temperature or even power the unit down entirely.  Both instances have occurred when being used, which resulted in wasted time and/or poor results.  One of my daughters other straighteners has a setting lock button, which can be used to prevent accidental button presses.  Philips should either relocate the buttons, design them to require a firmer finger press, or at least redesign the device to produce an audible beep when any button is pressed (so the user would be aware that a setting had been changed).  Although we don't consider this to be a huge flaw, it is enough to prevent a perfect rating.  But for the price, and for normal household use, I feel this straightener is a solid 4-1/2 star product, and I would easily recommend it to anyone other than maybe a professional hair stylist.

I am not a big fan of the Kukri-style blades, but even I have to admit that this one WORKS.This knife is smaller than my machetes-- small enough to fit in my backpack-- and yet it clears brush about as well as any machete.  It does a better job than my Woodsman's Pal, and MUCH better than any straight-bladed large knife I've tried.  I've tried just about all of the popular Bowies on the market, and this beats them all for clearing brush.It also performs well as a chopper, piling up firewood as quickly as any hatchet.  The handle is comfortable, and the lanyard provides plenty of peace-of-mind.Some folks complain about the sheath.  I am happy with it.  It does leave something to be desired, but I don't see any MAJOR problems with it.  Still, this knife would not ride on my belt.  This one would stay in my backpack, so for me the sheath is not so much a means of carrying the knife as it is merely a covering for the blade.My only real complaint is that the edge is not as sharp as I like, but that can be remedied quickly by a few minutes on a hone.  The reverse-curve of this blade makes for VERY efficient cutting, but also means that it can't be sharpened on a flat stone.  You'll need some kind of round hone.  Despite the lackluster edge, the knife cut very well, so I can't complain about dullness all that much.This knife will replace a hatchet, a machete, a folding saw, AND many smaller knives.  It does all of those jobs quite well.  It could even be used to pry or dig, if you had to.  When ounces count on a hike, this knife's versatility will be appreciated.  It is tough and made in the USA.  It is also not expensive, when compared to other quality knives.  I LOVE this knife, and I'll be ordering another one!

These dust/impact covers not only keep dust and debris out of the mags, but press down on the top round, which takes pressure off of the feed lips, preventing them from expanding if stored loaded for long periods. I really like these!  I bought two of them to use with my Asus Windows 7 laptop.  I found it a bit tricky to get them to work, so here's the process if you run into the same thing.  Pairing is pretty straight forward, to get sound to play you will probably have to update your bluetooth software from broadcom.com.  After doing that, you'll see a bluetooth "headset" device in your sound playback options in your control panel.  Make that device your default and you should have sound now on your bluetooth speakers (assuming they are already paired).  That said, I didn't like the sound quality at all...not the speaker's fault, I just must not have a great bluetooth signal out of my laptop.  It would fade in and out and was scratchy.  When I paired my phone to the speakers the sound and connection was just great.  I found using an audio cable to connect to my laptop is good, but then you can only use one speaker, no stereo (you could use a y-connection, but you'd have two mono speakers I think).

Everybody seemed to love these headphones. These buds were all over the internet with rave reviews. "Premium earbuds at a cheap price!". I have had them for a few years now and still don't find them to be worthy of such words.The S4i is pretty good. For an in-ear bud at the price, yes, it does sound good, but that's not saying a lot as there are a lot of cheap-sounding in-ear buds on the market. The S4i delivers on the bass that it promises but its trebles are also on the tinny side. There's a distinct lack of midrange in these buds that makes listening to vocals, jazz, and string orchestra less enjoyable. In-ear buds are never my go-to choice of earbud style, and I prefer to use these only when I truly need the sound isolation.Speaking of sound isolation, the S4i does that very well. The silicone tips are incredibly comfortable yet at the same time create a tight seal in your ear canal. They stay snugly on the buds and don't tend to get stuck in your ears when removing the buds.The inline controls seem to be rather fragile - it seemed like a bead of sweat destroyed my play volume buttons.The fact that all three buttons are round can also make it hard to navigate by feel.For the ~$60 price, I see no reason to buy this when $30 dollars gets you the Apple Earpods (which I think sound better, but that's just opinion!) and for $30 more you get the Bose in-ear buds that also sound distinctly better and are more comfortable and also isolate sound well. But that's ALL opinion. That said, I recommend these buds if you are accustomed to in-ear buds, enjoy them, and want a not-so-expensive pair that delivers on clarity and punchy bass.

The dark circles under my eyes are better, much diminished, but not gone yet. I think I'll see continued improvement over time. I don't expect perfection until there is a total cure for allergies, of course.You really do have to protect your skin from sunlight, because it will be more sensitive when you use anything with Retinol. I like RoC products more than most. The tubes seem small, but you don't use much and it seem to last for me and I'm not very careful with it. Someone who is careful could make it last a L-O-N-G time.

This product worked just fine to curl my hair but I did not notice any difference in the curls made with this roller set versus one which does not have heated clips. It didn't work any worse, either. These type of clips are way easier to use than the metal wire U shaped clips may sets have. *However* I did find the slick surface on the inside of the clips did not provide as much grip to make tight rolls as clips that had teeth on the ends. By pinning my hair up 1st then rolling the underside 1st, the middle part second, and the top part last, I find each roller sits on top of the last roller and avoids any problems of rollers slipping out. By placing the clips on the rollers so the opening was upwards and the pinch part of the clip was downwards, I avoided the line that many others have mentioned the rollers leaving in their hair but you do have to make sure you got it just right first. The extra heat this set provides with the heated clips may be the thing someone with harder to curl hair may need to gain a wave but my hair curls easily. *Longevity* of my curls has always been my problem. I did not notice my curls styling in any longer than with other heated roller sets. Just my two cents.

i have very mixed feelings about this case. i bought it based on it's color as well as all the rave reviews. but the longer i use it the more annoyed and frustrated i become. there are so many design flaws, it's absurd. but it is great for when i'm out & about, because it's snappy and sturdy. but i am a homebody, and that may be the reason why for me, the bads of this case outweigh the goods.the good things about this case are:1) PURPLE! it is so hard to find a kindle case that's purple. i love purple.2) protective. this is a very sturdy case that will protect the kindle from many kinds of bumps and bites. not water though, obviously.3) the stand is sturdy and has three convenient angles.4) it has a stylus holder.5) it has an elastic strap thingy to help you hold it while reading.6) it has a wake/sleep function.the bad and/or annoying things about this case are:1) i'm getting a hand injury from holding it. it's very uncomfortable to hold on to, unless i have my hand in the strap, which isn't convenient or even possible when i need both hands (which i do quite a bit.) also, having my hand in the strap is not comfortable.2) it's upside-down. this is hard to explain but i am always having to turn the kindle around, and i can never tell where the volume & power buttons are. it seems that everything was turned topsy turvy simply to accommodate the stylus holder. it's not worth it .3) you can't charge the kindle while using the stand. if the case wasn't upside-down, you could. also, you can't open/close the case while charging.4) when the case is open all the way (which is how it will generally be if you're not using the stand or carrying it around) the front cover blocks the speakers. this muffles the sound noticeably.5) there's an elastic strap to hold the case open, but it blocks the camera!6) if you put a stylus in the holder, it makes the kindle even harder to hold.7) because i keep the case open all the time at home (to more easily charge it, as you can't open/close the case while charging) the wake/sleep function is useless to me unless i go out.basically, this is a great case for being out & about with your kindle, when you're not charging it and you really need to protect it. it looks really nice and is very convenient for taking the kindle around in a bag. but for home use it's deeply flawed, both ergonomically and functionally. i have ordered a marware case (B00BGIQR3Efor around the house. i think that will save my hands from permanent injury, as i use my kindle at home 85% of the time. i'll keep this case and use it for whenever i take my kindle out.

This is an updated review - I am lowering my prior rating from 5 stars to 1 star because I no longer feel this is part of a long term backup and sharing solution.This change started when my daughter pressed the "pretty red button" on the UPS that was supporting the media smart home server (perhaps more than once) and the server suffered a hard drive failure on the primary drive and a hardware failure of some type.  As I began looking into my options for repair or replacement, I compared trying to fix the existing server, buying a new mediasmart home server, buying a comparable device from another manufacturer (I am interested in Synology), or moving my backup storage online.I chose the repair option first, as I had a spare hard drive and purchased a new CPU and memory stick, but was unsuccessful in bringing the unit back to life.  Unfortunately, the diagnostics on this unit are beyond terrible (the flashing LED on the face of the unit), so after installing a known-good hard drive, I could not tell what else was wrong with this unit.  Systematically swapping parts (memory, CPU) didn't help, and while I really want to connect a monitor and watch it try to boot, that's not possible with this unit.  So - after spending hours and dollars trying to fix this - a brick it is.  Fortunately I was able to recover all the files because all my folders were duplicated, so this hardware failure was not completely catastrophic.  Kudos to microsoft / HP for this design feature.  I really mean that - Thank You!  I would otherwise have lost some of the photos of the kids.However, based on this experience, I have ruled out another HP mediasmart home server, because I am not made of money or time, and I think there is a better overall solution.I am mildly interested in the Synology DS-410 because I still have 4 1TB hard drives that are still good, and Synology devices have very good reviews from other users on Amazon, and I want to re-establish music and picture sharing on my home network.  But this solution would still face the same risk from my daughter, so this device (or others like it) could not be relied upon as a backup solution.Instead, I am strongly leaning towards installing a "family computer" in the kitchen (I like the new Sony all-in-ones) which could act both as the central hub for the home media network and eliminate the home server concept entirely.  We could connect to this computer either directly or remotely to access these files, and the contents of this computer could be backed-up online.But at this time I have opted to move my backup files online using a subscription service.  I don't need to worry about hardware maintenance, which as a busy parent is a blessing.  I believe I will save money over time, because I will buy less computer parts going forward.  But most of all - I won't need to worry about it.

This Dayton leaves the mids much louder and sharper than humanly bearable. Those RC80i speakers naturally have strong mids, but comparing the sound produced by this Dayton receiver to what is now used (a Sony STRDH130, which is not much of a price difference from this guy) and what drives a nice Onkyo receiver that drives our Polk 5.1 setup, there was no question this amplifier should be sent back. It lacks tone adjustments, which is clear from the product description, but sorely needs it. Unless you are providing sound input that can adjust its own EQ (e.g. an iPod playing iTunes music and plugged in directly—Airplay or Spotify do not allow EQ adjustments), this Dayton leaves you exposed to bad sound.Other than this mids issue, the receiver is quite small and has high-quality finishes.

I really like the look of this Knife and I am pleased with my purchase.  Damascus steel knives are some of the most beautiful knives I own, yet they require some oil and attention, as they will oxidize if not kept oiled and rubbed.  This Dagger is my favorite and now that I have had it a while since I received it from Amazon, I am very pleased.  The dagger blade is approximately 6 inches long, a full 1.5 inches wide and heavy in the hand.  Both edges are very sharp, showing the damascus steel foldings from tip to hilt.  The pummel is damascus also and would prove a very good striking weapon itself.  The wood of the handle is seperated into three even sections by brass and at each end by two smaller sections of darker wood.  It's well laid out guard is decorated with as nice a looking raised design as can be found in any of my knives.  This would be a fighting knife, rather than a boot knife.  It is large and heavy and made to both slash and stab as needed. The heft would also provide a good striking weapon.  It is beautiful in the hand and would add to anyone's knife collection.  Even with concerns for rusting, due to high carbon steel's tendency to oxidation, it is still a good looking knife.  Damascus Steel does require more care, as it does rust in higher humidity. Just rub it with an oil cloth occasionally, to buff off surface oxidation and it comes back looking good.

Ok - so this is hands down the best little camera available right now. But is it good enough? My humble opinion - not so. I bought this camera from Amazon a month ago - with the 20mm 1.7 lens. I have used it for a vacation in Barbados as well as street photography and 'go anywhere' type of shots. Here are my impressions.Pros:- Nice form factor and small size. It can easily slip into a big pocket.- Finally, someone figured out the contrast detection autofocus. It's not perfect. On about 5% of my shots - the autofocus locked but the shot came out clearly misfocused. I found this to be acceptable, though.- The 20mm 1.7 is a great lens - very sharp, very fast and a pleasure to use.- The build quality is good.- The camera feels solid, the controls are generally well laid off, and it is speedy to shoot.- The features offered are on par or above the mid level DSLRs out there.- Good image quality up to ISO 200.- The display is good (especially when compared with the dinky display of the EP1), but not as good as the Nikons and Canons in the same price range (e.g. 3inch, 920k dots).Cons:- Overall image quality came below my expectations - for a sensor close to APS-C in size. It's head and shoulders above a compact camera, but for $900, you can get much better IQ from an entry level or mid level DSLR, especially above ISO 200.- Low light performance is at the level of APS-C sensor a generation or two ago (think Nikon D40, not Nikon D90). This is particularly problematic in the shadows - with significant noise visible at relatively low ISOs - e.g. ISO400. The noise is also of the worst type - chrominance as opposed to luminance.- I do not miss the viewfinder and I would not get the optional EVF for this. You have to get used to a new way of shooting with this camera - in front of you, using the screen in the back for composition. HOWEVER, in order for this to work, one needs two essential things (both missing in the GF1): an electronic horizon level (to make sure you're lining up those shots) and in body stabilization to compensate for the extra shake from not having this camera supported on three points (two hands + your face) as for a DSLR. This was the deal breaker for me. I found that the rule of thumb for DSLR - shoot at 1 over the 35mm focal length equivalent does not work for the GF1 way of shooting. For the 20mm, not image stabilized, to ensure a good shot - I would have to shoot at 1/80s.- Other than the 20mm lens (which is great), all the other m34 lenses out there are too big - they negate the size advantage of the format. So I don't really buy the interchangeability advantage - if you want a small package, the 20mm is going to live on your camera, and you shouldn't really pay an premium over non-interchangeable package such as Sigma DP1 or DP2.- It is overpriced at $900+. The G1 - the GF1's bigger brother (same sensor) sells as a kit for $700. Considering that G1 has a EVF incorporated, and the GF1 lacks that, I value the GF1 kit at around $600. I hope its price settles there after the initial feeding frenzy is over.- If you are serious about image quality, you shoot raw and part of your workflow involves post processing the image. In that workflow, the GF1 is held back by the fact that the in camera choices you make (film modes, contrast correction, etc.) are not carried over to the RAW developer included with the package - Silkypix. You have to start all over again - which is annoying and a waste of time. I wish Panasonic put some resources in developing their own raw developer - like Nikon's Capture NX or Canon's DPP - this way they can carry over the settings once you get to the computer.- If you don't shoot raw, well - you have another problem to deal with - the jpeg engine incorporated in the camera is mediocre at best. The colors are off (especially the blues) and the dynamic range of the jpegs is 2 or 3 stops below what the sensor is capable of.- Crappy flash sync speed of 1/160. This not only applies to the underpowered built in flash, but also external flashes. The GF1 does not offer high speed flash sync like the better DSLRs out there. This is very limiting when you want fill flash on a bright Caribbean day.So, what does this all mean? If you are looking for a small camera to carry around - either get one of the fixed lens packages from Sigma, Leica, etc. or wait until Olympus perfects the autofocus on the EP series. If you are looking for a travel camera - go for an entry level or mid level DSLR - the size and weight difference is not that great and the difference in flexibility and image quality is well worth the tradeoff. The m43 format has potential, but we're not quite there yet.

Between the E2C and SE110 I have owned 4 pairs of these.  The sound quality, noise isolation, comfort and value are great.  They do lack a little bass, but the iPod EQ handles it well enough.  Great for running because they fit snugly and the cord wraps around your ear holding them in place.  The reason I have had 4 pairs however is that like clockwork about a year after I use these (use everyday at the gym, not exactly an easy life for them) they seem to experience the same issue that they get fidgety when the cord is moved.  The sound crackles in one ear and fades in and out.  For about a week you can compensate by oddly contorting the cord around the ipod but they eventually die.  If you save the receipt and send it with the headphones back to Shure they will replace them.  I have done this 3 times and am about to do it a 4th.  The plus is they ship you back a retail-packaged set but if you intend to use them for years, good luck.  Would still recommend them because the cost of ownership is basically $30/yr.

I have a number of irreplaceable cassette tapes that I've been trying to figure out how to convert to CD for the past few years. I'm on a Mac and pretty technical but nothing I've looked at previously seemed quite right. I had looking into the Crossly all in one but that didn't have very good reviews and I looked into the iMic but it seemed a little complicated.When I read about the Tape2Pc I was very hopeful. I've had it for a few weeks now and it was very easy to set up. If you're on a Mac you'll have to go to the Ion website to download the EZ Tape Converter and Audacity soft wear, the included CD is supposed to be for PC or Macs but the files are .exe which Mac's can't use.I decided to try the EZ Tape soft wear as I don't have time at the moment to mess with the files and was hoping that it would be simple, and it was!  Just Open the program, click a few buttons and press play on the Tape2Pc deck and you're recording!  *Important Mac note - before you start, you need to go into your sound playback file and choose: USB audio codec - this is the opposite of what they say in the booklet. It's mentioned on the Ion forums too.The sound quality is wonderful - seems the same as on the cassette. You do need to stay nearby your computer when your making the file because if the CD has tracks that you'd like to keep separate for your CD,  you need to press a 'track' button between each one, then you need to press 'finish' when it's over.  After that it automatically uploads and converts the tracks into your iTunes (or any other CD burning program you choose).Finally an easy way to convert your cassettes - thanks Ion!

Two years ago I bit the bullet and added the Intel X25-M 80GB SSD to my Core I7 powered Dell Desktop.  I moved the stock HDD to become the data drive (lots of photos, video, etc).  The change was phenomenal.  Full cold boots in about 25 seconds.  And I'm not talking time to see your desktop.  I mean fully loaded and CPUs barely utilized.  There is basically no lag in loading programs and they run so much faster.  I use Lightroom 2.0 to process large RAW photos and this upgrade made a huge improvement in performance.Two years later, I bought a secondary computer, an Alienware 11x with a Core i5 processor.  I knew when I bought it that I would upgrade to SSD but didn't expect to do so right away because I wanted a higher capacity drive than the 80gb without breaking the bank since this would be the sole drive on the laptop.  When the 120 was released and priced right, I pulled the trigger.  All I can say is WOW again.  HUGE improvement in performance, no lag, less fan noise.  And battery life has improved about 15% in my unscientific observation (Windows estimated battery life on full charge before/after).After my two upgrade experiences, I can say with confidence that for higher end machines, the single biggest performance bottleneck is the hard drive.  If you have a decent processor and 2-4GB of RAM already, don't upgrade anything else until you get an SSD.  I briefly considered the Velociraptor 10,000rpm series HDD from Western Digital.  But for the price and higher energy use, I didn't find the slightly higher capacity worth it.  Performance wise, the Intel SSD will blow the Velociraptor out of the water.  [...]There are many brands of SSDs out there.  I make no claim to be an engineer but from what I've read, Intel SSDs are among the best in terms of reliability and long term performance.  Some SSDs' performance degrades with time as they become underoptimized.  Intel includes optimization software to keep your SSD cruising along. I can only speak from experience, but two years in, my 80GB drive is running just as fast as the day I installed it.One final thing.  This kit includes cables and a 3.5" adapter for desktop applications.  So save yourself $25 or so and don't buy those separately (or factor in when considering other brands).  Intel also provides Acronis disk transfer utility.  I opted to do a fresh install both times but it's good to know it's there for those who want to do a fast upgrade.If you can live with the size, I assure you that you will fall in love with this upgrade.

I reluctantly returned this product after a few weeks.  It just does not charge in sunlight the way I expected it to.Even the manual say it takes two days of sun to fully charge the battery.  I placed it in a West facing window for a week and it never fully charged.  The  company said the usual stuff.  Window coating can diminish results, clouds, haze, etc.  Plus the instructions say it has to be pointed right at the sun to charge correctly.  So if you are driving West with the device on the front windshield it still won't charge correctly.  You have to point it RIGHT at the sun.I laid it on my deck.  It was drenched in sun and still didn't get a good charge.If you plug it into a wall outlet overnight then it will do a good job as a backup battery.  But that isn't why I bought it.Plus it doesn't work quite right with iPods.  When you plug the iPod in and hit the start button on the Solio, the Solio will charge the iPod.  But after a few hours it will quit charging and the iPod will run off the internal battery until you hit start on the Solio again.Solio tech support said this is a known issue.I guess I will try again in a few years.Great idea, just not ready for prime time.

I purchased this drive for use with my AtomOS Ninja2 external recorder. For those unfamiliar with pro Video, this is a recording device that records directly to HDD or SSD. I preferred the use of a SSD because it reduces the risks of damage when moving my video camera around.I recorded about 9 hours of footage that was fine up until the last time I booted up the recorder. However when the time came to download the files, I could no longer access the drive either through the recorder, USB caddie, or even directly connected to the computer via Sata cable.I found that these drives "disappear" and that there are firmware problems. I also found that a number of people have had issues with these drives that do AWOL with only a couple of hundred hours of use.Unfortunately, I cannot return or exchange this with AMAZON due to their exchange policy (30 days I believe), so now I have to try to send this back to Crucial. I doubt I will be able to get my money back so I can purchase a different brand.On a brighter note, I was fortunate to have recorded my footage on SD cards as a safeguard! The downside, I lost all of my higher resolution footage thanks to this device.I would also like to note that after buying a Samsung 840 series and comparing, The Samsung is most definitely the better buy since it at least comes with data transfer/backup software and more documentation.

Today I had to wake up super early to take my daughter in for an oral surgery and didn't really feel like doing much with my hair so I figured I'd shower but skip washing my hair and use this Dry Shampoo instead.First of all, I have long, thick, wavy to curly hair and I'm not one of those people that can skip a day or more without washing my hair. I must wash it every morning or by about lunchtime my head starts to get oily, itchy and dirty looking. I can't stand that so I faithfully wash my hair daily even though hair stylists have told me many times that I shouldn't.So this morning I followed the instructions and applied the Dry Shampoo. Since it was really early in the morning, earlier than I normally wash my hair, my hair wasn't too bad yet but I did have bed head and that's something that I can't normally change until I've washed my hair. I was able to manage my hair quite nicely and style it the way I wanted to after using this product. I was pretty happy with the results for the most part. My hair was manageable and didn't look too bad at this point.By this afternoon I started getting that oily look again and by this evening my head was itchy and pretty dirty/greasy looking. I was quite relived when I was finally able to shower again and actually wash my hair this time.This product claims to refresh hair between washes. It doesn't replace a regular washing so, although this product helped delay my needing to wash my hair by a few hours, it certainly didn't replace a daily washing, which isn't what I was expecting anyway. I thought it would help a bit more with getting rid of the oiliness though since it says it absorbs oils. I know many women who don't wash their hair daily and think that I'm crazy for doing so so I thought this product would at least help me to be able to skip one day but I still can't do it. I get sticker shock when I see mascara going for twenty a pop. Really? For a product you really should discard after two or three months of use? I've been searching through "drugstore" brands of mascara to find one that will work as well as the high end brands, and there are quite a few that are more than acceptable. Maybelline has two that are usually in the five-buck range if on sale, and this is one of the two I really like,Maybelline Rocketis the other.Of the two, Falsies really lives up to the claim that it creates false eyelash-like effects. It is a lengthening mascara, and the curved brush seems to help put in some curl. One or two coats, and lashes are longer, darker and really stand out, somewhat separated.The Rocket mascara is more volumizing (leaving lashes thicker) but I like the effect of the Falsies behind eyeglasses. If you shop around, you generally can find this on sale from time to time, and stock up. I'm happier discarding mascara after two months as long as I'm not spending a bundle on it. And there is no need to fork out more, because this mascara is as good as any I've tried.I got Very Black, which is dark enough for me. If you want sooty black, there is a "blackest black" which is darker.

After less than a year, the motherboard on this puppy fried itself. (Search under "LG Gray Screen" if you want to read a bunch of posts about this problem.) On top of that, the "smart" features of this TV leave a lot to be desired. If you plan to use the on-board apps to watch your own videos, be aware that it won't play half the codecs out there, including the popular MKV format. The wi-fi module is dicey, as well. This is the second LG television I have owned that didn't last a year. Fortunately, this one is under warranty and will be repaired free of charge, which so far, has been a relatively hassle-free experience. The audio/video quality is fine when the set is functioning, but if the components are built to last months rather than years, what's the difference what kind of quality you get? My advice: do NOT buy this set unless you buy an extended warranty with it. If this had happened one month later, I would have been out of luck.

For my needs, I expect one jar to easily last a minimum of three months, probably more, but obviously that will vary with each individual usage.My hair is medium to darkish brown hair but the light brown shade I used was a perfect match. It is darker than what I would normally envision as a light brown color. I also sprinkled a small amount of the blonde product on top of the already covered area and found that by lightly layering the two colors it created variation and depth, with a final result that looked more natural. This is a lighted pocket microscope.  It has a little virtual stage, outlined in clear plastic to let natural light in; a little switch also activates an ultrabright LED that sheds a great deal of bright bluish light onto whatever is in the stage.Both magnification and zoom are adjustable, but this is most useful if you are observing things on a flat surface.  If you are freehanding and observing something that's freely mobile, you are best served to set the device on lowest mag, set the focus to focus on the plane of the bottom of the device, and then move the device up and down on the subject until the part you're wanting to examine comes into focus.  Doing it that way, specks of pollen, tiny mites, and the like are easily visible on leaves, even if you don't want to pull those leaves off the plant.  Color fidelity and resolution are pretty good, good enough to distinguish faint color variations such as clear, cloudy and amber plant resins.Eye relief would probably be my biggest complaint.  It's next to impossible to use this with the included rubber eyecup because it holds your eye further away from the objective than it needs to be to see the full field.  Even with the eyecup removed, my eyelashes were crashing onto the scope when my eye was properly positioned.  Forget about using this with eyeglasses; you have to take them off.Still, good value for the money.  If you've read this review carefully and you need a scope for your garden, this is the one to get.

NOTE FOR MAC OSX USERS and those setting this thing up manually without the Wizard!The default IP address of this device is: 192.168.10.100.  Since most devices use the far more common 192.168.1. as their primary IP string I couldn't connect to the device via ethernet using the more common string, and this prompted a call to the TrendNet 24 hour tech support line to find out what the mysterious IP string was they were using for this device.  I only had to wait a few minutes to speak to someone too!  None of this wait 20 minutes and then have to leave a message for a call back CRAP!This IP address is not mentioned in the documentation, nor could I find a reference to it on their website.  I suggested that they include this in future updates and POST IT on their website!  If they had provided this info in the product, I would have not needed to call them.TrendNet appears to not support the Mac OS in any meaningfull way based on their manuals and even their website, but I found their tech support person to be fully knowledgable about configuring this device using a Mac.  After getting the IP I needed I wanted to hang up, but the tech asked me to stay on the line and walk through the process with him anyway just to make sure that I got it working properly!  This was a pleasant surprise these days!  After about 5 minutes, we had the device configured the way I wanted it and I went ahead and hooked it up to our HR-22 DVR and our wireless network.I found the web based interface to be well laid out, simple and uncluttered.  It provides all the settings options you would need for either a repeater or in our case an AP client connected to our main router's SSID.  Reboot times after applying major changes were snappy with less than a minute of reboot time.  All in all, I couldn't find anything about the web interface that needed improvement.  It does the job, and the hardware reboots quickly.This unit is connected as a client to our NetGear 4500 router for streaming 1080i HDTV video from one Direct TV HR-22 DVR to the other over our wireless N network.  The unit has performed flawlessly so far and is picking up a very good signal from our 4500 from over 100 feet away.  Signal strength is between 80% and 90% and link quality is constant at nearly the same values.So far we have streamed over 10 gigabytes worth of programs over our network with the 368, all in 1080i video and according to the 638's status page, it hasn't logged a single dropped frame or CRC error.  Nor have we seen the slightest hiccup in any of the video or audio feeds so far. Scanning through commercials is almost as fast as when we are watching a program recorded on the host DVR, even though when using this device, we are actually viewing a recording from our other identical HR-22 DVR. (DTV's "Whole-Home" network feature)Based on this and the company's tech support savvy with Mac installation, and based on the stellar performance of the device on our network, I will not ding them a star for the IP address issue and can confidently give them and this product a very strong 5 STAR rating!  I do have to wonder why they just don't include the manual setup process I used in their manual so that Mac users can more easily configure the device.  The supplied CD only provides Windows based setup via a Wizard and no references are made to how to manually configure the device without using this Wizard, even though a fully configurable web based system is provided right on the hardware!  I find that very odd for a home network hardware company that you would think would want as many customers as they can get right?After recently upgrading our entire home network to wireless N, we have had to go through a half dozen routers/repeaters and two other wireless APs before finding the two products that could handle our 12 connected devices, half a dozen peripherals and be able to manage all the HD video and other media we have flying through our large family home network on any given day.  The NetGear 4500 router and this TrendNet 638APB make a great team, and if I ever need another wireless N AP, the 638APB will be my choice hands down!Hopefully by providing other Mac users with this device's IP address, I will save them a call to TrendNet, or worse, a return of a great little device before they even get a chance to use it!  ;o)

This pedometer is excellent in all ways, it has a nifty feature that begins to count steps after walking at least 10 steps, after the 10 steps it starts marking your steps from 11 and on. Its very accurate and it even shows how many calories you have burned! This is simply the perfect pedometer for the average user or any runners. It comes with a belt clip, but I like to hold my pedometers in the hand when running and this one doesn't restrict that use as you choose to have the belt clip on or off. It has a feature that automatically turns the pedometer to "sleep" mode, but this to me is a bit of an issue. I carried my old pedometer with me wherever I went, but only wanted to use it when I went running, this pedometer stays on as long as it detects movement, so I can't carry it with me unless I want the battery to drain. This is a minor inconvenience to me, but isn't in anyways a huge problem, simply a desire that it would have been nice to have a button that completely shut the device off. Overall though, this is a really nice pedometer that is small, portable, accurate, and easy to read and use. There was plentiful enough of instructions included with it, something that allows you to learn to use it even if you have never had a pedometer before.I rate this product 4 stars out of 5 stars!I hope this review has helped you out!I was provided this product for a completely honest & Unbiased review, the words stated above are my own.

Due to the way my house is built, I have no phone jacks in the basement, however all of our computer equipment is down there. So, with DSL being on the main floor, and the WAP in the basement, I had been doing a wireless link back up to the DSL modem via a Cisco Aironet 350 PCI card in my VMware platform, to a Linksys 54GL upstairs. That worked fairly well, but one piece of that wireless puzzle would have an issue from time to time. I decided to go with the Linksys powerline product, and so far, so good. Plug and go installation, performance seems to be great, and latency is definitely less than the aforementioned WAP solution. I also removed a couple hops, which is an additional latency bonus. Highly recommended.

I've been using these razors since I was 14 or 15 and have loved them since! Constant compliments on my brows almost every time I went somewhere, and they always wanted to know "where'd you get your brows done?", "excuse me, who did your eyebrows"…when they find out I do my own brows, they're shocked. It's amazing.There's no real way to review these razors because it boils down to personal preference and skill level. I know what I am doing and how to use these, but many people do not. I think because I have really bushy brows, they work wonderfully, but for someone with naturally tame brows (like some other women in my family), you may struggle.Anyway, I don't trust someone else to do my eyebrows so I always rely on these handy razors. I have a good 13-14 years experience now, but it is easy to pick up. I ALWAYS do my brows between Friday and Sunday (any free day will work)…and it's best to do them at night so you can enjoy your days and to sleep off any imperfections that may occur (discoloration; cuts etc). I hate my brows when I do them, but always look pretty the next morning. I personally trim the tops and bottoms of my brows. I get rid of the unibrow section first. Then I shave the bottom by brushing the hairs upwards and I slowly shave off hair (following my natural eyebrow shape). I hold my face taut while doing it. ALWAYS follow your own brow shape and never a template since we all have different face shapes and sizes. I never do an extreme arch. Once you shave off too much, the grow-back period is hideous. For the top, I shave upward and downward, depending on the angle of the hair. And I take small hair scissors to trim access. I also take the "finishing touch" with the eyebrow attachment to trim off any stragglers. For the back portion of the brow, I shave on an angle obviously since that part slopes downward. Sometimes I do them dry, but many times I will add an oil or shea butter type of product to smooth them down first. You may bleed at times, especially if you're a beginner, but it's not a big deal. When you wake up, clean your face, and they'll be no residue or blood there. There are tons of videos on youtube if you need further help. Just search for "eyebrow trimming", "trim brows with razor", "shape my eyebrows" etc…Good luck!

A couple things for context...1) I was upgrading from a 7 year old Samsung DLP 720p set.2) I bought this for $400 which feels like stealing.I know nothing about Plasma vs LCD vs LED so I don't know how to tell you this scientifically, but the picture looks really great - that's not what made me fall in love with this TV...There are three HDMI ports (two on the back, one sort of on the side but not visible from the front in anyway), 2 component (one doubles as a composite, I think, haven't tested the Wii yet), VGA+audio, and optical out - I would have liked one more HDMI out and an RCA audio out but that's nitpicking.  But that's not why I fell in love with this TV...The remote is solid if unspectacular - it seems to lack any sort of programmable mode which means you'll be using your other remotes to control the TV - however, there is one bit of functionality where you'll want to keep the remote handy...This thing is a full on DLNA client with Divx support!  I have a WD MyBook Live hooked up to my network where I have ripped all of my DVD's so that I can watch them in our living room PC (which doubles as a TV at times).  On the TV, this feature is completely undocumented!!!  A few simple clicks and an ethernet cable... and I was watching movies on my TV!  I hooked up the optical cable to my home theater system and it's passing through LPCM signal - I was so happy I almost passed out!  The remote situation makes this somewhat of a bummer as I now am managing three remotes (UVerse, TV, Home Theater) but I'm still so excited about the DLNA that I just don't care!!!  This also works (flawlessly) over USB.I will update this review after 30, 60, 90 days.  So far...love at first sight.

My last camera is a 35mm point & shoot auto-focus, auto-everything.  That's great for me, because I don't want to have to work at taking pictures. I can set it in full-auto and take great (albeit simple) pictures.  She can set settings to her heart's delight and take more artsy style pictures.  The pictures are great either way, and the camera is easy and fun to use.The downside is the software.  I wasn't expecting anything like the full version of Photoshop, but I did expect something easy to use.  I don't think it should be hard to take a picture at 4MP, download it to my computer, then resample it down to a much smaller file size to email it. Edited: Aug 10I have this lens for about a month and used it in a couple events, one of which was an outdoor wedding of a friend. I set the aperture to f/5.0 since I didn't want a totally blur background. The pictures are extremely sharp, perhaps too sharp. It shows all kinds of imperfections vividly. I had to use AF for the event and found AF to be much faster than I expected. Bottom line: I was the limiting factor and not the lens. I had no problem hand holding the lens all day. This is a fantastic lens. Go for it, you won't regret.Original reviewI picked this lens after extensive research but am biased by the fact that I love my Tamron 17-50 f/2.8. I tested this lens with a Pentax K-5. The 16MP sensor is quite demanding on resolution. This lens is sharp, sharp and very sharp ... even wide open. Images at 300% on screen magnification are still sharp, well defined and contrasty. I tested it against comparable zooms and primes and it can compete with primes ... let alone other zooms. Once you achieve precise focus, this lens can deliver superb results. I am not a sports photographer; so I cannot comment on AF speed. I sometimes even use manual focus on LV, which is what I used for testing this lens.I also checked out the claimed field curvature that some reported at 135mm FL. Yes, it does indeed has some minor field curvature but that is only relevant for near subjects and at wide open aperture. If the subject is 10 ft or further away, it may be detectable on screen at 100% or higher magnification. If you stopped it down to f/4.0, you can't even detect it. IOW, there is really nothing to worry IMO.The build quality of this 70-200 is substantially better than the 17-50. While the Tamron 17-50 is not built like a tank, I have nothing to complain. Many people thought it was heavy; I don't find it heavy at all. I also have a Sigma 100-400 f/4.0, now that is a heavy lens.Factoring in the "relatively" low cost compared to other 70-200 f/2.8, this lens has the best value in this segment. It is still not a cheap lens, but this lens must be on the short list if you are in the market for this class of zoom.

I am one of those people who does not like to believe that specialty hair care products (such as those used at salons) are worth the money. I tended to pick up my hair care products at Costco, whatever was cheapest in big bottles. This was fine until I got older (I'm in my late 40's) and my hair got dryer, coarser, and started to lose a lot of its shine. Now, it requires a lot more work and care to get it to look somewhat akin to what it once was.I've been sampling the entire line of "Motions at Home" products, and I have to say that I've been impressed. When I compare it to commercial shampoo and conditioner, it produces a better result for a smaller amount of product. The latter consideration is a bit of an adjustment since I'm used to having to fill my palm with conditioner, and you need a very small amount about this (a dab about the size of a quarter in my particular case). I've been using this moisturizer on the ends of my very long, thick hair at night after a shower. I rub it in to the last 6 inches starting at the tips and comb it through the bottom only. In the morning, my hair is noticeably shinier and easier to manage.The only thing I don't like about this is that the smell is quite strong and not particularly pleasant (unlike the other "Motions at Home" products, which tend to smell like coconut or citrus). Though it does disperse through time, it has a bit of the nasty chemical smell that one associates with salon perming or conditioning. I also wonder if those who have hair which is less voluminous and long may find that it is too heavy for their hair. I often joke that I have enough hair for 3 people, and it's not much of an exaggeration. I'd suggest anyone with less hair use it very sparingly and err on the side of too little rather than too much. I could easily see this weighing down someone's hair if they overdid it for their hair type.

First off I've installed many head units, so installation of this Parrot Asteroid was a snap. Just did a little bit of rewiring to get it installed with my system and away I went. One thing I did appreciate was it's shallow/reduced depth. This made getting all of the wires (both for the HU and auxiliary items) shoved back in place much easier.As for the functionality of the device... I'm not too happy with it.Navigating through the different menus was fairly straight forward and about as simple as can be expected for a HU that isn't touchscreen. The large 3.2'' screen is easy to read even at weird angles. I would have preferred to have the screen in the middle of the unit for easier reading while driving (though you really shouldn't be fiddling with the stereo while driving). This may just be a personal preference.Now the down side is pretty much everything else.The 4x55W MOSFET amplifier while fairly high powered for an HU sounds like it's underpowered and unable to push my speakers (unlike my previously installed Pioneer DEH-P690UB). Keep in mind this is 55W PEAK, not RMS.The included cables for connecting the different devices via USB or stereo are not high quality at all. Flimsy and cheap.The 'apps' are absolutely garbage. Not sure why they even include them.The buttons on the unit feel cheap.And it takes forever to get this sucker up and running and when it is running you're constantly waiting for it to reboot, mess-up, or do something stupid. It's quite frustrating.I think the only real upside to this is that it supports AAC files and can connect with a ton of different devices. But other than that, it's a headache.

I use this along with the Lemongrass Cleanser and the Concentrated Balancing Toner (Formerly the White Willow Toner) and WOW!!! What a difference in my face! I can not stress just how much this works wonders!I've used other face products by St. Ives, Neutrogena, Oil of Olay, Clean and Clear, basically any and all of those mainstream products... you name it, I've tried it.And then I read the book "Gorgeously Green" and saw the list of HORRIBLE ingredients that are IN those products and you want to scream because of the deception that drew you in (those darn commercials that make everything seem OH so peachy if you use those main stream products!) ...... in fact Suki's website, Sukipure.com has the whole list of horrible ingredients and what they can do to you!So anyways, back to the product. I tried the all natural product called "Talulah" which was ok, it didn't do anything amazing for my face. (My face seemed to stay the same, no improvement, but it didn't make anything worse either)..... so then I tried Suki since it was the other all natural product at a local "organic/natural" face and body care place. WOW!!!!!The VERY first night I used it, I woke up the next morning and could VISIBLY see that blemishes had diminished and that any redness on my face had become very muted, almost non-existent. And from that day on my face has become better and better. I use to have these little white heads along my chin that were RIGHT under my lower lip... I tried all kinds of scrubs and creams to try and clear them up, to no avail..... I try Suki and literally in 3-5 days they're ALL gone!!!!!  I could not believe my eyes!My face FEELS softer, and healthier and looks more vibrant and glowing. It's also a lot less oily!My regimen consists of the Lemongrass cleanser, and then I spray 4-5 sprays of the toner onto a cottonball and swipe across my whole face (Especially along the perimeter of my face) to make sure I get any makeup residue off (You can usually see the remaining residue on the cotton ball) and then I spray 3-4 sprays of the toner directly on my face and sorta pat it in lightly. While my face is still moist from the toner I'll take 3-4 pumps of the daily lotion and smooth it all over and continue to do the smoothing motion until I feel it soaking in, as a final thing I pat my face lightly to make sure my skin soaks up the lotion....Wow, wow, wow, wow. I don't even CARE that this stuff is on the pricey side because it has SAVED my face! And you can NOT put a price on keeping your face beautiful!

I have mild to moderate acne.  While it isn't terrible, it is stubborn to treatment.  Many dermatologist visits have yielded nothing but peeled skin and frustration.  I have tried almost every over the counter face wash in the acne aisle.  I stumbled upon Oxy when I ran out of Clearasil face wash (which did nothing for me), and it was reasonably priced, so I picked it up.  The consistency of this face wash is very watery - almost milky.  It takes a little getting used to because it doesn't really lather up very much, which is a sign that it doesn't have added chemicals.  It did not sting, and was pleasant enough to wash with.  It easily rinsed off and I did not notice any bleaching of hair or towels.  Initially, this made my skin very dry and I needed to use a much thicker moisturizer, but the results have definitely been worth it.  While my acne is still present, it is reduced, and the overall tone of my skin looks better.  The dryness has subsided, and I will continue to use this face wash in the hopes that it continues working even better with time.  I would recommend this to anyone, but do find a thick, non-comodogenic moisturizer for afterwards!

I vividly remember the first time I rode my bike without training wheels. I rode straight for about three houses; then, in the middle of an intersection, my bike spun around five times and threw me to the ground. (Hey, that's what it felt like, and my friends all agreed: five times!) I suppose avoiding that kind of learning experience was what prompted me to look into riding "assistants."I read the reviews and thought the Balance Buddy sounded like a great idea. My 3-year-old has aCars 12-Inch Boys BMX Bike by Huffyand he's just starting to want to ride it. The BB was easy to put on, but seemed (despite all the reviews talking about how it allows you to walk upright) a little short. I'm 6 feet tall and extended to its longest position, the BB is just about at my fingertips. It does move up and down (pivots at the axle) so that's helpful. The bike is only a 12-incher, so obviously the BB would be an easier reach on a larger bike.At first my son didn't want me to have anything to do with his riding; he would only let the 8-year-old neighbor boy "push" him. After a couple of days, though, he allowed me to help him ride. He switches back and forth between training wheels and the Balance Buddy, which might not be a good thing, as he has a tendency to lean over onto the training wheels. I can't seem to get across to him that balancing straight up is the goal. Plus when he rides his attention is on everything but what he's doing, so I'm sure that steepens the learning curve. We'll keep at it and see how he does. He's the next to the last kid in the neighborhood with training wheels and I'd like to see him out with the bigger boys.Update: My son is now five and too big for his bike, but before we got him a bigger bike I wanted him to be able to ride without training wheels. He kept arguing for the training wheels and I countered with the Balance Buddy; in the end I took the BB off and let him coast down the driveway. On the second trip, he started pedaling and we haven't looked back. Today we put him on a 16" bike. I still like the idea of the Balance Buddy, and I think that it helped somewhat, but in my case it wasn't as helpful as I'd hoped. As always, your mileage may vary.

Flower by Kenzo is not a mysterious fragrance. It's just another light, floral based fragrance that's all about the flower...but that's where the mystery comes in. What flower? I cannot pick up the top note floral. It defies my nose. I can get floral, okay. And orange. A little vanilla and, when the wind is right and the barometer cooperates and Leo is in retrograde, for all I know, some ginger and musk. Maybe some woodsmoke? If they are there, they are gossamer light.The fragrance is like a chameleon. I'm always thinking "Aha, there's the ginger..." and then it's gone. I don't even like Flower all that much, although every component scent I mentioned is a favorite. Somehow all together there is no "there" there. It seems a little insipid. But what do I know is this: Every time I wear Flower (which I admit, was purchased because of the bottle...who could resist), people stop me in the street and want to sniff my shoulder.In an elevator, a Flower wearer is mobbed. In restaurants, women passing on the way to their tables will stop and ask what you're wearing, men go goofy trying to nonchalantly smell your elbow. One day, a really mean, leathery-faced motorcycle cop pulled me over for making an illegal left turn. (I was not guilty on the grounds that I would never have done it if I knew there was a cop car behind me.) When he bent down to take my license and so on, he said "Wow you smell good." Didn't stop him from writing the ticket though. So much for the Fragrance Defense.Anyway, since the world loves Flower and I'm just confused by it, I'm giving it five stars. Smell it yourself, try it on. Could be I just am Flower-challenged. I like the attention Flower gets, but in my heart of hearts, I think if I were wearing Bulgari Blu, I'da gotten away with the hinky left turn.

From what I've read on the packaging, and from what I remember reading on the description online before having this item shipped to me, the main purpose for this facial cleanser is for removing make-up...that, or, it is a product that is highly optimal for the removal of makeup.Well, I don't wear makeup, and my work doesn't require me to get "dirty," (though I wouldn't mind;) however, those times that my face just becomes "grimy" from the days activities - ESPECIALLY those days I'm around and helping to take care of animals, specifically dogs - these cleansing wipes do an incredibly effective job when it comes to cleaning deep and cleansing the face (and neck, too...perfectly safe). My face FEELS fresh, clean, and - to a certain extent - new, or, refreshed.Now, I'm not a HUGE citrus fan, but the scent is not overwhelming/overpowering, AND, it's not as if it smells like one citrus fruit over another citrus ______(fill in the blank with a citrus-scented/flavoured food). I've used a product or two that used citrus - can't remember if it was for scent or for health purposes - and my skin kind of felt like it was "burning" or "stinging" a bit after the use of this(these) other product(s). NOT so for these wipes. I've yet to experience that stinging/burning/irritated feeling I've felt with other products in the past.The packet itself is easy to open, but not so easy that a toddler or young one wanting to reach up on the counter to grab and explore could open initially. However, I DID notice that over time, while I didn't use it every day, the more I opened and closed it, the more I had to make efforts to ensure that the seal was secure to ensure the wipes did not dry out overnight. Funny, though, speaking of drying out, when I first opened the packet, I almost got "squirted" because of the bit of excess liquid that is inside to keep the wipes moisturized!  In any case, other than the seal being a bit finicky (at least, on my package it was), I don't have any HUGE issues with the facial wipes.I noticed that the wipes go a LONG way, in that I can use 1 wipe to do a nice, slow, thorough "sweep" of my face and neck, and there is quite a bit that is picked up, more than what I did notice. Additionally, it would appear that one doesn't need to use MUCH of the cloth, that (the old adage) "A little bit goes a long way," is extremely applicable for these wipes. Now, would they function similarly/the same for makeup users? Well...yes and no. Now, granted, I've just said I'm not one to normally wear makeup, but for those SPECIAL occasions, I will, and afterwards, I would not like to use umpteen cloths/wipes and/or x number of bottles of makeup remover - well, maybe not to that extreme, but I say that to make a point, that the less of something I can use, thus making it last longer, the more I'm going to WANT to use it. Thus, for balls, weddings, and other events that might require makeup, such as a variety of performances (I did do some theatre a fair ways back in my past - and believe me, I would have LOVED to have had these back then!) Depending on how thick the makeup is - theatre makeup, I could see as working, but not working as effective/efficient as everyday makeup - will depend upon how effective it would be. I would say that for someone who wears makeup everyday, not tooooooo thick or heavy, mind you,  the way it works for me now just as a "typical" facial cleanser would be about the same. It might be a tad different if one wears heavy(ier) makeup, but as a whole, I can still seeing just ONE cloth - which feels like it's a HUGE cloth at times (especially when one does a nice, slow sweep over the face with the entire hand in circular/wave-like movements! ^_^)

I had an APC surge protector "protecting" my computer. Along came a power glitch that APC failed to protect my equipment, and they refused to honor their guarantee to repair or replace my equipment.My RAM was destroyed, as well as the power supply and the motherboard. I shipped the APC device to them with my claim info, and they responded with something similar to "since our device is working fine and nothing in it 'blew,' then there is no evidence that our device failed".My point is, if APC's equipment actually WORKED, it would have blown some resistors or whatever in the course of protecting my equipment. The FACT that there were NO blown resistors is the strongest of indicators that APC's device FAILED to protect my computer!Again, their guarantee to repair/replace is something which they fail to stand behind. I'll never buy from them again. They deserve to be ignored as a choice in protecting your equipment.

I was given a gel kit for Christmas last year and went online to purchase a light. There were so many options and prices ranging from $20-250+ for UV and LED lights. After reading reviews and researching the options I decided to get this particular light. After using it for about 4 months here's what I think of it:Positives:-Convenience: I was hooked on being to cure both my hands at once. I am already annoyed that I have to take off my old polish one hand at a time, I don't think I could handle having to cure and hand at a time also (except maybe with a quick curing LED).-Wattage: I've seen UV lamps with wattage as low as 9 and as high as about 54. This lamp is 45 watts, which seems powerful enough to accomplish what I want.-Settings: I use the 2 minute setting for everything and I've never had any problems. There's also a 3 minute and continuous setting. I'm glad I don't have to look at the clock or set a stopwatch to time myself.-Price: After spending a few hours researching these lamps I realized this is probably has the best features for the price. There are models from $20 up to about $300 (LED usually), this seemed to have everything I need for about $50. That's just a little over the price of one gel manicure at the salon.-Works with all my polish: I mostly have Gelish prodcuts, but also bought Red Carpet and FingerPaints products, which cure equally as well under this light.Cons:-UV vs LED: In hindsight I wish I would've sprung for a LED light. The bulbs last much longer, there's no cancer causing UV rays and the drying time is much quicker. The better LED lights are $60+, but I did see one that got pretty good reviews for about $33. The lower priced LED lamps generally only do one hand at a time, up to 4 fingers at a time (thumb separate). I'm not entirely sure if I'd be happy having to put my hand under a lamp 4 times for each coat of polish I do, even if it's for less time. The LED I want is $270 and does one whole hand at a time, 30 seconds each coat. Now that I've grown to love and rely on my gel manicures I know it's probably going to eventually be worth the extra investment.-Bulbs: I can't say exactly how long these bulbs will last, but the replacements are sure pricey. I looked it up and each bulb is right now $11.21, so the cost of 3 new bulbs will be $33.63. This is just a quick Amazon search, I didn't do any major price comparisons. That's not much cheaper than the cost of the whole unit plus bulbs. I think when I get to the point of needing to replace them I will be giving my unit away and upgrading to my desired LED dyer.-Size: Both a positive and negative, yes, you can dry both hands at once, but this thing is pretty big to store. If you are short on space this might not be the best option out there.All things considered, I'm not unhappy with my purchase and am giving 4 stars for "I like it". I wasn't sure if I was going to like doing my own manicures or if I would do it a couple times and would get bored of it, so I didn't want to spend too much on a light. Now that I know I'm going to be doing my own gel manicures for years to come, I will eventually upgrade. For anyone who wants a relatively inexpensive, larger, 2 handed UV lamp, I definitely recommend this unit. If you have the cash to spend it might be better just to initially go with the LED.

The hubby is always on the look out for something to control his itching and flaking, especially in the drier months. We recently tried Target's "t plus therapeutic shampoo" which worked well but had an odor that offended the great nose. I picked this shampoo up, hoping it would satisfy all the senses and here are the hubby's thoughts:"This is a 2 in 1 anti-dandruff shampoo & conditioner with Bio Nutrium 10, a blend of 10 Nutrients and botanical actives including Ginseng, Tea Tree, and Natural Mint.  The active ingredient is Pyrithione Zinc (1.0%) for anti-dandruff.  It lathers very nicely, smells great, and a nice consistency.  Packaging is very attractive.  The question is how good is it for controlling dandruff and itching?  I used it for about a month regularly.  Previous to this I had been using Head & Shoulders anti-dandruff 2 in 1 shampoo and conditioner with the same active ingredient.  For me Clean Man Scalp Therapy did a better job of controlling the dandruff & associated itching.  Maybe the Bio Nitrium 10 is the difference?  It left my hair manageable and smooth.  I found the price to be slightly higher in my area,  than H&S;, but comparable.  So if Pyrithione Zinc works for your dandruff and itching, Clear Man Scalp Therapy is an excellent choice that you must try.5/5 Stars"So, it is a rave from the big guy and here is hoping the irritation and flakes are a thing of the past.

Since having about six inches chopped off from the length of my hair, I decided to switch back to a one-inch straightening iron instead of my two-inch one (which I love). I used to think that the "good" straightening irons were the most expensive, top-of-the-line brands like Chi--until myRemington S7230 Wet 2 Straight Flat Iron with Soy Hydra Complex, 2 Inchconvinced me otherwise. I'd had the worst luck with the brands that the grocery stores and places like Target stock; I had several simply stop working on me or they just couldn't get my sort-of-wavy hair straight. I finally invested in the Chi and was very happy with it, until it stopped working after just a few years. I promptly threw it out and decided to go back to Target brands to see if I could find one that I liked. The first straightener I bough after my Chi died was from Revlon (Revlon Straightener - 1 Inch, and the only thing I found useful about it was the heat resistant storage pad--great if you travel a lot and need to pack up right after showering and getting ready for your day.PROS:1. The Design. The Nano Diamond Straightener is really lightweight, allows you to adjust the heat settings from 220 degrees to 420 and even save your preferred temperature, and automatically shuts down after one hour if you forget to turn it off. I like the placement of the buttons; they are right beneath the pads and I'm not worried about burning myself if I want to adjust the temperature while in use.2. The Price. I won't spend over $50 on a straightener anymore and on the various websites where I've seen it, the Nano Diamond straightener is offered for anywhere between $25-$35 (shipping may not be included).CON:1. The Performance. This is going to vary from person to person, so the only way to truly know how well it works for you is to try it out for yourself--and keep the receipt handy in case you want to return it. I have fine hair that breaks quite easily and often gets frizzy after being straightened. I use a few salon brand products to tame the fly-aways, but the Nano Diamond advertises smooth styling. Unfortunately for me, that was not the case. I've tested out a few of the higher temperature settings and my hair has held the style for a little while, but then goes right back to having the slightest wave to it. Revlon suggests a low heat temperature for fine hair, but I've never heat styled my hair at lower than 350 with any heat tool.FINAL THOUGHTS:Overall, there are more positives to the Nano Diamond than there are negative, but performance is the most important factor to me. I've been using the Nano Diamond for over a month now and I'm disappointed that my hair will not hold the style. The ends of my hair (about shoulder length) always flip out, I can never get a smooth/straight look on the right side, and it often sounds as if my hair catches between the blades. I'll continue to experiment with the temperature settings and maybe even switch shampoos to see if that could make a difference as well. It's not the straightener I would most recommend to someone, but it's closer to what I ultimately want in an affordable straightener.UPDATE 1/6/14:I have cut my hair to above shoulder-length (dramatic A-line cut) and have switched from Herbal Essences (I now believe this was the culprit for nearly all of my hair woes) to Matrix Biolage Colorcaretherapie Color Care shampoo and conditioner. I have used the Nano Diamond every day since my new haircut and have no complaints on the performance of this flat iron any longer.  so I have added on another star to my original rating. I am not planning on having long hair ever again, so I won't be able to test it again on my own hair to see if the flipping out of the ends no longer occur now that I've switched hair care products.

I'm a sports photographer. I've been using this camera for a while now. I decided to give it a good testing before even writing any remarks.There seems to be a lot of hype about the focus issue. Personally, I've never experienced the problem, and I bet the majority of photographers haven't either. Some people need to do research before blaming the camera. There are many user faults, causing the camera to seem as if it is not focusing.  They also need to check their lenses.I shoot different sports, from rugby to show jumping, polo and horse racing. So, tracking a fast moving object (person or horse) is very important. Out of about 100 images, maybe 2 would be out of focus. Those I would more blame on myself than the camera. People really need to RTFM. There are so many custom settings, especially for focus, to enable you to use the camera to it's full potential, for whatever you use it for.The camera is pure quality. I don't deny that there are some focus issues, but again, these are under specific circumstances. The body is solid and sturdy. It is good to hold and not too heavy. The battery life is brilliant (nearly 3,000 shots per charge). The fps is stunning. I've had to limit my burst rate to 25 frames, just so I don't fill my memory cards too quickly. :-) I did give it a good workout, using the full fps and burst rate at an event, and I was stunned.There are just so many custom functions, that allow you to setup the camera depending on what you are doing. That is brilliant for me, since some events are at night under lights, while others are during the day, either cloudy or bright. Different saved setting sets allow me to quickly change to suit my circumstances.For those who need a fast, reliable pro camera, this would very much be my camera of choice.
