The Seagate Expansion 1TB USB 3.0 hard drive is billed as a "PC Expansion" drive rather than a "take your data with you" drive, though of course you could use it as either.  The enclosure is a black plastic slab with one end having a USB 3 "Micro B" socket and the opposite end is slightly slanted.  A single, small blue LED indicates drive activity. A suitable connecting cable is provided. The socket used can also fit a USB 2 MicroUSB connector, but you get only USB 2.0 speed with that. Unlike some portable drives I have used, the Seagate will power up from just a USB 2.0 connector and doesn't require the extra power some of these drives do.The speed of the Seagate was impressive for a portable drive.  Using Atto, a popular benchmark program, I measured a maximum read/write rate of 117MB/second over a USB 3.0 link. This compares with about 200MB/sec for a 3.5" Seagate 2TB SATA-III drive and 47MB/sec for a USB 2.0 drive I had (a SimpleTech 160GB model that does require extra power.)The Seagate was quiet in operation - a gentle whir is all I heard. There are soft rubber bumpers on the underside of the drive to prevent any vibrations from being telegraphed.While the size of the drive is much the same as the other portable drives I have (indeed, a bit slimmer), the square corners and lack of any kind of carrying case might put you off carting this drive around.  It is, as stated, meant to sit on a desk or other surface, but it can be carried around and makes an easy and inconspicuous addition to a PC or laptop.The drive comes preformatted with a single NTFS partition. A registration program is provided on the disk, which you are free to ignore.  You can, of course, reformat the drive to whatever suits you - even for a Mac or Linux system.

I needed a wireless networking card for the new computer I was building so I began searching around for a PCI-Express x1 card that would fit my MicroATX board (Intel DH77EB). I didn't want to go for the USB dongles as I think those look tacky hanging off the computer's USB port. My computer is in my room on the top floor of a two-story house, and our router/modem are in the basement (trust me, I don't know why) so running a network cable up two floors wasn't an option.I always used a Linksys WMP54G for years in my older OptiPlex 745, but that's such an old card now, and it wouldn't fit the PCI slots on my new motherboard, so searching for a new card was the only option.I came across the ASUS PCE-N10 card and couldn't be happier. During the building process of the computer, I plugged in the wireless card, finalized all the other parts of the system, and then installed Windows 7 Pro 64-bit. I then put in the driver CD that came with the card and had Windows find the drivers manually through Device Manager because I can't stand it when vendors install their crapware. The Windows utilities are just fine. The card installed instantly with no problems at all. I didn't even have to reboot after the drivers installed, but I did anyway. The card instantly found our network, connected successfully, and is blazing fast! Much faster than the WMP54G on my older computer. We're on a ~3 Mbps DSL connection and sometimes I think web pages are cached on my machine already, because the speeds are that fast. We don't have a wireless-N router so the card obviously screams (and works nicely) on a wireless-G network.Overall I am very pleased with this card, and I get an even better signal with this card than I ever did with the older WMP54G. I would get between 3/5 and 4/5 bars in my room, but with this card, I get a full 5/5 bars on any side of my room (apparently wireless-N can reach farther distances, but we're using a Linksys WRT54GL router which is only wireless-G). We even have the plaster walls (I think; house made in the 1950s), so we don't have the thin walls of newer homes and the signal still reaches that far.

These "Moonrock" in-ear speakers by Moshaudio are priced in the mid-range spectrum of the genre. Far superior to the junky buds that come with an IPod, and much better than the bargain-basement jokes which fail to delivery much range or volume. They admittedly fall short of the higher end versions (Beats, Boze) in quality. So what distinguishes this item from the competition (Panasonic, JBuds, Sony)?First, the cutesy container (a "custom cable manager case") is not easy to use, but a cute gimmick.Second, sound quality. There's as broad a range of sound here as you'll find in any of the competitors. They approach the more expensive sets in base, although not with treble or volume.Third, the integrated microphone/click button works very easily with your Ipod or Iphone. Did NOT work on my Android.Fourth, the ear bud sizes are easily distinguished, and work quite well. While not really noise cancelling,selecting the correct size really enhances the sound impact.Finally, comfort. I had them on for more than a few hours and they remained quite comfortable.

I've used this pad several times for backpacking and tailgate camping, and I have to say they are just great! I've camped and backpacked all my life and I've never been as comfortable as I have been on this pad. I have of course used "another famous national brand" (you know who I'm talking about), and they don't even compare.This pad is very light and very compact (the Nalgene comparison is spot on), making it ideal for backpacking. Yeah, it takes a little breath to get it blown up, but c'mon, if you just hiked up a mountain, I think you can handle it, it's not that hard. Besides that, you don't want to fill it up full because then it becomes "bouncy". In fact, I've bounced right off mine before!As for durability, there's no question this thing will last if you take a little care of it. I'm north of 250 lbs and I'm not at all afraid of popping it. Just make sure there's nothing sharp in or under your tent and you should be fine. If you do end up getting a puncture, it comes with a small patch kit.Bottom line: for the price, size and weight, this is a great value for the level of comfort it brings. The ONLY disadvantage is that it is made of smooth nylon, and so is my sleeping bag, so there's a little bit of trouble with sliding off it, but IMHO that's a minor disadvantage.

I'm a long-distance runner and am coming off surgery for a broken ankle a year ago.  I've often wondered about compression socks and sleeves but found them too expensive to try.I have a pair of compression soccer socks and have worn them running without feeling any effect.  After the ankle surgery, I saw the benefit of compression on swelling.So, I tried these and for what they are, I like them.  They were tighter than I expected, which is good I guess--I got mediums and I wear an 8.5 medium-width shoe, although when running, I'm more comfortable with a size 9.I felt lighter with these socks.  I think it was mostly because of the way my foot felt inside the shoe, though.  Because they were tight, it took quite a while to get them on and off and, when running, every minute is sacred because the running time, itself, is long.The issue for me, after wearing them for a 6-mile run on the treadmill and a 10-mile run outside, is that I don't think the compression benefits touted are real.  I still ache and I don't feel the recovery was any faster.Still, if you are a fan of compression gear, I recommend these.  If you are on the fence about the value, I can't nudge you off the fence in that regard.

I love these bags. I own three, for me and my kids. These are my kids' favorite sleeping bags to use, even when they're just having a sleepover, and they could easily use a big, plush, comfy old-fashioned enormous sleeping bag.I bought one for my son about three years ago for a little over $30. As soon as I could find it in stock again at close to the same price, I bought two more. (These have fluctuated quite wildly in price in the past: from $31 to $99). We've used them camping several times each year, including a recent August back-packing weekend almost 6,000 feet up into Mount Rainier National Park, where the nighttime lows were well down into the 40s. We have always been very comfortable.Cons: They're a bit tricky to pack (Tip - don't try to establish a 'method'; just ram it into the stuff sack, then cinch it tight). You'll get the hang of it after a bit. My 11 year old can pack hers, so it shouldn't be beyond you.The zipper is fiddly, and prone to catching. Be gentle with it. (See below, and all the 1-star reviews).They seem as if they're not the sturdiest. The same 11 year old kicked the bottom of the zipper free of it's stitching the first time she used hers. A few minutes with the needle and thread put that right, and she hasn't done any more damage in the two years since.Pros: They are tiny. 7-8" inches in diameter by 12-14" in length when packed in the stuff sack. They're less than half the size of any other bag I've seen at close to this price. They fit easily sideways into the bottom of any of our backpacks.They are light.I find it very comfortable and perfectly roomy for me - but I'm only 5'6"/167cm and about 175lbs/80Kg.They are warm. Even at 46F or so, and only wearing shorts, I was unzipping my bag to cool off during the night. The bags are very adjustable to give you a snug 'mummy' fit around your neck and head. I wouldn't bank on these for winter camping, but during the rest of the year we've been very toasty despite making no special effort.They're still only $40.If you're after a mummy bag for spring/summer/fall backpacking, and you're a normal person on a normal budget, you should probably buy one of these.

When it comes to data cables remember this: they either deliver the data or they don't. Once the bits are flowing, increasing price does NOT yield increased performance. The consistently high pricing of premium cables (anywhere) and the often very high prices of data cabling in brick and mortar stores represent unfounded marketing hype or an immediate consumer need, and don't add enough value to justify their often "monstrous" prices.As HDMI becomes the de facto standard for both computing and A/V connections, don't be caught without the one cable you need to make your shiny new thing-that-plugs-in deliver its content.This 9to5 HDMI shows details in construction that belie its very affordable price.  Its connection terminals are firmly attached to the insulation. The terminals are marked with 9To5 branding on one side and "HDMI" marking on the reverse.While I've never had a data cable go bad after install, these cables carry a lifetime warranty, a surprising extra for such low-cost cable.The braided cloth insulation on this cable provides a visual cue to help differentiate from others in your bundle.While I believe that "cables are cables", this one shows attention to construction detail and arrives with a lifetime warranty, separating it from other unbranded commodity-priced cables.Select with confidence.Note: This item was provided for review purposes.

I have a Panasonic Lumix DMC-FZ28K that I use for most photography, but I was interested in this camera for two reasons: The 14MP resolution and the "share" feature. I am a bit disappointed in both.It may be unfair to compare this camera at under $200 to a camera that cost about twice that. However, the Kodak feels cheap and the pictures (other than those taken in very bright light) look soft and grainy.The share feature works to easily upload photos to sites such as Twitter, Facebook, Flickr and many others, but it tacks on a tag about the camera that I had to manually delete for each photo. There does not seem to be a way to set the default NOT to mention the camera. I had forgotten how bad Kodak software can be. (I have a Kodak zi8 video camera that I love, but the software is useless.) Kodak also uses a special formatting for the SD card, so when I move the card between this camera and my Panasonic it has to read and "process" any photos taken by the Panasonic. I am not sure if that is the camera or the software doing it, but it is annoying either way.The camera takes 4 - AA batteries. It includes the batteries and a charger. It takes a LONG time to charge the included batteries, but I was able to use the camera with standard non-rechargeable batteries right away and they seem to last quite a while. I haven't had to replace them so far. The cover for the battery compartment is difficult to close and feels very flimsy. I suspect that will be the first thing to break.This might be a good camera for someone who wants to take photos primarily for online use. It is easy to take photos, and they should look fine in the sizes and resolutions commonly used online. There are lots of modes (e.g., portrait, sports, landscape, burst, etc.) that make it simple to get good shots. The camera responds quickly and has modes, such as burst, that allow you to take several photos in a short time.One thing I especially like about this camera is the Panoramic mode. Choose Panorama, then take your first shot. The camera displays a piece of that shot on the LCD screen, over the lens view. Line up the piece of the previous shot with what you see on the screen and take the second picture. Repeat. The camera then stitches the shots together--no need for photo editing software. It is easy, especially if you use a tripod to keep the camera steady and level while taking the shots. Even without a tripod, though, you can get a seamless panoramic image. Nice feature!The Kodak EasyShare Z5010 Digital Camera is an OK camera, especially for the price. If you want high-quality, high-resolution images, though, you will be disappointed.

Essentials. Yet there's no delta ring tool. Ever try to remove a delta ring without one? No punches, but there are "push pins". No hammer. A nice inexpensive polymer head would be nice. The barrel nut tool only has two pegs. Some less expensive barrel nuts can fail with only two pegs, especially for those who use more force than finesse to "fit" parts. The torque wrench only starts to register force right at the minimum needed for mil spec barrel installation, making limited for anything but barrels. And if you've rechased your threads with appropriate taps and dies and lapped your receiver you may only need say 30lbs to get solid installation. The lower block is pretty good, if you have the ultimate workbench to mount it on, but it's about average otherwise. There's a couple others for about the same or a little less a bit more versatile. The upper block, is sufficient. The gas tube alignment tool is better than not having one, by far. The instructions while accurate are a bit sparse for a novice. There's little room for much of anything else in the bag. A couple of the screwdriver bits included are flat out awesome. Aside from the torque wrench, there's little here the average user, or even occasional builder really needs (and that's only going to get used changing barrels). Someone working as a smith or armorer is likely going to want tools with better performance. And not meaning necessarily increased overall price either. But as a kit, it will get the job done, and it keeps it together in one place. Selecting a few choice pieces, getting a smaller sized bag that'll hold your selection of tools, and a visit to harbor freight will render better value. Just more time invested in picking out the good value items from the not so good. This isn't a bad product. It just could be more effective, and similarly priced, or less expensive for what's included.

The King Tuk holster is one of the most comfortable on the market, but that does not make it "King." The slight forward cant helps to keep the majority of the grip, which is the hardest to conceal, forward rather than straight up and down like a lot of holsters.The holster will hold any length of 1911-based pistols from 3" to 5" barrels. The Para Ordinance P14.45 fits perfectly in this holster, even with its wide grip. The "Combat" cut protects the pistol from sweat while protecting the user from the hard edges of the pistol and affording the operator a good purchase on the pistol while removing the firearm from the holster.The holster is designed to work with tension; tension being the pressure that the body places against the backing of the holster and which helps keep the firearm in the holster. The more the tension, the tighter the fit of the holster to the firearm. This also means that the operator should consider a well-made belt for added tension by pulling the holster into the body.The twin, screw-mounted clips hold the holster firmly on the belt. I prefer; however a single screw in the clip to allow the clip to rotate slightly on the holster as the body moves while maintaining a positive hold on the belt. I also prefer a rough-out backing to help keep the holster in place.The Kydex portion of the holster is thick, well formed, and well secured to the backing. There are no sharp edges and it adequately protects the trigger so I have no problem carrying "Condition 1" with this holster. You will find; however, that if your pistol has an extended safety, the safety will eventually cut into the top layer of leather. This might cause some premature wear over time. However, it is also a plus as the holster develops an "indent" for the safety and may prevent it from coming "off-safe" without the operator's knowledge. I would prefer a single, thick piece of backing to the double-layer of the King Tuk but I still find the King Tuk more than adequate for its purpose of housing a pistol until I need it. The top layer is glued and stitched to the rear backing and the stitching appears well done.At first, while stiff, the holster eventually molds itself to the wearer's body contours and becomes more comfortable over time. The wearer can adjust the holster up or down easily. Out of the four King Tuk holsters that I own, I have not had to change the adjustment from the factory setting, as the top edge of the holster is just above the belt line and the forward cant is a plus. For an IWB holster, the lower the better; especially for birds who have a 6-foot wingspan like I have. The width of the holster is also a plus for comfort as well as stabilizing the holster.All in all, the King Tuk is a good IWB holster at a reasonable cost. While it may not be a "King" of IWB holsters, it certainty is at least a "Prince" among them.

The Healthy Infusions Wahl Pro Dryer has a powerful airflow and the typical heat settings on most common blow-dryers.  It is a little heavier and louder than most conventional dryers but it feels sturdy and fits comfortably while holding it. The conditioning cartridge seemed to get in the way since I use a brush to blow dry my hair.  I normally use some type of heat safe spray before drying or styling my hair, so I decided to just try drying it with no styling products in my hair. The conditioning strip left my hair feeling stiff and heavy.  I quickly washed my hair again and dried it without the conditioning strip and used my regular styling products and my hair came feeling and looking much, much better.  I do have coarse, thick hair so maybe it would work better for individuals with fine hair.  I will continue to use this dryer but without the conditioning strips.  The whole concept of a conditioning blow-dryer is great but I think Wahl needs to do some more fine tuning before this becomes the beauty invention of the decade.

The original seat on my mountain bike was only 5.5 inches wide by 10 inches long and being a big (6'2") guy, I found it uncomfortable on long rides, so was looking for an upgrade. Unfortunately, not much info on the actual size is included in this listing, so I thought I'd include it here.This seat is 3 inches in height, 8 inches at the widest point and 10 inches from the back to the horn. The extra 2.5 inches in width REALLY makes a difference, not to mention the seat contains extra padding and compression "springs" underneath which act as shock absorbers. (They're actually rubber bumpers, not springs...) Surprisingly, it doesn't look too big on my bike at all. (a Schwinn Moab DS3 - whose original seat always looked sort of small for the thick frame.) It also comes with a post mount which can be removed if you attach directly to the seat rails instead.As others have mentioned, the Amazon pics make it look like it's made of some sort of soft, neoprene fabric, but in actuality it's all plastic/vinyl. The additional pictures from another reviewer are much more accurate on how it looks. Overall, it's a very affordable upgrade for people with larger body frames or lower body pain from smaller seats.

My wife gave me the Stiletto 2 as a Christmas gift.  Here it is New Year's eve and I'm awaiting my 3rd return.  Yes, 3 defective units.In my case, the device will not hold a WiFi connection most of the time and will disconnect in as quick as 30 seconds.  It connects and buffers quickly, but drops connection (even while showing a strong signal) in moments.  Several times the dropped connection initiated a reboot of the device which takes an exceptionally long time to run as it is.I've had few problems with the Satellite signal with both the internal antenna as well as the headphones.  Both work as well as my GPS receiver in that as long as I have a fairly good view of the sky, I get a signal.  If I go inside, I lose it.  In fact, I've even had a weak but usable signal inside where I never expected one.In dealing with Sirius custom support, I sent a highly detailed e-mail with the results of testing with my second unit.  Over 48 hours later (they promise a 24 hour response) I got a reply that told me to call their Technical Support.I get a 'Supervisor' on Technical Support that insists that the problem with my WiFi is a bad internal Antenna.  This is the same reason I got for unit #1 that was malfunctioning in a similar manner.I find this diagnosis doubtful for several reasons.1) I can connect and initially download (buffering) from any distance from my router that I can get a signal on my laptop.2) Even sitting less than a foot from the router with full signal and no other RF devices (computer, phone, cell phone, etc) on, the unit drops connection as if I had a marginal connection.As an engineer who has developed software for networked devices and did some early packet radio work, the failure looks more like an issue with lost packets and time outs rather than a "Faulty Antenna".  Especially since the same antenna is used for the satellite reception and I've found that to be quite good given the size of the device.If you are looking for a portable satellite radio, this is a good unit, but if you are looking for a way to enjoy Sirius programming while working around the house where you can't get a clear view of the sky or the average boombox or stereo is not a good solution, this device seemed like a perfect solution, but is instead very disappointing.Hopefully Sirius will get the WiFi issues resolved soon, but until that occurs, I CAN NOT recommend this product as the primary use as portableradio is simply not well served.UPDATE:  There was a firmware update earlier this summer.  I have since set up a satellite repeater inside my home so that I do not have to use the WiFi service (the WiFi service also does not carry things like NFL Games, so this is just as well.)  However, since the latest firmware update, I have tried several times and succeeded in getting the WiFi service to work as advertised.I changed NOTHING on my network.  So, there must have been something in the Stiletto that wasn't working correctly.  In any case, it seems to be fixed.I'd now upgrade the Rating of this device to a 4.  I still have a few reservations, but it's now functioning as advertised.Things I still don't like:Recordings can act odd at times.  Generally a complete shutdown and restart solves whatever was wrong.  Be sure to clean up your recordings so that you don't fill the memory.  Once full, really weird things happen.If you have the device docked and it records something while in standby mode, you can not turn it on without either undocking it, or toggling the on/off several times.You can not delete Radio Replay items.  It says you did, but they are still there.Many times the processor can't keep up with operations from a remote control.  I can't tell you how often I have to hit a remote button more than once for the device to actually do what I ask.  This is most annoying when the phone rings and I can't pause the music.Things that are just AWESOME:Basically a Tivo for radio.  Record what you want, and you can fast forward, rewind, etc.  Pause live material and you can then pick up where you left off or move ahead to 'now'.  Can also rewind to hear something you might have missed.  This only works over the satellite, not on WiFi.Any music station you listen to is recorded for radio replay.  If you can't get a good signal, just replay an old recording.All in all I'm quite happy with this device now.

The Microsoft Wireless Optical Elite has been a less than satisfactory purchase for me.  Although I like the feel of the keyboard, I am still having trouble getting used to the layout, and have found that it is easy to hit the thumbwheel on the left side, causing the screen to scroll unexpectedly.  I've found the placement and layout of the group of buttons to the right of the main keys, and containing the Delete, PageUp and PageDown buttons, to be particularly troublesome: I always seem to be hitting the wrong key, and have sometmes brushed the Delete key while typing, with the usual unhappy results.The driver is constantly telling me that I have low signal strength, even though the wireless receiver is within 2 inches of the keyboard.  Replacing the batteries has not helped.  I've also found that it tends to skip characters while typing.The mouse is a bit better: it feels comfortable and works as expected, with no surprises.All in all, although better than the keyboard on my laptop, this is not a product I can recommend, particularly given the price. I don't know why everyone is complaining about the faint smell. Yes, it is faint when you open the bottle cap and sniff it. Once you dab it on you - just a quarter size and let it sit and go into your skin, the fragrance will start to grow. Initially when you first buy the perfume, you may need to put on about 12" of it on your body to detect the scent. Then go out for a day or so (walk around in the fresh air) and you'll start to notice the scent. After your nose can delineate between the fresh air and the scent, you'll notice that the scent is actually quite strong. Later, just a dab will last you 48 hours - through showers and repeated hand washing / dish washing.This fragrance reminds me of my Kindergarten teachers and older women in their late 30s up to 60s wearing this stuff. This is definitely not for women in their 20s, as it smells too "motherly" and "married." It's a comforting, sweet scent, but it smells old, as in a popular scent that we grew up with when we were little, so it lends itself as an older woman scent from the last generation.

It might be the winner. I have listened to a number of speakers and simply I am always kept wanting more. And by more, I mean real sound that is natural and able to fill a room with ease. For the $100 category-- this is by far the best thing I have listened to. Although not perfect, it is really good. What most sound like is an old Realistic handheld radio that now costs $200.... and that is truly how I feel they sound.There is a lot of hype with Bose, Ultimate Ears, Jambox, TDK, JBL--- you name it I have auditioned it. I even auditioned units in the $400-600 range... still disappointing for some which is a shame. Then there is what I have come to believe is three of the best units on the market with markedly different approaches.... one is the G-BOOM the other is Logitech UE boombox and lastly the TDK A73. The UE unit is the larger silver unit that many say is bass heavy... and I agree, but if your source has an equalizer you should be able to address it. I am waiting for the TDK unit to come it, but if the sound simply exceeds the A33 it will be what I am looking for. Otherwise, so far the G-BOOM and UE are good units. The G-BOOM is simply not as bass heavy as it has smaller drivers to start than the UE.So then there is packaging. I like the G-BOOM. Simple and feels like you actually have something to hang onto. UE, same deal--- weighty and competent. The TDK is still to be determined.So where does that leave me. It leaves me in an interesting position as the G-BOOM is under $100 the UE is $250 and the TDK is around $200.So with all of this there is the point that I am comparing a $100 speaker setup compared to $200+ and not finding that much of a difference so far. That is a huge plus for the G-BOOM. Bluetooth pairing was easy enough and it has stayed paired no problem. They do include a 3.5mm cable for direct connection and so far I have listened to it both ways.... pretty close and I am using my Moto X. From there the EQ settings are fine and are not tragically different between them. No display but three LED's on the front. One for power, one for which EQ you are using (it flashes every time you change it), and if you are using Bluetooth.So how does it sound? I am actually impressed. I listen to anything that the mood brings. Right now it is Lady Gaga and earlier was The Band Perry and the 80's on the way into work. It plays clean, nice and loud, and it is pleasant. Stereo separation is only OK because the drivers are so close together, but that is expected. The more I listen, the more I like it.My initial goal was something portable, then it became portable with GOOD sound, then it became as portable as it can be with GOOD sound as I kept being let down. Right now I am enjoying it.Bluetooth can have some interference as I have found having it close to a router.... when the volume is low or the music is paused you get the "computery" interference for a lack of better words. Moved it one foot away from the source of the interference and it all goes away. It does not exist on a wired connection.So far it is worth the money. As long as it stays rugged and lives half as long as my last boombox.... we'll be good.UPDATE: I have settled on what is going to fill the void: a TDK A33. The G-BOOM is good and gets plenty loud, but somehow TDK was able to stuff a lot of sound in the A33 and do so in a small compact chassis. So although I like the G-Boom and for the price it is good, the A33 just had a price drop and for the $30 difference, I would go for the A33 from TDK.

This, to me, is not really a commitment NOT to test on animals, but rather a marketing gimmick to sell to people that don't want products that aren't cruelty free.  It just means they won't ALWAYS test on animals.  The parent company does test on animals as well.  Animal testing, to me, is the antithesis of pure.With so many products out there that are just as good that have a better commitment to eliminate unnecessary testing on animals, I'd pass on buying this one again until they make a full commitment, not one that is ambiguous with some pretty big loop holes.

I'm a fitness nut and got one of these from my wife for my birthday.  The concept is very well thought out as you can wear it on your wrist, and the website is great for counting steps, estimating calories burned, sleep patterns, and very active minutes.  However, the biggest issue I have with the Fitbit Flex is that I believe it tracks alot more movements than steps.  As one reviewer pointed out, she can't wear the Fitflex when crocheting, as it counts those movements as steps.  At the office, when I'm working on my computer and typing alot, I notice that it is also counting these arm movements as steps, and I do wear this on my non-dominant hand.  When I lift weights, it also counts a number of the movements as steps, even though I might be stationary, such as during a bench press or overhead press.  This is a decent device that will motivate you to move more and walk, but take into account that it may count other movements as well.  The best advice is to follow the recommendations from a number of fitness organizations: engage in 30 minutes of moderate activity a day (such as brisk walking) and you won't need to count steps.

I hope this information can help others who may be having the same issue I was. My computer which is three years old and has been running 24/7 started having random issues. I did a factory image recovery and that did not help. My internet started lagging severely. At speedtest.net I would normally get approximately a 10 to 40 ms ping, 30 to 60 mb download and 4 to 4.2 mb upload speed on a 30 down/4 up internet speed package from Charter. At random times throughout the day I would notice web pages would freeze up my computer and when I went to Speedtest.net it would act very strange. The ping test will show a progress bar and it coincides with your results. Bar moving fast = low ping speed.  The bar would move across very slow but give a 10 ms result. The download test would show the needle on the meter jump up over 20 mb but then the result would read 0.68. This is worse than dial up. The upload test would end up at .004 mb. This happened while a service tech from Charter was here and he said it was probably my computer. I didn't believe him as internet speed should be dictated by the ISP and not affected by the computer. He checked everything inside and out and even replace my modem at my request. Problem persisted. I used windows disk check and it reported errors with the hard drive. I downloaded Seagate SeaTools and it reported that the SMART test was good but it failed the short drive self test, generic short test and would not fix the disk when running fix all. I bought this ST310005N1A1AS-RK hard drive from a retail store so I could return it easily if it was defective out of the box. I had the old drive out and the new drive installed in less than 15 minutes. I used my recovery disks to format the drive and reload the operating system. It has been 3 days and so far this has solved all of my speed issues. I have had NO freezing web pages and every test at Speedtest has been normal. My best guess is that since web pages are saved to the hard drive for faster loading in the future, the drive must have not been able to read them which caused a huge lag while the system tried to decide if it should continue trying to load a web page off the hard drive or download it again from the internet. That is the only thing that makes sense to me. I also talked to a tech at a retail store and he didn't believe a hard drive would be to blame for slow internet speed either.As for the ST310005N1A1AS-RK itself, there was a lot of confusion with the part #s. If you research ST310005N1A1AS-RK you will see specs (including these on Amazon) that it is a 3GB/s, 32 MB cache. When I opened the box, the sticker on the drive said it was a ST1000DM003. Researching this indicates the drive is a 6GB/s, 64 MB cache. I called Seagate and when I read off the two part #s and the different specs, he completely beat around the bush and said I had received the correct drive and it was not mis packaged. He also verified the warranty was still good for 27 months. I asked this question here on Amazon and received an answer that the ST310005N1A1AS-RK is the retail kit version of the ST1000DM003. The problem with that is I have seen reviews on this going back to 2009. They seem to have the same part # with different firmware revisions. Very confusing.All in all the new drive is working perfectly. I will update if there are any issues with it in the future.*Update 12-11-2013*Everything is still working great. Through much research I have discovered that these drives have advanced power management that will cause the head to park itself and slow down the speed of the hard drive to save energy. From what I have gathered, this is completely unnecessary on a desktop computer (it has some advantages on a laptop). It can cause a chirping/squeaking sound and also has the potential to shorten the life of the hard drive due to increased load/unload cycles. There is no firmware update from Seagate to correct this. I downloaded a free utility called hdparm (google it for the download) that seems to be the only fix to this issue. It allows you to set the hard disk for maximum performance, power saving or turn off power management altogether. The only drawback is that you have to run the utility every time you reboot your computer.

Princeton Tex Apex Pro 200 Lumen head lamp is very bright, made from storng but light weight plastics. Uses the CR123 Lithium batteries and lasts forever on low settings.I wanted to know if your can use the 4 LED and the 1 MAX LED at the same time. The answer is no.The LEDs are supposed to be regulated to where it keeps the same brightness throughout the battery's life on low. But it does not. Near the end of the battery life it will start too dim and sometimes even flicker.Order your CR123 batteries in bulk online for better prices.Headband is comfortable and soft for extended wearing. As for the waterproof.. I consider 1 Meter as water resistant rather than waterproof. Dunks, splashes, and playing in water no deeper than your average pool.I use the Princeton Tex Apex Pro 200 Lumen head lamp for trail riding on our dirt bikes. Single LED on high produces a bright and wide enough beam to get us through the trails on the darkest nights. Use the low settings at sunset and high settings at night.

They feel very secure when tightened down.-'s: The paint arround the thumb screws on the side panels comes off easily from ware and tare. This doesnt bother me as I dont have to look at it every day. *This was originally about "Camera Flash" but includes most of their colors that I tried, roughly 50% of their available color lineAs with a lot of Red Carpet polish products, this one frustrated me. I'm not a gel polish novice so it's not a matter of 'apply thinner coats' or anything of that nature. But dang!! Like many of their polishes this did not last for me, and I could not get an even, professional looking finish for ANYTHING! I also had some residual bubbling.In short, it looked like I went babysitting and let some little kid paint my nails. Oh so horrible, and not a recommendation for me.I like other brands better, including the Gelish brand, Lomasi, Shellac. I reviewed the Gelish Basix kit if you want a lengthy review and comparison.UPDATE: I did find a trick to make this look better on longer nails; nails that extend past your fingertips. After applying base coat (I don't like the Red Carpet base or topper; try Gelish/Lomasi/Shellac), your first color coat should be a horizontal 'stripe' across the free edge of your nail, wrapping around the edge. Think of it like you are making a French tip, but with color. Cure that, and then proceed with two color coats going from cuticle to free edge. The two color coats will easily fill in the ledge made by the first horizontal coat. There are simply some Red Carpet colors that even THIS amount of work won't make opaque, but at least you might be able to salvage some. I jut prefer to spend $10 for gel color, with a better brand.

Not fun to run a zipper backwards and try to get the teeth to line back up so you can get the pouch open, get your stuff out, and figure out how to fix the issue. I purchased 5 of the 2TB drives back in March of 2011.  Four of them were installed in a OWC Mercury Elite Pro Qx2 setup for RAID 5 (Stripe with Parity) and the 5th drive is a spare. I've used Deskstar drives back in the IBM days but fortunately never purchased one of the IBM "Deathstar" drives around 2001.  I read a number of reviews about data loss with these 2TB drives but purchased them anyhow based on past experience with the Deskstar brand.  I wonder if some of the failures are a result of the enclosure people used for their RAID solution.  I have had a very positive experience with these drives and they have not let me down.  I would buy them again if needed.Update 02/11/2014: Just wanted to mention I have not had a single issue with these drives.  They have been used to backup my home network every day just shy of three years.  They run 24/7 and I don't let them sleep.

I go on a lot of camping, hiking, and backpacking/trekking trips where no electricity is available. For months, my friend brought his Energizer external battery pack to charge his iPhone, and friends' phones on our return home. The gadget person I am, I set out to purchase my own. However, reviews of the Energizer pack and other brands were not favorable, until I came across New Trent's products.I decided to buy the IMP500 for its great look (love the blue accents and rounded/bezeled edges/corners) and excellent reviews. It claims to charge a smartphone several times before running out of juice, and it was the right size for me to take on a backpacking trip.I took it on my 2-week trip to Peru: a 4-day Inca Trail trek to Machu Picchu, and a 1-week stay at the Peruvian Amazon Forest lodge, Inca Terra, and was never once disappointed. It charged my Samsung Galaxy S II smartphone, and 2 iPod Touch devices.Comes with a cloth pouch to store the battery pack, but there was no way to include the charging cable without scratching the battery pack's glossy interior. Which now brings me to the biggest issue with the product:The charging cable is proprietary. One end is USB, and the other is proprietary. I wish it was micro-USB based on the proprietary end so that in the event I lose the charging cable, I could use my smartphone's cable.Another improvement suggestion would be to allow you to manually turn off the battery pack -- currently, you have to leave it on and let a timeout shut it off. Perhaps New Trent could allow the user to long-hold the power button to manually force shut off?With that said, a 2-device charging version came out weeks after I purchased the product, and seeing how well the IMP500 works, I wish that I would have bought the 2-device version instead. Either way, I'm very happy with this product and would recommend it to anyone. I may purchase the more powerful 11,000mAH sibling in the future (which also allows charging of tablets like the iPad), but have no need for it right now.UPDATE 1/8/2014: After 2 years, the product still works GREAT! It has not yet failed on me and still is one of the few items I take with me wherever I go.

I love this range bag.  It is high quality, really well thought out, and a good value. Below are salient points based on my use and experience with this bag:*  Pockets - It's got pockets galore.  There are two zippered compartments on either side, which can accommodate one full-size pistol each.  One side contains a padded pistol rug, also zippered, and the other side is fully padded.  Both compartments are padded on the inner side, protecting them against hard objects in the main compartment.  The main compartment has three huge pockets with flaps that secure with Velcro.  The inner compartment itself opens wide with a double-zippered flap that also secures with Velcro.  There are also additional pockets on the inside of one pistol pocket.*  Convenience - The bag can either be carried by the two handles, which secure together by a flap with Velcro, or by the wide, adjustable shoulder strap which can be removed entirely by releasing two large plastic buckles.*  Quality  - The zippers throughout are large and easy to operate, with no binding.  They seem heavy and of good quality and have held up through frequent use.  The webbing used for the handles and shoulder strap are wide and durable, and they are stitched all the way down the sides of the bag, providing excellent support.  Nice!  The hasps of the zippers on both pistol pockets, as well as a zippered pocket on the outside of one of the pistol pockets are lockable.  That is, both hasps can be secured with a padlock, securing the compartment.  The lock rings are large, too, at over 1/4 inch, so a decent sized padlock can be used.  Of course, the entire bag can be filched, but it's still a nice touch so one can't simply reach into one of those pockets and lift what's there.  Overall, the finish and quality of the bag, the construction, and the materials are quite good.*  Size -  This bag measures approx. 16" across, 12" deep, and 10" tall.  It is soft-sided, of course, so dimensions may vary depending on how it is stuffed.  For me, it will carry two pistols, spare magazines and ammo for both, full muff-style hearing protection for two, safety glasses, rags, sighting binoculars (small), a small cleaning kit, and still have room for snacks.  It is a great bag for two, even for an extended stay at the range, or for those singles who bring more than one pistol to the range.Other thoughts:The bottom of the bag is padded and strengthened by some sort of fiber board and there are two wide rubber feet that are sewn onto the fabric.  The feet aren't tall enough to stand the bag fully off the ground, however, so it'll get wet if you put it down on a wet surface.  For those who care, the words 'UNCLE MIKE'S' are embroidered on the lower corner of each side, in white thread.  It's rather nicely done, but personally I prefer to de-badge most of my gear and in this case a black permanent marker made for a lower profile.  As mentioned, the bag is well-padded in all the right areas; it's good to know that the contents are safe and not being knocked about.  Having used this bag for many cycles of loading, unloading, carrying, and temperature extremes no issues have come up either in wear or quality.For me, the size, price, and utility of this bag are all spot on.  Uncle Mike's got this one right!

I wanted a large width belt with some flexibility that would securely support heavy items attached to it without allowing them to wobble or droop. This belt does just that. I, like most computer users, have used many different USB hubs over the years.  I've had mixed results...  while you'd think that they'd all be similar, there are big differences, and I've thrown away about half of all the hubs I've ever bought.I needed a desktop hub for a job I took some time ago.  I wanted to be able to leave the workstation case out of the way, with nothing but a pair of video cables and a USB cable going to my desk.  The USB hub would connect to the webcam I use for teleconferencing, to my keyboard/mouse wireless pickup, and various other devices I'd plug in from time to time (such as a portable media player device, thumbdrives, a BD burner, etc).The device worked flawlessly on every (USB 2.0) device I connected to it, and I never had a lost connection.  So, when I decided to put in a cabinet-top USB hub (to go to my home theater PC, found inside a cabinet), this was my first choice.Since then, I've decided to replace the two Belkin 7-port devices I carry in my laptop bag with just one of these, which will save space in my bag and provide better power to my various laptop accessories.This is an attractive, durable, reliable choice.  The physical configuration (with six front-facing ports, two rear-facing ports, and two more rear-facing ports which can be tilted upwards, and with the power and mini-USB input port on the rear) is just about perfect, and you can even stack these if you like, something you can't do with most such devices (with "weird" shapes").If this were a USB 3.0 device, it would be perfect...  and if there were a similar USB 3.0 hub, I'd recommend that instead.  But, as of today, there isn't.The device works well enough without external power... but if you're going to use more than one or two devices on it, you'll need the power cord attached, of course.The one complaint I have...  not really a complaint for ME, mind you, but I can see it being an annoyance to others... is the brilliantly bright blue LED strip along the top.  This makes for a very vivid "night light" and stays on at all times... even if the computer it's attached to is powered off.  This is nice, in a way... but it sure would be nice to be able to disable the "night light" "feature."  A small LED would have been better...  this is just overpoweringly bright in a dark room.But, if you plan to use this in a room where nobody is supposed to be trying to sleep... and if you don't need the increased per-port power availability and faster data throughput of USB 3.0...  this is probably the best on the market right now.

My first thought is that the bottle shape/size wasn't great for those of us with smaller hands. It's smooth, not textured so it was a bit slippery when wet. I also didn't love the flip up securing mechanism on the bottom; if it's not latched securely I can see the wash leaking everywhere. Once you've squirted the wash onto your poof, it looks/feels more like conditioner or lotion rather than body wash. A little goes a long way, especially if you use a bath poof. There was a lot of lather and a mild, soapy scent. I'll admit, I like fruity scents, not soapy ones, so that was a bit of a negative for me. It wasn't strong, and thankfully didn't linger long after toweling off. After multiple uses, I don't really see any difference in how moisturized or toned my skin appears to be. I'm still having to use lotion after drying off, but I can't see any acne or dry spots from using the body wash. It certainly does a good job as a cleaner, and it is cost effective. Overall, if you're a fan of the scent or Dove products in general, you'll probably enjoy this body wash. For me, the rather large cumbersome bottle shape and scent are enough of a detractor I probably won't replace when it's gone.

The question was asked about using this brush on a dog.  I bought it to try on my Havanese. The Havanese have very long thick, fine hair (not fur-hair-they are hypoallergenic) and it tangles very easily. Most of the big tangles start out as tiny 'BB' size balls of tangles which turn into larger extremely tangled hair. I have, this week, been testing this brush on Tara. I used my regular comb to find those tiny balls of tangles (did not remove them) and then used this brush to brush over those tiny tangles. Then I went back and used the comb (to see if the brush got those out), most of those tiny balls of tangles were gone, some of which easily came out into the brush which happens with these types of tangles sometimes. It wouldn't budge the big, hard-core tangles but no brush or comb that I have found will get those out easily. That takes either a lot of patience to get out or a pair of scissors. I really do like the way this brush makes Tara look. She looks refined, soft and very well groomed, not slicked down as so many brushes do to her hair. I've waited a while to post this review, because I wanted to use it in lots of different places and ways so I could give an accurate review.  I feel I have appropriate experience now, so write I will.This review is written based primary on using the scope for birdwatching, but also a little skywatching at night. I'll go in order:1) Value.  The value is the real kicker here.  It's great.  You really can't get anything else like this for that good of a price.  Can't beat it.2) Image quality.  Keep in mind this is no Leica or Swarovski, but up to about 40x, the image quality is quite good. After that, the quality drops of a bit.  The colors are not fantastic, but in this line of equipment, you get what you pay for.3) Build quality.  Quite good.  The scope is well made and has already survived a couple bumps and an unfortunate drop.  It's holding up well and it seems like it will for a while.  It comes with a case, but I haven't found that much use for it.  Over all, it seems well made.In the end, I give this scope high marks because of it's great value.  If you don't have a whole lot of money to drop on a scope but need to get one to see far off things, cheap, than this might be your answer.  Like I said above, just don't expect it to perform like your friend's Leica.  Set reasonable expectations, and you'll probably be happy.

I tried this smoothing serum two ways, with mixed results. In each instance, I followed the directions and applied it to damp, towel-dried hair.1) In the first instance, after applying the serum, I let my hair air dry. I hated the way my hair looked and felt. The serum never dried and my hair retained a heavy residue. Plus, instead of having soft, silky waves, my hair looked like it had a lot of product on it.2)In the second instance, after applying the serum, I immediately blow-dried my hair. Unlike the first instance, my hair did not have any residue. It was light, silky, and smooth, as promised. It also retained the silky softness for up to 48 hours, as promised. Definitely, this is the way the serum should be used. It is effective only if the hair is styled with a blow dryer.

In the first place, this cream has 30 ingredients!  Many are fairly innocuous, like water, color, fragrance (if you're not allergic), vitamins, green tea and aloe vera, but others are of that multisyllabic variety that sound more like they should be used in your car, not on your skin.  But I'm not a chemist or biochemist, so let's just say they're all OK, but, really, must there be so very many?But that's the semi-purist in me talking.  The pragmatist would say if it works, ignore all those big words and just slather the stuff on.  But I don't like the way this feels on my skin.  No matter how small the amount, and I've tried a dab, a daub, a dib and a dot, it still feels like I have coated my face with something not quite pleasant and my skin is yelling at me, "Help! I can't breathe".So I tried, but I will not continue to use this on my face.  Hence I have no idea if it satisfactorily perfects the tone of my skin.  I would rather have skin that breathes than skin with perfect tone.  For me, this is a 1-star product but since I did not test it adequately I'm giving it 3 stars.  I even tried to test it on my hands, where even more tone perfecting would be in order, but it left the palms feeling icky, so I gave up.BTW, I'm a long-time user of Oil of Olay products.  My favorite moisturizer isOlay Regenerist Daily Regenerating Serum - Fragrance Free Moisturizer 1.7 Fl Oz.  That one leaves my skin looking and feeling really good.

I needed to update the head unit in my Ford Excursion.  At the time I didn't want an expensive unit with a screen but I *did* want handsfree bluetooth, streaming, XM and enough outputs to work with my sub, component speakers and amps.One of the features I like about this head unit is the App Remote app you can install on your smartphone that can control the head unit (and vice versa).  This makes it almost (read: almost) like having a more expensive head unit with a screen.  If you use an iPhone you will want to use a USB cable and you'll be limited to apps that Sony has approved to work with their own app.  If you have an android phone this can be done over bluetooth and almost every app will work.  Note that I am using an iPhone 5.FM Radio reception is great.  I haven't used any CD's yet so I can't comment on that.  The headunit is easy enough to use with the buttons.  The menu system can be confusing at first but just keep your manual close while you are learning the deck.What don't I like about this deck?- The single line display... sucks.  You'll find yourself hitting the DISP button to cycle information WAY too often.- Time is on that single line display, so if you're looking at categories, RDS or other information, the time is gone.  Also, when you do want to view the time it will just show the preset and the time (like "SX1  10:07AM" or FM2 10:07AM)- The knob is obnoxiously bright, regardless of your color choice which take me to the next con:- The color and brightness fluxuations on this unit from button to button make this head unit look kind of ghetto.  Not at all a clean look I was going for.- If you have the deck on a SiriusXM station that has adult material (like ch95 Comedy Central) the unit will NOT go back to that station once the deck is turned off.  I asked Sony about this and they couldn't make it do otherwise (including turning parental control off).  This royally sucks since it will default to XM1 if you leave it on that station.After a couple months with this unit, I think I'm going to move on to something else.  The negatives to this unit outweigh the pro's far too much and it looks gaudy in my vehicle.Edit 12/4/2013:  Aaand the USB port has stopped working.  AppRemote doesn't work anymore and it will no longer charge my iPhone. Lowering to 2 stars since it only lasted 2 months

For nearly a thousand years, ninjas have been using caltrops as part of their arsenal of weaponry because of their design. They hit something perfect when they designed a shape that has a point up , and two points down (as an anchor) no matter how you throw it. The caltrop is designed so that it is physically impossible for it not to land without a single point always sticking up.Ninjas would commonly use them in one of two ways - either to slow down persuers by tossing/dropping them all at once effectively scattering them, which would be quite an obstacle; Or as a possible ambush weapon. (think of a ninja hiding in the shadows, and just as the two guards walk by him he either has them already laiden on the ground for them to step on, or he throws all of them in their faces as a slight diversion to give him time to make his attack.)Okay so Enough history lessons.This set of Tashibishi Caltrops is about the closest to the real thing used by most ninjas throuout the years. They stack very well, are small, and fit between the thumb and middle or index finger of your hand as you can use your palm to conceal them. They're technically meant for puncturing the foot, and on some occasions it may actually work even if your persuers are wearing shoes, but they're actually more effective at puncturing tires. Not that I'm suggesting you do so.For the ninja in you, do yourself the favor of picking up these inexpensive yet important tools to help round out your arsenal. The metal is quite sharp, and sturdy, but always think of your caltrops as "disposable". They usually won't break but they occasionally do. Some may get bent, but you can bend them back with pliers and a pair of vice grips.Anyone further interested in other ninja weapons feel free to read any reviews on my profile page, theres plenty.I hope I can help some buyers out there to better understand the products they may be purchasing.

I've had some time to check out the Vanguard TBH-250 Ball Head and compare it to my currentVANGUARD BBH-200 Tripod Headand a previous Markins Q10 Ball Head.  (The Markins Q10 isn't sold here; it appears similar to thePhoto Clam PRO-42NS Photo Clam Pro 42NS Head.)  In the end I'd say that I prefer the TBH-250 to my current BBH-200 head, and though it is not as refined as the Markins Q10 it is still a good value at less than half the cost of the Markins product.The Vanguard TBH-250 Ball Head is functionally similar to the Markins Q10.  The quick shoe is attached to a large ball that rotates in a magnesium housing.  A large lever locks the ball in place, and a smaller lever can be released to allow the entire ball head to rotate for panorama shots.  A large "friction dial" is used to control the amount of resistance to movement of the ball.  The TBH-250 uses standard "Arca" style mounting plates, with a small model QS-60 included with the ball head. Two useful bubble levels are included on the quick shoe.  One issue with the quick shoe is that it doesn't open far enough to easily slide in/out other quick release plates I own - plates that did fit easily into the quick shoe of the Vanguard BBH-200.  Aside from this QC glitch, I didn't find any other signs of sloppy or poor workmanship with the TBH-250.One great feature on the Markins Q10 is the ability to set a minimum ball friction, so that there is always some resistance to the camera just flopping over when the main lock is released. In fact, with the Markins, if adjusted correctly you can use the ball head without having to tighten down the locking knob - the resistance allows you to move the camera if needed but keep the camera in place otherwise.  The TBH-250 has a similar friction knob, but there's no way to set a minimum friction; if you bump or move the knob by mistake with the lock not engaged the camera may move or flop over.  Summary - Vanguard's tension knob is quite useful, but the Markins implementation is a bit better.In testing with my current gear (Canon 7D and several different zoom lenses), the Vanguard has no issues holding the combo in place at any angle, and I observed no obvious slipping or sagging.  Two things I did notice using the TBH-250: the ball movement is OK, but not "buttery smooth" as with my old Markins Q10; really cranking down the locking knob of the TBH-250 does cause the camera to move just a bit.In all, I found I like this TBH-200 over the BBH-200 - it a bit smaller and lighter, seems just as secure, and has the friction adjustment knob the BBH-200 lacks.  And while the TBH-250 is a step below my old Markins Q10 in quality and features, it is also much less expensive and seems like a good value-priced ball head.

Good:Well manufactured unit, nice finish.  It's very strong and easily supports a 26 inch LCD TV.  If you've never done this before read what other reviewers have written about proper mounting on a stud wall.Seems to have everything needed to mount most TVs (see below).Easy to slip the TV onto the bracket (but see below).Very adjustable articulation, including up / down tilt.  I wanted to mount the TV so that the wall mount is off to one side in order to use a particular wall stud; this mount accomplished what I wanted.Not so Good:Not an attractive installation for a Panasonic TC-L26X1.  I was able to mount it using an adapter plate included with the unit (the TC-L26X1 has a VESA 100 x 200 mounting pattern).  There is only one way to mount the adapter plate and it makes about 2 inches of the top of that plate quite visible above the TV.  (Look at the picture of the PA740 on this page and imagine those top ears with the holes in them always visible above the TV.)  The problem is caused by the fact that in their wisdom Panasonic placed the TV's wall mount near the top of the back of the set, not in the middle like every other TV I've looked at.  So the TV itself causes the problem, but Peerless doesn't include an appropriate adapter for this TV--and I think they should.  I called Peerless and they offered me a different adapter plate for almost $30.  No thanks.  I'm thinking of trying to use this wall mount in another location in the house and finding a different mount for the Panasonic.The only other thing I don't like about the PA740 is that because of the way the mounting plate on the TV slips onto the arm's bracket the TV wiggles a bit (in the Z-axis I guess you'd say).  There is a TINY set screw on top of the bracket to prevent the TV from coming off the bracket, and this works, but the arrangement makes me a bit nervous about the TV getting bumped off the bracket and falling.  In every other way the unit is very sturdy.Bottom line:If your TV's mounting pattern is in the center of the TV you should be good to go with this unit.

First and foremost, if you are looking for purely Personal Information Management (PIM) functions and not generally inclined towards Linux, then do yourself a favor and buy yourself a Palm Pilot.The Zaurus is basically a mini Linux workstation with a lot of functionality you would normally find only on a desktop. With the Zaurus, you can:* Browse the web with a scalable and full featured browser* Read and edit MS-Office documents (Word, Excel and Powepoint)* Write and execute scripts and programs (including Java)* Play music and video* Control your desktop Windows PC (using VNC software)It is so powerful that you can actually use it as a Web or file server (using Apache and Samba). I use my Zaurus to administrate my Linux server and Oracle database server when I am away from my desk.Plus there are tons of applications, utilities, games, etc., that you can download. Zaurus has a very active user community forum (check the links from the Sharp Zaurus official web sites) where you can get a lot of useful tips from.Having said all that, the PIM functions that came with the Zaurus were pretty poor compared with the Palm Pilot. This lead me to hang on to my Palm Vx for dear life. I even took the drastic step of downloading and installing a Palm Pilot emulator (called QPose) onto my Zaurus. The performance hit on the Zaurus was so bad that I had to delete QPose and instead carry both my Palm Vx and Zaurus with me.However, now that Sharp has released a new ROM (v3.10, as of May 15, 2003, see below) the PIM functions have vastly improved. So much that I am now inclined to keep my Palm at home and take only the Zaurus along.Upon getting your Zaurus, do yourself a favor and install the terminal and file manager applications that come in the CD. The terminal application enables you to do a lot of tweaking and is mandatory if you plan to use the Zaurus to its full capabilty. It actually comes with a basic Linux shell environment and comes with such neat utilities as vi editor, ftp and telnet clients, ping, and a large number of Linux/Unix tools.Another application I found very useful is called "rotation". It enables you to rotate the application screens by 90 degrees, making such things as web browsing, file editing and using the terminal appication a lot more pleasing and functional. You can download the "qpe-rotation" and "qt-embedded-rotation" files (ipks) from the ZaurusSoft website.Also, an important suggestion to existing SL-5500 users:Sharp has just released the latest ROM (v3.10, May 15, 2003) which makes the SL-5500 look and behave like the newer SL-5600. I *strongly urge* SL-5500 users with the old ROM (v2.38) to download and install this new ROM. The user interface with the new ROM is a lot cleaner and neater and has more stable applications (including a vastly improved Opera browser and much better PIM applications).

Each of my upgrades from the D40x to D90 to D7000 resulted in major improvements in speed/handling and low light performance.A little less so from the D7000 to D7100, but still worth pursuing if the following reasons matter to you.  Otherwise, the D7000 remains a fantastic camera and a great bargain.* The low light focusing is significantly improved.  Where the D7000 would just cycle back and forth, the D7100 usually locks on reliably. 51 vs 39 focus points doesn't get me too excited.* Haven't run the tests yet, but no back-focusing so far.* The resolution of the 24mp sensor without the OLPF is simply fantastic at low ISOs.* Some better channel separation on the stereo mic would have been nice, but performance is adequate overall.  At least I don't have to lug around that separate mic anymore.  The cabling on the outside of the camera is a PITA.* I would reckon the low light performance is better by around a half stop in absolute terms.  But the grain is finer and smoother, so where I would stop at 3200 if possible on the D7000, I am going to 6400 on the D7100 without feeling too dirty.  Family pics, not pro stuff.  Things deteriorate pretty rapidly after that.GRIPES* My first copy had a bad left mic channel.  There seem to be some other QA issues reported here, so I would STRONGLY recommend checking out all the camera functions ASAP after receiving your unit.  2nd copy has been fine so far *knocks wood*.* Still haven't figured out how to do Auto ISO on the movie setting.  Adjusting manually for now.  Guess I should read the manual.Otherwise, I feel right at home with D7100, having used the D7000 since shortly after its release.I was considering the D600 heavily as well, but decided to stick with DX because I LIKE the 1.5x crop factor -- it gives a little extra reach and you're using only the sweet spot of FX lenses.  I also wanted the stereo mic and did not like the whole D600 oil spot issue.  Will see how the D7100 sensor holds up.I was going to wait for the D400, but wanted to have the best possible pics for a major family vacation.  Pretty happy with the purchase so far.The 24-70/2.8 stays on my camera 95% of the time.  Man I love that lens.I use the 70-200/2.8 sometimes for sports.  CRAZY sharp lens, but a beast to handle.On a side note, the way Nikon handled the whole comfort women uproar was disappointing and infuriating.  They totally caved to the imperialist pigs.  Really made me want to jump to Canon, but they did eventually hold the exhibition after a court order.  I also really dislike the Canon ergonomics.[...]

I wasn't expecting much from a bio-degradable, environmental friendly gun cleaner but I was pleasantly surprised by M-Pro 7.  I took my 9mm and 380 auto apart and soaked them in M-Pro 7 and let them sit for about 5 minutes (per the directions).  Afterwards I used a bore snake on the barrels and could not believe how much oil and gunk came out.  After I sprayed and soaked them a second time I used the bore snake again and I was done.  I swabbed the barrel with a solvent and pad after the second session and they came out clean!  I am used to using *stronger* solvents and cleaners and they do nowhere near as good a job on my guns.After you have cleaned everything make sure you let them dry and then re-oil as this product helps to remove all oils.  I highly recommend M-Pro 7 if you shoot a lot as it is a time saver for sure!

We have had this now for about six months and the TV is really quite useless as a TV simply because the tuner is so weak. We have tried a variety of antennae with it, both regular and amplified, and there is no significant change. The tuner can only pull in about 20% of the channels available in the area, if that. Upstairs, downstairs, changing directions, changing antennas - all futile. If you have cable, then you will be okay, but this is simply not for over the air TV reception. The screen sharpness is also not that good despite all adjustments. One other pain is that the TV will not keep the screen settings when you turn it off. So say you want to have a widescreen setting, when you turn the TV back on it reverts to normal (4:3) aspect. Add in the fact that there is no way to change the tilt angle and the switching between stations is slow and the TV is pretty much pointless. Basically it has become for us a glorified DVD player. As a TV, the cheapo 7-inch Axion for half the price has much better reception, picture, channel switching and portability.

You don't need to use a flat-iron or hair dryer to get some benefit from Chi's serum, but that is mainly what is is designed for.I bought the six-ounce bottle from my stylist's salon years ago. Only in the past two years began to use it regularly because I started to use a flat-iron on my grown out hair. I would recommend everyone who uses heat styling products to protect their hair with serum or balm and there are many good ones out there. I like Chi in particular for a few reasons: a little goes a long way. It is well-absorbed by hair leaving a silky feel and it does protect well. Heat can be a great styling tool but wow you need to protect your locks.I first put a small pea size amount through just the ends of my shoulder-length hair while it's wet  (after I put in styling foam) then blow dry it.After that, I add another dab on the ends before I use my flat iron. Doing that will keep my hair silky and smooth for days, depending of course on our humid weather (and getting a good scissor-cut).Another reason to use this is it will help keep your locks smooth when the weather wants to give you another hair style.Like everything the price of Chi has gone up since I bought the six ounce bottle, which lasted me more than five years thus far. It does the same thing that Moroccan oil does at half the price. Don't be fooled into thinking only that serum has the right stuff. Other good products I've been happy with for their heat protection are Frederick Fekkai's Glossing Cream and Kiehl's Silk Groom. Both are protectors, so for hold I use a light mouse or styling gel. Afterthought: I had to laugh when I saw the price of the Moroccan oil -- I have had basically the same thing in Chi sitting in my bathroom cabinet before it was the "thing."

First off, I don't use many fitness devices -- most seem like junk. But I've used the "perfect pushup" for some time and find that to be an excellent device (2 independent rotating handles for push ups).  It has a few benefits: ability to rotate trough the motion for a more natural arm position; ability to get lower (due to hands being up off of ground level); and, for me, a more natural wrist position -- pushups on the floor hurt my wrists.So with that background, I tried this out as a potential enhancement to the perfect pushup, and it (mostly) is  The ability to slide in and out easily during a set to narrow and wide positions and/or to do that within a rep as a sort of a fly motion, I found to be great for getting an excellent workout.  That's on top of the other benefits of the perfect pushup.  So I would recommend it.The couple flaws are:- handles are much less comfortable than the perfect pushup.  the shape and the hardness of the rubber aren't as good.- it does feel a little flimsy, I'm not sure how long its going to last if I keep doing flies and banging the restraints- the restraints themselves (that you can position to limit range of motion) are difficult to remove and insertHowever, overall, relative to the price, I think its a really good device.

First off as stated in other posts buy the larger size. I have a 10.5 foot and the extra large fits about right. Thought they would make the difference in insulation in my new boots 800 grams vs 1000 grams but my foot got a little bit cold even though the weather was not especially frigid. It was cold around 22 degrees but not extreme and my feet did get a little bit cold. Now all things being relative, cold feet often happen because the rest of you is getting cold so having to post while hunting much more than years past might have something to do with it. But they are wool and pretty thick so they will keep you warmer than many other socks that claim to keep you warm but are not wool.  I personally thing that you cannot get anything much better than wool so I am glad I got them although I wish they could be washed and dried like my wool military socks which have been dried with my other clothes many many times with no shrinking.  I have not screwed up yet so I am not sure how they would fair but they warn against doing it and at such a high cost I am not about to see the outcome. I have worn them several times and they seem to hold up to washing and gentle cycle drying just fine. I have yet to notice them sliding down into your boot like occurs with other wool socks I have used. They also hold their shape better than the wool socks I have had other than the GI ones which are no where as thick.  I would not hesitate to recommend these socks so far as I have used them. Given more experience with them if they hold up I very well might change the rating to a 5 star.

had one of these hanging on a tree for a few years. excellent battery life and never any problems...no matter what the weather conditions. would still have it if not for thieves stealing it from tree.  immediately bought this one again, but this time with a bear safe.  hoping that the bear safe will prevent theft if I hang it high enough and coil a little barbwire around tree below the camera to keep the cowardly thieves from hug-climbing the tree again and sealing this new camera. picture quality is great, especially when I look at the photos from the sd card on my laptop. bear safe case fits this camera vey well. looks like Bushnell hit another one out of the park with the cool and strong design of the bear safe. bear safe even comes with a lock. will make a wood wedge to place behind bear safe upon mounting to get the right downward shot.

Well what can I say that has not been said already? I bought this scope for my AR build because I did not feel like spending on a scope what I would end up spending on an entire upper. I looked around and bought it used but I doubt the original owner mounted the scope because it looked spotless. I took it to the range and it was shooting way too high to even get it on paper so I took it off and didnt bother sighting it in. Later I returned determined to sight it and once I got the shots on paper It took me about 10 shots at 50 yards to zero it (Given I had never zeroed a scope before.) Once I had it zeroed it stayed on the spot. I took it to the 100yd range and I was very surprised to see that it shot fairly tight groups, at this distance I was shooting groups within 2 to 5 inches of each other from a bench.I am very happy with the results, I only wish that the scope had some magnification since the sight cross is a little big for long distances.I will try to upload a picture of my grouping with a 223 round for comparison.

PROS: It simply works! I'm an African American male model with blemish prone skin from inflammatory acne which causes hyperpigmentation. Since I've been using this product, my existing hypermigmentation has continually faded away. My acne breakouts I've been getting since a young teen have greatly reduced and the hyperpigmentation I would get from an inflammed pimple is cut in half, if not more!Along with a few other products I use which includes: a tea tree soap and toner, and an spf 50 sunscreen, and a Morrocan Red Clay Mask(once a week); this product has played a huge part in my now and continuous even skin tone. Whenever I feel a future pimple coming, I use one drop morning and at night on the needed area and in about 3 days everything is back to normal with very little to no hyperpigmentaton.CONS: It's stings for about five minutes! Some might say it burns(on existing acne or inflammed skin) but the results are worth it.*Be sure to follow with a good sunscreen if using in the day time. The acid in it causes the affected area to be sensitive to sunlight.

What I realized after reviewing the ingredients again was that all of the Wen Cleansing Conditioners (including the special season conditioners) except the Sweet Almond contain Methosulfate. In addition so do the Wen Re-moist products. I have terrible allergies to sulfates and I believe that is the cause of the reaction.  The fragrance was great and it left my hair really soft, so I wish I could have continued to use it.  If you don't have sensitivity to any of the ingredients it would be an excellent product.
