I loved this pack for getitng through a trip to the Costa Rican jungle. It worked great and had a ton of storage.I would have given this bag a 5+ out of 5 other than one peculularity.  When I got the bag, opened the box, and tried to unzip the main compartment, the zipper pull pulled off in my hand, the first time I tried it.  I tried to get it back together with no success.  I shrugged this off and tried another pull on another pocket.  Same thing happened again.  At this point I said "to heck with this" and I cut every last zipper pull off the bag and replaced them with some spare para-cord I had.  I figured I'd rather completely address this problem at home before I left, rather than in the jungle.I totally understand this was a minor issue, and has no major overall bearing on the bag, but I was disheartened hat I had to address a poor-quality issue whatsoever. I cannot speak to the longevity of the bag, as I have only owned it for a short time.  I can say it took a beating on the trip we took, and is no worse off.  It feels very substantially built, in regards to the sewing, stitching, and the material itself.It is extremely comfortable to wear loaded down.  I really llike the adjustability of the bag.  I just hope the pulls are not indicative of other issues with quality.  I doubt this will be the case, but I just can't get myself to give 5 stars when there is just an shadow of doubt that I believe will ultimately disappear in the years to come.

This telescoping tire level works very well. Slip it under the bead, extend it, and clip the other end to the skewer. This baby slips around releasing your tire in a couple of seconds. If you can't thumb the tire back on again you can slide it back on with this.They're really tough - the only time I need to buy a new one is when I foolishly leave it by the side of the trail after fixing a flat (twice now!)The only downside I see is that it's a little long so it's a tight squeeze into some smaller saddle bags.Other than that it's the best on the market.Update: I've been using these levers exclusively for over 5 years and wondered why people think they are fragile. Well I finally broke one but it was only because I was stupid. I think they may get more fragile over time (the one I broke was over two years old) but given their reasonable price and how well they remove tight tires I think that's a fair trade-off.

Logitech's latest keyboard is an interesting one. It's a bit smaller than most, which means that in order to keep all the keys keyboads normally sport, they've had to move several of them to new locations.As a result the keyboard takes a little getting used to. If you're someone like me, who does a lot of stuff using the "delete" key and the "page up" and "page down" keys, it's going to throw you off a bit at first. Additionally, the arrow keys are rather small and awkward to use, which isn't a huge issue most of the time, but I do use them in gaming.Otherwise, it's an excellent keyboard. The keys spring back nciely and make a satisfying sound when you use them, the hotkeys to access your mail, calculator, home page, etc, are rather clever and a nice addition. I also like the fact that, unlike with my previous keyboard, the Windows key is nowhere near the "/" key. This is relevant, because I play "World of Warcraft" and the chat key is "/". It's very frustrating trying to send a message in the game and instead accidentally minimize it.Overall this is quite an excellent keyboard which just requires some mental adjustment as to key locations. I've not had it for long, but I've already replaced my old keyboard permanently, and I can't think of higher praise than that for a keyboard!

I've used many things to clean my camping cook wear, but the SCRUBR dishcloth is my new favorite. I have the Netgear PTV2000, which has worked well, so I thought I'd try the upgrade.  Well, to begin with, it is smaller.  Much smaller, which is nice.  It also connects to my laptop a little more quickly.  I also noticed slightly less lag with this one than the older one.  All good signs.  I then put my laptop away and tried to watch TV.  I noticed that I could no longer control the volume.  I have a Sony Bravia SmartTV connected to a Sony 7.1 surround sound receiver which is then connected to a Sony BluRay player (and a coupe of DVRs, etc.).  All of this works seamlessly through Bravia Sync.  Unfortunately, when I lost control I tried the Bravia Sync setup, and it found no compatible devices.  I went through the usual troubleshooting, unplugging the TV, receiver, and BluRay.  Disconnecting the HDMI cables.  Re-connecting all the devices.  Re-running the Bravia Sync setup.  Still nothing.  I went into the receiver and manually set up HDMI control, still nothing.  At that point, I remembered the PTV3000, and I disconnected it.  Bravia Sync started working immediately.  To control for coincidence, I re-connected the PTV3000, and lost control of the TV again.  I have put the PTV2000 back in there, since it caused no problems, and put this back in its box.  It may work well, but the trade-off isn't worth it.Bottom line, if you don't rely on HDMI control of your peripherals, then this is the WiDi solution for you.  If you do rely on HDMI control, stay away!

I bought these on a whim when Apple.com had their Black Friday sale.I had been thinking about them for a while, since I have nice over-ear headphones, but they're not very portable and I'd been looking for a nice set of in-ear headphones.These really sound impeccable!Before these I had a pair of Etymotic Research ER*6i.They were good, but these C5's are better in several ways:Fit better in ears, and easier to put in, pull out.  With the ER*6i's, when I'd pull them out, the rubber ends would disconnect from the phones and stay stuck in my ears.  Also, kind of gross, but those things did not do good with ear wax.  It'd get into the driver and eventually a clear/white headphone turned yellow.  Yeah, gross.With the C5's you can pull them out easily because of the way the wire is designed, and there's more space between the rubber end and the driver, so ear wax won't be as much of an issue.The carrying case for the C5's is well designed and convenient for these types of phones.And most importantly, the sound is super clean and enjoyable.  I have them plugged into my Macbook Air right now, listening to some Yann Tiersen, and it sounds extremely precise and full.Really glad I bought these.

I recently bought a new internal 1.5TB drive but neglected to realize that it was SATA rather than IDE/ATA. At first I thought I was never going to use the drive until I found this adapter.The installation couldn't be easier: It simply attaches as a daughtercard to the SATA port on the drive. The daughtercard features a male IDE connector and a power cable port along with a power cable. The card also has a Master/Slave jumper which makes it easy to replace an existing IDE slave drive with an SATA drive configured as a slave on the same IDE cable.The card doesn't require any drivers. My SATA drive was recognized automatically by the BIOS and XP-Pro. I was able to easily initialize and partition the drive using XP-Pro's disk managment tools.For anyone who has an IDE/ATA-based system and wants to check out the higher-capacity SATA drives this little card is a tiny investment which works wonders.

I work in a place with lots of Drobos. Here's some info (and opinion) to address common problems/questions...IMPORTANT: Download and install the latest version of Drobo Dashboard software from Drobo's website before connecting the Drobo to your computer.IMPORTANT: Use Drobo Dashboard to update the firmware in your new Drobo before inserting and erasing your drives. Firmware updates are potentially hazardous, so do them when you don't have data at risk. Then put the Drobo into Standby mode and unplug it before inserting drives in your Drobo.**I didn't take my own advice. When I powered up my new Drobo for the first time, I jumped the gun and had already popped new 3TB drives in my new Drobo. It reported that the drives were defective and blinked ominous red lights. I simply installed the latest firmware update via Drobo Dashboard and that took care of the problem. All green lights on reboot. No problem erasing the drives after the firmware update. A few lingering alert windows that had to be dismissed. It's working normally now....Many people don't know how to calculate the capacity of their Drobos. There is a calculator available on Drobo's website (do a Google search for "drobo capacity") that makes it very easy to see how much capacity will be lost to the parity information used when you set up the RAID, if one of the drives is replaced or if you add more drives.When you first erase your drives with Drobo Dashboard you will be prompted to pick a capacity for your RAID. This is not the *actual* capacity of the RAID. It is a theoretical maximum capacity of the RAID assuming that you maxed out the storage. For most purposes, you'll probably want to pick 16TB as you can then expand the capacity of the RAID volume with new drives up to 16TB total without erasing it. Beware that this will cause the Drobo to report that there is 16TB of capacity to your operating system even though this number is false. (One reason to run the Drobo Dashboard software is that it will alert you to problems such as your RAID filling up.) If you pick a capacity that's less than the effective capacity of your drives then Drobo Dashboard will create a second volume for the extra capacity.Drobos are slow. My new one is still running a modest copy operation that I started several hours ago. By my calculation, it will take more than 12 hours to copy 1TB of data to it via FW800. This seems to be about on-par or slightly faster than the speeds that most people get. Note that Drobos get even slower as they fill up.Drobos are good as redundant backup devices, but I would not count on one as a primary storage device or sole backup. They are very very slow, they tend to make annoying rattling-noises and they have a pretty high drive-failure rate, but they're relatively cheap and it's not too great an expense to replace a drive in a Drobo vs the expense of professional data-recovery, so they have a place in a home or small business.Just don't rely solely on the Drobo. Don't make it the sole repository of your data....And don't use "green" drives in a Drobo enclosure. I know that Drobo certifies green drives and it seems like a good idea to use those drives to save power, but they're just going to make your Drobo even slower and because they are not designed to work together in a RAID or to compensate for the vibrations in a multi-drive enclosure you're begging for trouble if you use green drives.

The John Frieda JF1 hair dryer is a lightweight hair dryer with a sleek design and ceramic heat technology.  Unfortunately, the concentrator has a tendency to get hair stuck in the space in between the concentrator and the barrel of the dryer, then pull hair out.  While it does dry pretty quickly, there isn't a noticeable difference in how shiny or voluminous it makes hair look.  It's also pretty loud.The buttons could use re-working as well, and would be better as switches, rather than buttons.  The temperature and speed buttons are prone to being switched on accidentally.  On the plus side, the hinged filter makes it easy to clean and the cord is a generous length (~8 feet).Overall, the John Frieda JF1 isn't an overly impressive hair dryer and I wouldn't recommend it for the price.

I've been doing networks for decades.  Since I started programming about 1980, it's not a surprise that I've been part of technology as soon as it's out, and wireless was no different.  Been at the bleeding edge since its inception, and i've got a basement full of old access points to prove it.So I've tried all the flavors, all the brands.  Up until this morning, I was most recently using a Belkin, since that was the only one that seemed to work very well with my (at that time) new 13" macbook pro.  But the last few days I was having issues with it, drops, couldn't touch my server on my network yet the internet still worked, flaky things like that.  Since I wanted to get a new computer anyway, I thought at the same time i'd finally take the expensive plunge for Apple's overpriced entry in this field.So this morning I got my new 15" macbook pro to replace my 2009 13" macbook pro, and grabbed an airport as well.Install was simple, it even gives you the chance to duplicate an existing wireless network.  You can bridge it or you can extend other networks, the options are all there.  And it's very simple since there's no web page or anything, just direct access via the software on the computer from the install CD.And all day, it has been rock solid with both macbooks, my iPhone, my iPad, my 7 year old's Dell Inspiron 15, even my HP photosmart printer, all are using 802.11n and all are working fine.  No drops.What impressed me the most was the speed.  I was never very happy with the Belkin, my file transfer speeds to my windows home server in my basement would average around 6MBps to some peaks at 7MBps.  Large file copies took a long time, and if i had a lot of them (say 50 or 60GB) I'd plug it into a gigabit cable in another room and let it do the copy that way.With this new airport, I jumped nearly 100%.  From an average of 6 to an average of 11MBps.  I peak over 13.  Not bad at all since the Belkin, at an average of 6, was the best I could get at the time (I tried three total, other two were even worse than that).  All were always set for 802.11n only, tried all channels, even removed encryption and just used mac filtering to see if it made a difference on them, nothing worked.This one, it's just fast, out the gate.  Wonderful for my file transfers.Now I wish I would've just bit the bullet and spent the extra on it.  I'm a windows guy since they started, and run windows even on my macbooks, but there's something about the hardware that Apple does that just seems to have no peer...  That's why I love their laptops so much, they're just designed so well, so functional.  And this airport is no different.  Worth the extra because it performs better than any other I've ever used.

I recently upgraded my computer from a Core 2 Duo with an ASUS P5W DH Deluxe motherboard to this EVGA motherboard with theIntel Core i7 870 2.93GHz Quad-Core LGA1156 Desktop ProcessorandOCZ Technology Gold Series PC3-10666 8 GB (2 x 4 GB) Low Voltage Dual Channel Kits OCZ3G1333LV8GK.  I am so pleased with everything, especially with this motherboard.It took me quite awhile to decide whether I was going to purchase this motherboard or not.  I have always had good experiences with my ASUS motherboards in the past and I was a little hesitant to go with EVGA since I hadn't heard much about them before.  After doing some research and visiting the EVGA forums, I decided to go for it, and I'm so glad that I did.The layout of the board is great.  Everything fits without being too cramped.  The 6 internal and 2 external SATA ports come in real handy with all of my drives, and the RAID features work great and were easy to set up.  I also love that you can connect up to 12 USB devices from the board.The onboard RealTek HD sound is great (I'm only using a quad speaker + subwoofer setup) and it is adaptive so when you plug something into one of the ports, the drivers ask you "what did you just plug in there?".  I also love the digital LED on this board that can be used for diagnostic purposes, and it shows the CPU temperature once your system has booted up.  There is also a little red glowing EVGA logo on the board that is a nice touch if you are using a case with a clear panel on the side.  There are also plenty of fan connectors for the CPU and case fans.If you are looking for an inexpensive yet effective CPU and board cooler that is compatible with this motherboard and the LGA 1156 chips, may I suggest theCooler Master GeminII S Copper Base Aluminum Fins 5 Heatpipes CPU Cooler?  It is reasonably priced and works well.I'm very happy I decided to give EVGA a chance!

This whistle is probably one of THE loudest whistles on the market, which is great, but the thing that really makes this whistle great is the fact that it's pealess. A pealess whistle means that it doesn't have the little ball on the inside that wiggles around to help the whistle make sound. The pea may seem great and all, but the "pea" inside the whistle will stick to the sides if water gets in it which will render the whistle useless. Therefore, the pealess whistle is the superior choice. The Fox 40 CMG Whistle also has a little rubber mouth guard around the opening. This mouth guard makes it much easier on your teeth and on the whistle if you plan on using it for prolonged periods of time (like at a sporting event or when training a dog). The metal ring that secures the whistle to a lanyard is a double loop, which is much stronger than just a single loop, so you don't have to worry about losing your whistle. If you're lost, don't even bother shouting for help. Blow on the whistle instead, you'll save energy.Pros:LightweightSmallVery loud (can be controlled)Has a mouth guard to protect your teeth and the whistleStrong lanyard ringGreat for calling your dog or calling for helpWorks when wetCons:None

I keep a large number of networkable devices in my bedroom, but my router is on the other side of my house.  Most of my devices have only wired interfaces, however.  I was looking for a way to connect all these devices through a standard gigabit switch, especially so that I could perform high-speed backups to my NAS, when necessary, but access it conveniently via wireless, when speed isn't a major concern.  I bought this device not really expecting much, based on previous experiences with these sorts of devices.  However, I am very pleased to report that this little box works like a charm.  It's been running for weeks without a hiccup.  It stays connected, always, and I've been able to do some pretty substantial data transfers with pretty good throughput.  Setup was also quite painless.I'm a power user, and I'm always trying to tweak my setup to make things a little easier or a little more convenient.  This is one of the rare instances where a new piece of gear just works.  If you need to make some Ethernet gear wireless, you're looking at the device you want.

First off, the reason this Beatbox Portable from Beats  says it's new while other from Monster says (old version) is, if some don't know Beats is now mainly owned by HTC and not Monster. So they basically all they did was remove the Monster from the box, the product itself is exactly the same and the quality is exactly the same, [Don't get Beatbox original and Beatbox Portable mixed]Ok, now that I got that out of the way, I'll give you guys the pros and cons...Pros:Bluetooth, this was a sell point for me, I wanted something that supports my iphone and also my ipod classic and also my macbook air (I have a bluetooth adaptor that plugs into the ipod ), the bluetooth was easy to use and connect with no issues.Design, as usual for a Beats product, their design really stand out from other brands and it looks really niceQuality, the speaker have a good weight, more towards the heavy side if you wanna be picky, but it has a nice built quality, although it's plastic around, it doesn't feel cheap and seems well built if it was to fall or accidents.Performance, this is a tricky part because everyone listen to different music, but if you're into rap, hip-hop, pop, r&b;, techno, dubstep, reggie and rock, this is good for you, I fit into the hip-hop, rap and techno category and it has a really strong base to support the song and it does get loud for its size.Cons:Price, this is a good quality speaker, but as usual with Beats product, it's not worth the price, there are other brands out there like Sony that can do similar quality.Batteries, for the price you pay for these, it's kinda of a shame they didn't include rechargeable batteries much like the Bose and other brands, this is a portable speaker and the idea of buying 6 large batteries which last only about 10 hours is not modern, sure you can buy rechargeable batteries, but these batteries are not small and you would need to buy 12 of them if you always want to have one charged.

In my house, the router is in the portion of the home most heavily used for Internet - the living room/dining room/family room end of the house.  But in between the router and the bedrooms are some large hunks of metal - furnace, refrig, and oven - that severely reduce wifi signals.So, the wifi reception in the bedrooms is spotty.  I have tried various different solutions (and reviewed some on Amazon), with varying degrees of success.  Part of the problem is that we have laptops with built-in antennas - and wireless G cards.  Some of the USB wifi adapters do well - but their smaller size means a tiny antenna.This Netgear AC1200 extender is essentially all the hardware for a typical router (in fact, it looks identical to my router, down to the two swiveling antennas), but re-purposed to act as a range extender.I have tried it with the fast WPS setup (i.e. the default settings), and then with the Fastlane technology (i.e. receiving from the router on 5ghz and broadcasting on 2.4ghz).  I also tried it in various positions - near the router, near the laptops, and half-way between.Overall, I found that - just like with OTA antenna reception of HD TV signals - there did not seem to be any clear cut principles that work consistently, and that one needs to just apply trial and error.Having said that, I did find the Netgear EX6200 (used with a Netgear USB N-300 adapter) provided better throughput than any other solution, after trying all the various combinations of settings described above.  The throughput was significantly better than a much less expensive (and smaller) extender that I was using previously.So, I would recommend the Netgear EX6200 for those in difficult wifi reception situations, as something to try after finding poor results with other, less expensive, extenders.UPDATE:  Just FYI, I am having good results with the EX6200 closer to the source (router), about 40% of the way to the remote laptop, using Fastlane with 5.8ghz only used to feed the extender, and no other purpose in my system, and then 2.4ghz to the remote laptop, using a Netgear N300 USB Wifi-N mini adapter.  This has resulted in the best throughput to that remote location of any solution.

We all have different kinds of hair, and different kinds of styling we do with our hair. So I'll begin by saying my hair is fairly thick, and it goes from short to waist length over 2-3 years. I then cut it, sell it for charity, and start growing it again. So my needs for a conditioner vary depending if it's frozen snowy out, if it's broiling hot out, and the length of my hair.In general I've had pretty poor luck with the other Suave offerings. They typically are sticky and gel-like, which then attracts dirt to my hair and makes it hard to brush. Especially when my hair is in its long stage this can be quite a chore.I've been generally pleased with the Professionals line. This almond shea butter conditioner is fairly thick, almost like a lotion. It stays on the hair well to let it soak in a bit. I would almost say it's a bit too thick; if it was a mite bit thinner it would work through the hair layers more easily to get to all the underneath hair.It rinses out fairly well and doesn't leave that hard-hat sticky residue of some of the other lines Suave offers.That all being said, it's still not an ideal detangler. So there are still some issues with brushing the hair. Again, most people might not mind. Since I'm trying to get every hair I can to stay on my head for the 3 years so I can then give it to charity, it does matter to me. I'm trying to be as careful as I can with my hair.So this is fine for the short to shoulder length stages of my hair. During that time the hair is short enough that it doesn't really tangle. The conditioner is inexpensive and keeps it clean.However, once it gets longer, I wouldn't use it. I'd go to other conditioners that are more robust in their conditioning and detangling abilities.

I just want ONE thing that does the job,.That said,This vest is awesome. I'm adding a machete on the back of this vest and attaching it to the Large molle loops. They're not standard size molle, they're very big, which actually ended up working out for the better. The large size Tek-Lok seems to fit in these loops just like a big size belt. I bought 2 Tek-loks and I'm attaching them to my custom made kydex sheath for my machete.I just learned on youtube how to make a custom kydex sheath. So simple. This is now my "go to" vest for everything. Whether it's shooting range or desert shoot or going to costa rica soon. Can't take the firearm, but the knives are fine. Or maybe the world goes crazy (like it hasn't already) lol.The holster for the firearm is at an angle and can be changed also. My previous vest was sewn in completely vertical. how stupid is that? The other thing that really mattered to me is how did it fit on my body. I'm 6'0" 215 lbs and I hate when things are too tight. The other vest was one size fits all too but it just couldn't adjust it to be comfortable. This vest is very comfortable.PROSEverything. It's really greatCONS1. You can't make it sit lower by adjusting the shoulders a bit.2. The other thing is that because of the weight in the front, you should be able to snap this to your belt.----------------UPDATE 3/18/14The way that this is designed to attach to the big green belt, it's not easy to adjust the belt to size. Even if you did, there's really nothing holding it to your body, and it's so big that it doesn't fit your belt loops. The solution I found is that no weight should be put on the green belt.I took the belt off of this vest. I still wear the belt with my knife attached sideways which is awesome btw, but it sits on my hips and is not attached to the vest. The loops that were used for the green belt, I attach to my regular belt on my pants. This is MUCH better now. The vest was sitting weird and way too heavy in the front because of all the stuff that goes on there obviously. Now if I don't want to wear the belt, I just unsnap it and continue on with my vest. My entire family carries these in their backpacks. In the event that a day hike turns into a shelter-from-the-storm event, these lightweight ESTs are good enough to keep the rain off in an emergency. Even in a treeless environment, using a backpack at one end and an upright stick or gollock at the other is enough to provide sufficient lift across the center-line to ensure you'll stay sufficiently dry to avoid hypothermia. Use a little paracord to maintain the center-seam above your body and some more paracord and a few small stones to close off one end and you've a very usable emergency shelter that can make the difference between discomfort and possible exposure. Much better than an emergency blanket (but good to have that as well, to climb inside once you're in this shelter) and not much heavier.

I really wasn't expecting anything great. Especially for the price I paid (~40 at Fry's).Sound:With the Dolby Surround sound software on, wow, everything, and I mean everything sounds so much different. I normally use a pair of Turtle Beach PX3's which sound decent. But with these headphones, commentary sounds so much more fuller and crisper. When gaming, it almost sounds scary. You can hear every little detail. The surround sound aspect makes such a difference. Even when listening to music, anything from rock to hip hop to drum and bass, everything I've thrown at it sounds phenomenal. But... when the surround sound is on, and poorly produced audio is on, it really sounds bad. It almost makes the good sounds sound great, and the bad sounds sound terrible.Microphone:I've been told that it sounds crisper and clearer than my Turtle Beach headset. I don't like how the microphone doesn't twist or anything, just goes up and down.Comfort:I have a pretty big head. I had previously purchased the other cheaper SONY version of this product (see my review of the Sony MDR-Q22LP Ear Headphones with Interchangeable Headphone Caps). These other SONY headphones are not this wind-up style and thus are also not retractable, but must be wound up by hand every time and then untangled as well when using them again. I have just received these retractable SONY phones for my birthday and I can readily say that they are much more comfortable (even with glasses worn over the top of them) than the SONY non-retractable style phones. Without the rather long cord of the other SONY phones to wind up every time, they are also much more practical too. (Depending on the radio however the total length of the phone wire may be the main fm antenna so for some radios the longer wires on the non-retractable SONY phones may definitely be a good thing regarding the available reception which of course is what this adventure is ultimately all about!). One nice feature is that if you are in a rush you don't have to wind-up the wires at all, but can leave them just where they are, completely adjusted for your own body and ears and for the radio itself. For my body and frame the length of wire needed is sufficiently short enough so that the storage of the headphones is quite simple in a small zippered case. I have tried these new phones on several of my small as well as on larger radios (Kaito KA1102, Degen DE1125, SONY SRF-M37w, Tecsun PL-310, etc.) and they all work with these phones just fine. I am really glad that I just got them too since the sound quality is great as well!

A yoga mat is really the only thing that you tend to use on every yoga workout. It cushions your feet and gives you stability during some of the stretching moves. This mat is a full 1/4" thick and provides great cushion for your yoga routines.First, a note about all yoga mats. Because of what they are made of, they tend to have a chemical type of smell when you first get them. This is normal! You simply air them out for a few days, perhaps wipe them down with soap and water, and this will dissipate. It's like breaking in a new pair of boots - you have to go through that initial phase.I found the mat to be really wonderful. It is cushiony enough that it supports me in a variety of poses, whether I'm standing, kneeling, laying down, stretching. It is far better than doing yoga on a bare floor - whether that might be wood, stone, carpet or whatever. The cushion is really important for many poses.Even though it has that nice cushion, it is also stable too. You feel quite firmly planted when you are doing balancing positions. It's not like you are trying to balance on top of a trampoline.The mat is a good middle ground between "resistant to sliding" and "too sticky". You can readjust your feet when you need to - but if you want to plant your hands, it helps keep your hands in place.I also really like that the mats comes in a wide variety of colors. People are more likely to exercise if they really like their exercise equipment. If you love green, go for green. I got the light lavender and really like it. To me it is calming and relaxing. Whatever color energizes or relaxes you, you probably can find it here.The mat might be a little bulky to carry around with you, but you can get a lighter mat for use when you travel. This one is just perfect as an at-home mat, to really give you a solid base for whatever level of yoga you are currently at.Highly recommended!

I have owned several Maxtor external drives including OneTouch versions I and II, and they are all still alive and well after years of use. These drives are the first totally unreliable products I have ever owned from Maxtor (now Seagate).I bought my first one in January 2008, and it failed in March. I got that one replaced under warranty and bought a second unit to back up the data on the first. (What are the odds that you would have a second failure on a Maxtor or Seagate product?)I got the new drive first, and gradually transferred data to it. Then I installed the second drive and backed up the data I had created on the first. Then they both failed (with different error codes) today. My data AND backup are unreachable, so far. Now they are sending me two replacement units (which will make a total of 5 drives for 2). Based on MTBF calculations, these should run forever, right? If someone wants to give you one of these, take it but don't put any data on it you want to have available for very long.

I am planning to try Two faced Lip Injection. I am currently using Stung(Lip Pulse). I get really good results with this lip plumper. But, not just anyone can handle the burning and painful sensations. It doesnt bother me. I kinda like it. Ive tried Lip Infusion XL. It worked OK. There are two methods that lip plumping companies use. The first method is they put really hot ingredients in their product to bring blood flow to your lips to cause them to plump. The second method is companies use collagen spheres that penetrate your lips and this causes water retention in your lips. This is how Lip Fusion XL works. Stung uses the hot, stimulating ingredients to plump. I noticed that Two Faced extreme has peptides in it. Peptides increases collagen. Stung claims to build permanent collagen, too. I got the manbug for my birthday, and ive had it on me every day since, normally for small tasks. i wanted it to try out the steel. ZDP-189 is a super steel. steel is considered high carbon when over .60 percent, so 1080, 1095 having .80/.95 percent are high carbon. well, ZDP has three percent carbon making it very strong. chromium raises tensile strength, and most stainlesses have 14 percent, zdp has 20 percent. it also has 5 percent tungsten, which means all together this steel is much harder and stronger than your average steel. i was intrigued to try it out. i began with my standard cardboard test, andr it took about 200 feet of cereal and pizza boxes to get it to stop shaving. of only a 2 inch blade. wow. i then re sharpened it and took it out to my garage and whittled away on a 2x6 for a while which was very enjoyable. i pried a sniper hole through the board and the tip did not bend or break. i wanted some smaller pieces to work with so i batoned it through and split the small board (tapped the top in and pushed down since there was no blade sticking out the other side) and  turned it to shavings. i batoned the knife through a liscense plate also. it never folded, bent, broke, and only minorly dulled, but stil lshaved. the handle is comfrtable, the knife is light, deploys easily one handed, and with a small lanyard for the pinky to wrap around, can have a secure grip. no rust so far

The Panasonic FH2 Lumix camera is one of the best I've seen/used when it comes to packing a punch in a small package.  This is a sleek and easy to transport camera--smaller than most phones (even flip phones).  It's easy to use and takes great pictures as well.  There is a lot to like and little to complain about:Likes--size (a huge plus, this really is a small camera), 14.1 Megapixels, 1280x720p HD video capable, on/off switch is difficult to accidentally trigger, battery life is excellent, 70MB built-in memory, Face Detection Auto Focus/Exposure feature (reduces blurring due to focus on other objects), and Intelligent ISO Motion Detection Sensor (a nice feature that reduces blurring due to motion).Complaints--very few, but to get nitpicky, the 4X optical zoom is too weak, the LCD is often difficult to see in bright light (although it is a good size for this small camera), and access to video (as opposed to camera mode) takes a couple of steps.  As I said, these are very minor complaints.This is a great camera and is perfect for those who like to take their camera everywhere.  Definitely recommended.

*****Bedhead Manipulator by Tigi is a unisex hair cream that can be used for styling, texturizing, and forming. This is a small size which is the perfect size for a handbag or purse or travel bag, and a small size of this product lasts a long time.Manipulator is not really a gel, more like a very sticky cream (almost like glue or paste) that is the consistency of honey. It dries very naturally though (not at all like glue or paste), and leaving a sort of fiber in your hair. Although all of this sounds weird, it seems to work much better than hairspray on my hair, which leaves a film OVER the hair; Manipulator is WITHIN the hair, so you can do more with it. It feels soft and nice when dry too---never greasy or waxy---and I can leave it in the next day without having the dirty feel to my hair that leaving the hairspray in does.The way I use it is I put a very small amount on my fingers, and then rub my hands together. Next I apply it throughout my hair, but especially to the roots for lift. Lastly I twist and sort of "tweak" the ends of my hair to add texture. I have a short haircut, and it is the perfect product for people who have very little time or skill but who want a tousled and textured look.Highly recommended.*****

I have a mini fridge that makes an electronic click every 30 minutes or so when it switches power modes. This "click" is both audible and causes my led/lcd monitor to turn black out for a second or two before it powers on again. I found the situation to be particularly worrying as both my computer, monitor and mini fridge share the same surge protector. I was worried that eventually this issue may cause power problems to my other devices shared by the same surge protector.So I decided to buy this Belkin SurgeCube surge protector in an attempt to isolate the power draw of the mini fridge from the other devices. After a day of using the device, I am happy to say that it does indeed prevent the click from occurring and now my monitor does not black out like it once did. As a result of this little experiment, I am recommending this device for users who have high draw appliances or electronics to prevent and stabilize power draws. It does the job well and is cost effective! Highly recommended!

I just received this in today, and it is a great addition to my tools. I bought this for use with air rifles for a couple of things: primarily to test different pellets at different distances to find the best one for each rifle, and second to check the parallax markings on my scope to see how they line up to true distances.For these two purposes, it has been great, eliminating myself out of the equation significantly. I will say that this is not as good as a true gun vise that straps the rifle down, ensuring it doesn't move, but if you understand that limitation, and use the fieldpod appropriately, it does quite well. I don't have a shooting table where I can use something that needs a platform, so I needed the Fieldpod with the tripod legs that can vary the height and still be stable.The Fieldpod can articulate in all directions quite well, and you can adjust the resistance to movement so that it stays roughly in the same spot, or allows you to be able to adjust but still give stability. I needed an SDHC card for my Sony SLT-A33 DSLR camera.  Since the camera could use inexpensive and common SDHC cards in addition to Memory Stick Pro, I went with the former.  The Sony was similar in price to others of the same 32GB capacity, but the Sony is a Class 10 (others were a Class 4).  With this camera set in "RAW+JPEG" mode (where it saves a RAW format image in addition to high quality JPEG) in highest resolution, I can store 1,472 photos on this card.  So far, the card has had solid reliability and has not corrupted any of the data for the images I've taken.  I will likely buy a few more of these as funds permit, and keep them in my camera kit if I need the capacity in the future.  Highly recommended, especially if you can get them at a reduced price!

I have 2 items that need constant care to keep their batteries charged, a gas powered, electric start generator and a Kawasaki 900 motorcycle. In normal years, the bike wouldn't need a maintenance charger like this, but health issues have prevented me from putting needed miles on the bike to keep the battery charged.  The last time I tried to go for a ride, the battery was dead. :-(So, I decided to get one of these chargers and so far, I really like this unit. It is very small and light weight, and is very easy to set up and use. It comes with 2 charging leads, 1 is attached permanently to the battery, allowing you to just plug in the charger in a very convenient and quick manner. The other has alligator clips, not designed for permanent installation.I put the charger on the bike's battery, using the alligator clips, just to make sure that the unit worked properly.  I let it do a slow charge and overnight, the battery was back to 100%.  I was quite pleased, to say the least. Before putting the bike back together, I installed the permanent leads on the battery, letting the plug lay in an easy place to get to, when it needs another charge.  I'll use the alligator clips on the generator until I can get another set of the permanent leads.Bottom line, I'm very satisfied!.  I like this unit much more than my old charger, which is large and unwieldy. It will probably go into the trash, after I get more leads to put the NOCO charger on our car batteries, should they ever need it.

A little while ago I was contacted by the importers of the Uspeed seven port USB 3 hub[Oct 2012 Upgraded Version - VIA VL812 Chipset and 12V 3A Power Adapter] Uspeed USB 3.0 7 Port Hub with 3ft USB 3.0 Cable and Power Adapter, Backward Compatible with USB 2.0. They offered me a hub to review, and in a sea of largely unreliable products, the product did everything I could hope for. It's still here, sitting on my desk, and an invaluable part of my computer setup. Now the company, that apparently goes by the name of Anker Uspeed, has sent me another product which is fairly similar but has a few extras.The USB hub uses the same chip as the hub I was sent before. The performance is just as good as my previous hub, with no problems about devices not being recognized. What makes this hub different is that it offers an eighth port which does not transfer data signals. Instead, the socket provides only a charging current. It's true that other USB hubs and sockets allow you to charge devices, but the advantage of this port is that it supplies a much greater charging current than the standard USB 3 socket. This means that my devices like the Kindle Fire HD charge much faster.Once again the importers have chosen a reliable hub, and if you compare the reviews here to similar products, you'll find that the importers supply a good quality hub along with reliable tech support. There are numerous touches that make this product worthy of a recommendation. The power supply is an in-line box that needs only a single space on the distribution board. All the cables apart from the individual device connectors are supplied. The socket to recharge your devices has a clearly marked red plastic tab while the rest of the sockets have the typical USB blue inserts.USB has become the main standard for connecting computer peripherals. I read today that USB Developers are already working on an even higher speed than the 5Gb/s of USB 3.0, that will transfer at double the speed - 10 Gb/s - which for most people will be overkill, at least for now. Naturally the new standard is compatible with all existing USB versions. It should take some time to appear, around the second half of 2013. But today this hub will transfer at very high speeds, and I don't think you'd notice if they were higher. Given that computers today come with two or maybe four USB sockets, there's a need for this hub for a lot of people.What's even more exciting is that faster speeds for USB is encouraging a whole bunch of new types of peripherals. I think you'll find that you're going to need it.(This hub was supplied to me by the importers. I've used the same standards for review as things I've bought or had supplied to me through the Amazon Vine program.)

Shallow mount subwoofers are becoming very popular because the technology has gotten to the point that they are actually good, not just thin. Shallow mount means that the box it fits into can be quite thin from front to back. In the case of the Pioneer TSSW251 the internal volume can be very small as well. This means the sub can be mounted in more locations, often out of the way and hidden.Note the rear view of the Pioneer sub. It is very unusual and clearly a lot of engineering effort has gone into making these things work. It is not just a flattened out conventional design.I was able to hide it away in a cubby hole and got rid of the big box that used to take up a lot of cargo area, could attract thieves and would probably hit me in the back of the head in a collision.Pioneer has recommended box volumes. Mine is home-made and is below the optimum size, but it still works very nicely, with a lot more bass than the previous traditional long-throw (deep dish) 8" sub in a far bigger box. It is designed for a sealed box, not a ported design.Any decent amp will power it. No need for 1000 watts. I used a 350 watt mono Alpine.I was very pleased with the price. It actually seems inexpensive for what you get.

I've owned a few sets of the Peltor 6S for the last 18 months or so. I bought them in early 2013 during a great sale, and scored three sets for $110 (total) shipped. 3M Peltor is arguably the most recognizeable name in Ear Pro, so I was confident that these things would fit the bill, instead of opting for Leight's or far more expensive alternatives.Sadly, they're barely worth the $37 I paid for them. Yes, they work satisfactorily at isolating gunfire noise and certainly keep your ears safe. That's about where the performance ends. I have other sets, including cheaper ones, that perform every bit as well at isolating the unwanted sounds. The difference comes in the sounds you actually WANT to hear. All three sets of the Peltor 6S' that I have perform identically, so I'm satisfied that my sample is reasonably representative of the whole. With the Peltor's, ambient sounds, including voices, etc, are dull and much of the surrounding noise isn't discernible at all -- even when turned ALL the way up. With virtually all of my other sets, including the Howard Leight sets, I can hear MUCH better than with the naked ear. All ambient noises are amplified while completely tuning out loud / harmful noises. This is great when you're out in the woods, or on a range where you need to hear the Marshall's instructions. In the woods, I can hear squirrels munching on nuts or crawling around in the trees, and even field mice under ground cover. None of these sounds are heard when using the Peltors.The battery design was conceived by Rube Goldberg himself. I mean, seriously 3M -- why?! The plastic construction feels very cheap, and the posts that connect the headgear to the muffs seem to scream "break me!" I don't mind plastic / "polymer" at all, and many things made from plastic materials are very robust and nearly indestructible, however these Peltors don't inspire confidence in their potential durability. The muffs / cups themselves also feel very cheaply made, but they do make a seal sufficient enough to block harmful noise.So, in a nutshell, these muffs "work", but they're barely satisfactory. I have a $20 set of cheap Caldwell's that run circles around these Peltors, and the Howard Leights are in another universe by comparison, for a small premium over the 3Ms.My personal recommendation is, in this price range, go with something else, or step up to more expensive Ear Pro. I have a set of 3M ComTac III's and they're insanely good -- perhaps the best-ever -- but at $600 you'd expect them to be.

Taking this computer out of the box, you immediately realize you've entered the next generation of computing. First, the setup is a breeze, and there is only one cord to hook up (yes, that's right, one!), the power cord that plugs into the wall. You pop the batteries into the mouse and the keyboard, plug in the little USB fob to wirelessly connect the two to the CPU, and you're up and running. The HD screen is beautiful. It has 1900 x 1080 resolution, giving a really bright, crisp, and clean picture. And then there's the touch screen. This Dell has a easy-to-use touch screen, much like most of us are getting used to with phones, MP3 players, and almost everywhere else. And it only makes sense to have it in a computer. It basically makes the mouse a relic, though I certainly see continued uses for the mouse depending on your application. But the touch is really easy to get used to. Windows 7 is ready for touch screens, and even offers a touch keyboard, so you can use this computer completely without mouse or keyboard if you wanted. And for a lot of things, especially those that don't require a lot of typing, it easily could be used that way. Plus there are some added touch features integrated into Windows 7, such as scrolling, zooming with two fingers, holding to "right click," shaking a window to reduce all of the others, etc. Most of the can be done with a mouse too, but they really show how the touch can be integrated into the user experience. I look forward to seeing where apps go as touch becomes more prominent.This Inspirion io2320 has 4GB RAM, a 1 TB hard drive, and an i3 Intel processor, making it fast, ready to take on even graphic-heavy applications. And it's got bluetooth and a wireless modem, so there truly is only one cord plugged into the machine most of the time. So it's a nice computer. But there's more. It also has HDMI and RCA inputs, along with a host of USB ports, a multi-port that can handle various camera memory cards, so it can switch inputs and double as a nice HD TV. The built in speakers are actually pretty good, especially for a computer, though maybe not up to the level of some TVs. The whole setup is attractive, as the design is smooth and clean: the keyboard can slide right under the machine, keeping it out of the way when it's not necessary. And it can be wall-mounted, and it would work really well to do so, since the touch screen means you could walk right up to it and do most things without needing a desk for your keyboard. One of the shortcomings I've found is that I'm not crazy about their system for standing up the computer. It stands on two short legs under the front of the machine and then has a third plastic leg that extends out from the back of the machine, so that it sits tripod style. But if the optimum viewing angle is close to vertical, as it is where I currently have the computer setting, it wouldn't take much force to tip it forward and send it crashing down (and if you need a viewing angle that is vertical or slightly tipped forward, you won't be able to do it unless you wall-mount it). I'd rather have seen a broader base allowing freer adjustment of the viewing angle. But if you've got a good safe place to set it, that isn't a fatal flaw, though it certainly leaves room for improvement.In all, this is a nice machine, with a beautiful picture. And the touch is totally the way to go: there won't be any going back for me. I have to stop myself from touching the screen on my PC at work now that I'm used to this. Dell seems to have put together a good array of features.

The item pretty much looks like the picture.  Got the blue one.  The inner fabric is like a "sueded" fake leather and the exterior is more of a smooth fake leather.  The colors are a little dull, not very bright, but I'm okay with that.  The iPod Touch 4G fits very nicely: easy to insert iPod and easy to remove.  Necessary ports are available for charger cable and headphones, however, I do remove it from the case before putting it into a dock.  The iPod's internal speaker area is also open.The cover keeps the iPod comfortable in your hands when it gets a bit warm from lots of use and is comfortable for using the keyboard when holding the iPod sideways.  For some apps, the cover blocks the edges of the screen slightly so you either have to account for that when using the iPod or remove it from the cover. I've had this product for a few weeks now and I have had a number of occasions to use it since I travel by air frequently. APC should be commended for a lightweight, elegant battery solution that looks great and works just as well.Here's how the APC UPB10 stacked up:PROS:- Very nice look and feel, looks very executive- Solid overall construction and nice metal buttons- Fashionable, 2.5 inch graduated charging LED- Charges from a USB source itself, including a laptop, so no extra charger to be carried- Has both mini USB and full size USB connector- Surprisingly lightweight given the charge capacity (10W I believe)- Can fast charge an iPhone as good as a wall connectionCONS:- Charges only when main metal "on" button is pushed, not just by jacking in the plug, a very minor complaint- Only charges 20 minutes at a time, then needs another goose of the button, though this makes sure you don't waste power but requires occasional attention- Very slow recharging, can take hours, though this probably will make the battery live a lot longerBottom line, I would buy this product again in a heartbeat.

It took me awhile to figure out how to enable the Digital Audio output ( couldn't find clear instructions on the web ) but all you need to do is plug in the cable and switch your screen resolution to duplicate displays, desktop 2, or projector. Then right click on the volume control in your tray, and choose playback devices. The HDMI device should show up in the tray (mine said Sharp HDMI), click on it and make it the default device. Now, anytime the cable is plugged in the laptop should automatically switch to the HDMI output and sound. If you duplicate the the monitors it should still feed the sound to the HDMI since it is now the default.When the cable is unplugged the sound reverts to the laptop speakers. Works perfectly on my Lenovo T410 Thinkpad with Windows 7 and massively cheaper than the Lenovo approved adapters. A heavy duty, well made cable.

Navigation:If you want navigation in your car, buy a separate unit. The navigation app crashed on me on my first test trip, and tried to get me to turn left where no left turn was permitted. The maps that came installed on the unit were from September of 2009. Jensen does not do map updates, it's handled by a company called Horizon Navigation. Oh, they only update the Navigation software. Maps are from another company, Tele Atlas. They want $89-$99 for a set of maps that are from March of 2011. When working with both company's support, I got a "The maps are fine, in a couple months we'll have a new head unit out that uses a better map provider".The Bluetooth:The hands free profile works fairly well. The mic is decent, it downloaded contacts and call logs from my android phone fairly easily. The problems start with A2DP. The only way to get it to play media from your BT device is by using the pause/play/next/previous buttons on the head unit. On android, this picks a random audio app, though most of the time it is the android music app, ignoring any audio apps you have open. I worked around this on other head units by hitting play in the app I wanted to use. If I do that while paired with this radio, the sound comes our of the phone's speaker, not through the car. So after 5 minutes of messing with it, I get pandora to come out the stereo. I press the navigation button and music stops, and starts coming out my phone. XM, aux in, FM radio, BT hands free all work while in nav, just not A2DP.The RemoteMine doesn't work. Tried new batteries, but nothing.XM unitHad to buy a cable to go from the Audiovox XM Direct2 to this unit, not a big deal, but annoying. The radio interface is OK. You can see the song, artist and station and your list of presets. You get a ton of presets, but only 5 display at a time and it's kind of a pain to get to the other ones.PowerMy stock radio sounded better.Other inputsThis thing has a crap ton of inputs. That's it's biggest strength besides the cheap price.I'm fed up with this unit. Amazon, expect an RMA request.

This sunscreen contains zinc oxide 20% providing an SPF of 40. There are essentially 2 kinds of sunscreens: physical sunscreens and chemical sunscreens. The physical sunscreens (those that contain zinc oxide or titanium dioxide) are much better for your skin/health than chemical sunscreens. Physical sunscreens sit on top of the skin and create a physical barrier to the UV rays. They work immediately.Chemical sunscreens have to be taken up by the cells to provide their UV protections. This delays the action of the sunscreen (so you have to apply it about an hour before you go out in the sun) and, more importantly, there is now discussion of the safety of these chemicals on the rest of the body (because they are absorbed into the bloodstream). So, while they may protect you from skin cancer, they expose you to other potential health problems.The biggest problem I have had when using physical sunscreens is the whitish hue they leave on the skin. They can now micronize the zinc so it disappears on the skin. This Cotz sunscreen does that and it goes on evenly and disappears leaving no whitish hue. Although it's labeled as `oil-free' and `non-greasy', I did find that it was a little greasy and I'm removing one star because of that.

PROS: plastic-free, smooth movements extending, retracting, tilting and/or tweaking to "get it just right"; heavy-duty feel to the frame helps one get past the "I dunno....I don't see how this will hold my TV..." feeling; many, many screw sizes provided (along with washers, 4 spacers, etc).  In the end, once installed, far better-looking and even greater feeling of a sturdy and safe system that not only freed a great deal of space, but also just looked nice and tidy/sleek.CONS and TIPS: CON 1: instructions, well, lack thereof really, unnecessarily vague and seemingly incomplete.  I installed this with a friend and I consider us both experienced DIYers, but the lack of being able to identify/match up screws from the instructions to the "baggies" was a huge frustration factor and a waste of time. However, as I was reading other reviews hoping to pick up some tips while we were struggling to affix the adapter plate, I read two great tips from another reviewer; TIP 1: take the time to open up each "baggie" of screws to match them (the letter of each "baggie" to the diagrams on the faux instructions - oddly enough, the "baggies" were clearly marked with a letter of the alphabet, but the faux instructions didn't include those letters, instead they included the size of each screw/item (helpful for con 2); TIP 2: a reviewer suggested taping the top set of screws/washers in place before mounting the TV to the wall plate and it was only after we were hanging the TV to the mount that I remembered reading that tip - we stopped what we were doing and taped the screws/washer in place and tada, fumbling and dropping the screws no more - course, the thing we did wrong was to allow the tape to cover the hex hole (at least we could laugh at ourselves as we realized our goof), but we used cellophane/desk tape so it was fairly easy to just punch through it with the little wrench; CON 2: despite the adapter rails/option and the spacers provided and all the different screw options, the rails did not fit our TV (42" Panasonic Plasma) as the TV is not completely flat in the back (just below the bottom plate hole the back of the TV swoops out about 1 1/2".  Although the spacers looked like they would "even out the back" so as to provie a solid vertical hold, none of the screws were long enough to go through the spacers and into the TV securely. Off to the local hardware store we went where we learned, upon returning, that a metric tooled screw was different enough from the 2 1/2" screws we purchased - maybe this is common knowledge to most, it wasn't to us (we had measured what we needed and converted the inches into metric and came up with 63.5mm - thank goodness for an online conversion chart!), and with the closest being 2 1/2" available at the hardware store, we opted for those, sigh). Alas, they simply would not fit the holes on the TV so off to a local auto parts store where we found/bought the longest size available, 60mm. Alas, this time just a bit too short (we could feel the screw catching the back of the TV, but it felt as though it was just barely catching which, in turn, just screamed "don't chance it!").  I could have ordered the "right part", but the wall section was installed (my friend made a template out of cardboard by tracing the part that would be fixed to the wall....this made it very easy to ensure the wall arm would be perfectly level and helped identify/mark the precise center of each stud) so we opted to improvise - we determined we had the option of allowing the vertical rails to stick up above the TV by about 6-8 inches by moving them to sit just above the "protrusion" below the bottom hole on the back of the TV (he thought it was fine, his wife and I groaned at the thought and sight) or chopping the excess off....or, as we opted, bending each rail at the extra 6-8" excess so as not to affect the fixed rail durability and allow each rail to mount completely flush to the TV, as is intended.WISHFUL THINKING: Had the rails fit the TV without tweaking we would not have needed different screws and we both felt the entire system would have been a breeze to install (had we matched the parts from the "baggies" to the faux instructions,  had we taped the screws/washers ahead of time and had we read the other reviews before starting the entire process :). One could probably install this without another set of hands, perhaps if the TV was on a table/counter at the same height as the arm or the TV was light enough/easy to lift/handle, but three of us lifted and positioned the TV to slide onto the arm, two lifting, one guiding - just made it easier in the end, for us.SUMMARY: Would I buy another/recommend this to others?  Yes....especially now that I know what to expect and how to prepare. This is a very sturdy and versatile unit, a great value for the quality and a bargain for the price. I would have given this 5 stars had there been a way to properly attach the rails to our particular TV (e.g., longer screws, shorter version of the rails, etc) and had the instructions been a bit more comprehensive. Had this fit the TV properly out of the box, 4.5 stars....reserving a 1/2 star "penalty" for the lack of detail in the instructions/diagrams (an easy fix, if only the vendor would read the reviews....oh, a girl can dream).

A lot of people don't like thAt aspect of it. But contrary to evey face wash commercial you have ever seen: oil is GOOD for your skin. We all have natural oils that are being stripped from our skin when we wash it or expose it to the elements. A good way to use this product is before bed time. You are not meant to wash off the oils. So do this at bedtime and let it absorb overnight. I go this and in the morning all I do is rinse with cool water as to not remove the oils. When I sleep with it on, I feel it gives the oils time to absorb. I wake up with smaller pores, plump skin, and even skin tone. Maybe the magnet is a bit of a gimmick, but I can't argue with results. Do the mask evey 1-2 weeks. People compliment my skin on a daily basis (but I suppose a good portion of that is just plain genetics).  I also moisturize with 100% organic virgin coconut oil at least every other day.

I purchased a selection of HotSnapz directly from them at least 4 years ago. Some are small like these and others are quite a bit bigger. We have used them over and over and over without any trouble.The smaller ones last (depending on where you put them, in a pocket they will last longer than exposed to the air) about 20 minutes or so.The larger ones last for quite a lot longer, 40-50 minutes at least.This is a nice set with a combination of both.One of the keys to longer lasting warmth is to keep kneading it - first when it is activating and also after it's been in use for a little while.When you are done using it, it is a solid inside. To reuse it, you need to cook it in boiling water until it is liquid again. Let it cool and when you want to use it again, just click the disk inside and it activates.I've tried the Little Hotties hand warmers and could never get them to work consistently. I've also tried another brand of reusable warmers like these and they didn't get hot enough.We've used ours over a hundred times with no issues. The seams have held up, they cook back to liquid and store until you want to use them and have yet to fail us.We use them at the bottom of a cold bed, for a sore spot that needs heat, for cold hands or feet, or just to warm up the air beneath a blanket on a cold day in the winter. For whatever reason, this little pack of warmth provides a sense of comfort as well, especially to my kids.I highly recommend this company and their products.

This tablet tote came at the perfect time as I am flying for the holidays and needed something to hold my Macbook Pro 15" laptop.  At first glance this Case Logic brand bag seems to be a run of the mill generic laptop carrier.  Upon further inspection I see that it is well designed.  The center compartment would hold up to a 15" laptop.  On one wall of this center compartment is a soft felt lined pocket just the right size to slip your Ipad (or similar tablet).  The other side of this center compartment houses a longer unlined pocket that would be good to hold file folders, etc.  The outside of the bag features on large zippered pocket on one side with 3 separate compartments in that to hold chargers, cellphones, eyeglasses.  This pocket also has the standard elastics to hold pens.  The opposite outside pocket features a smaller (4x6) zippered pocket as well as a larger unzippered pocket.  The bag is made of nylon with polyester linings.It seems to be well constructed however with all the seams finished and no strings hanging anywhere.  The zippers on this one all zip smoothly.  The Max dimensions of a laptop and Ipad are as follows:  Laptop-14.3x1.2x9.8"  Ipad-10.6x0.6x7.5".  This bag has a nice flat reinforced bottom so it will sit on the floor or table without falling over.  The handles are reinforced at the top but they are only about 12" long so if you put it on your shoulder you would wear it more like a woman's handbag.  I prefer a long strap that would allow me to put the bag crisscross over my shoulder.The bag doesn't seem very heavy even loaded up with my stuff. It is streamlined in design and not bulky.  Overall I like this bag and recommend it.  Hopefully after I carry it for a few months I will feel the same way.

This review is lengthy, detailed, and honest, but it reflects my personal experience which will likely differ greatly from your own, so keep that in mind. This review is geared towards budget minded people (I am a university student after all) with 1.6x crop-sensor cameras, e.g. Rebel, XT, 20D, 30D, etc. who probably started out with the kit lens and now have outgrown it.This is an optically excellent lens at a truly bargain price. Particularly if you are going to this straight from the kit lens, you will be amazed and even shocked at the quality of the images this lens can produce compared to the kit lens.Low-light performance is excellent, with the large aperture (still incredibly sharp wide open) allowing wonderful low light flash-free photography which will greatly impress you, your friends, and your family after you photograph people candidly at your next gathering.This brings me to the focal length limitation. For what I just described, candid low-light photography at a party or other gathering, as well as candid shots of children and pets, this lens excels because on a 1.6 crop camera (such as the Digital Rebel series, which I imagine most people who buy this lens have) this equals approximately an 85mm lens in 35mm film camera terms. This is a classic portrait focal length, and so naturally it is perfect for candid portraits (as well as posed ones, of course) because it lets you get pretty close to people without sticking the camera in their face, as well as providing pleasing out of focus backgrounds with very sharp subjects. Canon also makes an 85mm f/1.8 lens which people who own the more expensive full-frame cameras love for the same reasons those of us with the 1.6 cameras love this one, because the focal length and large aperture are perfect for the sort of photography I've just described.It's not that it has no use besides that, though; I've taken many landscape and cityscape photos with this lens that are wonderful. But usually, I feel very closed in by the long focal length. If you're a long distance away from your subject, this lens can often be great for landscape and nature photography, but eventually you'll be longing for a wider field of view and will at least be tempted to go back to the kit lens for many shots, and for some you will absolutely need it.And this is its biggest problem, which is the flip side of this being a great lens for low-light social gatherings - even if you only use this lens for low light situations, you will feel like you need a wider field of view at least half of the time. To get a group shot of four or five people with this lens in a small house, I literally had to shoot from across the next room through the door, and that still wasn't quite wide enough. My only other option was to use the kit lens, which with its f/3.5 maximum at the widest focal length is just not usable for most low-light situations without a tripod or flash, so I had no choice.To reiterate, it is very likely that if you aren't already spending a lot of money on your camera and lens collection, you will really feel the limitation of not having a wide enough field of view for a lot of your photography. Depending on your style and what you mainly shoot, you could easily get very frustrated with this lens despite its strengths.The other thing that is likely if you're upgrading to this from the kit lens is that you will get addicted to the insane increase in picture quality and low-light usability and will not ever want to use the kit lens again, which means you'll need another higher quality lens that gives you a wider field of view. I used this lens more or less exclusively for about three months, became frustrated at not having a wider field of view, and ended up buying the $350 Sigma 30mm f/1.4 for its "normal" ~50mm equivalent on my 1.6 crop camera, and for its large aperture. That lens is great, by the way, and I wholly recommend it instead of this 50mm lens if you've got the money. You do lose the advantages of the 50mm's long reach for portrait situations, but in general you can make up for this by getting closer to the subject, which admittedly is not always possible. Solution? Get both ;)Ultimate advice? If you've only got the kit lens and maybe a cheap telephoto zoom and you really don't want to or simply can't spend money on more expensive lenses in the near future, don't get this lens yet. Save your money up for a more versatile (and still optically excellent) lens in the $300-400 range, namely the "standard digital zooms" offered by Sigma, Tamron, and Tokina in that price range. I am currently saving up for one of these (probably the Sigma 17-70, the least expensive of them, since the advantages of the others are covered by the 30mm and 50mm lenses).If you crave the low-light performance (which was what attracted me), and you really don't feel like you can spend $300 or more for something like the Sigma 30mm f/1.4, then go for it, but don't say I didn't warn you. You'll be craving more expensive lenses in no time.Another disadvantage that you won't notice too much unless/until you have more expensive lenses is the build quality. In terms of durability I'd say this lens is great and it will hold up to normal abuse, but it definitely feels like a plastic toy, the manual focus ring is only marginally better than the one on the kit lens (which is a joke), and if you care about this sort of thing, it looks a little awkward on the camera (I have the Rebel XT). It is the same size as the 50mm lens for my 1978 Canon A1, but since the body styling on these new cameras is so different from the classic camera body style this size and shape looks a little silly. It's purely a cosmetic concern, but don't be surprised if you feel a little self-conscious about using it in public (this won't stop you from using it, of course, because you'll know that the silly look of the lens has no bearing on the outstanding results you get).Finally, the autofocus motor is a little noisy, but if you're used to the kit lens you won't mind it because it's only slightly louder than that (just wait until you're spoiled by the silent and super fast ultrasonic motors on the more expensive lenses, though...) The focus accuracy is excellent, even in low light, but there's no avoiding that it will occasionally have to hunt for focus in very low light and while this is extremely annoying, there's really not much you can do about it even with the more expensive lenses and cameras.Overall - it is hard not to recommend this lens, but only if you get it knowing its limitations ahead of time and you are ok with it, and accept that it will probably make you want more expensive lenses to go along with it.Again - for the things it is best for (portraits and candids, kids, etc., though of course you can do a lot of creative things with it), you can't do better than this lens, especially on a budget (but, this lens compares extremely well with the much more expensive similar lenses!) What it absolutely is not, though, is a "walk-around", general all-purpose lens that you can leave on your camera most of the time like in the film days when most people just had the 50mm f/1.8 lens that came with their camera and never took it off. The Sigma 30mm f/1.4 I mentioned fits that bill perfectly, if that's what you're looking for.

While this product is intended for men I also like to use a clarifying shampoo once every week or two in order to remove styling product buildup, and my husband uses them typically daily to wash the thick pomade he uses out fully. Neither of us like overly sweet or floral scents so "fresh clean" sounded great to me. I did notice the very small writing that said menthol & caffeine but didn't give either much thought.My husband used the shampoo first and when I asked him about it he said it seemed to clean really well without drying but that the scent was much too strong for him, this surprised me since "fresh clean" didn't sound too dangerous. I used the shampoo last night and was actually surprised, he was right in that it has a very strong scent but it's one I love. It reminds me of a slightly sweet Vicks Vapor Rub odor and I happen to really like the smell of Vicks LOL I can see specifically seeking this shampoo out when I have a head cold to help clear my sinus while showering. I always deep condition after using a clarifying shampoo so I can't comment as to how well I felt it conditioned but it did leave my hair feeling very clean and removed residue. The smell also did not linger on my hair. All in all a great and affordable option!

I was hoping this would be a little more light weight because I would have liked to use it for traveling. The handles are definitely a few ounces. I only use carry on luggage so besides looking for small items, I'm looking for light weight items. I'm not sure I would choose this over a resistance band for my pack. The equipment is fine if you have no weight concerns.I'd also like to use it in a hotel room, but for some exercises, you have to put something into a door and many hotel doors immediately connect to an narrow hallway inside the room and in these cases, you may not have a wide enough space to extend your arm(s) outward.That being said, I think I will use this product at home possibly to do some arm work right when I wake up, perhaps while watching the morning news. (I think for my full home workouts, I will still reach for my hand-held weights.)I also think this would be great if you are looking to speed up your metabolism or just wake up your body from sitting at the computer. I work long hours on the computer and this is perfect to keep nearby, get up for a 5-10 minute exercise break, and then back to work. Maybe busy parents might even do a few sets while cooking.I'm not very strong in the upper body, so the single band is enough for me. I think the three bands will provide some significant resistance for men. It was easy to assemble. The product is a little gimmicky with the three bands looping around your feet, but if you have ever had an exercise band snap, you will appreciate the thickness of these tube bands.I would definitely recommend this for short periods of additional strength training.

These have been a lifesaver for me.  I love playing video games but many of them have language that is not appropriate for my children or that doesn't need to be shared with others.  These have alleviated that problem completely as I can play any game without worrying about my children picking up words they don't need to hear.The sound quality is great...not as good as my home theater (especially when watching movies) but they leave me happy.  There is plenty of bass, although some explosions can feel a bit cheap because the sound can get a little distorted or muffled at high volumes.  I have used these on both my PS3 and PC to listen to music or play games and the sound is great.  I love that my ears are covered as that helps eliminate outside sounds...however, I can still hear noises around me which has been nice when needing to listen for sleeping children.The build quality was questionable first, but I have been happy with the way they feel.  They are entirely made out of plastic, and I would have preferred some metal construction, but I am still happy.  They feel comfortable when I am wearing them, even for long periods.  The longest I have worn them is about 3-4 hours.  The battery life seems to last about 5 hours but it only takes about an hour to charge them to full capacity.Overall, I am incredibly happy with my purchase.  I do wish they had the ability to work with other portable devices by connecting through Bluetooth or even an audio cord, but that is only a minor gripe and I knew what I was choosing when I bought these.  I love being able to play video games at night without worrying about disturbing my family.  I would definitely purchase these again.
