I ordered one of these for my PS3 Slim cause theeForCity USB Notebook Laptop Cooler Cooling Pad 3 Fan+Blue LedI was using died very quickly. Two of them infact...... i now have the working halves of both spliced together into one working unit. But I wanted a laptop cooler for the PS3 that wouldn't die on me, that could get air into the little slits on the bottom of the PS3 around the edges. The mesh cover on this one seemed like it would work. Whatever's placed on it would block the air forcing it into places it can escape to, like the little vents around the bottom edges of the PS3. And it is working just like that, the mesh from left to right is wider then the PS3 so I can feel the air that's being forced more to the edges. My PS3 is definitely cooler now and it doesnt's slide off this thing either (as long as you let the back legs (rubber squares) of the PS3 hang off the back to hold it on. This would work great on any laptop too (as long as the laptop HAS vents on the bottom...... I seen so many bad reviews on coolers from people whoose laptop didnt' have any bottom vents yet they actually expected it to work lol).However, the first one I ordered was dead on arrival, minus the 30 seconds it worked. I called Amazon, and within 5 minutes had a prepaid shipping label in my email. Dropped it off at the UPS store the same evening, and they shipped out my replacement the same evening as well. Two days later the 2nd one arrived. Awesome service (the first one was supposed to arrive on Sept. 7th.... the REPLACEMENT arrived on Sept. 7th lmao), all these years this is the first time I ever bought anything on here that didint' work. Of course i try to always buy straight from Amazon and not some random seller. It's been going a week now and no problems, and the PS3 is alot cooler now. The air coming out of the back vent of the PS3 is ... lukewarm now, not burning hot. And most of the case is cold now even after leaving the PS3 on for 24 hours.There's only one thing I dont' like about this. The LEDs are so dimm you can barely tell they're on even if the room's totally dark. On the fan ITSELF it's really bright, but unless you dont' put anything on the pad (which is kinda the point).... you'll never see the fan. All I can see is a REALLY faint light on the left and right sides through the mesh (PS3 isnt' as wide as the mesh cover, nor is it as wide as a laptop, but anyone with a laptop prolly won't see anything at all).An afterthought.... just cause I said the first one i received was a dud, dont' base your decision to get one on that. Keep in mine there's like 500 5-star reviews on this pad, and like 40 one star. IF I hadn't bothered to exchange and try a second one I probably would've put a 1-star as well....UPDATE: As of now, OCtober 3rd, it's still working fine. And I've left the PS3 on for the past 3 or 4 days without turning it off.Update 2: December 17th now and still fine, almost constant use (i still forget to turn the PS3 off alot lol)...Dec 4th, 2013: Still working flawlessly...

I buy everything I review. To receive FIVE stars, a product must meet >90% of the manufacturer's claims. I don’t subtract because I don’ t like the color or appearance (too subjective and personal). I review form, function, ease of use, and construction quality. Please forgive typos: I’m an evidence-based scientist, NOT a typist. If you find my review helpful, please click "YES". This helps me improve future evaluations (which I do for free to help people make more informed decisions).Their Claim: Allows you to route wires through the walls of a houseMy Verdict (details below): True, but only to a pointHere is how it works. You cut a hole in the drywall, behind where you're going to mount your TV (or whatever it is you're mounting) so it's hidden from view. You then put this in the newly formed hole and feed the wires through it. You then cut another hole lower on the wall, where the wires will exit and plug into the receiver (or whatever else), thereby hiding the wires from view. Instead of ugly holes, you have two attractive wall plates that are SUPPOSEDLY held in place with four screws.Here's the problem: cutting the hole to the proper size. As you can see, the four screws are VERY close to the cutout. When I cut the hole, the edges were so close to the screws that there was no place to PUT the screw (you need a plastic wall anchor for use in drywall). So, I ended up taping the plate sides to the wall.This needs to come with a template, which you tape to the wall and carefully trace the exact dimensions of the required hole. You will NEVER NEVER NEVER get the exact sized hole without such a template, and will, as I did, cut the hole so large that there is no drywall around the edge into which to secure the screws. While not that bad, it ain't good either. I did four of these, and of the 16 screws, only three found a home in the drywall. That's a design flaw that cannot be overcome without a template. They KNOW this, but have not done anything about it.Most wall junction boxes or TV mounts have such a template; why DataComm leaves this out is a real puzzle. That's why this gets three stars.

I have been using the Amazon Basics  Mini Travel Stand for a couple of weeks now with 2 different size tablets and a smartphone. Here are my observations about the stand.PROS:Works well with larger phones, 7" and 10" tablets too.Stable when tablet in used in landscape orientation. It is less stable when tablets are in portrait orientation, especially for vigorous gaming such as Temple Run and others with frequent or sudden poking and tapping.  The instability is most pronounced when you tap higher than the midpoint of the tablet.Rubber  tips keep the legs from sliding.Cutouts are wide enough to accommodate typical tablet in a fitted silicone skin (cut out is approximately 17 mm).Folds closed for compact transport. Fits very easily into my zipped case along with a Mifi hotspot.Snaps shut.Inexpensive.CONS:The joint seems weak. Essentially the stand is a long piece of plastic with symmetric legs. The center is scored which enables the stand to fold shut. However, just like when you fold a credit card in half, it weakens the plastic at that fold. After many repetitions of opening, adjusting, closing, and snapping shut, eventually the plastic at the fold will break.BOTTOM LINE:All in all, the low cost of the Amazon Basics stand and its compact size make it a good solution for support when light weight is desired, though there is some instability when a tablet is used in portrait orientation. I'm not sure it will last a long time with frequent use, but then again, if it does break, there are loads of colorful choices in duct tape these days for repairing it. For heavy duty use, of if weight is not a factor, you might do better opting for a folio type case with a built-in stand design.

I bought this amp from Overstock.com because it was a few bucks cheaper than amazon at the time.  I have a 2007 Dodge Ram Quad Cab that I just bought and wanted to upgrade the system.  I installed a pair of Infinity Reference 5.25" (75w RMS) components with a plate adapter in the front doors, and added a 12" RF (200w RMS) under the back seat in a custom box.  I searched around for an amp that would push all of these optimally for a good price, and the KAC-8405 is what I found. When it arrived about 2 hours ago, it came with an instruction manual, allen wrench, speaker lead input harness, and the amp itself.  It really does only have the footprint of a sheet of standard paper and fit nicely under my front driver seat.  I decided to keep the stock head unit, so I ran six strands of 16awg speaker cable from the head unit to the amp.  I ran four leads from every speaker channel (+/-) to the amplifier, only tapping into the rears, and ran 2 more leads going directly to the components.  Aside from the 6 speaker cables, the only other wiring I did was an 8awg from the battery, grounded an 8awg to the seat bolt, and bridged the "B" channel to the subwoofer.  I did not run a remote turn on wire, as the speaker level inputs turn the amp on/off when they send a signal.  Basically, when the amp came in, I simply wired everything right up, tucked the wires, tuned (front then rear), and replaced the cover plate.  All I can say is "wow".  For a little amp, this sucker packs a punch! I didn't even install my 1 farad cap and have virtually no dimming or voltage drop.  After a little tuning the high pass and gains for the front, and the low pass for the rear w/ bass boost, it sounds great.  Produces crisp, loud mids and highs without distortion, and solid - tight lows (even on the 12" sub I had lying around).  The remote turn on from the speaker leads work like a charm, and I leveled all of the settings to zero on the stock head unit.  For the price, I'm really impressed with this guy - and its not my first rodeo with mobile installs.  I highly recommend this one to push your 4 door channels, or to do a 2.1 3 channel setup as I just described.  You won't be disappointed.

Before buying this I was so tired of tangled headphone cables. I have tried many different potential solutions to wind up my headphone cables, but I have never found anything that works as well as this with a wide variety of ear-bud headsets. The key here is that this soft stretchable material allows it to work with oddly shaped headphone cables that contain either a cable length extension socket or volume controls in the middle of the cable. I use one for my headset in my car and another for my headset in my briefcase. I have also bought some as gifts for friends and family with ear-buds as this solves a universal problem.=======================================================================================UPDATE: I am adding a few comments to my own review based on other reviews I have read.PRICING: One reviewer suggests buying for $9.95 from the manufacturer. Well they add $4.95 S&H; = $14.90 total (+ they likely charge tax for IL residents). You can save $2.46 each by buying through Amazon (at the time of this post where one seller is offering $7.45 + $4.99 S&H; = $12.44 total). The only reason to consider paying more to buy manufacturer direct is to get the option colors (pink or green) for the same higher price of $14.90 at the time of this post.DUST: The only down side I have found after a few months of use is dust. The soft rubber does indeed attract dust. The solution is to remove your earbuds and rinse the rubber case under warm water. Let it air dry it will look factory fresh again with no signs of dust.

I got this stove directly from Etekcity but wanted to leave a review here as well.The stove comes in a small zippered pouch (with belt loop) that feels like it should hold up well. It comes in two parts that screw together and then screws onto a butane/propane fuel canister. There's a nice rubber washer to create an air-tight seal with the fuel canister. There are no instructions in/on the box but it's pretty obvious what you need to do (this is my first pocket stove). I picked up a fuel canister locally and tried out the stove on a very windy afternoon. (I'm doing a video review too but the wind was so bad I have to reshoot my video) The stove fired right up, burned well and never went out despite the 35MPH gusts we had. I used a Coleman cup to heat water and an infrared thermometer gun (also from Etekcity) to check the temps. The base of my cup was almost 400F after just a few minutes. I heated 20oz of cold water and could have made tea for two in about 18 minutes, with the stove on a low setting. It has a twist handle to control the burn but no marked settings so you just have to play with it to get the results you want. The stove seems to be made solidly enough for something in this price range. The fold-out extensions on the arms are a thinner metal with a pop rivet style hinge. You'll have to be careful to not snag them and bend them sideways but otherwise I find them to be of adequate construction. It's not an ultra-lite, ultra-compact (ultra expensive!) tool but will work well for those that like to use a fuel canister stove while camping or backpacking. Overall I really like this little stove, especially for something in this price range.*EDIT* I tried the stove again on a calmer day and had 20oz of cold water at a full, rolling boil in 9 minutes. I was losing a lot of heat to the wind the first time.

My search for an MP3 speaker ended with this.I own a Brookstone, a Veho, and an X-Mini-II speaker. They're all the pop-up resonator type. But when I saw that THIS one can play from an SD card, I was entranced because it eliminates the  need to carry around (and charge) a separate mp3 player.So I ordered this Satechi.Now, here's what's cool:SD STORAGE AND PLAYBACK (LARGE)It does play off SD cards and I ordered a couple of the Class 6 16GB SD cards so I could swap out "Classical" for "Soul & RB" and then "Dance Electronica" collections. If all these songs were on ONE card, then I would not be able to have "genres" for my different moods.NO SHUFFLE MODEAlso, this has NO SHUFFLE mode. And I loaded the SD card with albums, in order. My bad. Meaning, I'd hear the same song and artist all the way through. Workaround: I made ten folders. I arranged the songs in the donor folder by size. That mixed em up pretty well. From the donor folder, I transferred 10% of my music, all jumbled, into each of the ten folders I made on the SD card. (YES, the units seeks in sub-folders!) So when the unit powers up it goes through those folders and plays an unpredictable mish-mash of songs. The saving grace is that THE UNIT PICKS UP where you left off, unless you change the SD card's map (For example: add or delete files) or the unit dead-powers out. So if you loaded a 16GB card, jumbled, you would hear a different song and artist for a long long time, then it would start over.FEATURESAre essentially the same as the X Mini "Happy" for sale at $50.00. Satechi, multifunction jog switch, rechargeable battery, plays off SD card the whole nine yards....THE JOG SWITCHIs a pain. Push it in, to turn the unit on. Once it's on, push it again to pause. Bump it left or right to back up or go forward to another song, and hold left or right for volume. Sounds simple, meh. No shuffle mode.HEADPHONESPlays through headphones, headphones AND speaker, or just speaker and the units can be daisy-chained. You could buy a hundred of them and have a rock concert.SOUND QUALITYJust umbaleevable. UMBALEEVABLE. The magnet in the thing is the heavy part. And this thing weighs a 'sturdy' bit. Won't deform your jacket pocket but there's a satisfying "heft" to it. And when that magnet hops, it makes the unit hop. Try this: (pretty funny) lay the thing on it's side and it moves across the counter like an inchworm.It's rechargeable off USB, it's very solidly crafted with a grippable, almost rubber feel to the surface, the speaker on top is well protected from crush or pocket contents, the SD cards are cheap and the thing comes with all cords needed. In the pop-up resonator category of mp3 speakers, this is "it". I don't, (as a side note), understand how the X-Mini "Happy" is costing $20 more than this, since they have the same features.Good day

Update: I've finished the entire bottle and actually bought more in the sweet pom scent. It took a few uses of this to not feel crunchy, like my hair had to get used to it. But now that I've used the entire bottle, my hair craves it. I no longer have the issue of rinsing constantly, crunchy hair, or waxy feeling follicles. Personally, I'm adoring this stuff. I'll continue buying it as long as my hair wants it. I feel its a good price since a bottle will last me several months. A lot of people on here are saying that they have to use a ton of the product, but you really don't. If you follow the curly girl method of washing your hair it'll only take you 4-6 pumps to do your entire head (I have shoulder length hair).I'll start off by saying that I haven't used a sulfate product on my hair in 4 years and I went 'poo free almost a year ago, with amazing results in my naturally curly hair. But, as luck would have it, I lost my extra bars of homemade lye soap that I usually use and couldn't wait the time it would take to get more shipped. Off to Walgreens, I searched in vain for 10 minutes looking for Renpure Miracle Conditioner as I had used it in the past in a pinch and it worked to clean my hair without harsh chemicals. Alas, no such thing existed in my local Walgreens anymore. I slunk back to the hair care (using this term loosely because most of those products don't really care for your beautiful tresses) aisle after asking a blank-faced, gum snapping employee and started my search anew. This time I was searching out every single product that didn't contain sulfates. Piling them all into my buggy, I started eliminating them right off. Loreal? No. Organix? Tempting because I love the scents but no. It went back on the shelf. Then I came to this product. I've heard of cleansing conditioners, I mean who hasn't stayed up late and seen an infomercial? After reading the back of it and cringing at the unnecessary ingredients that I would be dumping onto my hair and into the water stream, I left it in my buggy. What's a girl to do when she only has one day to find something to wash her hair with before people start crossing the streets and avoiding eye contact?Earlier tonight, I got up for work and after making a naked mad dash into the livingroom where I had left this, I slathered it on and scrubbed. And waited. And rinsed. And rinsed. And rinsed again for good measure. I spent more time rinsing my hair tonight then I normally spend on a week in the shower. But, apparently, I didn't spend enough time. After watching my hard-earned dollars fall down the drain in the form of liquid, I stopped rinsing. My hair still felt waxy. Clean, but waxy. And it was as straight as a board that hasn't been exposed to moisture. This was my first sign that something was wrong. My hair normally curls up in the water. I went trucking on. To match my (hopefully) clean hair, I gave a quick scrub to the rest of me, rinsed everything (hair included)and jumped out.Pure bliss! After squeezing the water out of my hair with a 100% cotton t-shirt (to eliminate frizz), I was able to run a curl comb directly through it without snagging at all. I loved it. I scrunched it and was ready to go. Fast forward an hour. My hair is now completely dry. And crunchy. Good news is that there is no frizz. Bad news is that after wasting 100 gallons of water that third world countries would gladly murder me for, I didn't rinse it enough.My hair is crunchy even though I have no product in it. It feels light and airy (and a little waxy). Not heavy at all as others are reporting. It formed the most perfect curls my head has ever seen without the use of heated torture devices. But it is f-ing crunchy. Not cool Renpure. Not cool.I'll continue to use this on the chance that my hair may just need to get used to it. Maybe it's like growing up at a nudist resort and then suddenly being thrown into corporate America. Sure, they have casual Fridays, but that doesn't make up for the fact that you have to wear clothes. Maybe, just maybe, Renpure Cleansing Conditioner is casual Friday for my hair and it's rebelling like a teenager that just discovered that their parents can't physically remove them from a store for throwing a fit. I'll let you know after I devise a fitting punishment for the new teenaged attitude on my scalp.

Ok so let me not lie and say it's a walk in the park but it is by far the best cleanse I've ever tried and trust me I've tried a few. To be honest it's not that great tasting in water but I found it to be more pleasant in cranberry juice or even grapefruit juice (which helps with fat loss and curb your appetite). The second day was the hardest I did sneak a bite of a muffin that day but I didn't cheat any other time. The most shocking thing was my energy level was up, I expected to be very lightheaded and tired etc but I had a lot of energy, I actually went to the beach for hours on day 5. The best part of the cleanse is that you feel great after and it did help me with my bad eating habits for months which helped me to loose some weight even, but it's Christmas time now and I know I will be bad so I do look forward to doing this cleanse again in mid January. It's worth a try!!

I have the full-size in 9mm, and the rail notch is closer to the front of the firearm.  The notch on the light is further back, which basically means that the light is about 1.5-2 inches in front of the end of the barrel.  It still works, but it takes away from the aesthetics (if that is important to you).  You can slide the light further back, and it stays on fine and fixes the "look". After reading all the reviews on Amazon, the first thing I did when I unpacked my Flip was put the rechargeable batteries in a drawer and closed it.  You know what, the device works perfectly with standard AA's, and the standard Rayovac batt. recharger I had allowed me to charge older batteries while recording during the day.  I highly recommend this practice as you don't have to worry about cooking the CPU, dealing with bad customer service, etc.Also, in terms of battery length.  If you marathon minute after minute on this machine, you'll suck your batteries dry within 20 mins (after a fresh charge).  But, if you record in bursts of 1-3 minutes at a time, with pauses in-between, you can record for days.Keep with cloth bag that it comes with as it's a nice way to clean the screen and lens and DON'T UPDATE THE FIRMWARE.  The Flipshare version that comes with it could be more stable, but it exports without corruption 100% of the time (I've exported about 230 movies having it for about 2 months now).  Once I updated the firmware, I noticed that nearly 1 of every 5 movies I exported had some fatal corruption in it that could no longer be recognized by Movie Maker.  I was annoyed, uninstalled the program and re-installed the original Flipshare programming directly from the cam again with the older firmware to once again return to hassle-free exporting.Let me mention two other things, Zoom and Image Stabilization.  First of all, there isn't really a zoom on this camera.  2x zoom really just amounts to the difference of stretching out your arm a foot more forward--and if the object is directly in the face of the camera, it'll be blurry regardless due to auto-focus.  That said, the quality of the shots at 720p are so nice that third-party software can easily zooming without too much in the fidelity of the image.  Plus--flip doesn't make any promises about higher levels of zooms from the get go so you know what your getting from the start.  Therefore, I don't recommend this camera for up close shots of nature or babies--works fine for everything else. There's also no image stabilization so pics can get really jittery especially when filming and walking at the same time.  Once again, free third-party software like vReveal offers stabilization effects that perfectly reduce the jitters of any of your vids.That's about it, it couldn't be simpler to use and pulling it out and filming is quick and a breeze--the inboard mic is supremely sensitive and plays back wonderfully in full stereo.  Nice vibrant colors during playback on a brutally honest HD LCD screen.  Love this camera.

They aren't kidding when they say slim.  I didn't pay attention to any dimensions when ordering this one.  It's got to be about half as thick as the one I was using (though that one is several years old so it's probably a little thicker than the average run of the mill one out now).It is an external drive.  If you're looking for one you know what this is and what it does.  It is exactly what you're looking for.  It works just as you'd expect it to.  Honestly, not much to say there.Like most, if not all drives, it comes with its own management tools.  I never bother with those, but figured if other people do, well, I should try that out to see how it works.  Honestly, if I did care about those, I'd probably knock a star or two off of my review.  Soon as I went to install it (On a Mac) it installed.  But then popped up an error at the end of the install informing me that it indeed, failed.  Huh?  I rebooted. Launched the Seagate Dashboard app.  It had indeed installed.  As there was no specific error beyond "The installation failed." which it clearly had not, I went on exploring the app.  First things first, I tried the update option because if just one aspect failed surely the update would find it - nope I was good.  There's a test your drive option which shows you a progress bar as it tests your drive to show you that it is doing something, but yields no real results, I assume it will if the drive has a problem.  It shows you the size of your drive and how much space is used / available but doesn't seem to update real time (in other words you're better off using your OS's tools for this).  You can also use this app to set the HD up to sync with your  social networks (can't tell you about this one - I'm usually use pics from my computer or phone which I already back up  so I don't need to sync those and end up with duplicates).Oh - the cable looks proprietary.  Two part prong thing (yes, that is the technical term).  The hubby, who is much more tech savvy that I, was a bit annoyed by this but quickly asked me to grab my Kindle cord.  He insisted I try it (it  fits in half of their plug).  Sure enough it works.  Full disclosure - I am sure this not something the company supports as they want to sell the cables.  But, it's still nice to know.So as a drive, yup 5 stars!  If you do care about the tools that come with a drive?  Knock a star or two off.  They're just not impressive and I'd say do yourself a favor and learn to manage the drive within the OS instead.

I never take my UV filter off of my lens unless I am going to immediately put on a different filter.  There are basically two camps out there, those that feel you should always use a UV filter to protect your lens from dirt and scratches and those that feel it is a waste of time and only degrades your image.  Well, I tried going without one for awhile and then dirt started to build up on the front element of my very expense lens.  And although you can clean the front element of a lens, I feel much, much safer wiping my comparatively cheap UV filter time and time again rather than chancing any actual lens damage.  I think for the nearly unquantifiable loss in image quality you may suffer by having a UV filter on, it is well worth the extra piece of mind it gives me.

I came across this little gem while searching for a new tent. In the past, I'd always gone out to Walmart and bought one, but it was more for camping with some buddies overnight, and getting plastered. I was a bit tired of the low quality tents that you find in stores like Academy, Walmart, and Dicks, and wanted something a bit nicer. Of course, being a bargain hunter, I didn't want to pay an arm and a leg for it, which is what you'll pay if you want good quality most of the time.I ran across a list of "Amazon best selling tents" or something similar to that and saw this at the top of the list. I dismissed it at first, because of my bad experiences in the past with Coleman. They usually have poor quality tents, that won't fit back in the bag they came with. Further still, most of the bags themselves break after only a few uses, leaving you with a a mess of nylon and tarp.The Red Canyon, I can safely say, isn't what I'm used to from Coleman. My personal assumption is that it's good quality because it doesn't come from Walmart.Strengths:➺ Good quality material. It does not feel cheap or flimsy in any way.➺ Beautiful view from inside, with the rain tarp off➺ VERY spacious inside. I was able to fit an entire queen sized bed, table, several chairs, and equipment with room to spare.Weaknesses:➺ Some trouble getting the tent back into the bag, when folded carefully. I had to leave out a few of the things it came with.➺ Instructions are somewhat unclear. The tent comes with many dividers and sheets, many of which are not well explained.All in all, if you're looking for a massive tent for multiple people, at a good price, this is your tent. I can't recommend it highly enough!

This product comes in three parts:1) An Eye Activating Serum;2) An Eye Beautifying Creme; and3) A plastic stand which holds the eye products.The packaging indicates that we get 0.30 fl. oz. (9 mL) of each the serum and the creme, which (to me at least) is not readily apparent, since the two containers are not the same size. The serum comes in a slender tube, from which you squeeze a small amount of the product. The creme comes in a small cylindrical pump.The serum has a gel-like consistency and no fragrance that I could detect. The color of the gel was off-putting to me at first, as it is grey-black. It has a nice, silicone-like feel. Very smooth and gentle on the delicate eye area.The creme has the consistency of watered-down hand or body lotion and also has no fragrance that I could detect. This was a big plus for me, since I am very fragrance sensitive and can't use a lot of products due to their fragrance. It didn't seem to absorb fully and left a little bit of a coating on the skin.The packaging claims that the product "stimulates skin's natural process for creating brilliantly beautiful, younger-looking eyes" and "Immediately reduces the appearance of dark circles and puffiness." The word "brilliant" is thrown around a LOT on the packaging. I don't know how to judge brilliance in skin--at least not in older skin (I'm 57).Were my eyes brilliant and younger looking? Not that I could notice.Did the product immediately reduce the appearance of my dark circles? Nope.Did the product immediately reduce the appearance of puffiness? I don't know about immediately, but I did notice a reduction in puffiness around my eyes within the first few days of using it.The only difference I noticed in using the product was an improvement in puffiness. I certainly did not feel that this serum and creme kept my eye area better hydrated or smooth than my usual eye serum and lotion. I also found the packaging rather annoying...the serum has to go top down in the holder while the creme is top up, which causes confusion when you're trying to get ready quickly in the morning. Additionally, both products have caps you must remove, after removing them from the stand, before you can access the product. It just seemed like too much work to me.In summary, the product gets bonus points for having no (or very little) fragrance and for reducing puffiness in my eyes. However, beyond that, I did not feel that the product lived up to its promises or performed any better than my usual eye products (and I don't think it even performed as well as the usual products).

Summary-----------This is my second favorite trackball, just behind the Logitech Trackman Wheel (ancestor to the Logitech M570, which I'll be ordering shortly, due to a small ding in my TM Wheel's ball). It's well worth trying, especially if you either strongly dislike or can't use a thumb trackball like the M570, or if want a trackball that supports left handed use.Full review------------I need to use a trackball, due to RSI pain in my right shoulder, arm, and hand.  I've been using a Logitech Trackman Wheel Optical for years, with great results, but I wanted to try another trackball for two reasons:1. When I'm doing research (or just surfing), I tended to overdo the use of the scroll wheel, causing hand and arm pain.2. I was worried about over-stressing my thumb. No problems yet, but taking the "repetitive" out of RSI is typically a good idea, in my experience.I very much like the Trackman Marble, and have been switching it off with my Trackman Wheel for the past several months.The ball is a decent size, and three of the four buttons are well placed and have decent feel, so far. I run Linux, so working around the lack of scroll wheel was simple enough: I mapped the thumb side secondary button such that a click is still "browser back", but holding it causes the ball to act as a two dimensional scroll wheel. It took a bit to get my grip right so that holding the button and scrolling wasn't uncomfortable, but now I find it better than an actual scroll wheel, in may ways.As an aside, I found that I have been able to resolve my scroll wheel pain with the Trackman Wheel by taking advantage of the fact that it is stationary. I now mix the use of index and middle finger, as well as often scrolling by using actual arm motion, rather than just finger motion. By mixing things up that way, I no longer get much scroll induced pain with the wheel. I don't know if Windows or Mac users have a similar option -- I believe the Logitech software can do something similar under Windows, but, if my past experience with Logitech software holds, it might be less painful to either live without the scroll functionality, or switch to Linux!Back to the Trackman Marble, I have only two real gripes, other than perhaps the lack of a "real" scroll wheel (perhaps they could place one on the main body just aft of the ball), which I no longer much miss:My first gripe is that the finger side secondary button is hard to reach. I have mine mapped to button 2 (i.e., middle click), and have *almost* gotten used to pulling my hand back to click it, but have recently enabled middle click emulation (i.e., clicking both main buttons results in a middle click), because I miss too darn often, and use the middle-click paste function just often enough for it to be an annoyance.They could have placed the secondary buttons down and forward of the main buttons, instead of above and back. That way, both thumb and finger side would be easily reachable.On the other hand, that would make the use of the thumb side secondary button much less comfortable for scrolling, so I'd prefer they not move the buttons unless they add a scrolling affordance of some kind, though it would be fine if they *added* two buttons, leaving the current two where they are. It'd be fine for them to be wired as duplicates -- just providing alternative locations for better ergonomics.My second gripe is much more troublesome for me: the shape of the body is painful. I don't know if it's just the nerve pain I've been suffering from lately, but I find resting my hand on the body, even lightly, to be painful. It might be too hard, or it might be that the shape is to sharply convex. Regardless, I have to work to hover my hand over the trackball, which causes considerable fatigue and stress to my shoulder and arm over time. If it turns out that my pain *is* due to my current condition, I'll come back and update my review.I don't think my overall rating would change, in any case: it's currently about a 3.8. Pain free use might raise it to a 4.4, but not quite a five. That would require a solution to the button reach issue, and perhaps a soft touch surface on the body.

I was really happy to find a 24 pack available of Biore strips, because up until seeing these on Amazon, the largest package I'd seen was 14.I've been using biore strips since they first came out.  I've tried the alternatives--egg white/toilet paper masque popular on youtube, drugstore brand strips, the old Ponds (I think) strips... but nothing works as well as the Biore strips.Sometimes a certain portion will seem not to adhere, but I find I can lessen that by:1) Making sure I wash and dry my face first with a really good cleanser, such asCetaphil Gentle Skin Cleanser, For all skin types, 16-Ounce Bottles (Pack of 2)2) Wetting the area super duper well3) Cutting the strips in half or in thirds depending on the place I adhere it to.  For my nose, I basically cut the strip in half, place it normally, and then remove, pulling outward.  I tend to have clogged pores on the outside of my nose, and this maximizes what I get.For my chin area, I will usually put a full face strip down, and then cut another strip into 2-3 pieces to augment it on the sides and bottom.  Some times, I'll also just cut a strip in half, and place each half from the outside of my lips, angling down towards the center of my chin.Very very happy to find such a large size.  Nothing beats Biore strips--and I still need them even though I use a Clarisonic.

Two things...first, read this entire review before commenting. Second, repeat these words out loud right now..."This is a Generation-1 monocular". Thank you.My review. I bought this scope and the Armasight Helmet Mount #3 to use for hunting pigs at night ('cause that's when the little suckers are out you know!) I received it on Christmas Eve and took it to an undisclosed location adjacent to Delta National Forest to try it out. I had no difficulty adjusting the ocular lens and the objective lens to get a fairly crisp image (through out this review I am going to remind you that this is Gen-1 sight, ok?). It was a clear, starry night, no wind, and 27 degrees Fahrenheit (remember to use anit-fog coating whenever you stick your warm eyeball next to a cold lens). Yazoo City was 20 or so miles to my northeast, Vicksburg 40 miles to the south and Rolling Fork 15 miles over my right shoulder--the ambient light from these cities allowed me to see without the illuminator. I could see fairly well out to (my estimate) 20 or 30 yards. When I turned the illuminator on I got an excellent image out to (my estimate) about 20 or 30 yards. If you have ever tried to shine your AAA Mini-Maglite across a cotton field at night this is what I was seeing. The built-in illuminator is good but it has its limitations. When the moon came up I could see very well out to (my estimate again) 100 yards. I couldn't find any pigs to participate in my product review (#$%@^!!).A couple of things to keep in mind...what do you want this scope to do? I thought that I could use the scope to see my target and use my non-NVG eye to sight my shotgun. By the way, just for fun sometime shoot some 00-buck through a 20" slug barrel with NVGs. It will look like a flash cube! I am reevaluating my thinking and I am going to purchase a separate night vision sight for my shotgun and/or my AK. I will use the monocular to find my way through the brush and sight at the pigs with the rifle sight. No double duty.Also, check to see if it is legal to use nightvision to hunt (or whatever you intend to do) where you are. If you choose to be marginally legal and decide to go "active" by switching on your illuminator you may as well have a neon sign that gives away your location. Game wardens have NVGs, too, and theirs are WAY better than yours because the government bought them and they are probably Gen-3 or better.Just sayin'.(Later) I have no problem giving out the info that I did in this review because I was hunting legally with permission on private land. Obey the law! It's a good idea!!

I've heard the Bose Solo TV Sound System in action and I have to say, I am not impressed.  Calling it a "Sound System" is very misleading.  The Bose Solo is simply a speaker that hooks up through Optical, Coaxial, or Analog.  It does not have HDMI, which is unfortunate because you won't get lossless audio codecs like DTS-HD MA or Dolby TrueHD.  You won't get a surround sound effect that sound bars in $399.00 price range.The Bose Solo TV Sound System is an improvement over your TV speakers, that much is true.  However, for the amount of money you're going to spend on the Solo TV Sound System, you could have a true home theater system.For example, the Onkyo SKS-HT540 7.1 Home Theater Speaker System is selling for $199.00 and the Sony STRDH520 7.1 Channel 3D AV Receiver selling for $189.99 and you come up at $388.99, leaving you $10 to spend on a Blu-Ray movie.Don't waste your money on this glorified speaker.

We do a lot of camping, and lately road trips - with camping. We used to take the big and bulky Coleman lanterns, double mantle, as we thought the more light the better.It's been years taking that. Products have changed, and so have we. I have tried other lights, like some of the many LED or fluorescent lanterns, big and small. Most, or actually all, of them collect dust in the basement. Never have liked the quality of light from the LED, nor the archaic by now fluorescent. LED's have become better, but not so much the color characteristic of the light - too whitish/blue.So, I returned to a favorite light we used more than 20 years ago, the Mighty-Lite I think it was called. Basically, it was the same as this light, but with the advantage of the ignition piezo. I like it because it is compact, easily packed in its box, and into the camping box. It certainly doesn't compete in sheer light output like the big ones, and doesn't hiss aloud, but one doesn't have to shield their eyes either.I think the quality is nice, not a cheap looking/feeling product. The ignition works fine. We either hang it over the camping table, or on it, or anywhere one wants to put it. Mantels are hard to find in local sporting goods stores, even REI's flagship store here in Seattle. Save yourself looking for them - buy them online, and buy 4 or more to keep in the box as mantles can be damaged easily with too much bouncing, knocking around. The glass globe is well protected by the cage.

I have used these for many years. I have used them on high carbon swords, fine gold and silver, brass and bronze of all mixed content.  These absolutely work extremely well and with much less work than any other product I have tried (and I have tried a lot).  My fear thru the years is that they may someday discontinue them.  I have never used them on anything but metals.  The following is the best practice I have found and its easy.  First, the cloth turns black.  Don't worry just keep using it even the black parts.  As soon as it turns black it's actually working better.  Just rub around on whatever you're cleaning with the same part of the cloth or use more of it if you think you should.  It, your hands and the thing to be cleaned will turn black(ish).  When every part has been rubbed down use a towel (one you don't much care about much) and wipe it all off.  Do it aging the same way but start with a clean part of the cloth.  Wipe it down again.  Take the object that you just polished to a sink and wash it and your hands off at the same time with soap and water.  At this point you will see it really shine.  Then use another towel (this time any because it's clean) to wipe it off.  You will have a well polished item with no tarnish and completely shiny!  I did a very old incense burn from a Orthodox Church that had not been cleaned in decades.  That one took a few hours with years of tarnish and coal and incense caked all over it.  When I took it back the Priest took me aside and politely asked if I found the same burner new and just purchased it!  He actually saw it as a completely new one.  And it for sure looked new.  If you have any old metal and use this product as I have stated (depending on the lack of care you may have to repeat the steps) you  will have a brand new, shiny to the point where it's hard to look directly at result.

Over twenty years ago, I was at a store selecting backpacking gear for a long trip. The salesman threw some small wooden toy blocks down on the store floor, and then had me lay down on a simple foam pad on top of the blocks. Obviously, a bit "rocky" and uncomfortable. Then he did the same demo with a Thermarest. I couldn't even feel the blocks. Ever since then, I've been a fan. I have three Thermarest pads (my original model, one "luxury" model, and one "ultra-light" backpacking model).Recently, in looking for a nap pad to use at the office (tired of yoga mats), I decided to save a little money and buy this cheap Chinese Thermarest knockoff. It's not as good as the Thermarest.In fairness to this pad, it's cheaper, comes with a case, two straps, and a little patch kit. But the pad doesn't inflate to a full 1.5" thick (as advertised) unless you really, really blow hard and the quickly tighten the valve before it deflates. This brings up another interesting observation: This pad relies heavily on inflation with air rather than foam padding & inflation with air. My standard and luxury Thermarest pads feel thicker deflated than this ALPS pad feels fully-inflated. There just seems to be more foam in the Thermarest pads.I'll keep this ALPS pad for the office because it was cheap (not much more than the cost of a yoga mat, and a little more comfortable), but I would never consider taking this pad camping or backpacking. It's just not nearly as comfortable as my Thermarest pads.

Logitech's new air mouse is a fantastic little device -- and to use it feels like getting in on the first generation of the wave of the future, when mice become more like wands and are completely liberated from the table.While this is an excellent step forward, and worth having if you are in front of the computer more than a few hours in the day, it is not quite ready to be the new best thing.Part of the problem -- but part of what makes this so versatile -- is that the Logitech folks didn't want to make the leap to a wand but made this into a hybrid mouse/air mouse.  The optimal shape and button placement for one function is not optimal for the other.As a mouse it works great -- it has left and right click, a touch scroll and four programmable buttons.  It fits nicely into the hand and is very responsive on the ground.  Some optical mice seem to zone out here and there on the desk but this one catches my every move.As a wand it still has a lot going for it.  It takes some time to get the hang of it, and you have to get it centered and adjusted for your speed -- but once you do it can point and click all over the place and subtle hand movements can lead to high precision pointing.  The buttons are programmed for many standard functions -- back, play/pause, select and volume -- and can be reprogrammed to do almost anything you could do with a few key strokes.What is really cool is that there are some unique wand functions -- that work like magic.  Hold down the volume button and swing the mouse to the left and the volume goes down, swing to the right and the volume goes up - and you can program it so that up is up and down is down if you prefer.  Hold down the play/pause button and "twirl" the air mouse clockwise and it will advance to the next song (in iTunes) or next chapter (in a movie) or "forward" to the next webpage (if you have a browser open); twirl counterclockwise and it goes back.  I wish there was a way to set this so that it always controlled songs in iTunes -- since I'd like to be able to do that when I'm browsing, but unfortunately they don't give that option.  It only works in whatever program is currently on top.The difficulty is that the buttons are placed where they'd be easy to reach for someone whose hand is on top of the mouse -- when it is sitting on the table.  When you are holding it, your fingers reach around the mouse and you have to contort them a bit and sometimes adjust how the mouse sits in your hand to get your fingers to reach all of the buttons.  I've found that it works pretty well if I hold it sideways, with my palm on the bottom and my fingers reaching around to touch the left click button and my thumb on the right click button -- but it wasn't really designed and shaped to make that kind of holding natural.  It is really shaped like a table mouse and the air features feel like bonuses but not like they are the primary functionality of this piece.I'd like to see a next generation air mouse that really made the leap -- allowing users to use it on the table as a mouse if they prefer, but obviously designed for use in the air.  In other words, I'd like to see the next generation be a wand and only secondarily a mouse rather than a mouse that tries to fly but only manages to get partly off of the ground.  Having said that, I really like this mouse and will use it exclusively until that next generation thing comes around.

This is Plano's "mega-box". Plano's been around a long time and they make a good box. This one is designed with a drop-down front panel, behind which are 4 equal size drawers. There's also a very large compartment under the top lid for large items.PRO's: Plano makes a good box. This one has LOTS of storage. It's easily the capacity of 4 or 5 standard 2- or 3-tray type tackle boxes. It's sturdily built, and the drawer system (instead of hinged trays) makes it very solid. It's made of the same familiar plastic you see on smaller Plano boxes.Compartments are sized for saltwater three-hook spinners and they'll accommodate a lot. There are also two outside side racks with "toothbrush-holder" type open slots for tools, which makes this more versatile for other hobbies (sewing, art, painting, general tools, etc).CON's: This is a BIG box (pay attention to the measurements). This is about the size and weight of a Coleman cooler in the 24-can or 36-can size. So if you're going to fish from a boat or a fixed chair on a pier, great. But if your style is to move up and down a riverbank you may find this one a little large and heavy to be constantly carrying around ( and no, its shape will not fit in a typical backpack).The drawer dividers are fixed, which is a plus for some people, a minus for others. That's a pretty short list of possible Con's!SUMMARY: If you want a large high-capacity tackle box for a boat or other fixed position, this is a great choice. I like mine, I'm glad I have it, and it's well made.I like Plano products. No complaints at all. Just remember, it's pretty big.PS: I saw this last week at WalMart (for a higher price than currently on Amazon) so you could check one out in person there.Hope this was helpful. Happy fishing, everybody.

The first dry-shampoo I used was the original Suave in the silver can with the yellow lid. I liked it a lot for the money and the smell and for my first try! I didn't like how quickly it ran out and how its powdery self showed up wayyy more in my brown hair!I have tried several brands since, but the fumes were just way too strong to handle. The original Suave had the nicest smell, but it wasn't doing the job really well. When I heard about the Keratin Infusion, I really wanted to try it after reading the reviews about how it helped keep that shine. I have to say it is definitely the best dry-shampoo!!! The fumes are tolerable, but still pretty stinky. I am not sure why they can't create a nicely scented one that works well too! Oh well!People who think it's gross or weird to not wash your hair everyday have no idea what they are talking about! I only wash my hair about every 3 days, and sometimes I go longer. I have really nice healthy hair that doesn't drip with oil after a few hours. People who wash their hair everyday are stripping their locks of important nutrients. That's why your hair is a grease pit. Please, stop ruining your hair and making your head an oily mess!! This does not mean "Stop taking baths or showers"... just wear a shower cap!! :)Anyway, no more ranting... but if you are trying to make things right with your hair by giving it a break, start with an every-other-day wash, and use this to cover up any oils that make their way through. The cool thing about dry-shampoos is that they do not block oil, but they just cover it up. This means that your head is not getting signals to replenish oils, because for all it knows, it is still there! By using dry-shampoos you can "train" your head into thinking it only needs to replenish oils every few days instead of every few hours!! I am a total believer in these dry-shampoos, and I love that they are becoming mainstream now!! I just want some fruity scents or something! :)

I live in Waikiki and so whenever a product comes along that uses the word "Hawaiian" i'm instantly curious. Is there something that makes it "Hawaiian" or is it just the usual marketing gimmick?Hawaiian Tropical Silk Hydration SPF 30 sunscreen is a good enough product. SPF 30 is certainly adequate at Honolulu sea level, though if you're in Denver you might possibly want to go a little higher. I can't speak to the hydration claims for this product; it designed to hydrate your skin. You can't argue with that idea but I couldn't verify its effectiveness.The lotion is smooth without feeling greasy or heavy, and putting it on liberally doesn't lead to that gooey or sticky feeling that comes with some sunscreen products. But the problem was that I've tried using it for outdoor exercise, and it doesn't hold up for very long. Trips to the beach, though, seem to be no problem; it lasts about as long as any other common lotion and feels a lot better.So, then, it's a good product for light use, but what makes it supposedly Hawaiian? I guess it's the fairly pronounced coconut scent (at least they didn't go with the pineapple cliche). Yes, it's marketing. No, this product has nothing to do with Hawaii. But it's still a good item that will prove useful to beachgoers and sunbathers.

I have had this backpack and used it quite extensively for over 10 months.  During a trip, I found that one of the clasps that hold the shoulder strap had broken and was scratching me. My green laser came in one of the best after-packaging boxes I have received from Amazon.  My seller was Menvi and it came with a battery. This dot is amazingly bright and clear in the day, however; you are exposing yourself in the dark and lower light.  Depending on your lighting and application, this could be a terrible thing. The dot is visible well beyond 100+ yards in daylight.  In the dark or lower light, the laser is a solid beam that is easily origin traceable, thus exposing you.I do not gravitate towards barrel mounts, although I appreciate having the option, so I employed the other mount.  Pressure switches create more possibility for malfunction, therefore; I prefer the on/off button on the back.  I dig the strength of the dot in lit areas and the most difficult aspect, for me; is deciding which platform deserves it the most.Bottom Line: Get it now and pay less than I did.  This thing is awesome for a well lit area with a strong and visible dot providing instant target acquisition.  In the event you employed it at night, while traceable, you need to leave it on for that possibility.  If you are quick, you can eliminate the issue.Stay safe.

Okay, for years I couldn't see the need for a tablet. I have a new PC, a new laptop and a Galaxy S3. A tablet seemed unnecessary. Then I got a promotion and wanted to see about becoming a bit more productive and organized while on the go. The laptop is tough to haul out while I'm not stationary and the phone is tough to manage with it's tiny screen. So I finally opted to try out a tablet figuring something inexpensive as an experiment wouldn't be too much of a loss if I didn't find a use for it.Well I was wrong. I find it's very convenient when on the go. The organization software, calendar access and extra productivity made a definite improvement in my workflow. And for the price, this was an amazing deal.So I'm very happy with my Nexus 7, 2nd ed. It's quick and snappy and responsive on the processor end. I rarely notice any lag. Or maybe there hasn't even been any. I can't think of any time it lagged unless a program was crashing, which I don't think can be attributed to the device.It's a perfect size for easy portability and with a handstrap case, it's comfortable to hold for extended use.My only issue seems to be occasional problems with touch responsiveness. Sometimes it just seems to forget I'm tapping it. Responses get slower and eventually seem to just stop. This is especially problematic around the edges. A quick restart always fixes the issue. It's happened a handful of times over the past month, so not a major issue for me, but something to be aware of.I would definitely purchase again, though if I was to buy another tablet, I'd probably considering going with a 3g/4g version.

I've had my wireless configuration for quite some time, and never experienced a problem UNTIL I had an XP system built.  Then, 3-4 times, every hour, I'd drop and I'd have to tinker around to reconnect.After a bit of research, I found the solution!  XP has something called "Wireless Zero Configuration" .. and to the best of my knowledge, Linksys is simply not compatible with this.  Does that mean ditch your Linksys card or USB adapter?  NOPE!Just do a quick search on the web, and it'll tell you how to disable the Wireless Zero Configuration, and all will be well again =)

I purchased the package from Amazon consisting of the DB8 antennaAntennas Direct DB8 Multi-Directional Bowtie UHF Antenna with Extreme 70+ Miles Range, the Winegard preamplifierWinegard ANWI8700 Winegard AP Signal Amplifierand the j-Mount mastAntennas Direct 30 Inch Universal J-Mount for Antenna Mounting STM-715. They arrived in good condition and are now installed on my roof and working great (image provided).We are in Ventura, CA which is 70 miles due west of the Los Angeles transmitters on Mount Wilson. My Sony TV found a total of 112 signals, including sub-channels, when it searched! Reception is excellent here with the antenna only 3-feet above our one story roof. (In our VHF analog TV past we needed a big antenna 30-feet above the roof.)The only issue was the U-bolt that came with the Winegard preamplifier does not fit over the 1 1/2-inch J-mount mast (mast too big/bolt too small) so I mounted everything on a piece of 1-inch schedule 40 PVC pipe and set the bottom 8-inches of that pipe inside the 30-inch mast of the J-Mount. The PVC is secured to the J-Mount with two #10 screws. The J-Mount is lag screwed onto a wood pallet which sits loose on our tar and rock roof of very low slope.All I had to add was enough RG-6 coax cable to connect everything up and reach the TV.One further comment- speaking as a communications systems engineer, the term "multi-directional" is misleading for an antenna having the gain this antenna is advertised to have and the performance it obviously has. Any high gain antenna, this one included, has its best performance in one single direction only. To be sure, it will receive signals from other directions but with reduced performance.

I have tried all kinds of work lights for working on my car and around the house. None of them work that well to get dedicated light where you need it and are always in the way or have to be moved around. This is the perfect solution, I can't believe I didn't consider this earlier in my search. It is lightweight, once you have it on for a couple minutes you forget it is there. It is small enough so that it doesn't get in the way very often and gives you light exactly where you are looking. It has 4 clicks of adjustibility to fine tune the light even more. It also is raised around the lens area so when you bump it, it hits this raised area as opposed to scratching the lens, a very good design feature.My only complaint would be about the battery life claims. If you read the technical PDF online, you really only get the brightest light for the first couple of hours because the brightness drops off so quickly. The curve is like a skateboard ramp with the brightness dropping off very early and then once it is pretty low it hangs around forever. Petzl makes other lights that are programmable so you can get more brightness instead of more battery life. This one is not programmable, so the battery life is set to last as long as possible, even if the brightness is pretty much unusable at a certain point. To me, the 58 hour claim on maximum is bogus, because I would think to the average person maximum means the full 70 lumens, which is not even close to being the case here. I use rechargeable NiMH batteries and probably get about 5-6 hours of really good light before needing to recharge them. Once the brightness drops off, it isn't enough for me. I have considered purchasing the next model up (Tikka XP) which has about 10 more lumens that might help. However, ultimately I probably have to shell out for one that is programmable.Even though that was a long rant, I still think it is a great product, just something to be aware of before purchasing. Read the fine print!

It worked fine on my older Mac 10.5.X Mac Pro, my iPhone and both iPads but for some reason it wouldn't let me send from the Macbook which is where my business emails are answered.So, I spent the next hour plus reconfiguring my business email account settings to no avail. Meanwhile I had to answer some of the client emails faster than I was able to on my mac so I had to go to my providers email to respond.During an online chat with my email provider trying to debug the mail issue I lost communication wit the router, Not just on the Mac but all devices. I looked at the router and all systems said go but no joy.I rebooted it and it came back. I go back to my MBook and after 15 minute it was down again.I unplugged it and reinstalled the trusty slow old Cisco router and what do you know?  My mail worked again.So if you have half a day to spend for an hour or so of considerably faster internet speed this is the one, just don't get used to it.

I had a newer motherboard with only one IDE header and had 3 IDE devices on my computer.  I purchased this item, turned the computer off, opened the case and installed the adapter and connected the third device (a ZIP drive).  I restarted the computer and windows recognized the device and installed the driver.  That was all there was to it.  I tested to make sure that the drive was working properly and closed up the case and all has been well since.  Shipping time and price for this component were both excellent!Was a bit confusing that the adapter was shown as "(2 sata/2 pata)".  While it can power two pata (IDE) devices through a duplex ribbon, the write-up made one think that it had two pata (IDE) headers.  Photographs were not of a high enough resolution for the buyer to be certain of this issue and the product description did not make it clear.  Other than this one issue that did not affect me, the whole experience was excellent.

I have very sensitive, combination skin that is also prone to breakouts. I purchased this moisturizer a few weeks ago and so far I am satisfied. I'm starting to notice slight changes to my skin as I'm aging, and as one would expect, the area I'm noticing them most is around my eyes. Including an eye cream or otherwise-targeted eye product has become part of my daily routine. However, I still don't actually have dry skin, and the problem I seem to run into quite frequently when trying new eye products is that they are incredibly heavy and intense - far, far more than what I personally need.In that regard, this is a fantastic product. It's very light - closer to a lotion consistency than a typical cream, and it absorbs quickly. It moisturizes my skin plenty, and it does seem to have a bit of a brightening effect, as it claims. I can't attest to any real long-term benefits, as I haven't used it very consistently, but given the light moisturizing quality, I'm sure that it would encourage at least a slight improvement in the skin quality of your eye area.The one major complaint I have is that it doesn't sit well under makeup, and since that's a touted benefit, I think it's absolutely worth bringing up. I tried a few different concealers over it, and they all caused the product to ball and pill on my skin - one of my all-time greatest beauty pet peeves. If you don't wear any undereye concealer, then this can definitely work as a daytime product for you, but if you apply anything at all under your eyes during the day, this will strictly be a nighttime eye cream. This is the main reason I haven't managed to use it consistently...since I tend not to wear much on my face at night (again, I don't have dry skin, and I also prefer to let it breathe while I sleep), I just haven't used it a whole lot. If it didn't have this issue with my makeup, I would have used it daily without hesitation.For anyone that does consistently use skin care at night or doesn't wear much makeup, I'd say this is a very nice eye cream for people that don't need intense hydration. It's a lovely consistency and - at least instantly - does do what it claims in boosting radiance. I'll definitely keep using it on days I go makeup-free or nights when my skin is feeling a little dry.

This is a great solution to power management of your wall mounted TV without hiring an electrician. Most professional installers will provide cord management (run cords through the walls) but the power cable cannot be run in the wall. This leaves an unsightly cable in the middle of the wall. The only solution was to cover the cord with a plastic cover painted to match the wall, or hire an electrician to put in an outlet behind the TV. The Wallblade solves that problem. Setup was simple, just connect the top and bottom with your standard White, Black and Ground. There is a video on Youtube that walks you through the steps.The housing stays firmly in the wall with two flaps that secure themselves to the inside of the drywall, and the outlets are recessed to allow for flush mounting of the TV.  Awesome product, highly recommended!

First of all, this is the go-to 'L' bracket for the handheld photographer and videographer!  In most cases this will be perfect for the stable set up videographer.  Zeikos being the product of choice for many of us out there with enormous, hard to keep stable cameras.  It only handles two items, in my case flash and stereo microphones, but it stabilizes the set up by anchoring firmly to the added battery pack below the camera without increasing stress to the camera screw anchors themselves.You have to beware on knock-off products that are not genuine Zeikos that there is a comon problem of the company putting the full size anchor screw in but using thinner and substandard bracket metal.  What this means to you is a screw that will not tighten or for those who twist as hard as they can..a cracked camera body and dislodged screw port..this is an expensive fix!  If you order from a different dealer and get a knock off you can either try to return it (see Wickipedia under: "impossible or costly"... Or you might try either a layer of rubber a crossed the entire bottom bar to raise the camera from the screw or carefully remove about 1/8" of metal cleanly off the top of the screw using a fine metal file.  Leave a slight bevel for the threads to start and grab...Enough of that!  This is a superior product for what it is for.  Don't count on it being your go-to solution for video stabilization racks... Those cost $400 for a reason and they are a bargain at the price!

This is a wonderful router!  I bought this to replace the very old Time Warner modem that I've been leasing.  I wish I'd done this sooner!  Not only does this eliminate stupid modem rental fees (which has gone up to $6 bucks!!!), but it's also very stable and reliable.  I've noticed a difference in my internet connection instantly.  There is no more "glitchiness" as the connection never really cuts out anymore.As for the speed, it's about the same.  My internet tops out at 20 mbps/2 mbps and according to the speed tests, nothing's changed much since switching the mode.  However, some downloads are a noticeably faster.  I went from downloading a movie at roughly 1.8-2 mbps to almost 2.5-2.8 mbps which was what I was hoping for in the first place.The installation was a piece of cake.  I literally replaced all the plugs from the old modem and matched it to the new one.  In a few seconds, my internet browser directed to the TimeWarner website telling me to activate the new modem by calling TimeWarner and giving them the MAC address, which is easily displayed on the webpage (and the box and the back of the modem).  Called TimeWarner, the rep activated the modem in less than 2 minutes.  Easy peasy!Would highly recommend for those who want a more stable, reliable, faster internet connect and also eliminate the modem rental fees.. forever!!!

I am using this external microphone with the Sony MEXBT3900USony MEXBT3900U In-Dash CD Receiver MP3/WMA/AAC Player with Bluetooth.  I had the microphone and the car stereo installed at the same time, at Best Buy.  I have found the quality of the hands-free calling over Bluetooth to be excellent.  I can hear callers very well through my car speakers (it is loud and very easy to hear!).  Callers can hear me very well, even at high speeds (with windows closed!).  I haven't opened up the window going 55 mph and checked the quality of the call, since this is a not realistic condition for me; but, may be if you have a convertible I guess.I think the location where you install the microphone is very important.  It should be fairly close to your mouth.  After doing a lot of research on this, I had it installed on the visor (toward the left).  I added some photos to the product page (I can't add photos to this review) so you can see how it looks.  The microphone is fairly close to my mouth, so I think this contributes to the excellent quality I am getting.  Just keep in mind that, with this microphone location (at least on my car), if you want to lower your visor, the cord on the microphone can bend a little, which I guess could degrade the cord over time.  Therefore, if I want to lower the visor far down to block sun, I just remove the mic (mic is very easy to remove; it "clips" on and off easily).I guess you could also install to the right of the visor, like the Parrot Bluetooth systems do, but it's a bit farther from your mouth, and harder to install.  I've also seen it installed right behind the steering wheel on the "steering column", but heard more negatives about this location than positives, probably because it is farther from your mouth than the left of visor position.Can also see my review on the Sony MEXBT3900U car stereoSony MEXBT3900U In-Dash CD Receiver MP3/WMA/AAC Player with Bluetooth.

I purchased the KA-BAR 2-1245-1 Black Tanto and the Cold Steel Leatherneck Tanto at the same time to compare the two. Through Amazon Prime, the Cold Steel sold for $53.30 and the KA-BAR for $61.67, which I though was a tad overpriced, earning it only 4 stars instead of 5. Since I am not qualified to discuss the relative merits of the steel in both knives, I will stick to what I see.The KA-BAR Black Tanto is blacked out, knife and sheath, except for the sharpened edge on the blade. It has an 8" blade, the rear two inches of which are serrated. The spine measures 5/32" at the hilt, out two inches, where it presents a dull, false edge out to the tanto point. The hilt goes all the way through the handle and measures 13/32" deep and 5/32" wide where it exits the pinned, flat metal butt. The knife has a flat guard measuring 2.5" by 1/8". the handle is of man-made material and is oval, measuring 4 and 5/16" between the guard and butt. The grip surface is grooved at 3/4" intervals and has a rough surface that provides sufficient grip although not as good as the Cold Steel handle. The knife weighs 11.75 ounces without the sheath and measures 12 and 13/16" in length. The sheath appears to have been designed to fit several models of the KA-BAR knife, in that it is not molded to fit the tanto blade. It is made of molded and riveted plastic with integral nylon belt loop and two handle straps, one that snaps across the guard to the front of the hilt and the other snaps just below the butt. This design holds the knife in the sheath more securely than a single strap would but increases the time and effort to withdraw the knife. The sheath does not have MOLLE attachments but there are adequate openings along the edges to allow it to be either laced or strapped into web gear. The knife rattles somewhat in the sheath due in part to the fact that is was not molded specifically for this knife. The sheath has two large protrusions that retain the knife in the sheath once it is fully "snapped" in, and requires that the sheath be pushed back, away from the guard when withdrawing the knife. This design will probably outlast the smaller protrusions inside the Cold Steel sheath but may be a bit more clumsy to withdraw. The process of withdrawing the knife is somewhat stealthy but like the Cold Steel, the KA-BAR makes some noise as the blade comes out of the plastic sheath. The knife and sheath combined weigh 16.8 ounces and measure a long 14.75 inches. The KA-BAR also cut paper out of the box but the edge was just a bit courser than the Cold Steel blade, and will benefit from some medium then fine stone and strop work. As a battle knife, this is also a fine, well-made tool that should last a lifetime with proper care and use. On a personal note, I am 63 years old and I still carry my father's WWII Marine KA-BAR that is over 60 years old. Quality lasts...the knife, not me...The Cold Steel knife is blacked out, knife and sheath, except for the white lettering (which I thought was a tad ill conceived) on the blade, and along the bare, sharpened edge. It has a 7" blade with a spine that measures a full 3/16" thick all the way out to the beveled tanto point. The hilt goes all the way through the handle and measures 13/16" deep and 3/16" wide where it exits the pinned, flat steel butt. The knife has a slightly forward-curved guard measuring 2.5" by 1/8". The handle is of man-made material that is flattened and concave on the sides for a narrower profile than a rounded or oval grip, and measures 4 and 5/16" between the guard and butt. The grip surface is knobbed and ridged and provides a sufficient gripping surface when wet with blood, water, oil, ect. The knife weighs 12.25 ounces without the sheath and is 11.75" in length. The sheath is made for the tanto designed blade and is made of molded and riveted plastic with a screw-on attachment that appears to be able to be removed from the sheath, comprised of a nylon belt loop with hook and loop fastener as well as a snap, and knife handle nylon strap with snap. The sheath does not have MOLLE attachments but there are adequate openings along the edges to allow it to be either laced or strapped into web gear. The knife fits the sheath without rattling and two small raised areas in the sheath allow the knife to be "snapped" in and retained by the guard. Moderate thumb pressure on the sheath allows the knife to be withdrawn quickly and fairly silently although there is some noise created by the knife drawing out against plastic, so it's not 100% stealthy. The knife and sheath combined weight 16.15 ounces and measure 12 and 5/8 inches. The knife cuts paper out of the box but will benefit from additional work on a fine stone and strop. As a battle knife, this is an excellent, well made tool that should last a lifetime with proper care and use.I'm keeping both of these fine knives for my personal use...the KA-BAR in my bug-out bag and the Cold Steel a bit closer to hand. Neither of my son-in-laws will get their hands on them while I live becaues unfortunately, they would not know how to use or care for them. One final note on the matter of using either of these knives as survival gear or as camping tools. As survival knives, absolutely, use them and do what you have to do with them to keep yourself alive if the knife is the only edged tool you have at hand. If the edge and blade suffers as a result, buy yourself another one as a congratulatory gift when you return to civilization, having used one of these great knives to keep you alive. But, Come On Man...If you are going camping and need to chop something to pieces, buy yourself a hatchet for crying out loud! LOL

To evaluate the Sabrent docking station, I used a 3.5" Western Digital SATA internal hard drive (250GB, 7200 rpm) that I had retained from an old PC. Following the user guide, I plugged in the AC power converter and connected it to the docking station, connected the device to a USB 3.0 port on my laptop, inserted the hard drive, and pushed the power button. Within a few seconds, using Windows Explorer, I could see the hard drive as drive letter F, with all its directories and files.The docking station is a really useful device for looking at hard drives and copying files, and it couldn't be any simpler to use. From the time I opened the box until I was using it to see the files on the old hard drive took only a couple of minutes. I've heard people call devices like this a "hard drive toaster," which is an apt description, since you push a hard drive into a slot much as you drop bread into a toaster. And it's as simple to use as a toaster.Overall, I was very impressed with the usefulness and ease of operation of the docking station. You don't have to be a computer geek to operate it, since it's simple enough for anyone to use.A product sample was provided for review purposes.

Decent bag for the price, although the stitching is low quality.Pros:*Both compartments are a great size.*Larger compartment can fit two large pistols (eg. Sig P226) with 8 standard sized pistol magazines.*Smaller compartment can fit three boxes of ammo side by side (eg, the new large Winchester white box) or probably 300 reloads in plastic baggies.*Webbing for the handle is sewn completely around the bottom of the bag for strength.*Zippers permit small lock on both compartments, separately.*Zippers appear to be high quality.*Detachable shoulder strap also is included (in addition to sewn-in handle).*Two pistols, 8 mags, three boxes of ammo (front compartment), and that's it.  Not even room for a pack of Snackwell cookies.  For me, that's the perfect size, but I've read a lot comments saying that people want something bigger.Cons:*Material covering inside of larger compartment seems to be cheap plastic/nylon, similar to the type of plastic used for temporary ponchos.  It's loud and doesn't look like it will hold up over time.  I was surprised by the choice of materials.  It's sewn over another material that looks like soft cotton/flannel.*The thread used for sewing the exterior of the case seems thin and doesn't seem like it's the best (the thread weight seems light and the number of stitches per inch seems low?)  The stitching on the exterior compartment already is beginning to separate (after one day).*The exterior is 600 denier polyester, not ballistic nylon.  The material is softer and has a more cotton feel than ballistic nylon, which some will like.I also decided to buy the Galati bag that is almost identical to the US Peacekeeper (Galati Gear Deluxe Pistol Case), which I discovered uses better quality materials and better stitching.  In my experience the Galati's are far better constructed and my previous Galati lasted me 15 years of moderate use.  The US Peacekeeper will not, and I may return it.All in all, it's a decent range bag, not a tactical bag, and not worth more than a Hamilton plus a Lincoln.
