I've been using the IOGEAR GW3DHDKIT for about two months now and have mixed results on it.  I'm using it to connect my PS3 and my Xbox One to it then pass through to my TV it also transmits into my bedroom which is right behind the wall where my living room TV and the main box are.I've had some issues with the hardware and did two exchanges (which were very easy to do through Amazon) due to the IR port being flaky on the main transmitter and quality issues on the main TV that does pass through.Pros:- No quality issues on TV that is hooked up to the wireless receiver.  Zero lag or delay while gaming on it or watching movies.  However it is very close just separated by a wall.- Easy to setup- Wireless receiver can be powered by a mini usb cable (doesn't require a wall plug)- Plays nice with my Pioneer VSX822k receiver (some said they had issues)- It did work with my 3x1 HDMI switch allowing me to have the ability to run more than two devices over the wireless streamerCons- Issues with IR port on the transmitter.  I had to do two exchanges until I gave up.  Does not always take my command to change source and it isn't effected by distance it is inconsistent if its up close or away.- Quality issues on TV that uses pass through.  This is my biggest complaint from this unit and I almost it because of it and went with a different product.  While watching anything with a lot of action or playing games I would have a "line" of distortion run down my screen.  This would happen non stop.  I had to buy an HDMI splitter so I could play my Xbox One straight through the TV and not use pass through.  This is what I find unacceptable and an issue with the product not a defect since all three units did the same thing making pass through useless.  I could have bought a cheaper product that didn't offer this feature.  As a side note I did not have an issue on my secondary TV- Delaying in turning on.  This one I consider minor  but it does take 5 to 10 seconds to turn on and lock a signal.  This isn't a big deal but for me my TV also does a weird thing where it flashes back and forth on the input screen when its trying to connect which can lengthen the process out to about 30 seconds in my case.  Not a deal breaker for me but for some it might be.- Changing sources and power on/off can be weird when being used with my my Logitech Harmony remote.  This is because it's is setup to toggle between power on/off and source 1/2 which confuses the remote meaning a lot of times I'll be trying to turn on my system and the unit will be on then power off because I sent the command.- Would function better if it had an option for auto switchingMy Setup:Main TV- LG 50PV400 Plasma- Pioneer VSX 822k Receiver- Xbox One- Playstation 3Second TV- Insignia NS-42E440A13

I have been a long-time user of Iomega Zip drives...the internal type.  They have worked great and are convenient for quick access, transfer and back-up of daily computer files.  I have some 60 Zip disks filled with an assortment of important files.Since my internal Zip drive is on the same ribbon cable as my hard drive, and I wanted to add another hard drive to my computer, I had to give up the internal Zip drive (only two devices per ribbon cable).  Therefore, I purchased the external Iomega 250MB USB Zip drive.It is a plug-and-play device, so installation is instant.  I put in my first disk and presto...it came alive...with a clunk, a grind and an assortment of other strange noises...it started working.  First task...transfer about 24MB of info on to a disk.  I waited and waited and waited some more.  12 minutes later it was finished.  With the internal Zip drive this task would have taken about 12 seconds.  I could have gone out and washed the car during the time I waited.  My computer has USB2.0 but the device is only a USB1.1, so that might have something to do with it.Not only did this drive take forever to complete its task, it was uncomfortably noisy.  It makes all kinds of grinding noises, sometimes slowing down to a crawl then speeding up again.  My internal drive made a little noise, but nothing like this.  Forget about doing anything else on your computer while the Zip drive is chugging along.I cannot recommend this product at all.  It is slow and noisy and leaves me the general feeling of...well, being ripped off...because it is basically a cheap piece of junk!At one time Zip drives were the best way to archive files simply because there were no other convenient options available.  But, I think it is time to toss out all those old Zip disks and go to USB2.0 flash drives for easily accessible back-up and storage.  For the big, more permanent archiving I burn files to CD's or DVD's.  For daily back-ups and quick access, flash drives appear now to be the way to go.Iomega had better consider producing other storage products because if they keep making junk like this, they won't be in business much longer.

I owned a Polk PSW-10 sub amplified by a 50 watt amp and it was missing a LFE port (Low Frequency Effects.) Of course I knew all this when I purchased the PSW-10 and I gave that sub a 3 star review only after hearing the difference between it and this Polk 12" 300 watt powered sub.My Dad owns this PolkPSW505 12" 300 watt powered sub. So I barrowed it for a few weeks to see if it would meet my expectations? Well in the beginning I thought it was great. However after listening to music with it I was disappointed with it's performance.It has plenty of power and is built very well. It also has the (LFE) port and every other possible hookup you could need. It also sounded really good for movies. The reason it sounds better for movies is that you don't usually hear the rear slot port noise that this rear ported sub produces. The sound effects are usually so loud and often that you really don't hear the port noise during DTS or Dolby surround sound playback.However I use my sub for music as well and own some very high end Definitive Tech Studio Monitor bookshelf speakers( The 450 model )Which by the way are selling at a discounted price since they have been discontinued. Just a note that these are excellent speakers at a price point that you will never see in a speaker of this quality again.So the Polk 505 is worth buying at $180 on sale and would please most people. However it will not please the real music lovers out there that use the sub for listening to more than just movies!Now the fun part. After doing about a month of research on subs and listening to about 5 different models (POLK; Infinity; Kliptch; BIC; and a Definitive Pro 1000 that costs $500. Well I was just about to save up and buy the Definitive because it was by far the best sounding out of these subs and it was not even close!While saving for the Definitive I read a few reviews on this un known Subwoofer Brand called Dayton Audio! Well I thought it was way to cheap to actually be any good but one reviewer that I was exchanging comments with convinced me to give it a try! After all this guy owned a Polk505, a BIC F-12, and the Dayton 1200 12". In fact he had more than one of the Polk and the Dayton. So this guy really new his stuff when it came to subs.After exchanging about five comments with each other he convinced me to try the Dayton 12" that is priced at only $140 with shipping from Parts Express. It has a 45 day no questions asked free return shipping, money back return policy and I figured while I'm saving for the Definitive I'll buy this and test it out.Well all I can say is WOW! This Dayton sub is unreal with music and great with movies as well with a absolute minimum of port noise. It's a 120 watt R.M.S. real 12" driver and not a 7" like the Polk505 is. The Polk 505 has a 7" driver with a 5" surround. This Dayton sub has everything you could or would want in a sub as far as sound go's. I couldn't believe how great this sub is!I have been running it for a week and it's not even broken in yet and it sounds fantastic and it really gets down to 25 H.Z. without getting muddy! This Dayton sub looks just like the Polk125 model without the steel mesh covering the speaker grill. Plus it's about 10 L.B.s heavier than the Polk and get this it costs $220 less than this newer Polk model and has the same quality downward firing port for less port noise!The Polk 505 is priced at $230 for a reason. It's a very old subwoofer design with it's silver painted front panel and the rear slotted port. Rear ports made it really difficult to put your sub in a corner (Where they always sound the best) or against a wall.This is why all the new subs are using either a downward firing 12" driver and a side port, or a front 12" driver with a downward firing port. If you pick the Dayton you will not be sorry it really does rock and play low fast clean deep bass!A Note: The Dayton subs only flaw is that if you are setting it on thick carpet you will need to get a piece of 1" plywood and size it a little larger than the sub. This will allow that bottom port to really boost the overall bass response. Also it does not have high quality speaker wire binding posts like the Polk 505. So if your wiring the sub go with the Polk 505. Otherwise try the Dayton 120 12" from Amazon or Parts Express!I was just waiting for this Dayton sub to disappoint me and it just won't! So now I'm spreading the word about this great sub. It looks almost exactly like the Polk125 12" but it has a round speaker cover and it's all black and not with cheap paint like the BIC-F12 has. It's veneered with solid black look without any seams so it looks as nice as it sounds. It's also double boxed for shipping with high quality foam on the top an the bottom!The Dayton is a real winner! The Polk 505, well it's o.k. for movies and has a better warranty as well. But as far as I'm concerned I don't need that Definitive sub anymore! That's how good this Dayton 120 12" sub really is complete with a working (LFE) port that will shake you house to the foundation with clean, tight bass exactly as it should be. Hope You try it out. S. Level

I bought these to replace a smaller, cheaper set of saddlebag panniers I got from another site that started to fall apart after a couple uses.  I'll start off by saying regardless of price, they are great panniers.  Toss in the fact I think they're very reasonably priced and they're a no-brainer.  Very roomy; I use them to commute and they're more than enough to carry a day's clothes, lunch, shoes, and plenty of room left over (I have a separate belt/hip pack for my U-lock and bike accessories like tire levers/tubes/lights/multi-tool, but these would easily fit with room to spare, as well).  They attach pretty easily in my opinion; I put them on and take them off twice a day and it takes as little as 30 to no more than 60 seconds to get them on and tight.  And I like the saddlebag design with carrying straps; again I use them to commute and they're very easily to throw on then take off at work and carry in.My concerns were reports of things getting caught in spokes and my heels hitting the bags.  After several rides I can say I have no idea how people get stuff caught in the spokes, can only figure that's operator error.  And thankfully my heels easily clear the bags with no contact at all, which even a little can become a nuisance.  I wear a size 45 Shimano shoe (US men's 11 generally), and this particular bike has a 175mm crankset, so depending on your variables could possibly be some rubbing, but I have a bit of clearance so I don't see it being a problem unless you have huge feet and/or a much larger crank.The only possible knock I can think of is they rattle a bit.  But, metal-on-metal is going to do that.  Trade off with plastic you lose the noise but it's not as durable.  Personally I can put up with a little rattle on bumps to know the hooks aren't going to break.  And if need be, something could be rigged up to stop the rattle, just not worth the hassle to me.  Very highly recommend them tho.

All hair care products affect hair different based on hair type and the mix of products that you use on your hair. I'm 49 (older, duller hair) and my hair is very long (mid-back length), thick, soft, and neither dry nor oily. It has not been color treated and I don't have any salon treatments. I do regulalry rotate my shampoos and conditioners so that I don't use a single product all of the time (which can create build-ups or color changes).With that out of the way, I will say that I had a very good experience with this shampoo. I have very hard water, but this lathered up beautifully. In fact, I think I need to use less shampoo next time as it was more than I needed. It left my hair feeling very clean (I can tell by how hard it is to run my hands along wet hair pre-conditioning if all of the previous product has been stripped out) and left my scalp very clean as well (again, I can tell this by feeling the hair near my scalp at the very back of my head which is prone to feeling oily or coarse if it isn't thoroughly clean).I also really liked the scent of this. It reminded me of Irish Spring soap (the not as intense) in its chlorophyll-like notes. Given that this is a relatively economical choice, I think it represents a good quality product at a good price.

Although I have only been using this product for a few weeks, I have noticed a difference in my skin. The serum seems to have softened the appearance of some of my lines and wrinkles, especially on my cheeks. I am impressed with the generous size of the bottle, and, as a little goes a long way, I expect that it will last for quite a while. Prior to applying the serum, I wash my face thoroughly, let my skin dry, and then apply a small amount of the product to my face, especially on my cheeks and around my mouth. After it dries, I apply my usual moisturizer. If I use it during the day, I use it with a face cream with at least a 15 spf. One reviewer said it was not for skin that broke out or became red from products -- I have found it to be totally non-irritating, and I sometimes react badly to skin products that are "too strong." The only thing I can add in that regard is that if you have had issues in the past you may want to use less of the product to start, and contact your doctor if you have an adverse reaction. But I have experienced nothing but good results. The only thing that might keep me from using the serum again (after I run out of this bottle) would be the price.UPDATE: I have been using this product 2-3 times a week since I wrote my review so that it will last longer. There has been an improvement in my skin as noted in the original review, but there has also not been a total transformation. The only thing that can result in a total transformation is expensive surgery or an expensive, temporary injectable. Due to genetics and long exposure to the sun as a child and a teen, my skin weathered prematurely, and the best I can hope for in the long run is to soften the appearance of the damage. This product, combined with a sunblocking face cream, is a good choice. And it is still non-irratating on my skin after several months.

As a former pro getting back into photography I wish I hadn't gotten rid of a lot of my equipment and accessories that would still be viable today -- especially my favorite camera bag, that seemed to hold twice as much equipment as its size indicated. A colleague suggested Thank Tank products, so I took a look at their web site and immediately liked the look of the Retrospective series. Of course, the problem with buying a camera bag you haven't actually seen is that it's hard to judge whether or not it's really going to hold the equipment you want it to hold. I wanted something that could handle everything I'm taking with me to Kauai--so that I can carry all my equipment on the plane, then rearrange things on the other end and carry what I need for each day's shoot. I wavered between the model 10 and wider model 20. I dismissed the 30 as too tall--stuff would just get buried in the bottom. I finally took a risk on the smaller model 10.  From the moment it arrived there was no question I had made the right choice of manufacturer and series. Then I laid out all my equipment and started moving and adjusting the dividers, packing and unpacking, rearranging--for two hours! I was about to give up and exchange the 10 for the 20, but after giving my brain a 10-minute break, I came back to it and everything just fell into place. The bag now holds both Canon 60D and XS camera bodies (with lenses removed so the TSA won't fiddle with them), 18-55 and 55-250 S-series lenses, a 50mm 1.8, a chunky 10-20mm including saucer-sized hood, hoods for the other three lenses (stacked, not reversed), a Rode Pro microphone, a CarrySpeed viewfinder in its case, a 430EX flash, two rain sleeves, six connecting cords, a six-filter wallet, four battery packs with two chargers (airline regs say those will have to be moved to the checked luggage), two camera straps, and the rain cover that came with the bag. All snug but well padded. I've had one job since the initial pack, and was able to remember where everything goes--in fact, it went together better the second time. So the bag ended up being perfect for my needs. HOWEVER...If you are not patient enough to go through what I went through; if you don't like everything fitting snugly; or if you don't like having to bury less used items in the bottom of your bag; this bag will likely be too small for you. As I said, when I take it into the field I will carry about half of what will carry on the plane. The only flaw I can see in the design (not enough to deduct a star) is that accessory pockets on the front, back, and sides can't help but take away from the interior space if you put anything at all of size in them. The two end pockets are so flat as to be useless for anything other than sunglasses or a small notepad. (Interestingly, they are lined with waterproof material as though they were intended for water bottles--that would only work if the bottles were less than 1-inch thick.  But, bottom line--I love this bag and I expect that it will be my bag of choice for many years to come. After all, it's guaranteed for as long as the original owner uses it.

Decent quality, good deal for the price. I was very satisfied with them for my use as a part time enthusiast that does the occasional gigs ever few weeks.  I wouldn't get them for daily use, but any pro should know better.I'm amazed at how many people complained that sand leaked out.  The construction is decent enough that you could toss sand in it when out on location and in a pinch, but if you're on location and there's sand, you're probably not worried about some leaky sand.  I'm sure plenty of photographers are using these on less sandy shoots and just leave them full.  Just put the sand in quart sized ziplocks.  I wrapped my ziplock in duct tape, just to make sure. I didn't stuff mine full, and they weigh about 8-10 lbs each.Several people mentioned using rocks over sand.  I found it amusing they also mentioned they were also complaining that they got holes in the material.  Hard pointy filler + drag sandbag across hard flat surface = holes.

I have taken this jacket out twice in the last week.  The first ride was 20 miles in 43-48 degree temperatures and about 20-30mph winds.  It was not a fun ride due to the wind, but the jacket did a wonderful job of keeping the wind off of me. All I had was a summer jersey with light arm-warmers underneath, and I was plenty warm.  The second ride was 36 miles in temperatures ranging from 37-40 degrees or so.  I stuck with a lighter jersey and arm warmers, and the jacket performed flawlessly.  I would not hesitate to recommend this jersey. The only improvements that I can see are the zipper in the front and the pocket in the back.  I am hoping that the zipper will last a few years of heavy use, and storing and accessing items in the back pocket can get a little annoying with just a flap and Velcro.  I'm guessing PI made these choices in order to keep the jacket lightweight and packable, so I don't really mind them too much. I am 5'8 170lbs and the medium fits perfectly.  I have uploaded a picture of the blue jacket so that the fit can be seen.  Hopefully it helps in selecting this jacket!

I purchased this 1.5" belt to supplement my 1.75" BDU belts.  I was using a 1.25" military brass buckle belt for dressier casual wear but found the narrow width too uncomfortable, especially if attaching gadget pouches.  Not wishing to use a 1.75" belt on Dockers, I opted for this belt instead.The belt is well made and the material weave is quite good.  There is adequate stiffness, not too stiff nor too soft.  The width of the belt is just enough for comfortable retention with the capability of supporting some weight from attachments without looking like a uniform/BDU/warehouse belt.  The buckle is synthetic and appears quite durable by way of being massive.  This detracts from the belt somewhat as it bulges noticeably.  I would have preferred a flatter profile.I purchased a larger size to be sure of fit as it is no trouble to shorten nylon to my individual specs.I prefer nylon belts and have exclusively used them for 20 years.  I have yet to wear one out.  My jeans BDU belt still looks original after 20 years of casual wear and camping. I have a spare but I have yet to wear it.The difference in comfort was so pronounced that I got rid of all my leather belts and will never buy or wear one again.  I had some intermittent back problems/pain when I wore leather belts but since I switched to nylon, I've never had any pains since.As an added bonus, nylon is cheaper and more durable.  I've worn out leather belts even with consistent leather care.  With a nylon belt I just bundle it in a t-shirt and toss it in the washer now and then.Except for the large profile buckle, this belt is exactly what I was looking for.

This curling iron does heat up fast.  I haven't used one for a while; I've used straightening irons for some time just to build the look of body in my fine hair.  I remember when curling irons had to sit and preheat like an oven.  This is much better.I confess I don't get dramatic results, but that is largely due to my lack of skill and aforementioned fine hair.  But I do get a bit of wave that holds all day, as long as I don't mess with my hair until it's cool.This curling iron has a decent length cord, and all attributes for a curling iron are as expected.  Forget the rose gold bit.  That's marketing.  It has adjustable heat setting.  This is the one feature I'd like to see improved.  It's an old-fashioned analog dial that goes from low to high.  I'd prefer being able to set the temperature, even if it is from a limited number of presets.  I know I don't want to use 400 degrees on my hair, but what temperature is low?  I can tell you it's still pretty hot to the touch, but have no idea what the actual temperature is.This is a good choice for a curling iron.  It will work as well as any expensive curling iron with the same features.

It is quick and effortless and I dont have to worry about uneven lines around my chin to help improve my overall skin tone. It comes in various shade groups and hasnt let me down thus far! My only complaint is the lavender smell that overwhelms my senses as i apply it. Im not a fan of the lavender scent in general so this is a huge turn off. however, I only notice it for the first few minutes after using it and then it seems to dissapate. i havent had any complaints from my boyfriend or my baby so Im sticking with it!This product is intended and perfect for women who dont wear an abundance of makeup and just want a product that hydrates, nourishes, and evens out your complexion. I love that it doesnt look like im wearing makeup, so no matter what my day brings Im not checking myself in the mirror to ensure my face is in place. It is all natural and hypo allergenic unlike other popular drugstore foundations that leave more belmishes than they cover. I would highly recommend this product!

I have been looking for the all around Mtn bike tire for all my needs for a while--I think this is it.My number one objective was a tire for winter commuting for all road conditions. Although this is not quite as grippy on deep snow as my off road nobibies, it is close enough.  The biggest benefit is on bare pavement. The rolling resistance is much lower than off road nobbies, so much so that I don't mind a road ride, something I hated with nobbies on a commute. I bought the double defense so I ride over glass and sharp rocks with no flats,  some that always happened with off rd tires.Second objective is off road touring on converted rail road beds. These have a load rating of 240 lbs with the double defense version with great puncture protection even loaded. I would get flats on crushed basalt on a loaded tour on the iron horse trail--no flats in about 100 miles. These also roll really fast on hard pack gravel then off road tires.Off road. These are really fast on hard pack single tract. Soft  mud and snow and sand, they are acceptable and will get you by. If the road is hard pack you will go real fast. I took these on a climb on Badger Mountain, 800 foot climb in 3 miles with gravel hard pack and switch backs. Climbing is ok at about 660 grams for the 2.25 double defense. The performance line is 100 grams lighter but not as puncture proof.  The remarkable ride is the down hill. These roll so fast I wast stunned at how much harder I need to break in the switch backs.  Also no flats on the goat heads.One caution. When cornering on pavement, the sides of the tire nobs are much softer than the center and the handling changes noticeably be aware the grip is real sticky.Overall these are great. Expensive, but you do not need to change you tires anymore for different rides. Only place to get the double defense at the best price is in England with free shipping.[...]

I bought this card because the Gigabyte/AMD mobo, apu and memory bundle advertisement stated you could go crossfire mode with ANY (without an * ) AMD 6xxx series graphics card.  installing the HD6670 and not being able to get into crossfire, I found out the A4 apu can ONLY crossfire with the 6450 cards so I bought one to see what the difference would be.  after installing it and getting into crossfire I was severely disappointed in the performance of my machine.  the windows experience dropped from the 5.8 it had been using the HD 6670 as a stand alone card down to 3.8 with the 6450 crossfired with the mobo's integrated graphics.  needless to say, the 6450 went back into the packaging it came in and will be saved as a spare for someone else's computer.for a cheap card and in my cheap gaming rig (only spent about $200USD), I am more than impressed.  I am able to play the online MMO's I could no longer play on my intel rig which i built in 2001 with the P4 3.06 HT cpu and it could no longer process todays instructions.I would give this card a five star rating just because it pairs well with the Gigabyte MoBo and AMD apu combo BUT... to crossfire,  the ad said ANY 6 Series card and this wasn't true.  is not the card's fault but either Gigabyte, AMD or Amazon.  it worked out well for me (other than buying the 6450 card after the fact) because I might have sworn off Gigabyte and AMD forever had I tried the 6450 first.  maybe the advertisements need to be a little bit more clearer when issues like this are possible?

Don't know if someone covered laptops, so I thought I would save the next person the trouble. It has three 1/8" computer audio cords that are color coded [green, orange, black] These three colors cover the rear & sub, the front speakers and the center. They are made for desktop computers with installed sound cards.  Well guess what?!?!  Most laptops don't have a nice sound card that has three 1/8" computer sound plugs. You only get the little green audio input.I bought the "Logitech 5.1 Game Console Adapter" which is only like $7 on ebay. It changes [green, orange, black] to regular stereo plugs. Then you need an adapter I already have, which is just stereo plugs (red/white) that adapts to one green input (1/8" computer green audio input). Then you need a single green to green cord and a regular stereo cord (red/white to red/white). HAHAHAHA ya I know, pretty stupid that you have to do this right? It's amazing that they don't have something for $5-$10 that converts [green, orange, black] to [one green 1/8" computer audio cord].Cross my fingers that it works because I'm using these speakers for Lake Powell houseboat in 1 week!! (I'll update later if it has problems) I didn't know I would have to buy these and then another pricey adapter, I thought logitech was master of connectivity!?! Anyways, I just need to duplicate the sound on all 5 surround speakers (Stereo sound out of 5+1 speakers). Your other option is something nicer like: $32 "StarTech.com 7.1 USB Audio Adapter External Sound Card" here on Amazon, which is what I'll get next if this one doesn't work, but it better!! If you have the time and money, definitely buy the Startech adapter, totally worth not hassling with cords.  I just already had the other cords is all and I'm being cheap because I need to save my moola for Lake Powell. I have 5.1 surround sound coming out of my ears, I don't need it on my laptop too. Just lots of sound for THE PARTY!!!!!

this bottle is unique in the realm of reusable drink bottles due to the fact that the spout from which one drinks has a screw-top lid that can be flipped away (once unscrewed).  The incorporation of this feature is likely due to the design intent for the bottle, namely as a container for mixing smoothies and such with ice, thus a screw top provides more secure lid retention than a press-and-flip lid.  Another feature is the "mixing mechanism," which is a hard, plastic, fanned ball that looks somewhat like an asterisk, included to facilitate, I suppose, better mixing of thicker beverages.  I typically leave it out when mixing powders (e.g. lemonade, Gatorade) with water so I have one less thing to wash . . . and everything mixes up fine.All in all, this bottle is great for water and other drinks, whether they're being mixed or not.  Construction and design are solid and attractive.  Includes a hoop you can hook a finger into for carrying.

I ordered this to go on a Remington 700 that I had acquired and wanted to go with a more solid swivel mount over the barrel clamp style I have been using for all my rifles. (Universal was nice, but it can only take you so far, had to upgrade for my bolt action) This fits super easy if you already have a swivel stud installed. It took all of about 30 seconds to install once my dumb brain figured out the clamp had a protective plastic cover over the bits that hold the swivel. I have had this on the rifle for a while and shot with it and handled it in several situations including shooting and moving and it holds up well. It hasn't shaken itself loose at all and the legs snap easily into place and are easy to get back up when you want to move. They also extent to a full length at the click of a button and if you want to hold them at a shorter than max extension length they will hold in place at several adjustments. The swivel works well for uneven terrain and you won't even notice it unless you are looking for it. Your body will naturally align the scope to be right with the target but the swivel isn't so loose that it will fall over on you if you don't hold onto it. Basically there are no drawbacks to this and there is no reason to spend money on anything else more expensive. I plan to grab a few more for other rifles I have. This size is perfect for almost any real world hunting or competition shooting position you can think of.

I am a huge fan of the Venus razors and frankly didn't really see how they could improve upon the version I was using.  So it came as some surprise that this latest generation really did up the ante a bit from a performance standpoint.  Not only do you get a really close shave, but so far I haven't so much as gotten a nick.  I've used the same razor blade several times and am still getting a great shave.  While I haven't attempted to use the razor without shaving cream, as the claims say you can (I have dry/sensitive skin that I feared would not react well), I will say that I think the skin of my legs gets less dry with this razor and I almost didn't need body lotion afterward.The razor itself is a girly pink that is visually appealing - and the handle has a soft grip.  The razor comes with a case that I believe is intended to adhere to the wall of your shower, with storage for razor blades, which come individually packaged.  I don't want to store my razor in the shower, so I can't attest to how that works - but the concept is a nice one.The only downside - and one that isn't specific to this Venus Spa Breeze - is the ever increasing and now nearly exorbitant cost of razor blades.  If only you could use the blade to shave the price!  That said, this product is so good that if you're trying to select between comparably priced razors/blades from competitors, I strongly recommend you try this one - I think you'll be pleased in every way.

I work in a clinic and walked by a patient one day who happened to be wearing this gorgeous, warm, sweet and rich-smelling perfume. I asked what she had on and she told me "Wish"...I became obsessed and knew I had to have it!! I'm a little nervous buying it without having tried it on myself first, so I hope it smells just as good on me as it did on her. I can't wait to receive my order!! Bottle looks beautiful too. Hopefully there won't be any issues with product color/age like was mentioned in a few earlier reviews...the color of the perfume in the amazon picture looks very old! :-(UPDATE: I got my perfume last night. Absolutely no problem at all with color/age/packaging - box was wrapped and perfume is a gorgeous blue color. The bottle is the most beautiful one I've ever owned! The scent is very...transformative. I sprayed a tiny bit into the box at first and it smelled very much like Thierry Mugler's Angel, which I am not a huge fan of. Then I sprayed it on my wrists, and I thought it smelled like cheap soap. Then a bit later, it morphed into the gorgeous, sweet, delicious smell I must have smelled on our patient, and I was soooooo excited and could not stop smelling myself. Unfortunately, that smell changed again a few hours later and I'm left with a dry-down that smells a bit like something my grandma used to wear. :(  I love my grandma to pieces, but I don't want to smell like her. I asked 3 friends for their opinions today; one said "It smells classic, not old lady" and the other two proffered "old lady" without me even asking leading questions. So, I'm not quite sure about it. I think I'll wear it this weekend and see what I think. I will probably keep it though, just because the bottle is so gorgeous and I want to be able to smell the lovely "phase 2" scent. ;)

Update:I tried an offset scope mount with a Nikon P22 2-7x32 scope and this mount (UTG Integral QD Offset Mount with 2 Picatinny Slot, 1-Inch, Black) on my Ruger 10/22 Takedown and the stock *does* fit into the large pocket of the factory case without removing the scope. It is a very *tight* fit but it does fit without removing the scope. I used this picatinny rail (UTG .22 Tactical Low Profile Rail Mount (#22)). I used blue Locktite and will see how it holds up. The offset mount above should work fine with this scope because it is very nearly the same size as the P22.Second Update:Previously I had incorrectly stated the following about the picatinny rail: "..but I cannot recommend it because only 2 of the 4 screw holes line up with the 4 holes in the top of the receiver."That was incorrect. As another reviewer pointed out in the comments, the spacing of the mounting holes on the 10/22 receiver is not symmetrical. I reversed the mount and all 4 screw holes lined up and the rail is solidly mounted with all 4 screws. I apologize for the misinformation.------------------------I got this scope for a Ruger 10/22 Takedown and have used it several times at ranges from 15 yds to 100 yds.The good:* Simple to use* Gives sharp, clear sight picture* Reasonably pricedThe bad:* Nothing bad to say about this scopeOther comments:* With the combination of the above rail and this mount (UTG Max Strength QD Lever Lock Integral Picatinny Mounting Systems (1-Inch)), I have to remove the scope before putting the gun in its factory case but the mount does a good job of returning to zero when I reassemble the gun.* This is a great .22 scope value for 50 yd shooting and good for 75 yd shots, but you had better have really good eyesight and high-velocity ammo for 100+ yd shots. I do not consider this to be a negative.Bottom line is this is a very nice value. It's simple to use, gives a sharp sight picture, and price is very reasonable.I give it 4 stars only because they don't give the option for 4-1/2. Would be 5 stars if the price made it an absolute steal.

Update 8/7/2011 - I am still pleased with lens.  When I get focus right it is by far the sharpest lens I own (granted I don't have any pro lenses).  I wanted to update this to make it clear to Nikon shooters that EXCEPT FOR MANUAL FOCUS This lens works PERFECTLY in P, A, S, and M modes.  In these modes my D5000 WORKS JUST LIKE IT DOES WITH MY NIKON LENS.  THE CAMERA ADJUSTS (DEPENDING ON THE MODE OF COURSE) APERTURE, SHUTTER SPEED, AND ISO AUTOMATICALLY BASED ON AVAILABLE LIGHT ETC.  YOU DO NOT HAVE TO ADJUST THE APERTURE MANUALLY AS THE SPECIFICATION ON THE AMAZON PAGE (OR ROKINON WEBSITE FOR THAT MATTER) IMPLIES. The only thing I have noticed so far that doesn't work is 3D focus (probably dynamic area either) but, after thinking about it that doesn't surprise me.  If you set for spot focus and turn on the rangefinder the camera tells you when it's in focus just like with Nikon lenses.Ok, I only received this lens today and have played with it for an hour or so.  So far it is living up to all the reviews.  If you're reading this review you probably already have an idea of how to use this type of lens but, just in case I bought it specifically for wide open short depth of field images.  Portraits, flowers etc. anything that isn't moving to fast for manual focus where you want to play with depth of field. This chipped version seems to meter and focus on my D5000.  I get a focus confirmation light and speed adjusts with aperture.  Aperture adjusts with thumbwheel in A-mode.  Depth of field (or lack of) is very fun at f/1.4.  Have taken some very neat pictures handheld wide open.  Background disappears to a blur in the right ranges.  Appears very sharp.  It is difficult to focus well on small objects handheld but, with practice I think I can improve with technique.  Right now my initial impression is WOW!  I'll update if anything changes.p.s. I am just an amateur so, take this review in that context.  5 stars because it is exactly what is advertised.  Manual focus, sharp, chipped for EXIF data, etc.  Quality appears very good. All for a very reasonable price.

I tried this one while hiding out from the world in Crisfield, Maryland for the Christmas-New Year holiday season with my relatives.  I like it a lot!  I am an "Olay" fan anyway, but have also used some other products.  I heard on the TV that the Olay Regenerist line was "the best" or something like that, so here is my story:It has a nice creamy texture.  It goes on your face smooth and it just really feels good going on.  It does have a semi-heavy scent blast that hits you when you put the first smear on, but it is not overwhelming and it seems to disipate quickly.  My preference would be to have it without the scent.  The cream just feels really good going on your face, kind of like putting your face on a cool satin pillow.  The cream soaks in really nice too.  It does not feel like it's going to rub off on the pillow when you hit the bed 5 minutes later like some other creams do.  It also does not feel sticky like some creams do.  After 4 days use on my face and neck, morning and evening, my face and neck feel really nice and soft (silky soft).  I would think (based on my initial results) that this cream would have really nice results when used over an extended period of time.I believe this cream holds its own against the other face products that I use, which include the Victoria Principal line, the Olay line, and the Seacret line (in the malls, from the Dead Sea). (I switch around between the products.)The directions are really hard to read on this 1.7 oz red jar but they say the usual.  They tell you to apply in small circular motions and avoid getting it into your eyes and to rinse with water if you do. (Duh.  A no-brainer.) It also suggests that you focus on areas like the brow and jaw line.  Olay is a Proctor and Gamble product.I think it is also important to tell you that I am NOT one of those 25 year olds who has young skin anyway and is trying to tell you how beautiful my skin is. LOL.  No offense, but the young whippersnappers haven't had time to mess up their skin yet!  I am 60 years old and am very pleased with my skin.  It is equally important to tell you that I did nothing special to my skin until about 4 years ago when I began a regular face cream routine.  Those 4 years have paid off big time, so if you think it is too late or you are to old to bother, that is probably not true.  I have had plenty of compliments on my skin.With all that said, I recommend a good quality face cream, before bed and upon waking.  This (and the other Olay products) seem to be worth your time and money.  When you do your face, include your neck too.  It shows.  Rub it on in small circles, like they suggest, and rub up (not down). Ohhhh, might I also suggest no frowning.  Only laugh lines are allowed :-)

Bought my first Easy Pro in 2008. Our second was added a few months later.We need to polish a fair number of discs - about 250 a month.Here is why I have two: Due to the friction between the buffing pad and the disc, there is a fair amount of heat generated. In my experience, you cannot buff disc after disc without some "cool down" period between. What needs to cool down? The steel plate that the disc mounts against and the buffing pad. The plate, when hot, heats up subsequent discs and in turn the pad becomes warm. Because the primary buffing pads are made out of a foam material, they become fragile when hot.So, to keep our volume up, I bought a second machine. That way we could alternate between the two machines, giving each machine a cool down time before the next disc. (most of the discs we buff are books on cd, and there can be up to 20 discs in a set).We bought the second machine about 4 to 6 months after the first. In that time, the lid/mount assembly appears to have been updated/upgraded by the manufacturer resulting in a better end result and less "oscillating" of the disc. There is enough difference in the machines that we no longer use the older machine, only the new version.Having buffed several thousand CDs and DVDs with the Easy Pro, there are a few things we have learned for best results (IMHO):- go easy on the buffing compounds. When the pad is new, apply a light coating over the entire face of the pad. Only re-apply more compound after you have buffed 5-10 discs. Then apply another very light coating.- before using the "sandpaper" discs, try using the two basic steps: compounds 1 & 2. We only use the sandpaper discs as a "nuke option".- we always use two steps: 2 minutes each, pad with #1 and pad with #2.- we use cloth towels to carefully wipe the discs after buffing. Be especially careful to wipe then inspect the outer edge of each finished disc to insure there is no remnants of the buffing compound.- if you buff more than 100 discs a month, buy the 12 oz. bottles of compound. It is much less expensive per oz.- always keep a spare pad on hand. We had one pad come to pieces (I do not remember the circumstances, but I do remember it was my fault). We did not have any spares on hand and had to wait several days for a replacement to arrive. )These aren't the kind of consumables that you can just pick up at your local office supply store.)- If the label side of your disc is damaged (scratch through label exposing disc), all the buffing in the world won't fix it. This is a little know fact, but the label plays a roll in reflecting back the laser (coming from the other side). If there is a defect in the label, the laser light just goes straight through!For what you get (a commercial quality machine - it even has a steel case), the machine is a steal. I think it is a little bit like razors/razor blades. In this case, the machine is very low cost - but they will make it up on the supplies.Bottom line is I would buy it again in a heart beat. Could not be happier.

This knife is an unbelievable deal! I am not a "bushcrafter," I just wanted a multi-purpose knife to carry in the woods that would prove useful in any situation, including survival. I am no expert, but I believe this knife fits the bill. I bought it after watching David's review on YouTube, and he was right on the money! It has a thick, full tang blade and a comfortable handle. This knife is built for a beating. I wanted a "bushcraft/survival " knife that would chop, baton, pry, crush, and oh yeah...cut stuff. I also wanted it to be big enough for "real work," but didn't want it to be machete-sized (like the "survival knives" made popular in the movies) I did not want to spend triple digit money for a knife that all the "experts" love. Is the Schrade made out of the best steel available? I don't know, but it is so thick and tough, I can't imagine ever breaking it. If I had to mention things I didn't like, there would be a few: I'm not a fan of the black coating. It seems to me to be similar to thick paint, and will probably add drag when cutting through wood and such...but that's an easy fix. I would have preferred it to have the same "antique" finish as my SCHF13 & 14. Secondly, while mine came with a serviceable edge (fairly "keen"), it is not what I consider acceptably sharp. Again, an easy fix. What's not so easy is the sheath...it is pretty terrible (and the only reason for the Four Star Rating vs. Five!). Sure, it holds the knife securely, but has Velcro (which I despise) instead of a snap to secure the grip. It has no accommodation for molle systems, and is not capable of being worn truly ambidextrously (blade only goes in one way). Lastly, it rides low on the belt. I typically prefer a sheath that rides higher, as I don't plan to have to clear body armor when wearing this knife (tho, you never know...). If Schrade improves the sheath that comes with this knife, they would have a five star winner every time, I think! In closing: this knife is great, the sheath is crap, and the price...that's just unbeatable!

These Peltor Earmuffs are lightweight and comfortable considering the level of protection they give.If you're not familiar with Peltor, it is a big and respected name in safety products for head, face and hearing.  They call this type of protector "earmuffs."  Their earmuffs have graduated levels of protection starting at 20 dB and going all the way to 106 dBA (decibels adjusted).My first impression holding these earmuffs was that they were too small and lightweight to be sufficiently protective.  However, I was wrong about that.  This beats using the larger and heavier brands, and something larger in a Peltor would risk overprotecting.  Overprotecting can occur when you can't hear signals and sounds that you need to hear, and overprotecting can lead to not using earmuffs when you should.If you use this hearing protector a lot, you can get replacement inside foam pads.  Peltor calls these replacements "hygiene kits."Peltor also makes earplugs.  I used to use them in the artillery (Army), and like earmuffs they can be fatiguing to wear. Not only that, but I don't like jamming typical earplugs into the ear canal because it's sometimes necessary to really jam them in there to make them stay. The big thing Peltor did that helped make them famous in the earplug arena is develop their "skull screws."Peltor's Skull Screws are really famous.  I use them also and like them more than other earplugs.  They are shaped like conical screw grips so you can twist them into your ear canal until they seal (and thus stay).  Many of us (like me) have different size ear canals, so we'd have to literally buy two sets of different sizes just to get a set that fits both ears, and this screw grip solves that.Peltor's screwgrip plugs are rated for 30 dB (25 on Amazon, however).  But here's the kicker on the screw grip earplugs - on Amazon they are more expensive!  By a lot!  We're talking about something very innovative but having next to no weight costing more than these earmuffs here on Amazon.  Take a look:Peltor P1300 Skull Screw Earplugs, Gray, 120-Pair.

I just received my two new soft boxes and found the assembly extremely simple. If you've ever worked with lighting before, it will be very clear to you. BUT, for those who haven't, the ring is held in by the tension of soft box itself when it 'pops' into place. Merely slide the ring into the back side of the soft box and you've got it. From there, you have ultimate control over the positioning of the flash within the opening. To get the 'triangle' [soft box collapsed] back into the bag, simply pull one of the corners toward the center of the other two. It will begin rolling itself into a nice tight ring that will easily fit back into the box. Hope this helps those who have posted questions.

This lens' maximum aperture of f/12 makes 3D pictures impossible to take in all of the brightest of summer afternoons. At least I can clean up grainy pictures to an extent with Lightroom.It's not just camera shake. It's also the slow shutter speeds dictated by the f/12 aperture. Even if the subject is moving the slightest bit, the picture will come out blurred.Conclusion: If your taking 3D pictures of stationary objects, then your fine if you use a tripod. If you want to take pictures of your children in 3D, thinking it would be cool to view later, then forget this lens. For the price I expect much better pictures. I'm going to stick with my 14-140mm kit lens.

As an "avid adventurer" I am always looking for more foolproof ways to start a warming campfire in the outdoors should the weather turn cold or rotten. I have owned them all--from the old-fashioned "firesteel", waterproof matches (never waterproof), and disposable butane lighters to the more modern magnesium/steel starters. This one works better than anything I've tried, and it's beautifully made. Here's what you do for a foolproof fire source: take some cotton balls (100% cotton only), smear some vaseline on them and stash them in a film cannister or old prescription bottle. When you're ready to start a fire, gather some sticks, twigs and larger dry wood, pull a cotton ball out, scratch the magnesium stick toward it as instructed, and that's all she wrote. The flame burns long enough to get a good blaze going. Might save your life, one day.

This cooler is almost-perfect for the single camper. Even when filled with ice and food it doesn't require two people to carry and lift it into the car. It will easily hold a couple days worth of food and ice without needing ice replacement. Short on cargo space? This cooler is compact and easy to pack.I recommend using sealed ice containers rather than real ice. The cooler lacks a drainage spout. When the ice melts, the food inside gets saturated with water, so pack things carefully. Keep things like bacon on top where the water won't get to it. Stack soda cans in the bottom.On the plus side, having no drainage spout means there no chance of springing a leak around a spout's seal.I've been using this same cooler for over a decade. The lid still creates a air-tight seal. The carrying handle hasn't loosened over the years. It's rugged, solid and it still keeps things ice cold. I even use this thing as a foot stool at the campfire. The warmth of the fire doesn't affect the cooling capacity, nor has it shown any signs of melting on the outside. I like that I can move it from here to there without assistance.

Only if everything was this easy :)I happen to be pretty good with 'computers' but I've never done DSL before so I was a little apprehensive about setting this up. It turns out... there were no reasons to worry. The Actiontec is as close to 'plug it in and you're done' as they come, at least for when we're talking about home networks. The hardest part is entering the very long initial password required for the initial set up. Once you're 'in' and you change it to something easier to type, all is quick, fun and easy.The modem/router comes with many pre-configured DSL profiles besides AT&T; which is the default. In my case, all I had to do was to pick my provider from the list, rename the network from the default, pick the security level and assign a new password. One minute later my 8 yr. old was happy because her tablet could play Pandora again.By the way, besides the 'wireless' part which works great (2 antennas), this comes with 4 Ethernet ports that are great for wired devices such as most Blu players or gaming consoles when performing high-bandwidth tasks such as playing movies - Netflix plays great on our the wire-connected Blu player.Overall, when it comes to ease of setup, reliability (comes back on by itself after being unplugged) and performance, this is a high-five stars because it can't get any better when it comes to DSL.

This rack/carrier mounts on the spare tire and is adjustable for different width wheel tire combos by changing the length of the mounting post ("tongue") that pokes through the center of the spare tire. Yakima states that the shortest tongue will handle P195-P235 tires, the next longest: P235-P275 and the longest: P285-12.5". However on my modified 1995 Suzuki Sidekick with P235/15 tires on wider wheels (I forget how wide) I had to use the longest tongue to have it mount properly. Wheel off set will also dictate the appropriate tongue length.The mounting plate can be rotated to fit different hub stud configurations (e.g. 5 or 6) but to fit my 5 stud hole wheel the plate is rotated with the tongue at 12 o'clock instead of 6 o'clock which means that the carrier sits higher on the spare tire thus partially blocking the view out of the rear window. It also blocks the CHMSL. The plate can clear a tire support bracket on the spare tire mount such as are found on some Toyotas, from what I've read posted on other reviews of other brand carriers. Because the rack sits so high, when the bikes are carried, the view out the rear window is entirely blocked, and, without the bikes, the view directly behind the car is blocked (by a part of the rack that is 3" tall and 12" wide), hence the two star rating. If the rack had a second attachment spot on the upright that would allow the upright to be mounted lower, or an alternative mounting plate, so that the view to the rear was not compromised, I would give it five stars.The carrier can be off-set to the right or to the left to partially center the bike when the spare is mounted to the left or right of the vehicle. The rack has a tightening knob that can be locked in place so that the rack cannot be removed. This is an excellent feature because the rack can easily be attached and removed without the need for a wrench.The upright that is pulled against the spare is sturdy metal but the part that attaches to that upright that has the adjustable arms with which to hold the bikes is made out of plastic so I am concerned as to how it will hold up to the elements.The end of each arm has a built in bottle opener.There are three major allen head machine screws that I intend to disassemble and re-assemble using Lock-tite on the threads.Yakima warns to never drive off-road with bicycles loaded, not to carry bikes over 35#, not to carry tandem bikes and not to install the carrier onto trailers, campers or RVs.I would NOT recommend this rack to anyone with a vehicle that uses wheels with a five lug hole pattern that are the same as the 95 Suzuki. Other five hole patterns with different hole spacing distances may mount lower. I will be looking for a replacement. Currently I don't believe that a better solution can be found for my vehicle.However, I did see a small Toyota, most likely a RAV, with a Spare Time mounted and the top of the rack was even with the top of the spare tire. As Gigi reports, on the 2010 Rav4s the rack fits nicely.

I did the big chop back in June of this year into a short close crop cut. I was worried about overloading my hair with products as it began to grow back. I am leaning towards homemade, natural products and so far I've found a replacement for everything except for hair gel. I saw excellent reviews on various natural hair care websites and decided to give it a try. I found this at my local beauty supply store and I bought a small jar just to test it out. I rinse my hair like I do every morning with a mix of Bragg's Apple Cider Vinegar and water, dry it with a towel but still leave it somewhat damp them I fill my palm with the gel and work it through. The gel typically dries before I get to work. I do like that it dries without white flaking and that it does not dry hard. Although I would prefer my hair  not to dry, just the curls to be more defined instead. This is a great system that provides instant feedback with respect to proper pistol sight alignment and trigger squeeze.  When paired with the LaserLyte Pistol Laser Trainer you are able to see where you were pointing when the hammer fell.  I used it indoors from 50 feet down to 6 feet, and was impressed with its ability to register where I would have hit had I been actually firing my Kahr P9.  Now for the down side.  After just 75 shots on target, the LaserLyte Pistol Laser Trainer (NOT the Laser Trainer Target)failed.  After checking the batteries and the installation, it was determined that the laser in the Pistol Laser Trainer had become defective.  When operating properly, the device would activate a strong laser beam when the hammer on the pistol fell.  In between shots, no laser beam could be detected at all.  The device was either full on, or full off, no in between.  After 75 shots, I found the Pistol Laser Trainer would no longer activate the laser beam when the hammer fell, and the device was now emitting a very dim laser light that could not be turned off.  It was now on full time at what appeared to be a low power setting.  I contacted LaserLyte and they asked me to send it in for repairs.  A return authorization number was provided, but no return postage assistance was offered.  I was told to insure the package and have it tracked at my own expense.  The customer service rep said they would inspect the device, and either repair or replace it at their option.  I mailed it back two days ago, and I'm now awaiting its repair.  I have several LaserLyte products, and to date they have all functioned flawlessly.  Had the device continued to work with the LaserLyte Laser Trainer Target, I would have given it five stars.  As LaserLyte is a reputable organization with a good products, I anticipate a positive final outcome on its repair/replacement.  Keep in mind that had I been firing live ammo, the 75 practice shots would have cost me around $25.00 factoring in ammo, target cost and gas to the range.  As you can see, just a few hundred practice shots would pay for the cost of both of these LaserLyte products.

This drive is fast.  Thunderbolt is truly an impressive technology.  I have been getting sustained sequential write speeds of around 240 - 250MB/s from an internal SSD to this drive.But I have to say I was taken by surprise that this drive (and its smaller version the 6TB Thunderbolt Duo) use a different RAID implementation than their cousins - the WD My Book Studio Edition II series.  The older drives used a hardware RAID that created a single logical volume such that the OS only saw one volume.  With the Thunderbolt Duo units, both drives are seen.  At least on my iMac (late 2012) they are.  The RAID level is easily configurable using the WD Drive Utilities app.  But this app is really just applying the same software-based AppleRAID that could be configured using Apple's own Disk Utility.Why does this matter?  Because I like to use whole disk encryption using FileVault 2.  But FileVault does not support software RAID volumes. So I cannot encrypt these drives AND use RAID 0 (striping) or RAID 1 (mirroring).  This is a major bummer.As mentioned, the older MyBook Studio Edition II units created a single logical volume so I can apply whole disk encryption using FileVault.  I was assuming I could with these as well, but I learned what assuming does the hard way.

As is the case with many other products on Amazon, the product page you're looking at is for the overall product line with multiple varieties. If you go to the top of the page, you'll see 3 in 1, Exfoliating, and Hydrating each in a separate selection box. When you click on each one, the descriptions change...but the reviews don't. So, this isn't a complaint, only a warning that the three different varieties are all evaluated here on this one page and all their reviews are combined into one rating as though they were one product. With, say, blue, red, and black smartphone cases, that's not much of a problem. Who cares that much about the color when rating a product. But with a personal-care item like body wash, not only is the color different but also some of the properties and capabilities of the product. I can't think of a better way for Amazon to do it, though, unless they have a review-filtering scheme that allows the reviewer to select the "flavor" they're reviewing and then allows the reader to click to filter out the other reviews, like they do on many travel sites like TripAdvisor, etc.OK, enough of that.This review is for the blue, 3 in 1 variety.I like the product. I've used Irish Spring bar soap for years. I actually still like using bar soaps, but the Vine program allows me the opportunity to try alternatives and test out all different kinds of products. I like that bar soaps lather up easily and that I don't have to use a wash towel or pouf to soap up. The thing I don't like about bar soaps is that, if I'm not careful about rinsing, sometimes I can leave soap residue on my skin, and I have sensitive skin and can get rashes from soap residue.I can't say that the blue 3 in 1 body wash matches up completely with any of the Irish Spring scents I've used, but that doesn't really matter. The thing that's sort of the common thread is that there is a lack of the flowery or perfume-y scent that is in so many women's soaps. Also there's a bit of a piny tang--something more "manly," I guess. So that's there. In the blue variety, they attribute that to eucalyptus. I actually love the smell of eucalyptus. It always makes me think of walking through Golden Gate Park or the Presidio in San Francisco. The scent is good.The body wash doesn't lather up well when applied by hand. I like a better lather, so I use my wife's pouf. That also has the benefit of scraping the skin gently, so it helps give a cleaner feel and it also helps make sure there's no residue left after washing.As for the 3 in 3 in 1, I tried it on my hair, and I have to say I wasn't disappointed. My hair (much more sparse than in the olden days) felt clean all day and it left no itchiness or dryness. I'm not sure if that would remain the same if I used it every day. I don't really want to use it as a shampoo, but I can if I want so that's a good option.As for the face, I made the mistake of using the pouf the first time to wash my face. It irritated my skin and I had a rash in a couple of spots all day long. The few other times I tried it on my face, I just pulled some lather off the pouf and washed my face using my hands, and all went well.All that being said, I can still buy a package of 4 or 6 or maybe 8 bars of soap for the price of one container of body wash--and I can already tell that the body wash won't last nearly as long as a package of bar soap. So I haven't decided yet what I'll do when this bottle runs out.If you're a man who likes mass-market soaps like Irish Spring and you also like body wash, order this. It's definitely worth a try.

Overall, this is an adequate blow dryer.  There is nothing wrong with the design or functionality.  But there is nothing noteworthy either.  Add to that a couple of minor short-comings and its really just OK and probably not worth the price.The box includes the blow dryer, a small concentrator attachment, a travel bag and a small instruction pamphlet.At 1,875 watts the unit is powerful and will get the job done.  It is a metallic green, with a 6 foot long cord, and a collapsible handle.It is touted as a travel dryer.  While it is a solid, heavy unit as others have mentioned, it does fold down to a small size, making it easy to pack.PROs:* 1,875 watts* Twist and fold handle which operates easily.* 4 year warrantyCONs:Overall my issue with this product is that the instructions are vague, which I've broken down into three items-* The instructions say the blow dryer is 2 speed with 3 temperature levels (low/warm, low/cool, and high/hot).  That's somewhat true but not in the conventional sense.  Typically a dryer with multiple settings will have controls to manage the temperature and the speed.  The Infinitipro has just a control that goes from OFF to LOW and HIGH.  Both the LOW and HIGH settings produce warm/hot air.  The way to get cool air is to hold down the "cold shot" button.  While many dryers have cold shot buttons, they are usually considered a separate feature, and not part of the core temperature settings.  What is really awkward is that you would have to hold down the cool shot button on the back of the handle, where the paln of your hand is apt to be, instead of the front of the handle, where your thumb or fingers would typically be.* Advertised as having a filter that can be cleaned, but no documentation on how to open the compartment. I tried unscrewing the round cap of the dryer and when that didn't work, I squeezed the raised potions of the opposing ends of the round cap, to no avail.  I next tried getting the tip of my fingers just under the cap and that did it.  The filter is actually just a mesh screen, which is not atypical of blow dryers.* Arrows on dryer handle point in the wrong direction to twist open and close (fold) the handle.  That's easy enough tp figure out by simply twisting in the other direction, but its sloppy quality control.In the end, its a basic blow dryer and gets the job done.  I just prefer a dryer that has the option for low/high and warm/hot so that you can set it to a preferred power

I bought this to replace an ancient WRT54GS whose radio finally failed, with the intention of putting OpenWRT on it.  I was a bit worried since the OpenWRT wiki lists this router as a work in progress, but it turns out the recently released Backfire 10.03.1 works very well, and my router has been quite stable for about a week now.  Installation was trivial through the OEM web interface.  32MB is plenty of RAM for most things you'd want to do, the CPU at 266MHz is not especially fast but gets the job done, and the USB 2.0 port allows for indefinite storage space if you want to run a NAS or DLNA server on the router.  I don't (the physical Ethernet ports are only 100Mbit/s and I already have my NAS on a gigabit switch), but I did stick an old 4GB USB flash drive in the back to get more than ample storage space for installing additional OpenWRT packages. Without it, the ~4MB left after installing the OpenWRT image would be pretty cramped.Also, at the current price of under seventy bucks, this thing really is a steal--it's a general purpose Linux computer with a largish (for embedded) RAM and USB2.0, built-in WiFi and Ethernet switch.  If you're building robots or adding telemetry to your backyard chicken coop, you probably won't find a more powerful platform at this price.

I bought is as an office productivity lap-top replacement for my 81-year old mother, who has never used mobile touch technology.  DOESN'T WORK FOR THIS.  This is still Android, and even if file management, etc. is a little more convenient than iOS, it’s a tablet.  To print a photo, you have to got out of the gallery, open a printing app, find the picture again, and go through many touch-steps (like a dozen touches) to choose all the options.  Compared to a PC, it’s a bear.  And for my 81-year-old non-techno mom to learn this?  Forget it!  That alone would take forever to teach her.  But every basic PC function she’s used to, other than Chrome, is that much more complicated.Hword margins are in mm, not inches.  (Maybe there’s a way to change it, but if so, it’s non-intuitive).  The fonts are better than most Android WP apps, but limited compared to Word.  There is no way to print non-gmail emails from any printing apps I tried.  (Google only wants you to use Gmail, I guess.)  And let’s face it – mobile operating systems were never designed to DO the data management and manipulation which Windows or Mac OS do so naturally.  Mobile devices are still computer ACCESSORIES, designed primarily for communication and media consumption.  Of course, they do many things a computer CAN’T DO.  The touch-world and app-world are amazing.  But not full-on office productivity.Theoretically, yes, you probably could do 80% of what a laptop or PC does on this, but yikes, what a nuisance!  You could also write a novel on your smartphone, but would you want to?I’m bummed, because I love so much about this tablet.  Here’s what I wish it could do, the future of mobile devices as I see it:  Customizable voice-command presets:  “Print 4x6 photo glossy” – “Print letter draft” – etc.  You know, HAL from 2001.  Sigh….Bottom line:  This is an amazing tablet (probably the best), but NOT a laptop replacement, unless all you do on your laptop is gmail and internet.  Or if you’re a dedicated techno-lover who has lots of time and energy.

So, I bought a 30GB iPod Photo a few years ago, and can't say enough good things about it.  The thing is still rockin' along, and I love everything about it - the sound quality is top-notch, it's easy to operate, and iTunes is so simple to use, does just about everything I need it to do.I just got a 30GB iPod Video, and I have to say, I'm a little disappointed - the first time I powered it on and pumped out some tunes, I was MORE than just a little disappointed, but read on...When it comes to music, I am meticulous (to say the least), and demand a certain sound and a high level of performance out of my equipment; the iPod photo fit all those needs.  However, the iPod video...well, it didn't sound nearly as good as the photo, even after jacking with the EQ's and upgrading my firmware to the latest version (the latest when I received it, anyway).  I bought new headphones, revamped the quality of some MP3 files, all with the same result - the sound quality was much more tinny and had too much mid-range, causing distortion, not just on a few songs, but on ALL songs.  I compared it with my iPod photo, which still sounded fantastic, and concluded it must be the iPod.  So, over the past week, I've pretty much ignored the video iPod, and stuck with my photo iPod.  However, the new version 1.2 firmware came out, and I just upgraded to it...I don't know what happened, but it sounds much better (still not on par with my Photo, though).The sound quality I can live with; as far as the video quality, all I have to say is WOW.  Such high quality for such a little screen!  It's absolutely phenomenal (and I'm just as picky with my video output as I am my sound quality).  If videos/shows/movies are your thing, then the video iPod will blow you away.What I was truly disappointed with - and why I only give the iPod 4 stars, and not 5 - is the battery life.  Again, my only point of comparison is my iPod photo, and with continuous use (about 8 hours a night), I had to charge it up about once every three days.  The video iPod?  It barely lasted 8 hours before the battery died, the first night I used it (yes, I gave it a full charge the day I got it).  I was not pleased, and I'm still not pleased; to me, it's the most annoying feature, since I work 10 hour shifts.  The primary function of my video iPod is a music player, so I knew it wasn't sucking up battery due to video playback.  Either I got lucky with the battery in my iPod photo, or I have a bad battery in this iPod video.  I know Apple got bigtime grief over their batteries in some of the early generation iPods, so I suppose I should count myself lucky, but come on!  If we're going to shell out 300 bucks for an MP3 player, the battery life should AT LEAST live up to what it says - up to 14 hours.  A max battery life of 8 hours is just not good enough for the price you pay.So, overall, as long as you upgrade the firmware, you should be good for sound quality; the video quality is excellent, but the battery life didn't cut it for me at all (8 hours max out of a promised "up to 14."

Tried a new pair of 26x2.35 on a full suspension trail bike at 48 PSI on trails having a mix of rocky, dusty, and hard dirt.  Steep hills were a breeze going up and down.  These tires are very light weight with excellent acceleration and deceleration grip.  I wasn't wasting any power to slippage or bouncing when I pedaled.  They don't sink or drag much on soft dirt and they remained quiet and didn't throw up pebbles on gravel.Cornering was really frightening.  Both my front and back tires were sliding out not only on turns, but on hillside trails that were no longer level.  I was fighting for traction when I should have been enjoying the view.  These tires need more knobs down the center.Forget about bicycling from your house to the trail.  You'll never make it.  These tires on asphalt is like riding with the brakes on.

I am an eyeliner junky.  I have always liked a bold eye.  I generally prefer the richness and convenience of a liquid brush eyeliner (which is getting harder and harder to find a good one), but my old L'Oreal ran out and does tend to dry too crusty which is annoying, so I am venturing back into the cream eyeliners again.  In the past my complaints about them have been having to carry a brush around with it, and that they dry out too quickly, but if you use it often, I find it dries out less, and if the price is right, I guess it's not the worse problem to have (this is true of all water-based smooth products, like gel pens, too).  It's much more important to me that it is creamy, goes on smoothly without tugging, doesn't get stiff and crusty, washes off easily, and preferably doesn't smudge.  For me, this e.l.f. is the best, and for a fraction of the cost of other name brands (Mac/Sephora).  It scores well on all of the above I mentioned, except on the last one.  I would not call it waterproof, and if you sleep in it, it will smudge and you'll have a bit of raccoon eyes, but then we shouldn't be sleeping in our makeup anyway.  I am also quite sensitive to chemicals, etc. and this product does not make my eyes run or be irritated like some others.  The black is dark and rich.  The blue is a nice cool deep navy, which looks classic and sophisticated, not too young or trendy.  I liked them so much I bought the olive and plum for my sister as gifts.  When mine run out or dry up, I will definitely buy again.

I use chemicals in my hair about four times a year between keratin treatment and professional coloring. Dullness and brittle ends set in mid-autumn and by the early winter, I have to deal with the often unpleasant task of getting my hair back in shape.  I don't like adding oils to my hair so the traditional moroccan oil was not an option.  The Suave Professionals products were affordable enough to try the shampoo and conditioner and see how my hair responded.  I am totally impressed, and satisfied with both the scent and the creamy texture of the conditioner.  I comb the conditioner through and let it soak into my wet hair while in the shower to get the full benefit of the moisturizing elements.  It even works to detangle my usually curly locks.  The overall shine left when I blow dry my hair is closer to what I get at the salon than any other similar conditioner I've tried.  I am very pleased with this Suave product and recommend it to anyone with normal to dry hair.

I ordered this prior to a vacation to China. I obviously wanted my DSLR with me the whole time, but did not want it  around my neck the whole time. It rests so comfortably on my shoulder at times I forgot it was even there. I can easily slide the camera from my hip to my face for quick shooting. It also has some clips that slide along the same strap (one in front, one behind the camera) that you can position to keep your camera from going too far forward or behind you. This is the perfect product for anyone who walks around with their DSLR for any length of time!!

I got this for my Camelbak HAWG to expand on it's carrying capacity and make it easier to carry lunch or first-aid in the rare event that my pack gets full of extra people's sweaters or something. It took a bit of shopping around to find the right size, including a previously disappointing bad order (the Rothco First-Aid Kit). This is a Condor product, and I found myself pleasantly surprised by a few unexpected features.I was a bit confused by the different size specifications of this I had seen on the internet because the numbers were not consistent. This is because it is expandable/collapsible in depth. Actually, you can expand it a bit bigger than the 2.5" on the product info on this page. More like 3.5". But it also flattens out very nicely. There is also an adjustable, horizontal cord underneath the flap that aids in this, and the sturdy buckles and straps are adjustable as well. This was well designed.There are no zippers here. It is just the buckles you see on the picture, and a lot of velcro. There is actually very little open space when everything is closed. There is so much velcro that I would feel comfortable leaving it unbuckled while running around. This would be great if you needed to grab something fast!The MOLLE straps and buttons on this are very well made, as opposed to the inferior Rothco product. Strapping this product on is easy and secure; when it's on, it's on and not coming off.I got the ACU pattern, which matches my Camelbak ACU pattern perfectly. It looks like it came with it on there and that it is just part of the pack. The velcro square is a nice addition and makes great use of a velcro morale patch. I used a patch with a pin-up girl design (riding the gatling gun of an A-10) that says "danger close delivery". Cool! You can find it on Amazon.I would strongly recommend this pouch for a variety of applications. And if you have the HAWG, this makes a perfect accessory!
