If you want to make your inner nerd feel like the Master (or Mistress) of the Universe, this is the device to do it.It has so many uses. Let's suppose you have bought a Solid State Drive (SSD) for your laptop. Without this docking station you'll need to do a lot of fiddling inside a desktop computer to do the replacement. But with this dock you can simply put the SSD inside, use a copy program like Acronis True Image or Paragon's Backup and Recovery (check your freeware site), and copy the entire original hard drive (including the Operating System) to the SSD. Obviously if your original hard drive has more installed on it than the SSD, you'll only copy the Operating System, but you need only an SSD of 64GB or more to do this.Now take out the hard drive in the laptop and replace it with the SSD. Try booting the system, and if that works, you can keep your old hard drive in the docking station and take it along if the ssd doesn't hold enough for what you'll need. Since I have Windows 8 (64-bit) and a whole bunch of other software in around 50GB (including several GB that should be on a hard drives, like photos and MP3s) you'll probably find that you have enough space doing this method. If you had a 256GB hard drive, you now have over 300GB to play with.One thing that's often overlooked is the portability of a docking station. This one has a Power Supply Unit (PSU) that simply plugs into the wall, and a USB cable that works with USB 2.0 or USB 3.0. If you buy an extra hard drive you can fit it inside your desktop computer. However, if you use this docking station you can simply unplug the cables and you'll have a hard drive that will work with just about any other computer. If you have the family's collection of photos, you can take the docking station and drive, and plug it in to a relative's computer to share them.In fact, security is yet another reason to get this docking station. Years ago I remember hearing about a guy who was so concerned about losing his hard drive contents he had a separate backup drive which he uninstalled from the computer each night, and he'd leave the day's business on the main drive. Then he'd take the backup hard drive home with him, and he reasoned that he wouldn't have a catastrophe at home and at the office on the same night, he was certain that as soon as he got another computer he'd be able to start work again. With this docking station he would have been able to just back up to the drive in the device and take it out at the end of the day.As you can imagine, I'm very enthused about this docking station. I don't think we're yet at the stage where we can rely on the cloud, and besides, some activities require components to be on your desktop. The Uspeed is well-made and seems very reliable, and at the price, with USB 3.0 plus eSATA connections, it's almost as fast as a direct install.So if you consider the options that the Uspeed opens up to you, it's a no-brainer.(And a thank you to the people at Uspeed who supplied this device for review. The above is my personal opinion and, since I get items on the same basis from the Amazon Vine program, I think this a fair and accurate view.)

My experience with antennas started with a $10 wal mart indoor antenna and ended with this one.  I tried the wal mart special in my living room and got 6 channels, only 2 of them strong which IMHO means I got 2 channels.  I purchased the mohu leaf (a superior indoor antenna) and still got 6 channels but with improved strength.  I put the leaf in the attic and picked up 10 channels, but still only 2 of them strong.  I dropped $38 on the leaf which I regret because I could have purchased an outdoor antenna for $38 and placed it in the attic.  Which is what I did since I was past the return period for the leaf.  I purchased the RCA ANT751 for $38, placed it in the attic and got the same 10 channels but now with 6 of them strong, so I got 6 channels.  FYI, I was using WMC to test signal strength and most stations were less than 20 miles away.  I purchased a CPA 19 preamp to try to fix the signal strength on the other 4 channels and it didn't work.  I didn't wait this time, I returned both the CPA 19 and ANT751 and purchased the CM4228.  One other improvement was to replace a splitter I was using to extend my cable run with a GE23203 cable extender to save about 3db of gain.  After the CM4228 in the attic, I get 28 channels, 26 of them at 100 signal strength.  The VHF channel that is 77 signal strength is understandable since this is a UHF antenna.  I purchased the CM4228 because of its best in class reception at high VHF (based on my research).  The only disappointment is not getting ABC.  I pointed to 104 degrees according to tvfool which also stated that ABC had 15.8 NM (db).  I also used tvfool and google earth tv maps to verify that the signal for ABC is weak here, but was hoping the CM4228 would overcome it.  My guess is that if I put the antenna on the roof with a 10 ft mast, I would get ABC.  So, I leave the rating at 5 stars.

UPDATE: The 5 day battery life only applies if you keep Background Sync enabled. I highly recommend turning this off in your smartphone app to conserve battery life. After talking to more people that have used the Flex and Force, they have the same exact complaints about the wrist band. Most of us have switched to the Fitbit One.FitBit FlexThe Flex is a water-resistant fitness tracker device that slips into a large or small wrist-band (both included in the package). It tracks steps with an internal digital/MEMS accelerometer, and calculates calories burned based on the user's dimensions (age, weight, sex, etc...) and works well with the Fitbit App that is available on Android and iPhone smartphones. It communicates wirelessly via Bluetooth 4.0 which makes it a snap to sync. However, the syncing is very slow. I've encountered a few instances where it refused to sync and I had to close the app and try again.My wrist measures 6.5" in circumference, which puts me in-between the small and large bands. I can just fit into the largest setting of the smallest band, but the large band is too large on my wrist. The wrist band easily traps water and sweat. I am astounded at how quickly it will trap water inside the band. I found that if I do not clean it on a daily basis, it will start to smell and grow bacteria. Showering isn't even necessary to do this, simply washing your hands will cause water to become trapped and make squishy noises.The battery life is advertised at 5 days and that is about what I am seeing. It takes quite a long time to fully charge up, even when using a 10W / 2A Belkin charger. There aren't any real helpful instructions upfront for calibrating the stride distance, so the first few times I went for a jog to compare the Flex against my MotoACTV and Garmin Footpod, the distances were off but the number of steps matched almost perfectly. The Garmin Footpod also uses a digital/MEMS accelerometer to track steps but it is actually tied to a shoe for the ultimate in precision. Once I entered my correct stride distances, the running mileage recorded by the Flex matched that of the GPS in the MotoACTV. The amount of calories burned is calculated higher with the Flex than the MotoACTV.The sleep tracking is neat, but it basically detects how often you move your hand while sleeping. I found myself trying not to move while sleeping to "boost" my sleep efficiency score, but the band never felt uncomfortable. Entering and exiting sleep mode takes some practice because it is very finicky. However, when it comes to riding my bicycle or working in the garage with a hammer, it somehow loves to enter and exit sleep mode due to the vibrations it detects through my hand. The Flex is totally useless while riding a bicycle and I imagine it isn't going to be very useful while swimming. While riding the bike, the Flex became unresponsive to actual taps. It somehow wouldn't detect my taps, no matter how much force I applied. Even when golfing, it started to enter/exit sleep mode. A friend gently grabbed my wrist to look at the Flex and it started blinking the status LED for how much I've walked.The Jawbone app looks more appealing graphically. I find that I have to use the FitBit web portal to log most of my activities more easily than the bland app.My biggest complaints are that the battery life isn't easily monitored. When my battery was low, I never received an email or notification. It takes 2 hours or more to fully charge, and it syncs very slowly. The wristband and Flex are water resistant, but the band traps water and sweat inside the cavity that holds the Flex. Washing hands, taking a shower, and sweating will result in a plethora of bacteria building up inside the band. I'm sure over time the band will stretch or deform to the point it won't hold the Flex as tightly. Replacement bands are available at a relatively high price. The metal clasp can easily fall out of the band. If you lose it or break it, replacement clasps are available, but it can be a pain to lose such a small item when you're trying to adjust the strap.Overall: 3/5 stars, basically a pedometer that attaches to wrist and can transmit data via wireless to a smartphone. It's water resistant, but you have to constantly clean the sensor and band or else it will grow bacteria. I can understand why they designed it this way, I just found the overall execution to be lacking. It works, don't get me wrong, I Just didn't care for the app or the method of logging my activities and how I need to take it off before working in the garage or riding a bicycle or anything that involves vibrations which might trip the sensor into thinking it is going into sleep mode.

I gave this item a 4 instead of a 5 as a compromise.  If you are looking for a small backpack designed to carry only cameras and technical equipment, this is probably a 5.  If you are looking for a daypack that will also hold cameras and technical equipment along with your regular stuff, it is probably a 3.  The problem is that the things that make it a 5 as a camera pack make it a 3 as a daypack.In general, it is narrow and you can move easily with it.  Shoulder straps are padded and comfortable, but there is no cross strap for hiking and climbing and such.  The fasteners are secure, and the material appears quite durable.  The zippers are heavy and solid.There are two large flat storage areas in it.  One is behind the back-pad, and the other opens from the side for a laptop.  There is a solid compartment on the top that will keep a good sized camera secure.  It is secured by a solid zipper as well as two clips.  The straps for the clips are reflectorized for visibility.  There is a small flat zipper pouch on the top of the compartment.  There is a little pouch on the back for small items.  It has a pull out water bottle holder in it that I found neat.  There are straps on the back that can be used to hold a tripod, yoga mat, or other long but thin objects.  It is quite secure at the top, but the bottom end could flop around.  There are small d rings near the bottom that could be used to secure the other end, and the strap on the water bottle holder could be used for that as well.  I checked it by strapping a yoga mat to it and jumping up and down.  It held well.  The big compartment is interesting.  It is about twice as large as the camera compartment on top.  It has various dividers that you can use to customize the compartment for your specific equipment.  I took them all out and put them back in in various configurations and it held well.  This compartment holds well and opens from both right and left sides.  ON the flaps to the lower compartment there are small mesh and elastic pouches for holding small misc. items.  There is a grab handle on the top (like most daypacks), but there is also a padded handle on the center of the back as well.  There is a strip of nylon web on the wearer's left side that could be used to attach carabineers or small pouches.The sides are semi-rigid, and there is not a lot of give to them.  This would make carrying irregularly shaped or oversize objects difficult. It is not going to hold a lot of stuff like clothes, books etc.Again, this is a really great equipment pack, but only a so-so daypack.  If you go out to take pictures or do something equipment intensive, this is a great pack.  On the other hand, if you're going on a picnic or a day trip and you're bringing a camera, maybe you need a smaller separate camera bag and a larger daypack.

I'm a semi-pro photographer and I was looking to speed up my workflow, particularly with weddings. I was using auto white balance in camera and color correcting in post processing. You can imagine how time consuming this process was. I saw several photographers recommend this product and I was skeptical. After a few sessions my verdict is: it really works!!I use it to take my exposure reading first, then custom white balance on my Nikon D800. That's it. You can change your exposure and don't have to take another white balance. If you change areas or the light changes, you have to take a white balance reading again. It takes a little practicing just to get fast with it. It's worth the 30 seconds it takes. The color is spot on and I don't adjust white balance anymore. I get that done during the session or event.You can take the white balance by standing where your subject will be standing and pointing the camera where you will be standing OR by pointing the camera towards the primary light source. In my experience both methods worked. I find in a shaded area, it's best to point towards the primary light source so that your white balance isn't too warm. Some people get caught up in the exact method, but really either works. You can watch video tutorials on their website.The other benefit of this method is that I can show a quick preview to the client from my camera and the photo looks really nice. Not all cold and dark like it would with auto white balance. If you're debating getting it, just do it! I must have for semi-pro or pro and good addition for photography lovers. I would order again without thinking about it.

After trying countless other creams within the same price range, including Burt's Bees, I decided to try this and those problems are slowly but surely fading after several months of use. That's without the use of my usual serums/oils and other products that have worked so well. I also have really enjoyed using products that have fewer chemicals and more beneficial, natural ingredients, as it seems to be improving my skin long-term--though it did take some getting used to.Is it an all-in-one miracle cream? Not really, or at least not by itself and not with immediate results, but the fact that it has good ingredients, is inexpensive, lasts a long time, and does its job has me continuing to use it. I also enjoy the fact that I feel like I'm not only keeping my skin moisturized, but also nourished and with fewer chemicals involved (it is not all-natural, unfortunately, but bears a low level of toxicity). I do believe in switching products up in order to benefit from other ingredients, but this is the one I turn to the most.This is a really thick, moisturizing cream, but it's not sticky, it absorbs well into the skin, and you don't need a lot, which means it'll last a while. I just dab a few small dots along my forehead, cheeks, chin and nose. It's made my skin much more supple and less saggy, pores are smaller, and any forming lines are less noticeable and continue to improve. The paperiness that tends to form around my eyes (no wrinkles yet) is more supple as well, which is something I've always struggled with. It does, however, have a tendency to make my combination-dry skin oily at night and by the time I wake up in the morning, but it's not horrible and usually depends on the weather. If I feel that might happen, I'll switch it up with Mychelle Grapefruit cream or the Maximum Moisture Cream by BWC.Although I am sensitive to smells and perfumes, the light fragrance isn't hard to handle, unlike the Maximum Moisturizing Cream that has a heavy floral scent (I like that cream and use it occasionally, it's just overly scented and a little heavier). Whatever scent is there eventually fades in a few minutes, and it's not half as smelly as Burt's or similar, lacking that herbaceous smell some products have--it smells more citrus mixed with a hint of floral to me. It also hasn't caused problems with acne, short of the hormonal issues I get once a month. During those times, I use the Mychelle Grapefruit Cream, which works great for that, and may top it off with this cream once the other product sets in. I also try to wear sunscreen over this moisturizer when I go outside for prolonged period of time, generally due to the vitamin C.I definitely recommend it to those who like products that are more natural, nourishing, and that won't break the bank, especially if they have mature, dry, or combination-dry skin. Even though I plan to repurchase some of the serums I used before, just for added benefit, I will continue to use this as my staple moisturizer. I also plan to get the body lotion version since this has worked so well on my face, and eventually plan to use collagen and CoQ10 supplements.If you're brand new to using mostly-natural items (like Burts and BWC), but rather are used to lotions like Oil of Olay, you may be in for a bit of a shock due to the thickness and it can take some getting used to. With products like this, you don't need a lot--too much and you will go into grease bomb mode. Start off with a very, very small amount and add more later if you need it. I do a dot on each quadrant. I also love their leave-in conditioner for my Hermione Granger type hair, though that does have kind of an herbaceous smell that fades. :)The full, current ingredients are: Purified Water, Aloe Barbadensis (Aloe Vera) Leaf Juice (organic), Glycerin, Magnesium Ascorbyl Phosphate, Polysorbate 60, Cetearyl Alcohol, Helianthus Annuus (Sunflower) Seed Oil (organic), Stearic Acid, Glyceryl Monolaurate, Ethyl Hexyl Palmitate, Linum Usitatissimum Oil (Flax seed) (organic), Rosa Canina (Rose Hip) Oil (organic), Anthemis Nobilis (Chamomile) Extract (organic), Calendula Officinalis (Calendula) Extract (organic), Cucumis Sativus (Cucumber) Extract (organic), Camellia Sinensis (Green Tea) Leaf Extract, Theobroma Cacao (Cocoa) Seed Butter (organic), Citrus Aurantium (Neroli) Oil (organic), Citrus Aurantium (Sweet Orange) Oil (organic), Cananga Odorata (Ylang Ylang) Flower Essential Oil, Lavandula Hybrida (Lavandin) Oil (organic), Anthemis Nobilis (Chamomile) Oil (organic), Butyrospermum Parkii (Shea) Butter, Prunus Dulcis (Sweet Almond) Oil, Calophyllum Inophyllum (tamanu) Oil, Squalane (Vegetable source), Coenzyme Q10, Tocopherols, Panthenol, Tocopherol Acetate, Hydrolyzed Soy Protein, Soy Lecithin, Allantoin, Glyceryl Stearate, Dimethicone, Phenoxyethanol, ethyl hexyl glycerin, Benzyl Alcohol

This is my first pair of Brainwavz anything. I purchased them under the assumption, based on credible reviewers, they would outperform my previous IEMs/earbuds the Klipsch S4. To say they did would be an understatement. Brainwavz M5 is a warm earbud that offers up a more natural bass than that of other recognized brands in the sub $50 range. The overall quality could easily compete with more expensive models even reaching the sub $100 range.If it sounds too good to be true, it does not end there just yet. These earbuds are housed in metal, they do not look cheap. The oxygen free cables and gold plated 3.5 mm L shaped tip only further the value. Speaking of value Brainwavz has outdone themselves...Ready?Contents & Accessories-Hard carry case: A nice secure but spongy case that sports the Brainwavz logo.Eight earbuds to choose from-One set Comply Foam Tips S-Series: comfortable thanks to `memory foam', and supposed super attenuation. I noticed the bass became warmer with these on, could be just my ears playing tricks on me.One set of Bi-flange tip: I use these the most. Very comfortable, I'll tag them in the photo I am in the process of uploading.Six sets of silicone ear tips: Ranging from small, medium, and large. Two sets for each size insures one they will never have to go without, or mix and match a size out of need.Shirt Clip: Need to fight off microphonics? Shirt clip! Wire getting in your way? Shirt Clip!Some audiophiles believe `burning them in' (hours of use set at specific volumes and genres) help open them up more or round out harsher sounds, so on so forth. So I decided to give it a try. I have not really noticed any significant changes. The soundstage was naturally pretty good. The lower mids sound strong, and the bass is certainly not boomy, but full. The highs were not as harsh as the Klipsch thankfully. I used a variety of tracks ranging from classical to death metal all in FLAC format to test these earbuds. No, you don't need to go and grab yourself a headphone amp to enjoy these. These earbuds have dynamic drivers, some people prefer balanced armature drivers as they tend to provide more clarity, but the the dynamic driver easily makes do.These are not analytical earbuds, but they are capable of revealing some lower quality recordings. I tend to listen to a lot of music like Boards of Canada, I like warm organic electronics, ambient music, and dark bloated tones. These work great for them. I have also given them a go with the video game soundtrack for Silent Hill II which sounded superb as well.For the low price, one walks out with an armory of audio comfort goodies, a lively set of well-made earbuds.Just for the sake of it all I have used a variety of headphones, from the Sennheiser HD 515, the HD 598, the Klipsch S4, JVC Mallows, some koss earbuds, and the Sennheiser CX 250 I believe they were called. The M5 were leagues above the earbuds I have owned.Feel free to ask any questions regarding this product, I'd be glad to assist.----------------Update 2/23/2013This pair broke. Literally no sound in either bud. I contacted MP4Nation multiple times with no response. I still find the build quality to be superb to other models in this price range, hoping I simply received a dud.----------------Update 5/29/2013MP4Nation was not only quick to respond to my above inquiry but even better with customer service than any company I can recall in recent years. Perhaps I was a bit impatient with my Brainwavz simply due to the desire to be up and listening to them again. To verify with the previous update my issue was handled more promptly than my response here. The company held a good sense of humor and was clearly committed. I recently even purchased a pair of Sennheiser HD 598, and the M5 still stand their ground. I truly have no desire to purchase another IEM due to the satisfaction and value the M5 provide.

On my other reviews, I constantly talk about knives from a defensive/tactical line as the priority and then everything else as a plus afterwards. Since I live in an urban setting, fixed blades are out so large easily carried folders is what i look for. I do use a three knife EDC system and i do love to collect 'em. Buying good folders is my downfall.... So with that said, i bought this Police used lightly from an Ebay seller. The knife came with no marks, scratches, dings or dents.  What a great buy!One of my all time favorite knives is the Spyderco Endura that I bought in 1990. Still in good shape that knife is now retired. I replaced it with a gen 4 endura and im still as happy as ever. When i originally looked at the Police, I really wanted it but the cost (im retired)put it out of my range. So from a distance i viewed the Police as an Endura on steroids....my kinda knife. When it popped up on ebay at a very low price..."it was on" I have not been disappointed. It is a well made larger folder that is built to last. It sits well in the pocket and its slim & thin profile does not bother me when carried throughout the day. The handle is very long (i have large hands) so it might be a too large for small to medium sized hands. If you fall into that category, visit a knife store so that you can see ifthis is the right knife for you. The G10 handles are grippy enough and the jimping is good enough so that this would be a decent knife for defensive puposes especially with the approximately 4 1/4" blade. It is as they say...a beast of a knife.Now the downside, the blade is long and with a lockback system is slow to deloy without using some force and momentum to get that 4'+ blade moving. However, in my case, i do believe that the spyderhole deployment is about as good as it gets (with the exception of a good auto) especially under stress. Also, for whatever reason i do like the FRN handle on my endura and wished the Police had an option for FRN. I'm saying that and i do like G10. maybe on this size knife, the FRN handles wouldve worked better for me. Now for the main problem (if you want to call it that). I did save some money and purchased the Spyderco military at almost the same time. Unfortunately, the Military might be one of the best large folders out there; lighter than the police with better ergonomics....ok, i'll leave it at that as this is about the Spyderco Police. If you want a good quality long blade folder...the Police is one of them. It will outlive you! One other downside. If youre like me and love to play with the blades while you watch TV ( when the wife is watching a LifeTime movie in the other room) this one can put a hurt on your finger. I only carry knives that can be deployed with one hand so i had to loosen the pivot to allow the blade recasso to drop on my finger when i pushed the lockback, then finish closing the blade. You really begin to respect the "heavy metal" of this blade as it beats on your finger with the end result being ...next knife to play with!!Is the Spyderco Police the best knife for the Police? As a retired cop from a very large city, i wouldn't throw it outta bed but the Military is the keeper....  make sense??? Both are my favorites...but the Millie gets more pocket time....

I am used to Canon flashes. When my two Canon 550s stopped working after a few years of use, I decided to get non-Canon flashes because I could not afford two Canons at the same time.  I purchased three Sunpak 383s before they were discountinued. I shot countless weddings with my  Sunpaks with great success.The Sunpaks lacked the TTL capabilities of my Canons. So i started looking around for other possible inexpensive solutions. A friend recommended the YongNuo brand. I purchase this unit with high hopes that it would be a great replacement for a Canon which I do not have a budget for.I just shot a wedding this past weekend. The flash performed well until it stopped working in a critical moment in the wedding. I quickly switched to my trustee Sunpak and finished the work.When I had a minute, I opened the flash battery compartment to see what was going on. To my surprise the batteries were so hot I could barely touch them. I replaced the batteries with a fresh set and continued using the YN-565EX. After about another 60 or 70 shots the unit stopped working again. I missed two critical action shots because the flash stopped firing due to the heat problem.In all my years of photography my Canons or Sunpaks never ever had this heated battery problem.I initiated a return request today. I cannot risk another assignment with this flash.I am disappointed because the unit is built well and it looks as good as the Canon 550 I had. It performs well in terms of matching with my camera and delivering correct exposure. The heating problem eliminates this flash for me. I cannot afford to miss shots due to a flash that keeps heating up to a point that the circuitry shuts down the unit.I am giving it a two star because the unit is really nice. But if I had to rate the performance and reliability, I would have to rate it a one star.

My parents have thousands of 35mm slides. They aren't much good sitting in boxes. I purchased another brand several years ago but it did a poor job and I returned it to the store. Since then I've been looking for reviews and information that would guide me to a good choice. It seemed that Wolverine had the most evaluation stars. But there were some comments that bothered me on other models. This scanner had only one review which was positive so I decided to try it. Well, for me that was the right choice. Within a few minutes I was scanning slides. I worked with it for most of the afternoon and am pleased with the result. I'm not a photo professional but I think it does a really good job! It's very fast. I ordered the video-out cable so I can show the images on a TV. Also ordered a 3-pack of slide trays. The one thing that slows down the scanning process is loading the slide tray so it would be great to have a partner loading those while you scan. Another thing that's handy to have is a can of spray air. I wouldn't shoot it into the machine but it's great for getting dust out of the area or even the slide trays. Overall, I think the machine is well made and the slide tray snaps into place smartly. The instructions state to be careful and not handle it roughly. Probably the same as dropping a camera? I suggest keeping the shipping box because it is very protective and well made. It's easy to put all the components back into the box. For me, this is a home run!

Have seen them go off six times on deer and not write ANY data to a card, so they aren't perfect, but for the money, pretty good.  Cheap and mostly dependable with an easy-to-learn operating system.  D-batts.  Replace batts when they hit 25% battery charge.I have a Video on Youtube of how to set up your gamecam on the Blackfork6 Youtube channel.  Most folks don't get their cams set right.I did have one of these that got submerged when our lake dam broke, (dried out and started working), was stolen and thrown in the creek, (dried off and started working again).  I finally pulled it when it died.  It told me the card was locked for about a year before it gave up the ghost, though it WAS writing to the card.I'm sure I will switch to the newer models in the next few years.  Nice to run them on AA batts instead of the D's.

I've been using a FitBit Ultra for about 7 months now.1. When it works it's OK to Pretty Good. It records steps (motion), stairs, and it's fairly accurate--when it works.It's not accurate enough to match real distances. Close enough for comparison. 5.6 miles today may not be 5.6 earth miles, but it is more than 5.5 miles yesterday.2. It fits on your belt--good. The extra clip thing seems redundant and useless. It consistently falls out of the  wrist band for logging sleep.3. It's not waterproof -- DEAL BREAKER. I kayak, to use the FitBit for this activity I slip it on a card jammed into a pill container. The FitBit shape is just a little too big to fit in anything reasonable. To log paddling, I have to wear it on my wrist, or three hours of hard paddling records like a nap.3.5 THE BIGGEST RISK it's small, it clips on your clothes. It's very easy to toss it into the wash. Not being waterproof is silly.4. Did I mention it's not waterproof? Sweat starts out as water.... I think this is why when I jam a session on the treadmill and I check the FitBit it starts reading strange, starts the stop watch.....5. Unnecessary Dongle -- you have to connect a dongle to the USB port in your computer or FitBit doesn't record anything to your online account. In the day of bluetooth everything--this is ridiculous.6. Frequently will not log data into my record (Dashboard). This might be partly due to sweat, (it seems like the more I exercise the more likely it will not transfer my data)  or it might just be an interface problem.I use an Apple computer OS 10.6.8 Every 4th or 5th day my Dashboard (record) won't update. Only after I re-start the computer will it work. There are numerous posts on this on their site--the responses are not helpful.7. I have not seen any improvement in software, analyzing or logging activities in the 7 months I've used it. Surely other users are kayaking, FitBit collects all this data, but doesn't seem to be doing anything useful (to the user) with the info. This crowd source should be improving the device's usefulness--I haven't seen any signs.Bottom line--I got my money's worth, but I'm looking for a better device/system. This is a great idea,(pedometers were the  great idea,)  but it doesn't seem to be going anywhere.Did I mention it's not waterproof and has frequent interface problems?

This Audio-Technica LP120 turntable comes with a very long tail of negative and very negative reviews. But the positive reviews suggested this was what would satisfy my particular needs. -- I read every single Amazon review, including all the Q&As; everything  about the LP120 on YouTube; and every "independent" review I could find on line. Given all the negative reviews this was a hedged-bet to buy. If the positives were right I'd get what I wanted at my price point. If the negatives were right, then at least I could rely on A-T's good customer service and Amazon's return policy.Turns out it was a good bet and I am well pleased with this turntable. I ran it through a range of sonic inputs (solo voice, chamber ensemble, piano, Baroque organ, symphonic orchestral and an old, much abused and original Rolling Stones mono from the 60's) Some vinyl is in good condition: Some not so much. And I ran some recordings up to the maximum amp power output. No distortion I could hear.The rest of this review tries to summarize everything I learned from reading all those reviews and my own experience with this turntable.But first, let's deal with the two huge gorillas sitting here with me as I write this review.(1) Yes, sure, this can be described as a "cheap, plastic, Chinese knock-off" of  the much venerated and beloved original Japanese Technics turntable. But from what I can see the plastic is not so cheap and perfectly functional. I assume the 6 lb chunk of sheet steel in the base serves as a damper. Though I have mine sitting on 30+lbs of solid baked clay. The direct drive motor has a quartz lock as well as pitch control. Another more technical reviewer tested for durability, satisfactorily. A-T has upgraded the cartridge/stylus to one that is well established and generally very highly regarded. The set up is easy. I won't need the pre amp as my amp has a phono input. Don't know about the usb port and circuitry. But this is a common, inexpensive component. The plastic looks good. The unit is heavy and feels solid. The arm is probably not up to the original Japanese standard. But seems solid to me. Aesthetically it is retro (70s -80's) styling rather than the attractively sleek and minimalist higher priced ($450-$850 and above) tables.(2) This is a review for people much like me who want decent quality at a reasonable cost. Not looking for a turntable aimed at professional DJ's, audio engineers or audiophiles, (high testosterone or obsessive compulsive - whatever). I have a very decent a/v system - including the top end Oppo D for digital audio and video service, top end Yamaha for driving the speakers and JBL studio speakers for output. And Sennheiser 600s for listening in private. No, not audiophile level, but meets my idea of "decent". No distractions, just good sound. So I needed to complete the picture with a turntable that was solid, reliable, with a cartridge/stylus that would produce sufficient quality to fit with the digital audio sources I have but be forgiving of the poor quality of some of my vinyl.The LP 120 does this well.So: A summary Review -The set up is straight forward especially if you watch the Audio-Technica set up video on You Tube. (If you are not familiar with this type of set up.) I found this arm and weight/balance/ anti skate the easiest I've used in three previous tables.- There have been many complaints about the platter being warped. So, out of the box I checked mine VERY carefully. Perfect! I'm using my own rubber mat from a previous turntable. Do not like the slip mat AD packed with the table.- I doubt I'll use the pitch-slider or the reverse motor switch. But sometimes there are issues with old vinyl recordings and a small pitch adjustment availability is welcome.-This is direct drive with a quartz clock/lock. There have been complaints about feedback through the motor. (I've had belt drives in the past and the rubber belt mitigates this possibility.) But, even through the Sennheisers which are  sensitive, I can hear no feedback, hiss, rumble or anything. I have knocked the arm a couple times and got a crackle that then faded. I think the table was telling me to be gentler with it (in electro-Mandarin?).- The other big issue is tracking. Not an expert but I think one way to solve tracking problems is to put a crude, gouging stylus (ceramic or similar) with a big weight on it and hope it stays in the groove - tearing up your record and wearing it out as it does so.Otherwise the stylus/head shell alignment, anti skate and arm geometry do matter a lot. Fortunately Audio-Technica include a very decent stylus in a tried and true mid range quality cartridge, already aligned and set up. Treat it as the manual directs and a quick turn of the locking ring sets the cartridge in place. And properly aligned. If you set the counter balance correctly and the anti skate according to their directions (1.5-2 on their scales works for me.) - well I have found no distortion in either track from the beginning of the tracking in either channel from the outside of the record to the inside near the center spindle.- The cartridge is described by audiophiles as "warm" and "musical". No experience to judge. But to my ear I'm listening to a performance and not being distracted by a too sharp or fuzzy sound field.  I feel the sibilants are just right and the base response is not bad. The mid range has a bit of a glow to it. Will have to try the digital (CD) versions I have of some of my records to check into this more. But I have to say that I was well please with the  feel of the sound coming from the old vinyl, through the LP120 and out into the room via the JBL speakers.Actually rather good. --There are some pops and rattles from time to time. This just means I have to clean the vinyl more thoroughly. Otherwise this stylus (AT95e) seems both responsive and forgiving. "Better",  more expensive cartridges, the audiophiles say, are not so forgiving.-I do plan to use the usb port into my computer to transfer some of my more precious vinyl. I doubt I'll use the included software. There are better, easier to use, software options on line.So, there we are. I could have spent $200 on a good cartridge, and $600-1200 on a low end audiophile quality table. But then have to live with such a superfine system showing me every blip and dip in my vinyl. No point. I've tried the entire dynamic range from vocals to chamber music to organ to mono orchestra to Rocknroll. Pristine vinyl, and very, very worn.-Last issue is the fact that this is a totally non automatic table. You put the stylus over the record and release the lever. At the end you lift the stylus and return it to its rest. The LP60 costs a significant amount less and includes auto. I doubt the two models are very comparable.I like it. Time to go clean those vinyls with warm, gentle soap and filtered water and some care.

I bought this player to replace my cheapo chinese Initial 5.8" portable dvd player.  My application is straightforward (no DIVIX, memory sticks, etc), just viewing movies on the plane to kill time.  I tested the units with 3 movies: "Miracle", "In Good Company" and "Black Hawk Down".  Here's my take:1. Build/ qualityThe Toshiba is stunning.  Beautiful metal top and control panel, nice buttons, high quality throughout.  Although my Initial has served me well over the years, it is all plastic and looks dated and junky by comparison.2. DesignI love the fact that the battery fits on the BOTTOM instead of hanging off the back like most portable DVD players.  It's much more compact and seems more secure.  Battery lasted about 4 hours as advertised (very impressive!)  Buttons are intuitive and well placed.  The entire size and weight of the thing is nearly identical to my Initial, despite having a larger 7" screen.3. SoundA little low using the speakers, but I'm primarily a headphone guy so it doesn't really matter.4. PictureHere's the surprising part: the Toshiba is MUCH grainier than my cheapo chinese Initial 5.8" unit.  It almost seems as though they increased the screen size without increasing the pixel count!  Very disappointing.5. ReliabilityI dont' know how well the Toshiba will hold up over time, but I had to exchange the Initial player 3 TIMES before finding a unit that did not freeze up.  Quality control with the no-name brands is spotty at best.  Having found a working unit though, it's been 100% reliable over the years.  The Toshiba unit worked flawlessly out of the box, no freeze ups.THE VERDICT:The Toshiba is a beautiful unit, but I have to say the picture quality leaves something to be desired.  The fact that a cheap chinese brand purchased 2+ years ago has better picture quality is laughable.  In the end I chose to keep it, but the decision was marginal-- really based on the larger screen size and nice compact design (my Initial has too many batteries, chargers, cords and cables to lug around).  I am happy... but not thrilled!

I installed this a couple years ago for my wall mounted flat screen TV. It does it's job well, hiding the cables easily and making for a slick installation. It's against code in most areas to route 110 volt power cables behind a wall (you're supposed to install a new outlet behind the tv to make it legal), so keep that in mind. This is meant for low voltage video and sound cables only. I broke went ahead and broke code and ran my power cable back there anyways, because I didn't have the time to run new electrical wires, but I haven't burnt down the house yet or had any other issues. Honestly, I didn't need the double gang version of this, but it'll come in handy if I ever have to expand or get a TV that needs that many cables.Installation was fairly easy for a DIY person like myself, using a drywall saw and a flat blade screwdriver. I just measured the height of the other outlets for the bottom passthrough, and just eyeballed the height and location of the top one behind the TV. Took about 15 minutes to install and run the cables.

I have looked into numerous options to improve the grip of my Glock.I first tried Decal Grips (that is the brand name) on my Glock 19 which I liked and they stuck on pretty well but even the rubber type was just too rough and uncomfortable for concealed carry so I removed it.This Glock Grip Tape is the second adhesive grip I have tried but it only lasted about 2 weeks before it lost all adhesion and just slid off. And yes, I thoroughly cleaned the grip with 90% isopropyl alcohol before application. If you just put this on your range gun it might last long enough, but not on the gun you wear all day. Also, when it was applied it was not that "grippy." It was smooth; more "plasticy" than "rubbery."I now have a Talon Grip on it, which appears to be made of similar stuff but so far it has been on for a week with no signs of it coming off.Hogue grip is another option, but I am wary of it because they can slip down and impede your ability to change magazines.I would also rather not get the grip stippled because it's permanent and only makes the grip rougher, not more "sticky" like rubber does.I honestly wish this stuff worked because it would be a tremendous value. If it lasted only a few months it would still be a good value since it is inexpensive to replace, but 2 weeks is not worth it.

My waterproof Ipod stopped working one day, about a year after I bought it. It no longer would hold a charge. Now, I don't know if there is another person who loves the ipod shuffle as much as I do. It is my exercise buddy and motivator when working out. I need it OK?  I use it while swimming, lifting weights, while hiking, and walking my dog. Ironically, it ceased to turn on while I was using it in a dry environment during the winter months. I dug up my Square Trade warranty agreement. They had guaranteed my ipod against dropping and wet or dry malfunctioning. A 2 year agreement was only $24.48. I wondered if they would make excuses like so many electronic stores and online auction sites that I had done business with in the past, or would they honor their agreement?I called up Square Trade. Their customer service rep, a gal named Ann was polite and welcoming.  I told Ann about my ipod not working and to my surprise she assured me that she could help. Ann asked me for the following information; if I had purchased the item on Amazon, if I still had the purchase invoice and if I had my square trade agreement. I gave Ann my Square Trade customer number, and emailed her a copy of my original purchase invoice. While I was on the line, Anne immediately verified my purchase and warranty.  Then it happened; without hesitation or excuses, Ann awarded me a refund. Hooray!Now, how can a company please a customer any more than that? Square Trade is the first company that I have ever encountered, whose customer service and follow through is even better than Amazon's own great service. If you want be on the receiving end of a final product developed out of a philosophy of total quality management, then Square Trade is for you. They do what other businesses only talk about and what professors teach students who are earning their MBAs.You see, there is something more that I have not told you about. I received my refund in the form of an Amazon.com giftcard in my email the next day. Do you know what else? The refund came for a dollar amount greater than the actual value of the original Square Trade warranty. I couldn't believe it. I rubbed my eyes and looked again. The increased dollar amount stood there staring back at me unchanged.  Yes! Yes! Yes!Can you guess what I did? I went right over to Amazon.com and purchased yet another waterproof ipod from the same original company, and then I purchased another Square Trade 2 year agreement. I had a little change left over so I bought a silicon soft case for the ipod shuffle and a few books as a gift for a friend of mine.Do you think that I am a happy camper? You bet I am. Square Trade exceeded my expectations. Need I say more?No, but I will; I highly recommend Square Trade to anyone wanting to obtain an extended warranty to safeguard their online purchase.Square Trade offers both 2 and 3 year warranties in both home appliances and electronics.

For example you can shoot over your head to get pictures from behind a crowd or shoot from waist level to get pictures without attracting attention to yourself.Most other digital cameras on the market are powered by a proprietary battery that can cost upwards of $40 and have a typical service life of less than one year. After looking around for a holster for my new Glock 19 Gen 4, I saw this relatively inexpensive IWB holster. After many hours online window shopping at different websites I finally came back to this holster. And I'm glad.I have never owned another holster except for an Uncle Mike's IWB pouch, for a snub nose .38 Smith and Wesson 637 Airweight.However this holster seems to work 100%. It does what it needs to do. It was a bit uncomfortable the first day I wore it.. but once the leather softens a bit and molds it is very comfortable. I have had no problems.The screws that attach the belt clips come loose every so often, so keep a flat head screw driver handy.There are 3 holes to adjust the cant, and ride height of the holster to the belt, and once I dropped the belt clips to the top hole and the holster rode as low as it could, I was very happy.Retention is good. There is an audible snap as the firearm seats into the sheath.Retention perhaps isn't as good as some others. I imagine if you turned it upside down and shake it, the firearm might slip out.BUT an IWB holster uses the pressure of your belt to body to keep the retention.And I have had no problems at all.Overall this is a great purchase, quality materials for the price. I am wearing it now. It really does everything it is supposed to do and is comfortable.

It has certain citrus overtones, which must come from the lemon peel oil, and the ingredients listed include oils and extracts that one might find in a salad and can actually pronounce and recognize if you're familiar with the Latin plant names.As you put a dollop of this synthetic free lotion in the palm of your hand it feels very light, and goes on your skin like a thin cream, absorbing very quickly. This is a great knife for the money, and if used for everyday tasks it is a great, very high quality knife. I am rating it four stars however, because if you get into a situation where you need a very sturdy knife this knife may not be up to the task. I have the serrated version, and just cutting some branches up and cutting through some thick plastic several of the serrations snapped off. The design of the serrations on this knife is not too great for strength. The other reason is the weakness of the tip. Again for light tasks the razor sharp tip on this knife does great, but when I needed to cut through some heavy plastic the very point of the tip snapped off. If you so not plan to do any medium to heavy tasks however this knife is AWESOME.PROS: Insanely fast one hand opening, very sleek and nice looking, very high quality materials, solid lock up, feels great in hand and is very fun to use, open and close.CONS: The serrations round off and snap fairly easily, the tip is very pointy (which can be good) but makes the tip weak and prone to snapping if under any lateral stress.

This thing is excellent. I started out looking for a BDC unit for my 300 blackout upper, but ended up settling on this. The reviews for some of the BDC 300BLK units indicated that the reticle was useless, and for the price I couldn't beat this combo. Spent the afternoon today getting it squared away, and I was very impressed with both the optics and the caliber. I was shooting 1 inch groups easily at 100 yards both supersonic and subsonic (more on that later). Even with the holo sight I was nailing bullseyes consistently. Note that it actually comes with a Fastfire III, not a Fastfire II - which was a welcome surprise as I prefer the 3MOA dot on the Fastfire III.My only complaint is that the center dot on the scope's reticle is a little bigger than I'd prefer - probably 3-4 MOA when zoomed to 4X. I gave it a pass, though since the kind of thing this unit is meant for lends itself to that being acceptable. Yeah, at 1X zoom it would cover a cantaloupe at 100 yards, but if you cover that cantaloupe, it *will* explode when you pull the trigger. And a dead cantaloupe is a good cantaloupe.BONUS - there are four dots on the reticle - three of which extend downward from the primary zero dot in the center. I zeroed that center dot at 100 yards with supersonic 300 blackout match rounds. I then put in some subsonic rounds, and started experimenting. I quickly realized that the bottom of the four dots put the rounds *exactly* on target at 100 yards with subsonic. So I ended up with a great scope that makes full use of the caliber at a great price. This package is a heck of a bargain.

(see update at bottom....)I am a full on Androidian Googlearian (and anti-appletarian), so of course most anything Android or Google I love. I was very excited by the co-star and it was really great while it lasted. Unfortunately, as several others have mentioned, it seems to die if left in a too warm media cabinet...What I loved before it died:1. SUPER easy to set up - easier than Roku if you have a home theater set up. You just stick the HDMI that's currently plugged into the back of your cable box (the one that connects the cable box and the tv) into the co-star, then take the other HDMI cable (that you bought yourself - be sure to do this...) and plug it into the cable box and into the other plug in the back of the co-star (there are two HDMI plugs on the back of the co-star, an in and an out). Of course you plug in the power cord and Ethernet cable (unless you're doing wireless, but we're hardwired for better performance...)and Voila - that's it. the Co-star initiates a set up on the screen that's super easy, and everything you had before the co-star will still work as it did before without modifications. The Roku is 'supposed' to be easy to set up, but because it has to go through your receiver (if you have a home theater set up) that means you have to program the new input so that it works with your system and remote, and that alone creates several extra steps. Plus, you can't turn off the Roku without unplugging it - not cool IMO. Anyways...2. Easy to navigate, mini keyboard on the back makes entering information MUCH easier than playing the 'hunt and search' game for letters to spell things using the basic number inputs on a remote.3. You can turn it off. This is important to me, both to be eco-consious and not run power when not needed, as well as to turn off the wireless that constantly beams out of it to reduce our household EMF load.4. Searching for shows offered by many different sources/providers. Let's say you want some old Brady bunch shows or something - you can type (using the mini keyboard buttons - yay!) Brady bunch and it will search all the providers - your cable/satellite offerings, netflix, amazon instant video, etc. and then show you where you can get it and which ones are free and which ones you need to pay for.What I didn't like:1. Well, obviously that it died after only 3 weeks.2. That there isn't a power light on the front telling you if it's on or off (there is one on the back, but that's not very helpful if it's in a cabinet. You can also tell if it's off if you can't play regular TV, because the pass through doesn't work if it's off.3. That the pass through doesn't work if it's off - I'd like to be able to only have it on if I'm going to use it. Having to have it on when just watching my regular directv stuff is a bummer.4. That you can't really have just one remote for your system, as your universal can't do all the internet stuff, and the co-star remote can't do the receiver stuff other than volume, really...What I'm going to do now:Well, I still don't want to hook Roku up to my home theater set up as it will be a hassle to get everything programmed (and it won't do Amazon streaming). So, I'm going to check out the other devices that offer Google TV (Sony, Logitec, etc.) and likely give a different unit a go. It seems that just over the past few weeks since I bought my co-star there are a lot more critical reviews and the star rating has dropped from when I bought mine (lots of folks having issues it seems). The other Google TV devices have better ratings at this point so even though most are more money, I'm going to give one (or more) of them a try. I'll come and update this review if I end up with one that seems better (or worse...) than my experience with the co-star.Summary: Easy to install and great while it works, but many seem to die early, especially if in a home theater cabinet. Perhaps wait for a 2nd generation that (hopefully) will be better built, or give a different google tv device a try (which is what I'm going to do)PS - There is a guy posting extensively long, repetitive, comments to every negative post (including mine) for the co-star. Just a heads-up that his comments are effusively positive about the device, and sometimes insulting to the reviewer, however the majority of each of his posts are an identical cut/paste promoting a website that he claims will sort out all problems. Beware - something fishy is going on and other reviewers have commented on his comments and expressed the same concerns...Feb 2013 Update: I've ordered the Sony Google TV device, so I will do a comparison after I've had it for awhile and can compare.MARCH 2013 UPDATE:  OK, I've now had the Sony Google internet TV deviceSony NSZ-GS7 Internet Player with Google TVfor a couple weeks and I have to say that I do like it better than the Vizio Co-Star. Here's why:The Remotes:The Sony remote is better - the alpha keyboard on the back lights up (easier to see if the lights are low or off), and for whatever reason the way the keys are laid out on the remote it is easier to type on the Sony's remote keyboard than the Co-star's remote keyboard. The Sony remote just feels better in my hand when using it and the touch pad seems easier to navigate as well. It doesn't have insta-buttons on it for particular apps, such as amazon, etc. as the Co-Star's remote does,  but those apps are very easy to access from the Sony home menu, and the home menu is easily accessed via a little house button on the remote that takes you to home. The channel up and down and volume up and down are located on the side of the remote as well as mute on the Sony, so the remote front and back seems less crowded.The Sony device vs. the Co-Star device:The Sony pass-through works better...You can turn it off and your regular TV set up still works! The way the Co-star's pass through (Your cable/satellite box goes through the Co-star then to the TV) is set up, if you turn off the Co-star NOTHING will work - Your DVR/Cable box can't communicate to your TV. However, with the Sony (which is also a pass-through) if you turn it off everything will still work (yay!)This is great because I only want my internet TV on when I'm using it - why have it running and on if I'm just watching my regular old DVR stuff? I also think the pass-through set up is much easier than the set-up required of the Roku if you have a home theater set up with a receiver (more about that in my earlier review above), plus it enables google TV to search your providers listings as well as the internet. This is a great feature, for example,  it has helped me find a movie that was free on my Directv that I didn't even realize I could watch & would've likely paid money to rent on Amazon otherwise.You can visually see when the Sony device is turned off...The Sony has a light at the front that is on when it's on and off when it's off. The Co-Star does not, so you only know that it's off if your TV system doesn't work.The Sony has a more simple, streamlined user interface...I had read commentary that Vizio was putting alot of boatware and extra stuff on the user interface but I didn't really see what they meant until I switched to the Sony. With the Sony it's pretty simple and clean...you get a menu at the bottom when you press home, the menu has about 5 app shortcuts you can click or you click the icon for the 'all apps' screen and select whichever one you want. Easy, no fuss, not too much stuff in the way, simple, intuitive.The only thing the Vizio has that the Sony doesn't have is that you can still see/hear your live TV when you activate the internet TV menu/interface. With the Sony, when I activate the home menu, the back-screen goes black and sound goes off from the live TV. Now, this doesn't happen with other internet TV programs, only with your cable box stuff. For example, if I have music on pandora, it will keep playing while I pull up the home menu, search the internet, etc., so it only applies to whatever is being fed from your Cable box to the internet TV device.I expect the Sony will be more reliable and won't overheat or breakdown quickly like the Co-Star did. This is based on other reviews for the Sony device plus Sony's overall reputation for quality products.My overall summary:Go for the Sony. The Sony is more user friendly, intuitive, and has a simpler interface. The Sony remote is also nicer and easier to use. The only feature the Co-Star has that the Sony doesn't is the ability to see/hear your 'live' TV feed from your cable box when you activate and go into your internet TV interface.

This is for my iPad 4. To begin, I MUCH prefer my SNUGG case. As a case, I give this just 2 stars at best. As a speaker, I give this 3 stars out of 5. I have hearing loss so I REALLY appreciate that it does allow me to hear distinct instruments in music where normally the sounds blur quite a lot. The sound is crisp, offers great separation and even adjustability of that separation. So why a 3 star rating? Several reasons actually.Again, I have to surrender my much more versatile and protective SNUGG case. I also much prefer theThe OontZ Red Ultra-Portable Wireless Bluetooth Speaker by Cambridge SoundWorksfor mu iPad and other Bluetooth products and prefer myAmazon Basics 3.5mm Aux Portable Speakerfor my older Sandisc MP3 Players and iRiver MP3 players. Grand total of just fifty to 60 bucks for BOTH depending on the price any given day. And I MUCH prefer that with the Amazon Basics I just toss in AA rechargeable batteries on the fly. I am not a fan of proprietary batteries that require me to find a house to plug into & recharge....This is of VERY nice quality and sound wonderful. If & when it gets down to a MUCH more realistic $100.00 price, it may then (and ONLY then) represent some type of value. Again, I like it but only for very specialized use (like at my hot tub) so when it dies, I can recharge it. Too bulky and lacking much protective quality to be used for a case in travel. 3 stars to be fair and I am grateful to have this!UPDATE 8/28/2013:After more time I have had several more incidents I do NOT like.....the worst is the magnetic cover. I have it literally pop off almost ANY time I handle it, vey UNLIKE the Microsoft tablet it mimics. The worst and downright scary problem is the instability of this same cover when attempting to hold the iPad upright when listening to my MP3 songs at my hot tub. I have had this fall over more times than it has NOT....I now take extraordinary measures to make sure the ONLY place it can fall is onto a well place towel away from the water and in no way in danger of landing in thatwater.

I purchased this bottle to fill a role previously filled by some traditional Nalgene bottles. It was convenient to switch to this bottle, as opposed to similar options from other vendors (like Klean Kanteen) because it was more or less a straight swap. Remove the old plastic Nalgene from the pouch and put this in it's place. No having to adjust cords, no worrying that the bottle was different dimensions and would fall out, or change decades of packing habits to account for a new size and shape bottle. As an added bonus - since this was actually designed as a Guyot product - my Guyot Gription fits perfectly, which is not the case with other wide mouth bottles of this size that I tried. I did not test it with other bottle accessories, but trust that other accessories built for traditional plastic Nalgenes will fit this one which is great if you've already spent money on those.I did purchase a Klean Kanteen wide-mouth of a similar size as well for comparison. Both bottles are nice, but with the KK being slightly larger and not being able to use the Gription lid it's not as easy to swap into a routine as the Nalgene stainless product. I also found the Nalgene to be easier to drink with when drinking directly from the bottle, especially in the car. The transition on the Nalgene is sharper, and it also has a longer  and slightly narrower neck, than the KK product and to me that seems to reduce sloshing and make drinking more comfortable than the KK option. I also appreciate that the Nalgene has a tether to keep the cap and bottle together. This is great while using it and a "nice to have" while storing it.The bottle has withstood drops without issue, it's not tippy, and it is easy to keep clean. All good things.Just about the only downside to this bottle is that it's quite expensive which is actually a huge problem. Perhaps this is due to the bottle being discontinued and more difficult to obtain, thus commanding a premium (grab one while you can!) It costs significantly more than Klean Kanteens, which is not a big deal when you order one, but becomes a huge problem when you're trying to outfit yourself and others with multiple bottles. For the price of this bottle + shipping you can actually get two Klean Kanteen wide mouths. Based on what I've seen in using them both, while this bottle is slightly better than the KK offering, it's not twice as good, especially given that many companies are now advertising accessories that fit both Nalgene and Klean Kanteen offerings.If you must have a bottle that is a stainless clone of your existing Nalgene - this is a great buy. However, if you're just looking for a wide-mouth bottle that you can take on the trail with you - consider your options.

I don't wear makeup too often.  But when I do I don't like to sleep in it.  Since water doesn't seem to do the trick, I thought I'd give these a try.They smell good.  A light citrusy scent that wasn't overpowering, even when you were cleaning your face.  But they weren't exactly wet wipes.  They were a little damp, but not very saturated, even when I first opened the brand new pack.  Which I think had an impact on their ability to clean.  I first used them on my face to remove eye makeup. Just some shadow and liner, nothing extreme.  It took off the shadow well but didn't get the liner off.  Intrigued, I tested all the products on my arm and took the wipe to them.  Again, the shadow came off easily but it took some hard scrubbing to get the liner off (a couple of different kinds of pencil eyeliner) and I can't see someone rubbing at their eyes that hard with this wipe.I wasn't overly enthused with the ingredients either.  They aren't terrible, but they aren't great either.  It has Vitamin C and Ginseng, which is good, but then it had a lot of unpronounceables as well.  But at least nothing was tested on animals, which is almost an important decision when buying these types of products.I wouldn't buy these.  I'm sure there's a better product out there that will work on the type of makeup I use.  But for those that want a gentle wipe for light makeup, these may work out ok.Review by M. Reynard 2013

The feel of these sticks are nice and heavy and come in a three pack.  Even though my X58SO motherboard has to be adjusted to recognize 1600 ram they are still very fast.  Keep in mind unless you use 64 bit Vista it doesn't matter how many you use since 32 bit will only recognize about 3 and a half.  These are probably the best ram sticks I have ever owned and with the way they are made heat dissipation is very nice.  Get yourself a good board and get to overclocking to get the most out of these sticks.  Though the price is steep considering three sticks of DDR3 can be found for under a hundred dollars they just aren't as overclock friendly as these are.  I would recommend these to people who are trying to get everything out of their system they have invested in.  Why would you get all awesome parts in your rig only to drop cheap ram into it (by that I mean the green sticks of ram that come factory with most computers).  I would say casual computer people shouldn't invest and these are probably only for those who want to push quality and performance.  Most people don't think about the quality of RAM that they have so its not a necessity for them to have the best.  Overall nice addition to my self made rig and so far everything is super fast and I am very happy with this RAM.

First just a note, I own a fair amount of Sennheiser headphones, but no earbuds.  I also consider myself a bit of an audiophile.  Amazon had these as their deal of the day and I think I paid $50 for them - so significantly less than the current price.DesignI'm really surprised by the design decisions the company made.  First the good... the plug is angled.  Second... the cord is not obnoxiously long or stiff.  Third... the earbud adapters are soft and feel comfortable in your ear.  Confusingly they made the volume control obnoxiously large... seriously it must be an inch and a half.  It is "plasticy" to the feel and is heavy enough to "dangle" from the earbuds... oddly flopping back and forth on your shirt as you walk.  Second, the provided case (a necessary accessory for expensive earbuds) is ridiculously large.  When I first saw it I figured it must be because the volume control is so big... but laughably, the case is actually filled with an "oddly" shaped design effectively doubling the volume and halving the usable volume.  The odd design makes it feel large in your pocket.  It is larger than my wallet.  It has an odd "wrap around" design, similar to the cheap case you would get for free with a make-up purchase.  What is truly ridiculous is after about 10 tries I can't fit the earbuds in the case.  Note, I found a small Altoids gum case fits the earbuds perfectly (2"x1"x1/2").  Anyway...SoundI don't know... fine I guess.  High highs, over-balanced lows... squished mid-range, but better than my other sets.  Nothing hear that makes me think I would want to pay too much extra for these.I wouldn't recommend unless you got them in the $50 range and won't be bothered by the volume control or need for a custom somewhat large case.

The scope itself is advertised as being fog, shock,and water proof. I haven't dropped it yet but I have used it in light rain and humid conditions. After four years of use I don't see any adverse affects from being exposed to wet weather. Comes with lens covers and sunshade.Turning the (45 degree angle) eyepiece adjusts the magnification from 20-60X. The focus is situated down and to the right of the eye piece. In overcast (lower light) conditions using full magnification, objects are not inconveniently dark but there is a noticeable difference in brightness.Tripod height(collapsed): 16 inchesTripod height (fully extended): 48 inches.With the scope attached and tripod fully extended one has to bend over to use the scope from a standing position. To use the scope while laying down (tripod fully collapsed) the user needs to bunch on the elbows to peer through eyepiece. The most convenient feature about this tripod is that one can adjust the scope over the right so it is situated at a 90 degree angle. In other words the scope is "kicked" over so the eye piece sits on a horizontal plane rather than vertical.The scope and tripod come in a low quality padded traveler case that "gets the job done" adequately. The entire unit itself is light and portable.Bottom line: If you have high expectations, namely clarity and brightness at full magnification in low light conditions, you will be disappointed. In my very limited experience with spotting, to achieve the latter you simply need buy a more expensive scope.My application of this spotter: I purchased it for everyday target spotting from 25 to 200 yards. I can easily see .22 caliber holes at 100 yards. I have not tested this yet, but I imagine thirty caliber would be visible at 200 yards depending on the type of target used. I don't recommend this unit for  competition however if this was all that was available it would be fine. It wouldn't be the most convenient or comfortable but it could be made to work. I'm very glad I purchased it and if I had to do it all over again I would make the same choice. If you're a regular "plinker" at the range and want to save yourself a lot of time and effort from checking targets this scope is for you!Summary: For the given price this scope can't be beat. This is an EXCELLENT spotting unit and I highly recommend it if you have a limited budget. This is a great tool to have for common everyday usage.

I was initially torn about needing something like this.  Thing is, I like to do a lot of hiking and camping.  Typically I'd use my old Garmin GPS with its LCD screen and AA batteries for my navigation.  However, with some of the excellent hiking apps out there, I've found myself relying more on my iPhone for navigation duty, and it works well with some significant advantages over my 15 year old Garmin.  Thing is, those iPhones run through their batteries like crazy.Then this device hit my radar and I'm very glad it did.  The footprint on this isn't so large that making room for it in my ruck is a hassle, and it really is slim - the size of a small paperback book.  It does have some weight to it (which is a welcome thing, I think...beware of manufacturers claiming outrageous capacities that are really light) so you'll notice it in your pack.  But for multiday hikes it's just perfect for when you find yourself away from Normal and need to charge your device.I'm able to get 9-10 iPhone charges out of it before it runs out of power, so for those long hikes I think the sheer power capacity justifies the weight.  I won't rehash the technicals that other reviewers have gone through.  I'll just say that this device offers serious value and advantages for the money.  It's well built and solid.  If you and your hiking buddies have need for remote charging of devices far away from any power outlet, this is the item to buy.

I listen to lots of lengthy files such as podcasts and (free from our library) downloaded audiobooks.  When you pause for a while, you want to find the exact same spot in a lengthy track, so you don't have to search around.  The "bookmark" feature, increasingly rare among MP3 players, is present in this model - and it bookmarks automatically!  NOTE: you won't find this automatic bookmarking feature listed in its features nor in its instruction "manual" (which comes on a supplied 3" CDRom). I do assure you it is there.  If you're like me and alternate among many books and podcasts, and you'll like how it memorizes multiple bookmark spots (at least 7 or 8 and then I gave up counting!).You take advantage of the Fuze's "bookmark" feature by putting such files in its PODCAST folder or its AUDIOBOOKS folder.  When you put your - presumably shorter - music selections on the unit, be sure to put them in the MUSIC folder.As icing on the cake, you can speed up or s l o w  d o w n playback of podcasts and audiobooks assigned to their respective folders.Enjoy the FUZE! It's a well-thought-out player.

Love the color and the duration of time that it stays in your hair.  I bleach my hair to a pretty light color.  There are two different tones of blond in my hair.  One is a yellowish blond...light yellow, and one is almost white blond.  The light yellow blond keeps the color so well I might touch up the color ever other month.  By the time you are faded out enough to color it again, you really shoud be bleaching the roots again any way.  The super light/white blond doesn't keep the color is as long.  It's dry and damaged and just can't hold the color as well.  The more dry and damaged the hair, the less it can hold the color.  BUT on the other hand, if you hair is smooth and not processed at all it wont absorb the color well.  I used to think that the dryer it was the better it took the color.  And that is true till you hit a line.  Once your hair is so damaged and dry it simply just cannot hold the color as well.  That being said, I still only have to dye it once a month and don't need to touch up the color between bleaching the roots.  That's some pretty good dye, as I've tried ALOT of different brands.  Nothing has ever stayed in like this has. And when I've gone months without touching up the color, and not bleaching my roots it fades in to (on the white blond hair) a lovely icy mint looking color.  Like a super 50's retro color.  And on the yellowish blond it fades like you would expect from a dark blue/aquamarine to picking up more green tints every week or so.

My dad carried one of these in Vietnam for about eighteen months , and he was an infantry grunt so it saw some rough use. He won't admit to using it as a weapon, but has always spoke highly of it as an all-purpose multi-tool that was an indispensable part of life in the field. In fact, he usually only mentions the knife when other guys in camp are showing off their latest, greatest Leatherman-type thing. Due to serving in the ridiculously high humidity of Vietnam, the leather on my dad's knife and sheath had discolored and picked up a strong mildew scent, which I fondly remember from the few times I was allowed to handle that knife during my childhood. Stinky or not, my dad loved that knife , and so did I.A few years back , my dad quit carrying his Kabar on our hunting and fishing trips, claiming it held too many memories to risk losing. He told me he wanted to pass it down to me, and wanted me to pass it along to my son someday. Once he retired it, he tried a few more modern style blades and but occasionally wished out loud for his old Kabar , usually when he needed a prybar, chopper, or screwdriver ...something his new high-tech folder wasn't suitable for .Meanwhile, I am realizing that since the original knife is still in production and reasonably affordable , I don't have to wait to inherit one. I can buy one (!) , albeit one without a family history behind it. Great price on Amazon,decent quality on the knife and sheath. A light touch of the fine stone had it razor sharp , and ready for our autumn hunting camp. First chance I get , I whip that thing out in front of dad and start hammering away with it on a can of corned beef hash. He shot off his stump and said "lemme see that!" and reached for it...right about the time I reached for the SECOND one I had bought, as a gift to him.I have given a lot of gifts in my life,and some cost a whole lot more than 50 dollars. Never got more bang for my buck , ever. When the sad day comes that I inherit the original, I will remember the look on my dad's face when I handed him that "replacement" knife. We aren't real emotional guys, but it was a moment. I got a kick out of watching dad strap that thing on and look around for something to hack, pry , or pound on with it. He got his chance soon enough, and pronounced it a fair and suitable stand-in for his Old Faithful.This is a mass-produced knife, made to be tough, efficient ,and somewhat cheap to build . It sharpens easily, holds an edge pretty well, and is a tested tough design that won't fall apart on you when you need it most. It's a big mother for carrying in town , but perfect for hunting , fishing, camping, etc. I like it because I can beat the crap out of it and not feel guilty, like I would my nice Buck 6" fixed blade, or expensive S.O.G. folders. This is a rough tool made for rough use. If you need a tool like this, you could do a LOT worse than this choice, especially for around 50 bucks. Great gift for ANY veteran , Marine or otherwise.
