I have shoulder length slightly wavy, very fine hair-- and a lot of it. And although I have a lot of hair, my biggest problem is flat hair because it is so fine. I've spent a lot of money on products to help increase volume, but I really hadn't found anything that worked all day.A friend was telling me about all the bad things in regular shampoo, so I decided to switch to all natural products. I did some research and decided to initially try the home made stuff--- water, castile soap, and essential oils = shampoo and diluted apple cider vinegar for the conditioner. I'll say the conditioner was better than the shampoo, but it wasn't really doing it for me so I decided to switch again. I tried a combo of three products: JR Liggett's bar shampoo, Dr. Bonners citrus conditioner rinse, and Dr. Bonner's Lavender and coconut conditioner and styling creme.They all came at different times (only days apart), but I got to try the Lavender and Coconut hair creme first with my homemade products. I used just a tiny drop, but it really did make a huge difference in the smoothness and softness of my hair. It also has a pleasant, but not over powering smell. I really didn't see any difference in the volume of my hair thoughNext came the bar shampoo-- big improvement over the handmade shampoo and in combination with the dr. bonner hair creme my hair was even softer. The bar shampoo says it can be used without any other products-- eh. I tried it without the conditioning products, but it isn't something I'd do again. My hair wasn't stringy but it had a very strange texture-- not very soft. Still not a lot of increase in volume.Next came the Dr. Bonner Citrus Rinse. Awesome. The combination of all three products definitely seems to be the winner. It is important to use all the products according to the package instructions or your hair may not be exactly what you want it to be, but when it's used properly-- great results. My hair is soft, smooth, feels thicker and it typically looks and feels even better the next day.. and the day after that. No more problems with oily hair and I haven't had any dandruff. As for volume, I have seen an improvement. I even wore my hair in a ponytail for work, wore it up all day, and then took it down when I went to sleep. When I woke up-- volume! That has never, ever, ever happened before.It is just worth mentioning that I did try to go without the hair creme and just use the bar soap and the citrus rinse, but my hair wasn't as smooth and soft as before. It wasn't a HUGE difference, but it was noticeable to me.Also, be aware that the switch did make my hair feel different in a very indescribable way. It isn't bad at all, but it is a little different. I think it feels healthier, thicker and I'm just not used to it.

This hair wrap is such a great invention.  It's basically a headscarf with compartments that provide a place for gel pack inserts so that you can deep condition your hair without being tied to a bulky hairdryer. I purchased this product because it was suggested on a natural hair blog and because, as a student, I didn't have time to sit under a dryer all day. I had to study! This product was definitely the solution.To use this hair wrap, all you have to do is microwave the gel packs per the instructions written on each pack and then put them into the the wrap's compartments. Then you fasten it on your head and you are free to go about your business for about 30 minutes of deep conditioning. After 30 minutes, the heat is gone. I prefer to boil water and place the gel packs in it for 5 minutes rather than using the microwave because I have heard that the inserts can burst if heated too long in the microwave. I have never had a problem with boiled water method. When using this method, I even find that the wrap is often too hot to put on my head so I have to let it cool a bit before wearing it.I have two reasons for giving this product 4 stars instead of 5. The first is that when you put the wrap on your head, part of your head will not come into contact with the heat. You have to reheat the gel packs and then turn the wrap around to condition your entire head and that's just annoying. The second reason is because the material is really abrasive for your hair. You definitely can't just put the wrap on your hair without some sort ofElastic Plastic Processing Capto protect your hair. Even when just the edges of my hair touch the wrap, I notice some breakage. I just have to be extra careful to make sure that my entire hairline is under the plastic cap when I use this hair wrap.That said, I would suggest this product because it has made my life easier and served its intended purpose. Just make sure that you carefully heat the gel packs and wear a processing cap this hair wrap. Happy conditioning!

I have a small stack of camp chairs in my garage -- got some as gifts, got some in raffles, etc. I use them frequently, at the beach, while camping, or at outdoor concerts / tailgating. They're handy for their utility and portability. However, I've NEVER seen or used one as elaborate and as 'baller' as this chair. This thing is serious.Pros:+ built-in footrest? Amazing.+ built-in cooler pocket on the right armrest, with room for ~3 beers? Amazing.+ built-in bottle opener in the front of the seat, with a zipper pouch for storage? Amazing.+ built-in additional storage pouch and drink holder on the left armrest.+ built-in adjustable, collapsible shade umbrella? What an awesome idea.+ no assembly, easily collapsible and fits nicely in its own bag.+ heavy-duty construction, with reinforcing bands of webbing, rugged plastic/metal frame components.Cons:- somewhat heavier and bulkier than simpler camp chairs- the umbrella isn't broad enough to provide rain coverage. Also, while it's adjustable, it's quirky in its orientation, making it tough to properly get shade at midday.Bottom line:With this chair, you'll be the baller of the tailgate / beach party. Storage, shade, comfort, and drink storage make this the ultimate collapsible / camp chair. It's a bit ridiculous looking, but I think in a good way. Maybe a bit extravagant for many, but if you find yourself using these kinds of chairs often, this might be for you. I enjoyed this at the beach and expect to get a lot of enjoyment out of it.

If you want something that is inexpensive and to keep an eye for movement, this is ideal. It doesn't have the resolution to see a license plate at 100 yards but for general movement, day or night, its great. I have used it for a week to keep an eye out over the parking lot and the yard around my house. During the day I can make out a human form over 500 yards away, I see colors of cars and trucks at the same distance. Night time in a well lit parking lot I can see human forms at 300 yards but since it changes to black and white I can tell the difference between car and truck. In straight dark, I can only see about 10 yards as that is the distance of the infrared bulbs but those that are mounted close to the doors it sees just fine. With a little fiddling you can use the remote systems by just typing in the IP address in the address bar. I installed TeamViewer and can see my video on my Windows 8 phone, it will have about a 3 second delay doing this but since there is no app for Windows 8 phone yet, it works just fine. On my android tablet I have an app, so I share my internet from my Windows 8 phone and can see it that way as well. Later I will get a motorized camera to go with this system and see how I can control it then. Over all its easy and quick to install, minimal requirements and your on the net. Out of the box everything is included for at least a 60ft run, anything over that you will have to buy little things, like adapter to change RCA plug for power feed to two wire adapter and Coaxial cable adapters so you can put ends on bulk coax wire.

I like it!  Bought this after checking out the Nikon P510 (and a newer comparable model) and the FZ200. Price was right. Have been using Nikon pro-level SLRs (film) and digital for as long as I can remember, wanted something smaller/lighter for those times when I don't want to lug around a larger body.The Lumix is great. Many features, accessing them using menu system quite intuitive, or at least easily accomplished with a quick check of the "advanced features" manual/file (downloaded as PDF). The Leica lens is quite sharp, reasonably fast. I don't have anything but favorable things to say re: lens sharpness, freedom from distortion, and so on. I suspect that although the lens is marked Leica, optically it's probably not on par with more advanced Leica lenses, and I doubt Leica would harm its reputation by putting out a bad lens with their brand name on it.One major limitation (at least for me): No RAW file output. Nonetheless, the hi-res JPEG images are great, so long as you don't keep editing and repeatedly saving the edited files. (Edit copies of the JPEGs, not the originals.)Low light focusing a bit slow, but nothing insurmountable, although none of the 100+ photos I took the first weekend the camera was in my possession were of fast action. Zooming in or out seems sort of tentative: zoom goes slowly at first, then suddenly (it seems) continues to zoom in or out as operator desired.  The lens might actually benefit from slower but less tentative or jerky changes of focal lenght.Viewfinder (as opposed to LCD, to which one can easily switch) takes a bit of getting used to, as once a photo is taken the stilled image of the photo remains in the viewfinder for several moments before returning to a live view. The nature of the beast, I guess. On the other hand, there seemed to be exceedingly little shutter lag.One reason why I opted for this as opposed to, say, the Nikon Coolpix P510, is that the front of the lens is threaded for various photo filters, the most important of which to me is a polarizer. Sadly, and it was a deal-braker for me re: the Nikon, the Coolpix lacked filter threads on the front of the lens. And, unfortunately (and in contrast with the FZ60's more expensive sibling, the 200), no lens shade was provided. However, appropriately sized snap on hoods are readily available and for not much money.While I said I was pleased that the Leica lens has front threads for filters (whereas the Nikon does not), I should mention that I think the threads on the FZ-60 weren't deep enough. A polarizing filter indeed screws on, but if rotating it counterclockwise the shallow threads make it easy for the filter to unscrew completely and fall if not being held securely..The Lumix IS light, and although I have somewhat large hands, it poses no problems with grip or manipulating the controls.In short, I'm highly pleased with this little Lumix, especially considering its feature set and the price. I can't think of (nor have found) any issues with this camera that would lead me to give it a poorer recommendation. Indeed, I probably short-changed the camera with a 4 star rating. No regrets whatsoever about the purchase.

This router is a nice piece of hardware, too bad that Asus appears unable to write stable firmware for it.  However, there is alternative firmware that has fixed many bugs that Asus apparently could not, that makes this a fast and reliable router.  The alternative router firmware project is hosted at [...], also referred to as "Padavan's firmware".  I upgraded to the latest version of this firmware as soon as I got the router, and everything is working very well, very solid.  IPSec Passthrough is turned on so that I can use my work VPN, and "Hardware NAT" is also turned on at the same time, which Asus couldn't seem to manage in their firmware.  The router was easy to configure, it was only a few minutes from taking it out of the box to having a connection to the internet up and running.  The 2.4GHz. wireless range is great, covers my house.The only drawbacks that I've run into so far are that IPv6 is not currently supported, and there is no guest network.  There's work going on right now to get IPv6 support into the alternative firmware, but this work is still ongoing.  A guest network is currently missing in action.  So, if you're concerned about IPv6 right now, and need a guest network, you might want to look at the Asus RT-N66U instead.  Again, with this newer router, Asus has not been able to produce stable firmware that fully supports the router's feature set, but there is tomato firmware that apparently works well on it.Update 3/7/2013: IPv6 is now working, Asus firmware version .342 is now working well.  There is still no guest network, but this is a fast router with solid wireless performance with the new Asus firmware.

Having kids in the house, I did a TON of research trying to find the best safe for my handgun. I had 2 priorities. My first priority was keeping the gun locked up away from the kids. My second priority was having a reliable safe next to my bed that I could get to in a hurry. For me, it came down with this V Line safe or a biometric safe that relied on fingerprint scanning. I ended up choosing the V Line safe and I have no regrets.Reasons why I decided against other safes:I decided not to purchase other safes that relied on keys because didn't want the chance of my kids finding the keys when they got older. I decided not to go with the Biometric safes because I've read reviews where there was an error rate when the safe didn't accept the programmed fingerprint and failed to open AND when the safe would open for someone who's fingerprint was not programmed. This was something that I totally couldn't accept. I'm sure in the future biometric safes will become more reliable as the technology for it advances. Unfortunately, that time is not here yet. I also didn't want to rely on a safe that relied on batteries or electricity.Reasons why I chose the V-Line Safe:The V-Line safe uses the Simplex lock system which I love. The safe has 1081 possible combinations so that satisfied my first priority. It is also easy to program, can be accessed in a hurry and does not rely on any battery or electricity which satisfied my second priority. It's the most reliable in my opinion.

UPDATE, Sep. 19, 2013: Nearly a year after this review, the iPad streaming still works, but has become a bit glitchy.  I'm chalking it up to iOS 7 (I'm now getting warnings that the Apple HDMI adapter is not compatible).  See the comments thread for some information about how I still manage to get iPad streaming to work with this projector.  It's not as simple as it once was, but it still works.UPDATE: Thanks to commenters for clarifying...this ACCEPTS 720p input.  This does not PROJECT in 720p.  Take the time to read the comments attached to this review.Before I got this, I read a lot of forums where people speculated about the quality to expect.  They said things like "don't expect better than 480p resolution" and "at best, this will only work in a very dark room".  Well I am happy to say that "they" were wrong.  This is not an awesome projector, but it is awesome FOR THE PRICE.There are 4 reasons for me to recommend this:1) It's bright enough to use during the day.  Right now, it's 4:30pm, on an overcast day, and daylight is entering my two south-facing living room windows.  With the projector 9 feet from a wall directly opposite the windows (giving me an image that is roughly 6 feet across), the image is acceptable.  Pull the curtains, and the image is better.  Wait until night time?  Better still.  The point is, although this is not a high-powered projector (it couldn't possibly be, for the price), it's quite acceptable.  Are the kids having a sleepover?  This would be a great way to entertain them.2) The Roku Streaming Stick setup was as smooth as could be.  In fact, I did all the setup without even opening the manual.  Since I already have a Roku box, setting this up was even easier.  Once I logged in with my Roku account, the setup included putting all the same channels on the stick that were already on the box.  I also have the iPhone app that lets me control my Roku box.  I tried the app with the Roku stick, and it worked flawlessly.  Really, this could not have been an easier process.  (Side note: Even though it put the Netflix, Amazon, and Hulu channels on there for me, I still need to go through the one-time "linking" of each account with the stick.)3) The output is 720p.  At first, I doubted this...the projector started up in 480p mode.  But as soon as I connected the streaming stick, it automatically switched to the higher-resolution 720p mode. [UPDATE...this accepts inputs up to 720p...but output is NOT 720p.  Be sure to read the comment thread after this review.]4) It works with my iPad!  This was a pleasant surprise.  Knowing nothing about the Roku streaming stick, I had expected that the stick had a USB interface.  I was wrong.  It has an HDMI interface.  So, I wondered, since I have an HDMI connector for my iPad: Could I connect the iPad to the projector?  The answer: YES!  I was able to mirror my iPad on the 3M projector.  In fact, I tried the Netflix iPad app, and it worked perfectly (including pushing the audio through to the projector).My ONLY complaint is that the sound from the projector is too quiet.  For something that is supposed to be able to project a large image, one might expect that the sound would be comparable.  It is not.  In fact, the projector has a cooling fan that runs all the time, and I can actually hear the fan over the audio.  Fortunately, there is a 3.5mm jack that can be used for external speakers or for headphones.Don't expect to be blown away, and you won't be disappointed.  This is a handy little projector, with better than expected versatility and image quality.  Definitely good for the price!

Recent reviewers have spelled out in fine detail the benefits of the T505.I have been testing cell phones and peripherals since 2002. Every bluetooth earpiece dramatically distorted or added echo to the conversation.This solved that problem!I bought this unit around July 2008 and wore it out! (to avoid this: see CON below).  I am buying a replacement today at almost half price of my first.-PROS-This visor unit solves significant PROBLEMs that ear pieces have:1. Sound quality: many you speak with can't tell you're  using it -2. Safety & Convenient to all parties:Hear just like when talking to someone face to face. Microphone in front.This add safety and does not block your hearing in left or right ear.3. Near immediate automatic no-fuss pairing / sync-ing with your phone4. Flawless redial, voice activation - zero handling of phone needed.5. Volume is adequate in any noise level that I've encountered.Even front and back seat passengers can join the conversation.-NEUTRAL-1. The FM benefit was never a dramatic benefit in my 2008 van. It's fun to try.The button to turn it on is easily hit by accident when you move or activate this unit. You can revert to normal operation by a 'simple' sequence which I never remember: I just cycle the power off and on.  It's smart enough to change frequencies on long drives. This extra feature requires human intervention so I eliminated the middleman by using only the device. Would buy the cheaper little brother T305 except for a reviewer's complaint of blue light and lack of the FM feature!2. Bluetooth 2.0 makes this great, EXCEPT when you are away from your car, AND within range, this unit will faithfully answer your phone almost 100 feet from you and your phone. You need to train yourself to TURN OFF unit or your phone's bluetooth when stepping out of car unless you're sure you're out of range. You'll quickly learn to find your phone menu option to abandon bluetooth connection.-CON-The "in-car" charger has a coiled cable that will extend from outlet to visor.DOING THIS DESTROYED THE UNIT OVER TIME. The tension slowly broke the connector inside the unit free from the circuit board.  When I opened it to resolder it. (a big mistake) I found it is all surface mounted with the bare minimum of glue and copper cladding ... no base nor surface area for even the finest solder skills. I never got it back together. SOLUTION: remove from visor to relieve tension.This makes a great gift for any loved one you'd like to keep alive... including yourself!!

I guess I'm a bit of a bag junkie. For the longest time I had a Timbuk2 messenger bag, and it served me well. In fact, I carried that bag for about 6 years. Then, as backpacks got better and better (that, and I wanted to carry a (legal) concealed pistol) I realized that the messenger bag wasn't really cutting it. So, I went to a 5.11 COVRT backpack. It's awesome, and I still have it and use it from time to time.So that's the background. Recently I changed jobs and my requirements in a bag changed as well. I needed something a little more professional than a backpack, I needed something that would conceal a pistol, and rather than carrying a full-sized laptop I needed something I could slip an iPad into.So I looked and looked, and it came down to another messenger bag, or one of the Maxpedition bags. Since I had done the messenger bag thing, I researched the Maxpedition stuff and landed on the Jumbo EDC. And from a bag junkie I can tell you that I absolutely love it. I actually like the Jumbo LEO for the phone holder, but I had no use for the M4 mag holder. I'm glad I got the EDC version because I use the water bottle holder daily, and I added a 5.11 phone holder to the webbing on the right side so I'm not missing that feature of the LEO version.I was concerned that it would look a little bit like a murse/man bag, but in reality it's just a small messenger bag. I love the look of it. It's easy to carry and very comfortable. I keep the following in it:- iPad- Checkbook- Multi-tool and some pens- Small powerpoint remote control- Small Maxpedition bag with phone charger and various cables.- Travel mug (in water bottle carrier)- Sunglasses and cleaning cloth (in top zippered compartment)- Small notebook- Folded bandana- Sig P229 and 1 extra mag (in concealment compartment)All this and i still have room for a book or other small items. Now to be sure: This is a small bag compared to the Gearslingers or backpacks. If you are wanting to take everything and the kitchen sink this bag will probably disappoint you.  But there's an old saying that the problem with a large bag is that you'll fill it up, meaning that we humans have the uncanny ability to pack whatever we have to the gills, and that's not always a good thing.  And for that reason I love this bag: it keeps me honest (and in the process doesn't break my shoulder). If I can't fit it in the bag, something else needs to come out, or I need to keep it somewhere else.I have no complaints about this bag, at all. None. It's well made, well designed, and the price point is pretty reasonable for what you get. I've been carrying this bag with me from home to my office every day for almost 4 months, and on some weekend trips as well, and it's just great. I love being able to grab one bag and know I have everything I need. 5 big stars for the Maxpedition Jumbo EDC!

My wife is in charge of phones in our home.  She put us onto Vonage a few years ago and we were happy with the service - I was responsible for setting it up and it was a bit of an undertaking to get the Vonage router working behind my main router, but I got it going. When she investigated MagicJack and decided to give it a go, I agreed (saving over $400 a year on phone service was hard to ignore).  But I knew I would be the one responsible for the setup/tech end.  I bought the MagicJack at Fry's Electronics as I had a few hours available to do the install, still recalling my Vonage setup.I read the most helpful post on this product link here at Amazon and it looked like this was worth a shot.Setup was simple, following the very simplistic instructions in the package.  Plug the MagicJack into my laptop, log onto MagicJack and create an account.  I had to choose a phone number to be assigned to the MagicJack as part of the account setup.  The account setup took less than 15 minutes.Then I took the MagicJack out of my laptop USB port.  The kit comes with a standard two prong plug with a USB port on the opposite end.  Plug the two prongs into a power outlet and plug the MagicJack into the USB connection and you are powered up.  No trick to that.The MagicJack itself has the aforementioned USB connector on one end and on the other end are a standard phone line port and an ethernet port.  I wanted to plug in a several year old VTech base phone and a Caption Call phone so that both could access the phone service.  All it took was this: I plugged a telephone cable into the MagicJack at one end and plugged in an old $2.00 three plug telephone adapter at the other end.  Then I plugged the VTech base unit and the Caption Call phone into the adapter.  Now my phones were connected.Lastly, I had to connect the MagicJack to the internet using an ethernet cable.  I didn't run the ethernet directly to my router (which is a NetGear N600 WNDR3700) but into a 4 port switch which is connected to my router via a 25 feet of cabling -- so I did not have to physically plug the MagicJack into my router, just into a pathway to my router.After this was complete, my wife went back into the MagicJack account to see about call forwarding.  This was different from Vonage.  Vonage let her call forward calls to her cell phone, but with Vonage, the main number rang 4 times before forwarding so that the original number can be answered or the call will go to the cell if not answered.  With MagicJack, the calls are forwarded without the main number ringing so there is no way to answer at the main number, only at the cell phone.  There was as second option, an answering service.  She opted to have any unanswered calls go to an answering mode which sends an e-mail with a WAV sound file so you can listen to the messages remotely.  She opted for the second route because we could listen to messages when traveling abroad where call forwarding to her cell wouldn't work.All in all we are happy with the quality (we can't discern any difference from Vonage quality).  I do have cable internet service.Voila.  Both phones worked.  The VTech base unit lets me run remote wireless VTech phones from several rooms in the house.  The Caption Call phone is a hard wired unit, no wireless connections.After testing the unit for a couple of days, we decided to go ahead and transfer our phone number of 8 years to the MagicJack.  My wife did that transfer online with MagicJack.  We kept the onage account alive until the transfer was complete, about a week.  We received e-mail notification that the transfer was complete and we had to unplug the MagicJack for 60 seconds and plug it back in to reset it to the transferred number.  No muss, no fuss.

I had Sennheiser HD497 for over a year which I loved except for that its Bass performance was not good.Then came the SR60, which I soon exchanged for SR80 (because of its superior Bass performance) and also bought a Total Bithead Headphone amp and I absolutely love the setup.Q. How do you know a good can from bad??Ans. I use a simple solution that works for me.1. Firstly, Close your eyes while listening to music.2. Put a rather noise song (something that has many instruments playing simultaneously) and try to figure out--- diffrentiate exactly what instruments are playing-- in which ear and at what pitch level they are playing?-- how audible the soft ones like bells and chimes are?-- How defined, spacial and NATURAL sounding the Bass is? (There are many people for whom loud Boom-Boom Bass means good Bass. What they fail to understand is how NATURAL it sounds is more important.)-- How the overall experience is?Do this much with a Grado and you are sure to take one home.

As many people state at the start of their reviews, I will too. I had an iPod for several years and was never dissatisfied with its performance. Thus, the Zune had a lot to live up to in my eyes.I believe that the program used on the computer in order to load music is perhaps the most important part of an mp3 player. After using iTunes for years, I knew what I wanted in the Zune program. I ALMOST got it. For the average user, I believe that the Zune program is flawless. It has a beautiful layout, has podcast subscriptions, mp3, m4a AND wma file compatibility, and functions well with the mp3 player. However I have a few complaints. I am an organization freak. I like to have all of my music in the genre that I believe it is, not that of the internet. So when I rip a CD I will change the information accordingly. With the Zune program it takes more time because (at least that I find) it is not possible to edit the information for multiple items at once. You can just type in each one by one, or go find the files and do it through properties on windows explorer. That is my biggest problem; there is a lack of ability when it comes to editing the information that songs have. As for syncing with the Zune, there is one weird thing that happens where it keeps "adding" the same group of songs every time I connect my Zune. I read another review in which the person had the same issue. It doesn't add the songs again, it simply says it does. Kind of strange.Onto the player itself. First of all the controls are wonderful. At first I was curious if I would be annoyed at having to flick my thumb a bunch to find the artist I was looking for. This never has happened. It is force sensitive, meaning the faster I flick it the further it goes. Or, if I don't want to flick my thumb, I can simply hold down the pad in the direction I want to go, great idea. The interface in the Zune is great as well. There are functions that let you go to the artist of the song you are currently listening to and other little things that make navigating through it a little easier than the iPod.The screen is great for viewing pictures as well as watching video mainly because it is big. At least compared to the iPod's screen. Also when viewing pictures, there is a zoom option, which I do not believe the iPod has.The FM radio is a great little bonus. Just in case one ever wants to listen to their favorite talk show on the walk home, or anything such as that, it's there. I haven't used it much, but its reception and quality seem great to me.Sound quality is something I have had no problem with, even without the EQ that was apparently on the original Zune. I rather dislike the quality of the in ear headphones that came with it, but I have my over ear phones, and it sounds wonderful through those.I have nothing to say about the "social" feature because no one I know owns a Zune. Hopefully they will gain popularity so I can try out the feature, however I don't think I would put it to much use, because the song only stays for 3 plays, which isn't saying much.The Zune met and exceeded my expectation for it in many ways. Hopefully program bugs will get sorted out soon and maybe a little bit more functionality. Other than that I love the Zune player and will not stray from it any time soon.

I became tired of wanting to pull my (non-existent) hair out of my head whenever it was time to adjust my cantilever brakes, so finally I took my bike to a bicycle shop so I could see what method they used for adjusting brakes (namely tightening). I saw the mechanic pull out Park Tool's version of this, and he had my brakes tightened and adjusted in less than a minute. I was floored!Cantilever brakes can be finicky. I would try to adjust them by using just regular old pliers and patience, but my patience was very rarely rewarded. It's a pain to pull the cable at just the right tension, hold the pliers with one hand, and screw the anchor bolt with the other, only to give it a test run and find out the brakes are way too squishy, usually so bad that barrel adjustments (typically used for fine tuning) wouldn't really help all that much. Typically this means a retry, so I would try to cinch it down a little tighter, only to go too tight and find that my pad is rubbing against my rim. Maybe I'm just mechanically inept, but sometimes I would spend upwards of a half an hour just to try and get the brakes just right, only to give up and settle for squishy brakes.I discovered that this tool is called a 'fourth hand tool' (or simply 'cable puller'). I didn't want to spend $45 at the shop for one of these, so after doing a little searching on Amazon I found this one for $13. WHAT A GODSEND!Sure, the name is stupid (IceToolz...?) but I bet it's every bit as functional as Park Tool's version. It may not be quite as rugged, but I bet this thing will still last for years. This isn't the type of tool you need to pull out every week, and it doesn't undergo a lot of stress when you do use it.It may seem a bit confusing to use at first, but once you figure it out you'll be amazed at how easy cable adjustments can be. When I used it the first time it almost seemed *too* easy. The beauty in this thing is that it allows you to set cable tension to where you like it, lock it in place at that tension, remove your hand from the tool (so you can make sure you still have clearance for the wheel, for instance) and then make micro adjustments to get the tension exactly where you need it.This tool is also great for setting the cable tension on your front derailleur. Setting a RD isn't too hard without one of these, but FDs tend to be a bit more of a pain.If adjusting your FD then just set your FD in the lowest gear (smallest chainring). Use this tool to pull the tension, squeeze until the FD begins to shift, then just back off a bit and anchor your cable. Voila! Setting the FD tension couldn't be easier!

I am transitioning to natural and wear my hair in twist outs or wet sets most of the time, so I am looking for products to use now and to take me into my curly-hair when I am ready to chop off the relaxer.  I bought the 2 oz jar of this product from the beauty supply, along with the Curly Buttercreme, just to try it out.  I must comment on price, since price is one of the things I consider when buying something.  I am one of those people who is willing to pay top dollar for beauty products...if they work.  I paid $10 for this 2 oz jar, which is $5 an ounce.  That is outrageous, even for me.Now first I'd like to say - I didn't use this incorrectly.  I wasn't trying to make my hair "look like a Jheri curl" and I didn't use half the jar at once - I followed the directions on the jar and the Miss Jessie's website.  My 2 oz jar has a small dent at the top where you can see I used the product, but the vast majority of the jar is still there, and I figure I could probably get 5-6 braid outs out of a 2 oz jar.  People shouldn't assume that just because something doesn't work for others the way it works for them, that those others must be using it wrong.  I know how to do a twist-out on my own hair.  I'm not super vigilant about product ingredients - I prefer natural products if possible but some of my favorite products do have silicone and other no-nos in them.  I'm a big proponent of "if it works for you, then it's great."  But the fact is Miss Jessie's uses mineral oil in the Curly Pudding, which is known to weigh down hair.  There's also silicone in this product which can give hair a slimy coating to it, especially naturally curly hair.In any case, I did a braid-out - washed my hair, then applied the pudding to sections and braided it.  I put the pudding between my palms, rubbed together, and smoothed my palms down the sections.  I do this to avoid overloading my hair with product.  I use very little because most of my hair is still relaxed so obviously it needs less product.  Then I braided, slept, the next morning unbraided slowly with a little oil on my fingers to prevent frizz.  The first day I would've given the CP four stars - my hair was shiny and beautiful-looking, but it was hard and stiff especially in the back (texture of my back is different than texture of my front).  The braid-out was well defined, more tightly curled and looked exactly how I like it.Over the course of the next week, though, my rating steadily decreased.  At night I pincurl my braid-out in big sections (I twist it around itself and then use a big bobby pin to secure) or I rebraid if I'm not too lazy.  This time I pincurled because I could tell the hold in this pudding was great.  I was right - my curls looked shiny and beautiful!  But they FELT horrible.  The second day they were dry, so I put a little Shea Moisture on them as I am wont to do.  A few hours later, it was dry again.  None of the moisturizer I used for the rest of the week did anything to make my hair moisturized again.  Even the Curly Buttercreme - actually, that was worse.  It just sat on top of my hair and made it limp and heavy.  I only put it on like two sections before I gave up on it completely.  Again - the curls were still defined, held like a champ and looked great.  But my hair felt like straw.  And even pincurling it at night didn't prevent the horrible snarls and tangles that I started getting by the third or fourth day.  My cousin touched my hair on day 5 and commented that it looked like it was locking up.  It was dry and brittle and my ends felt like they would turn to dust if I touched them too hard.I was like, I need this stuff out of my hair, so I went to find a gentle clarifying shampoo (I needed to clarify anyway but this stuff made it necessary).  When I rinsed under the water it indeed felt slimy and gross coming out of my hair.Now.  For some people Miss Jessie's really works for them and it is the best thing that ever happened to their hair.  Power to them - I'm glad they found something that works!  That's what the natural journey is all about.  For me, the facts are these:Pros:1. My hair looked shiny and pretty from the outside.2. The curl lasted for 5 days straight.Cons1. The price is too high!2. The product has ingredients that I'd rather not put in my hair.3. It made my hair stiff when I would prefer a softer hold.4. It made my hair dry and desperately thirsty.5. My hair became a tangly knotted mess.So I am still on the hunt for a twist-out/braid-out/setting creme!  And I don't think I'll be trying any more of Miss Jessie's products.

I purchased a Serpa CQC Size 25 for my M&P; 40 full size.  It fits nicely and the holster holds it securely.  There is a detent screw just under the release latch to snug things up if needed but I haven't had to touch it. When the pistol is inserted the latch engages it on the trigger guard and doesn't let go until you release it.  It is released by running your trigger finger across it as you draw, and doing this puts my hand in a perfect ready position.The paddle does a good job of securing the holster as it has a nice wide engagement surface with your hip, and it also has a hook at the bottom that catches on the inside and actually makes it a little tough to remove from my waist at times.  There are also two adjustable hooks on the outside that you rotate after the holster is in position so they catch under your belt.  So it's pretty secure on the inside and pretty secure on the outside.  I guess my point is that it's pretty secure for being a paddle.This is my first external holster and I haven't used it enough yet to determine whether I would like to move it from straight vertical, but I like that there are multiple positions to adjust the cant in either direction so I can experiment a little.  Overall I'm very pleased with it.

I bought this TV because I was looking for a small TV that had wireless media player capability for my new home gym. Didn't want to buy an additional media player and wireless dongle to attach to a TV and wanted these built in to the TV. Its hard to find a 24" TV that has the features this TV has, wireless, internet apps (Netflix, Blockbuster, Yahoo) and DLNA to play media files off my home network, it even has a 5Ghz wireless band.I was thrilled to find this TV but like others have commented, the sound is lacking, it's advertised to have 2 5 watt speakers as opposed to others in this size that have 2 3 watt speakers. Don't know if Toshiba's 5 watt spec is accurate because I can't hear this TV over the sound of my treadmill, I had to go out and buy a small wall mountable sound bar to be able hear the TV when I exercise. For those interested, it will play MP4 and AVI video files but not MKV files though I have some MP4 video files that play without sound. I have only tested MP3 music files and haven't found any problems so far playing music. It is also a little slow to respond to the remote functions like powering on and getting file information off of my network, sometimes it takes a few minutes before I can see the media files on my network. When it does start playing my media it doesn't have a problem buffering and seems to play movies and music without pauses and interruptions. I also don't think it up converts because some of my lower resolution movies look better on my big screen TV's but it plays 1080p media perfectly, it has a nice picture.I give it 4 stars because it is such a feature rich TV but deducting a star because of the above flaws.

When I first pulled this detangling brush from the package I was highly skeptical; the grip was nice but I and one of my two daughters have long and thick hair and the bristles did not seem to be capable of detangling such hair.Well, we went away for the weekend and I took this brush with me.  We were going swimming and chlorine really dries out the hair which even after a good washing and condition our hair tends to still be a bit of a tangled mess.  In the past tears would flow from my eldest daughter who has long and thick hair as I tried to get her chlorine dried hair under control.  We also had three other girls with us whose hair I would have to see too after swimming so I figured that would be the best time to try out this brush.It worked excellently on three girls with short hair.  I moved on to the two girls with long hair thinking the brush would fail to perform especially since one of them had hair that was thicker than the other.  To my surprise there were no tears, no "ouch", no fighting.  They sat there completely still as this brush made quick work of the tangles.  It was almost like magic!  Once that was all done we went and decided to put it through the most rigorous of tests we could dream up and that was my hair.  I have very long hair that has a lot of body.  To my knowledge every stylist I have ever gone to has declared it the thickest hair they have ever worked with as well.  My husband sat behind me and got to it.  I was absolutely floored and fell in love with this brush.  It worked through my hair as easily as it had worked through the other heads it had been tested on!This is hands down the BEST detangling product I have ever come across; no dentangling serum, no detangling spray, and most certainly no detangling brush has ever managed to perform to the standards it advertised, at least never on my hair!  While I received this brush as part of a product testing program in order to review I can honestly say if it wasn't something I got to keep and had to instead send it back or pay for it I would happily spend the money because it would be worth every penny!

This Winchester "Brass and Steel Punch Set" is decent overall quality and has most of the required punches needed for "everyday" gunsmithing tasks. We own a small gun shop that provides gunsmithing services and we also build custom AR15/M4s, so need a good set of punches. We have several sets, including some very expensive ones from another well known on-line source, but wanted a convenient small set to keep up at our sales counter for "light" work.This set contains:1) Small Hammer with a Steel Face and a Plastic Face2 & 3) two 1/16 Steel Punches4 & 5) two 3/32 Steel Punches6) 1/8 Brass Punch7) 9/64 Brass Punch8) 5/32 Brass Punch9) 3/16 Brass Punch10) 7/32 Brass Punch11) 1/4 Brass Punch12) 9/32 Brass Punch13) 15/16 Brass Punch14) 15/64 Plastic Punch15) 1/8 Plastic Punch16) Plastic Storage CaseThe brass and steel punches have nice knurled steel shafts that make them easy to grip, even when oily. The two plastic punches are useful when working on softer metals and fragile parts.This set will not stand up to a lot of abuse but if used correctly, and with due care, the punches should last a long time. Also, punches do break regularly, even the very expensive ones, so we consider them to be "consumable" and we replace them when they break.Recommended!CFH

We all have been bombarded by all those info commercials that say their product walks on water and if you are a skeptic like me then you will shake your head and say "YEAH RIGHT".  I have been watching the Wen commercial for a while before deciding to try it.  I am 43 and have reasonably good hair (fine hair that is wavy with barely any grey).  Lately though I had been feeling like my hair is blah.  It seems dull with no style to it even though I get it cut at an upscale salon.  I tried the Aveda Pure and Invati products.  Liked the Pure.  The Invati did not work for me.  One of the things that appealed to me about Wen is that it is 5 products in one bottle.  As the mom of a very active 6 year old, my motto is:  the simpler, the better.  I went to Sephora as I was told they sold Wen.  I would have ordered on Amazon but I did not want to wait.  All I can say is wow.  The commercials are true.  After just one shampoo with Wen my hair looked and felt different.  I didn't style it any differently.  However, my hair looked full and shiny.  Also, my hair tends to get really curly when I blow dry it without a brush and with Wen, it blow dried straighter.  I swear I did not do anything differently.It is a bit pricey at $32 and change at Sephora but look at it this way, you are getting 5 products in one.  Also by buying at Sephora and Amazon, you don't have to sign up and get a bottle every month like they do with Proactive.  I don't like that.  Also, the instruction says 10-15 pumps everytime you wash your hair but I think each person is going to find that they are going to use way more or less depending on the length of their hair.  My hair just touches my shoulder blade and I use 5 to 6 pumps.  If I were to use 10-15 pumps a shampoo then it would only last 2 weeks.  LOLOne more thing, with every other shampoo, I have to wash my hair every day because if I don't, it gets greasy. Ugh.  With Wen, my hair is not greasy the next day which confounds me because this is more of a conditioning shampoo so you would think with all the products in that one bottle, the hair would be greasy.  It is not.TRY IT ONCE AND YOU WILL SEE THE DIFFERENCE!

I've had one in my everyday carry bag for about a month now, and I've never actually tasted one of the bars.However, just last night I was checking in my bag and the "brick" of bars I have in there must have done something to puncture the mylar bag because I could tell it had lost its really nice vacuum.  It probably did get banged around as I'm walking with my bag at my side, so I can't say I'm surprised.So now I have a little brick with a lost vacuum, I figured I might as well try one since they probably don't last too long once the seal is broken.  To my surprise it wasn't too bad.  I don't know what exactly I imagined, but the taste was mildly sweet... enough that it reminded me of a super crumbly shortbread cookie.Yeah, super crumbly, but, you know, these are e-rations and I won't be too picky if this is all I have available.  I'm just glad it didn't taste horrible.  I could easily chow down on these if/when the time comes.So now I'm digging through my old plastic containers to see what I have that's the right size to fit a whole brick into.  Something that I can pop the brick into it, stuff it in my bag, and not worry about my flashlight or other miscellaneous stuff breaking the seal again.  But not a container so big that it's adding extra size to the already considerable brick of bars.I have my now-open brick in a plastic container now, just something I had, but you might check around and see what you have for such a purpose before you just toss one of these into your everyday carry or bug-out bags, otherwise you'll be sure to lose the vacuum seal, and then the clock will be ticking on the shelf-life.All in all though, good value.  I'm glad I had the chance to try one... now to do a taste test with the wife and hopefully she agrees.Oh, have water to go along with this... your mouth will thank you.

I ordered this at the same time as the CRKT Ripple (K415KXP). Side by side, the handle length is nearly identical and the blade length is very close (about .25 inches off). Yet somehow, this knife leaves me greatly wanting. In hand, the handle is just too short. I have a hard time explaining exactly why this is. The finger choil is in roughly the same position, but the handle is way too short. It's not comfortable, and that means I'm likely not to use it or even carry it.The blade steel is AUS-8. Not great, but there are worse steels. However, what is not okay is a blade that is so dull that it requires sharpening on unboxing. It is also very tight and is not easily deployed. I'm sure with use, it will probably break in and open easily one-handed, but from the manufacturer, its too tight.Blade lock-up is good, blade centering is spot on, and there is zero blade play.I really did want to like this knife. Maybe I should have gotten the RAT 1 instead.

This is my review for the 64 oz version of this water bottle.I got this water bottle as a backup for the field and camping trips to leave in the car or bag to replace those plastic bladders. It has done an excellent job of this. I really like that the cap is the same cap that fits on my 16 oz insulated Klean Kanteen bottle, since this means that, if I want, I can screw on the café cap. The loop cap (black plastic) only has metal touching the water, since the inside is metal.I have also used this water bottle as a growler for cider (no beer yet). It works well for this, but won't stay cold forever. I have cut up an old wading sock to use as a koozie for it. One thing to note is that if you are using it for something carbonated and it warms up while it is on its side, it will leak a little bit. This could probably be avoided by keeping it cold and upright and opening the bottle every so often to let some of the CO2 escape.Washing: If you use this for anything besides water, even if you wash it with soap, the bottle will still taste faintly of whatever you had in there last. I've found that doing one of these will get it out: 1) scrub it with baking soda, 2) pour boiling water in it and let it sit the minute (fastest option), or 3) put some vinegar and water in it, let sit for a day. If one of these options doesn't work, try the other ones.

O how so human to get tired of the familiar and wish for something else. This is how I began eyeing the Sony SS-F5000 speakers to replace my 25 year old Sony SS-U500 front speakers. The U500 came with the sound system and over the years I replaced each component with a later model (but kept the speakers).I spent well over a year checking out these Sony speakers at this website (Amazon.com) and at the Best Buy store, wondering if they sound as good as they look. Finally I ordered the SS-F5000 and was underwhelmed after set up. They are not bad speakers but given their height and rating you'd expect more from them. They have a rating of 150 watts (max) per speaker but they are not louder than the U500 with its 12 inch subwoofer (rated 80 watts max). They'll probably do well as surround back speakers or bedroom speakers, but not as front speakers in a standard size living room in a 5.1 or 7.1 sound system.I'm keeping the Sony SS-U500 old faithfuls as my front speakers, and will find another use for the new SS-F5000.

We moved into a house with mostly wood floors, and in the winter, they get COLD and regular socks just slip like crazy--and with a baby that I carry around all the time, slipping is NOT an option. I've tried many different type of grippy socks and slippers, and haven't loved any of them--the gripper dots usually come off within one or two washings or trips around the house. So when someone recommended these yoga socks, I had to give them a try.Unfortunately, I don't really like them. The grippy knobs are nice--they've stayed on through a few washings already and they're small enough that you can't feel them when walking on them (a problem with some other grippy socks). But the actual toe part of the socks? Egads. It's hard to get your toes in the holes and takes some finessing (something I don't feel you should have to do with something as basic as socks). Then, once you have them on, the socks feel SO thick they are almost uncomfortable (I now know I can't stand to have things between my toes).As far as sizing, I wear a 10-11 and I ordered the Large and they fit just right. They do help with not slipping on the wood floors, but the irritation they cause when wearing them push them to the back of my drawer.

As with most naturals, I go through a whole lotta conditioner. Some don't have the best slip, so I use those for my co-washes. Some have incredible slip and are great at moisturizing so I keep those for regular conditioning after shampoos. This is what I use for REAL moisturizing and detangling. It smells great and provides the slip I need to get my comb through this forest of curls with remarkable ease. Leave it on for about 10 minutes and it works even better. Though kinky curly hair does usually require more conditioner than straight hair (my curls laugh at the "dime size" amount recommended on most bottles), I have found that this product works so well, that I can use half the amount I use with other conditioners. I start by dividing my wet freshly washed hair into 4 sections and smoothing the conditioner on each one, combing through with a wide toothed comb, and then doing a quick twist when I'm done with each one. I then rinse my hair with the twists still in. When that's done, I can untwist each section and either add my favorite oil, gel or moisturizing product to each section and retwist, then spin them on either side of my head. The next day when my hair is dry, I'm rewarded with lovely, soft stretched out curls. This and Trader Joe's Nourish Spa Conditioner are my top picks for best conditioner for thirsty curls. Love this stuff and highly recommend it.

I put safety first with any bicycle accessory, so even though this MetroFlash light is inexpensive, it is one of the brightest and more efficient bike lights I have seen. The MetroFlash is powered by two AAA Batteries (it comes with zinc oxide batteries, but I would recommend switching to alkaline). The case breaks away with the use of a coin or key for installation of the batteries. The fit is snug and although not waterproof, I doubt it will ever have a problem is very wet weather, it is so tightly fit.The light goes on with a simple light push of a button and gives you three modes of flash. I was stunned at how bright the light was when I first turned it on. My eyes literally took a beating - that's good news for bikers and motorists.One mode simply has both lights on. The second mode has a fast flashing on/off sequence that is noticeable from very far away. The third mode is a quick alternating flash between the two lights. The lens on each light is slightly different to diffuse in two slightly different angled directions. This means that you are visible from a wide angle and not just from directly behind. This is a simple, inexpensive, but very smart design feature. Battery life claims to be about 124 hours, but that is tough to figure until you use a certain type of battery and gauge your usage.There is a mount included that allows you to tightly and securely attach it to your frame (with a rubberized gasket included), or use the clip for securing it to anything else - a backpack, belt, bicycle part, etc.For the price, this is a necessary feature for every bicyclist. Even at twice this cost, it would be a bargain. This product was generously supplied by the manufacturer for an honest review.

Bought this for a 5 day stay in a partially heated cabin with indoor temps down into the 50s and 60s at night...for about thirty bucks it did the job well, although I am one of those people whose body temp drops hugely when sleeping so I always wear more clothes when sleeping: a midweight fleece jacket with 1-2 tee shirts underneath, and microfiber pj pants.  The scoop top is very useful, if you flip the bag over (so that the scoop is on top, rather than on the bottom as shown in the product photo) then you can use it to cover part or all of your head, which is a godsend in a drafty location especially if you don't like to wear a cap while sleeping.Most of all, despite being of a lean medium build and average height, I appreciated the extra width and length of this sleeping bag, because I'm quite an active sleeper who kicks and thrashes and tosses and turns a lot while sleeping, so those little mummy style bags would drive me nuts.  The extra space does mean that when you first get into the bed, it takes a good 15-30 minutes for your accumulated body heat to warm up the bag, but that's a small price to pay for all-night freedom of movement.Has a nice 2-way double zipper, although I think the construction quality of the zipper could be better.  But as someone who will probably use this bag no more than 3-4 times a year, and always in a mild indoor environment (I hate camping!), this more than gets the job done.

First of all let's start saying that at the price I bought this T.V in Amazon ($579) this is a steal!!!. I have read almost all the reviews not only in here but in Cnet, Tiger Direct, etc. and I was afraid of the problems that some people mentioned. Let's go 1 by 1 so if you decide to buy this T.V you will avoid all the hassle others has been through.1, Problems with clarity of Signal in 3D while using games consoles etc. Bad 3d lenes.  To avoid any problems don't be cheap and stop using your old cables ot eh glasses you "forgot" to return in your movie theater. this is new technology and to enjoy it properly you have to keep with the new requirements. You don't have to break the bank to get the best of this T.V. I bought the new series 2 certified cables on this same web page the Mediabridge Ultra series ($9.99) and 3 pairs of new RealD compatible Polarized Glasses ($8.51). The best money you ever spend!.In my case the 3D image is crisp, vibrant and left almost nothing to desire in comparison to a $2.000 T.V with lenses costing $200 each!!! Yes you have to be in front of the T.V because when you moved to the sides or too much up or down the image deteriorate a bit but give me a $1.000 in savings and I will seat in the middle happy with the money I saved!!!2. Too much Ghosting - Soap Opera effect, not as vibrant image in 2D  than "more Expensive" T.Vs.The solution for these problems in my case was rather simple. I follow some of the advices from C.Rickel "Turn the auto-motion off. On this Vizio, Menu>Picture>More>Advanced>Smooth Picture Mode and turn it to "off." Always can be turned back on for a game.:  I also changed the picture quality to "Game". I adjusted the colors to increase blue to 130 decrease green 110, increase red 130. Turn off the light dimming thing. The soap opera effect disappeared, not more ghosting but a clear vibrant image.  I get my signal from a Time Warner Box that actually only sends signal at 720p (lower end of HD but some theorist and studies actually recommend this for watching fast paced action games and action movies). I get my 3d signal from a 3d/High def capable DVD.3. Bad Sound. Yes, this Vizio won't win any sound or aesthetic competition or put Bose on the brink of Bankruptcy but you can play with the settings as well.  I set "the surround sound on", "Reduced Noise" is "On" and the sound improved a bit still kind of flat. I have not done that yet but I think I will buy the VIZIO VSB200 Universal HD Sound Bar ($100) I already saved around $1.000 in comparison with the TV's I was looking for so this won't be a biggie. Sound on the other hand is "Acceptable" bass is the one that is missed like in all cheap speakers. Trust me if you are a regular user the sound is average/acceptable for a regular size room.4. Useless remote Control. Read lots of complains with the remote. Have this people ever complain while sending a text message in a I-phone or other cell phones? In comparison to the cellular phones QWERTY keyboards the keys in this remote are actually BIG and have some spacing between the keys (Try that with an i-phone 4s for example).  Yes the keys felt a little bit soft and unresponsive you have to press them to the bottom but they do the work again a regular user that has not read the negative comments most likely would not even notice.So far when I Bought the T.V there were 205 "5 stars" reviews from a 314 total reviews that means a absolute positive factor of almost 65% add the 4 stars (56 at the time I bought the T.V) and the positive appreciation factor jumps to 86%!!! I read the negatives and it is my understanding that for bad luck or careless shipping these unfortunate people received defective units. Remember there are almost around 3-5% of defective units not matter the quality of production line this is almost a fact of life and also there is around 5% of people that would never be happy with anything....Get your T.V, make your kiddos happy, save yourself some money and start enjoying 3D. I hope I help you with this, this is a great T.V good size and more than capable in any aspect you consider, easy to set up my wireless network works the apps like a charm and by the way all of this for a GREAT PRICE!!

I've used two other handlebar mounts to secure Garmin GPS devices to my dirt bike's bars. This is the first one that's actually impressed me.The RAM mount holds the GPS right where you want it with very little vibration.You can quickly adjust the bracket's arm to position the GPS wherever you want it (almost).The mount itself (the part that actually holds the GPS) is firm enough to securely hold the GPS with no worries that it might fall out, but it's also really simple to insert and remove the GPS. The top fingers of the mount spread apart with a little pressure and the GPS just slides out (no tools or adjustments required).My only complaint is that the bracket's arm swivels to any position you want, but once you have it positioned, you can't necessarily rotate the GPS in every direction (tilt up/down, tilt side to side, and rotate clockwise/counterclockwise), so you sometimes have to compromise between the angle of the GPS and the position of the arm. The workable positions aren't infinite, but there are enough good positions that it's not hard to find one that's OK.I'm not an engineer, so I don't know how they could improve that without sacrificing the security of the bracket arm's connection and the ease of adjustment.So it's actually 4.75 stars, but I'll give it a 5.

I bought this for the 1st time in Grapefruit & tried it out on a cashmere cardigan (I refuse to dry clean clothes as far as I can help it). I was intrigued by the claim that you don't have to rinse when using Eucalan. I followed instructions but decided to rinse gently just once in fresh water - I didn't want the old dirty soak water to remain in the cashmere. I was impressed how much dirt came out from soaking. I squeezed out the sweater & laid it out to dry on a towel - the next day it was SO SOFT & FLUFFY! Cashmere is known to be very soft, warm & cozy, BUT on my sensitive skin it's still uncomfortably itchy (neck & chest), so I must always wear an undershirt beneath it. The lanolin in Eucalan must have softened the yarn fibers so well that it no longer itches my bare skin after washing. It's like a super fabric softener as well. I LOVE THIS PRODUCT! I only wish I learned about a long time ago. Now I can REALLY enjoy wearing cashmere more often since I know it'll come out softer than ever. It's so easy to use this product & it takes surprisingly little to do the job!

I bought this from Best Buy and the more I use it, the more i love it. Being an audio engineer, I'm picky but don't expect much from this type of sound system, but you won't find a better sounding player for the price.I compared this to about 10 other similar products in the Best Buy store and this one was hands down the best for sound, especially bass and volume. All others sounded flimsy and thin comparatively.The setup was fast and easy and is intuitive to use and accepted my older generation iPod classic without issue. The remote needs to be pointed right at the box, but that's no big deal. The remote will control your iPods volume (this prevents speakers from being blown out), next and previous tracks, pause/play, and turn repeat/shuffle on and off.What's especially sweet about this box are two things:1) The rechargeable batteries. It has about 7-8 hours of battery life depending on how loud you keep your volume. If the batteries die after multiple uses, they can be replaced with another set of batteries, so you don't have to dispose of the whole boombox.2) Aux in. If you don't have an iPod, there is a 1/8" headphone jack input for any other device you may have - even your computer.This quite possibly the best sounding portable boom box I have heard under $150. Truly worth every penny.

I was impressed with the build quality of this scope, when first removing it from the box. It is well built, with nice rubberized armor, and the illumintaed blue rectile is nice and bright. Unfortunately, I purchased this scope without knowing what its eye relief TRULY was. The description says it's 3", but after measuring it myself, it is more like 2-1/4"...way too short. Typical rifle stocks have a length of pull (LOP) of 13.5", but to use this scope without neck strain, you need a LOP of about 9", and I don't know of any stock that is made that small. I would have to break out my table saw, and cut off over 4" of my stock to get the proper eye relief with this scope! It leaves me to wonder what kind of rifle stock this scope, and others like it, are intended to be used with. I had this scope set back as far is it would go, and even switched my weaver mount backwards to give it another inch, but it was still too far away from my eye. Even if I had a LOP that could accomodate this smaller scope, I would still be worried about getting a Barska tattoo on my eyeball from the recoil. I can almost feel my eyelash touching the lens on this scope!Also, as other reviewers have mentioned, the scope's focus adjustment leaves much to be desired. Yes, the sight picture can be focused by turning the focus adjustment on the eye peice, but you only get 1-1/2 turns out of it, and it is counter clockwise only. So, if your target is at a distance of over 150 yards, and your sight picture is a little blurry, you're stuck with it...turning the focus adjustment only makes it worse, because you can only focus in one direction. For targets at medium range, say 75-100 yards, that may be a little out of focus, adjusting the focus does help, but for long range targets, forget it! I also found that adjusting the focus more than 1 full turn, makes the crosshairs extremely blury...to the point where the bullet drop tick marks are no longer visible, and this has nothing to do with eyesight, or background focusing. I thought the whole purpose of focus adjustment was to improve the sight picture? I have never seen a scope blur out the crosshairs like this one, regardless of magnification setting.So, it's a well built scope as far as construction goes, but functionality is horrid at best. Barska seems to be making more and more crap these days, and this was my 3rd attempt to give them the chance to redeem themselves. So, I will be looking for a different brand from now on, and this scope is going back to Amazon for a refund. This might be a nice scope for a crossbow, or very small carbine with a short LOP, but for a full sized target rifle, it's useless. I love the look of short scopes, but it seems like if you want long eye relief, you have to buy a longer scope.

So...it's supposed to come formatted for PC or Mac, which I would think would mean I can plug it in to either of those computers and it would show up as a disk drive so I could start dragging files to copy to it. Nope. This is 100% not the case... Instead, it shows up as a "virtual CD drive" and requires you to install their shabby software and use their ridgid automatic backup system. You select the files and folders you want to back up and it automatically backs them up. And that's it. It shows up as a hard drive that you do not have the option to write to...Every other external hard drive I have used shows up instantly as an external drive which you can immediately start using. Including the Western Digital drives I have had before. They often come with backup software but I have never heard of or seen one like that seems to only work with their software (which works poorly). I planned to use this as a backup drive on my wife's Mac. I tried plugging it into my Windows laptop to see if it came up any different...maybe I could use windows to format it and then use it however I wanted. Nope. In Windows it still showed up as an unwritable CD drive.Fortunately I found some forums on the web which told of how people were able to format these drives to use as a regular external hard drive. I opened Disk Utility in Mac OS X and did a partition format. Wiped the drive. Bam. Now I can actually use it. It was a pain and I had to follow other people's exact instructions. I basically feel I had to "hack" this hard drive to be able to use it.So...I recommend this drive based on price alone. It seems to work fine, runs quietly and not screaming speed but alright. However, I would not recommend this to anyone with expectations that it would be easy to use. And next time I will pay a little more for a better brand. I will not buy a Western Digital again.

I felt guilty for giving this iron a bad review previously, so am back to edit now.  I previously said that I'd used this iron for 2 weeks and thought it weighed my hair down.  I've now used it for a few more weeks and I think what was weighing my hair down was the tresseme heat protector spray.  I only wash my hair every 3rd day, so I kept using that spray every day I ironed my hair.  I suppose that probably isn't necessary (?) Now, I spray once, well, the 1st day, and then on the following days, I do not spray near the roots at all, but just a little on the ends and my hair is not weighed down as much.  It wasn't this iron's fault!  So I feel bad for saying it was ;(  Anyway, this iron heats up very quickly and beeps to let you know when it's ready.  Also, you can lock the temperature in by just pressing the minus key and holding it down.  Some more expensive irons don't have this feature and the temperature or power buttons are easy to hit when just trying to hold the iron.  The lowest setting is 300 degrees and I've found that even this temperature can straighten my hair and then i don't risk as much damage.  But, I've had some help straightening it too.  I had a Brazilian Blowout 4 months ago, and I do straighten it as a blow-dry it too.I've been using this iron daily for 2 weeks now. I think the blowout may have worn out by now though!?  About.com says that the plates of this flatiron have a porous ceramic coating that actually emits a silicon-based conditioner and that the liquid inside it lasts about 4 years.  I wonder why they don't put that on the outside of the box somewhere, or in the user manual inside the box?  I wonder if maybe they want customers to think the conditioning properties could last 4-ever?  Regardless of it being a good iron for the money, I've already ordered a different iron from amazon.com: BaByliss Pro BABNT3073T Nano Titanium Straightening Iron, only because the length of the plates on this one are so short, that I can't get a good-sized chunk of hair in it without pieces falling off the ends of the plate.  I'm thinking I could possibly save time ironing my hair if I were able to straighten larger chunks of hair at a time!  As for drugstore irons though (I got mine at Target) - I'd say this is about the best one for the money that you're going to find.

A 5-star camera with 0-star packaging.Now that GoPro is a global brand and traded publicly, I'm hoping they don't need to use packaging as a way to aggrandize their brand.  I'm wondering if they can re-think their packaging to be more in alignment with conservation and other forward thinking companies like Apple (Have you seen how a Mac Mini ships?).  There is so much plastic and paper waste involved in buying a GoPro  (buy a GoPro battery back and see how a small item is entombed in the stuff) - it may look cool because it creates the feeling that the product is floating inside empty space but I personally can't feel good about buying product that has so much packaging waste.  It makes me feel guilty. Why not put it in a cool cardboard box with an awesome drawing or a small window cut-out instead?  After getting all the accessories, I had a mountain of waste to deal with for relatively tiny objects.  It was a bummer.Save the planet so we can continue to photograph its beauty.  Reduce packing waste.My background with GoPro: My photos have been featured on GoPro's websites (and on instagram, Facebook, etc) and two have also been Photo of the Day.  I love their products and their company and the H3+ is amazing and I fully support the company.  Here's one:  http://gopro.com/photos/photo-of-the-day/2013/2/8/It would be awesome if others let GoPro know that we don't need this much packaging hoopla for such a small device.  Thanks.

Afterwards you can switch to any registered device already present on your account yourself.Yes, you do need to either have a new phone number or be prepared to login to your Vonage account and change which device you use. Then it can be worthwhile.What I don't know is if this device will work with wireless connections since it is slower than using wired. I will find out later on.

Some background is needed before I get into a review of this product. I purchased the Archos 5 MIT when it first came out for close to $500.00. I got the unit and the only thing good I could say about it was the screen was outstanding. Everything else about it really did not work between freezing and rebooting and not syncing my music I returned the unit. Life went on with my Ipod Touch, Zen vision W and my new Zune HD. I got sick of Apple and the touch the 3rd gen was not much of an upgrade and knew that apple would continue to milk things out year after year trying to get you to buy the next unit. Also being tied to Apple and Itunes was no fun I like to use Amazon Unbox for my Tivo and my Zen Vision W. Now the Zune HD very nice product top of the line screen but the support / growth of the system is taking way to long the app store is a joke and still no video play list and I want bigger screen size.Now the Archos 7 MIT a unit I thought I would never buy muchless look at but the price drop the HD size and the screen size said hey take a look. I read just about every review I could find on the unit both good bad and very bad.I accept the following about the unit:1) The program guide will not work in USA2) Have to buy plug-ins3) The brower will not play all video on the netKeeping those in mind I have to say I am very happy with the unit. The screen is great the size is perfect (ok it does not fit in my pocket) I have added all my music and my music video's. I have also added my Amazon Unbox shows and movies and I still have well over 100gigs left to fill. Again keeping in mind the 3 items above I have to say the unit exceeds what I was expecting.If you are looking for a great video player that also happens to have wifi and offers some nice add-ons you will love this player. Just do not buy it for DVR stuff and heavy web browsing you will be very happy with this unit. You can find alot of the add-ons here on Amazon and also on Ebay.The only add-ons I am looking at is the mini dock or the dvr dock if I can find it used at a good price and mybe the video podcast plugin but you could by pass the plug in by converting the podcast your self with a ton of free software [...]Alot of reviews said the unit does work great until you hit around the 30 day mark. Well I will post any update to this review if anything goes wrong with the unit. Beyond adding more memory, IMO this is the best thing you can do for your computer. I have done a lot of profiling of code and the amount of time the CPU sits around waiting for data because the hard disk is relatively slow is amazing.  For years CPU's got faster and faster and it was barely noticeable because if you had any disk I/O the bottleneck was at the hard drive.  Everyone can relate to a bloated windows or ms office application (ie outlook) that would sit there and do God knows what for lengthy periods of time while you are essentially waiting. No more.  Forget about any "please wait" messages.  Finally decent SSD's at affordable prices are now available.  My current machine does not even have a hard drive.  I back up to one but I don't want it in my computer.  I have two of the 960GB crucials in my box and it is sweet.  No noise, low power kick ass speed far more reliable than a mechanical drive. Finally the CPU can be used to it's maximum.Boots up in seconds now.  Outlook pops open in a second or two as well.  Ubuntu (linux) boots up like you turn on light a light switch.  No more pauses with software.  What is really impressive is a full disk search.  Hit the return, look at the screen and it is done.If you have a computer and it is a few years old and it lags as the hard drive sounds like it is making popcorn...get one one of these. and ditch the mechanical drive or use it as a storage depot.  Do not put an OS on a hard disk, put it on the SSD and your life will be a lot more pleasant.Yes they cost  more than hard drives but this is worth every last cent and you will not regret your purchase. It will make your old computer feel like a brand new one.  This is one of those purchases that will have you asking why you waited so long to do it.Best of luck...
